# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:**
> - **Part A (3 marks × 4 Q's)** — 12 marks total (Module 1 = 1 question minimum)
> - **Part B (14 marks × ~5 Q's with internal choice)** — 70 marks total
> - Each Part B question has sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concentration Ratio and its Significance
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

> **Q:** Define **concentration ratio** of a solar collector. Why is it essential for **solar thermal electric power generation** but not for domestic water heating?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The concentration ratio $C$ is defined as the ratio of the **aperture area** $A_a$ (where solar radiation enters) to the **absorber area** $A_r$ (where the radiation is focused):

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$

- For an **FPC**: $C = 1$ (no concentration), and operating temperature is limited to ~ 80°C.
- For **concentrating collectors** (trough, dish, tower): $C$ ranges from 15 to 3000, enabling temperatures of 150–1500°C.

For **solar thermal electric power generation**, high temperatures (> 300°C) are essential to drive a **Rankine or Stirling cycle** efficiently. Concentrators achieve these temperatures. For **domestic water heating**, low temperatures (40–80°C) suffice, so an FPC is adequate and cheaper. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: Selective Surface Coating
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **Q:** What is a **selective surface** in a flat plate collector? State its desired properties.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **selective surface** is a specialized coating applied to the absorber plate that exhibits **high solar absorptance** ($\alpha \approx 0.95$) in the visible/near-IR spectrum (0.3 – 2.5 μm) and **low thermal emittance** ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$) in the far-IR spectrum (2.5 – 50 μm) at operating temperatures.

**Desired properties:**
1. High $\alpha$ (> 0.9) — absorbs maximum sunlight.
2. Low $\varepsilon$ (< 0.1) — minimizes re-radiation loss.
3. Thermal stability at operating temperature (up to 200°C).
4. Weather resistance and durability.
5. Good adhesion to the absorber substrate.

**Common examples:** Black chrome, blue titanium nitride ($\text{TiN}_x\text{O}_y$), black nickel. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 pattern, each Part B question provides internal choice. We present Question A and its alternative Question B below.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation & FPC Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Most Expected]**

> **Q:** (a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** for the instantaneous thermal efficiency of a flat plate solar collector. State clearly the assumptions made.
>
> (b) **[7 Marks]** A flat plate solar collector has the following specifications:
> - $A_c = 2.5 \, m^2$, $F_R = 0.82$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.83$, $U_L = 5.0 \, W/m^2K$, $G_T = 750 \, W/m^2$, $T_{in} = 35°C$, $T_a = 28°C$, $\dot{m} = 0.015 \, kg/s$, $c_p = 4180 \, J/kgK$.
> Calculate: (i) Useful heat gain $Q_u$, (ii) Outlet temperature $T_{out}$, (iii) Collector efficiency $\eta_{FPC}$, (iv) The fluid temperature rise at which efficiency becomes zero.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Refer to **SECTION 3, Derivation 1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The student should present the 9-step derivation from energy balance to the final boxed Bliss equation:

$$\boxed{\eta_{FPC} = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{in} - T_a}{G_T}}$$

**Assumptions to state explicitly (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. One-dimensional heat flow.
3. Uniform solar flux on the absorber.
4. Constant fluid and material properties.
5. Bottom and side losses negligible (well-insulated).
6. Linear fluid temperature rise along the collector.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**(i) Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ G_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

Substituting:

$$G_T (\tau\alpha) = 750 \times 0.83 = 622.5 \, W/m^2$$

$$U_L (T_{in} - T_a) = 5.0 \times (35 - 28) = 5.0 \times 7 = 35 \, W/m^2$$

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.82 \times (622.5 - 35) = 2.5 \times 0.82 \times 587.5$$

$$Q_u = 2.05 \times 587.5 = 1204.4 \, W$$

$$\boxed{Q_u \approx 1.20 \, kW}$$

**(ii) Outlet Temperature:**

$$T_{out} = T_{in} + \frac{Q_u}{\dot{m} c_p} = 35 + \frac{1204.4}{0.015 \times 4180}$$

$$= 35 + \frac{1204.4}{62.7} = 35 + 19.21 = 54.21°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{out} \approx 54.2°C}$$

**(iii) Collector Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{FPC} = \frac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = \frac{1204.4}{2.5 \times 750} = \frac{1204.4}{1875} = 0.6423$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FPC} \approx 64.2\%}$$

**(iv) Stagnation Temperature (Efficiency = 0):**

Set $\eta_{FPC} = 0$:

$$0 = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{T_{stag} - T_a}{G_T}$$

$$F_R U_L \frac{T_{stag} - T_a}{G_T} = F_R (\tau\alpha)$$

$$T_{stag} - T_a = \frac{G_T (\tau\alpha)}{U_L} = \frac{622.5}{5.0} = 124.5°C$$

$$T_{stag} = 28 + 124.5 = 152.5°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{stag} = 152.5°C}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: Solar Concentrators & TES

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Most Expected]**

> **Q:** (a) **[7 Marks]** Compare **Parabolic Trough**, **Parabolic Dish**, and **Central Tower** collectors in terms of concentration ratio, tracking mechanism, working fluid, achievable temperature, and applications. Mention the typical optical efficiency of each.
>
> (b) **[7 Marks]** A solar thermal power plant uses **molten salt thermal energy storage** (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃). The hot tank holds 1500 tonnes of salt at 565°C, the cold tank at 290°C. The specific heat is $1.55 \, kJ/kgK$. The plant generates 50 MW of electricity for 6 hours.
> Calculate: (i) Mass of salt in the hot tank, (ii) Total thermal energy stored, (iii) Round-trip efficiency if the input thermal energy was 1200 MWh, (iv) Justify why molten salt (and not water/steam) is used in this system.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

Present a detailed comparison table (refer to **Section 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix** for full data). Key points to include:

| Feature | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Central Tower |
|---------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Concentration Ratio** | 15–80 | 500–3000 | 300–1500 |
| **Tracking** | Single-axis | Two-axis | Heliostats (two-axis) |
| **HTF** | Therminol oil | Air/He/H₂ | Molten salt / Sodium |
| **Temperature** | 300–400°C | 500–800°C | 500–1000°C |
| **Optical Efficiency** | 0.70–0.80 | 0.85–0.90 | 0.70–0.80 |
| **Application** | Utility power | Distributed power | Utility power + storage |

(7 marks for the table and 2-3 lines of explanation for each type)

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Molten Salt TES (7 marks)

**(i) Mass of Salt:**

Given:
- Plant output = 50 MW × 6 h = 300 MWh of electricity
- Typical Rankine cycle efficiency = 40% (assumed or given)
- Hence, thermal energy required = $300/0.40 = 750 \, MWh$

If input thermal energy is given as **1200 MWh** (from part iv wording), use 1200 MWh:

$$Q_{TES} = m c_p (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

$$1200 \, MWh = m \times 1.55 \, kJ/kgK \times (565 - 290)$$

Convert: $1200 \, MWh = 1200 \times 3600 \, MJ = 4,320,000 \, MJ = 4.32 \times 10^9 \, kJ$

$$m = \frac{4.32 \times 10^9}{1.55 \times 275} = \frac{4.32 \times 10^9}{426.25} = 1.0135 \times 10^7 \, kg$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 10,135 \, tonnes}$$

*Note: If 1500 tonnes was already given as the salt mass, the student would compute thermal energy from it, not mass. Re-read the problem carefully — KTU gives either the mass or the energy; compute the other.*

**Correcting interpretation:** If **1500 tonnes** = $1.5 \times 10^6$ kg is the *given* mass, then:

$$Q_{TES} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.55 \times 275 = 6.39 \times 10^8 \, kJ = 639,375 \, MJ$$

Convert to MWh: $639,375 / 3600 = 177.6 \, MWh$ (thermal).

**(ii) Total Thermal Energy Stored:**

$$\boxed{Q_{TES} \approx 177.6 \, MWh_{th}}$$

**(iii) Round-Trip Efficiency:**

If the electrical output for 6 h = 50 × 6 = 300 MWh, but the available thermal energy is only 177.6 MWh, the cycle efficiency gives electric output = $177.6 \times 0.40 = 71.04 \, MWh_e$.

Round-trip efficiency = $\frac{\text{Electric out}}{\text{Thermal in stored}}$:

Assuming input thermal energy to storage was 1200 MWh (per the problem), the **storage round-trip efficiency** is the ratio of energy out to energy in:

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{177.6}{1200} \times 100\% \approx 14.8\%$$

This is unusually low — KTU may instead be asking for cycle efficiency. The exact answer depends on interpretation. (3 marks for method)

**(iv) Justification — Why Molten Salt (3 marks):**

1. **High operating temperature range** (290–565°C) — higher than water/steam can store without pressure.
2. **High volumetric heat capacity** (compact storage).
3. **Non-flammable, non-toxic** (safer than synthetic oils).
4. **Low vapor pressure** at 565°C — eliminates need for thick pressure vessels.
5. **Reusable over thousands of cycles** (10,000+).
6. **Low cost** compared to liquid sodium or therminol oil.

(3 marks for the justification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in TES calculations** — Always convert kJ ↔ MJ ↔ MWh consistently. $1 \, MWh = 3600 \, MJ = 3.6 \times 10^6 \, kJ$.
> 2. **Confusing $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$** in the Hottel-Whillier equation — use **$T_{in}$** (inlet) for first-order analysis.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by $F_R$** — students often write $\eta = (\tau\alpha) - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)/G_T$ and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Stagnation temperature formula** is a frequent 2-mark question — memorize $T_{stag} = T_a + G_T(\tau\alpha)/U_L$.
> 5. **Concentration ratio of FPC is 1** — students write 1.5 or 2, losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Molten salt composition** — 60% $\text{NaNO}_3$ + 40% $\text{KNO}_3$ is the KTU standard.
> 7. **Tracking types** — Trough = single-axis; Dish & Heliostats = two-axis. A common 3-mark question.
> 8. **For the FPC efficiency, ensure $G_T$ is in W/m² and $U_L$ is in W/m²K** — units cancellation is the most-failed step in KTU answer scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Solar Thermal Systems**

### 🔑 Critical Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ **Solar Thermal Collector** = A device that absorbs solar radiation and transfers the heat to a working fluid.
- ✅ **Concentration Ratio (C)** = $A_a / A_r$ = 1 for FPC, 15–80 for Trough, 500–3000 for Dish, 300–1500 for Tower.
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency** = $Q_u / (A_c G_T)$ = Useful heat ÷ Incident solar energy.
- ✅ **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation** = $\eta_{FPC} = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a)/G_T$.
- ✅ **Selective Surface** = High $\alpha$ (≈0.95), low $\varepsilon$ (≈0.05).
- ✅ **Heliostat** = Two-axis tracking flat mirror.

### 🔑 Key Numbers to Remember
- ✅ **Solar disk half-angle** $\theta_s = 0.265°$ → $C_{max,2D} \approx 46,050$; $C_{max,1D} \approx 216$.
- ✅ **FPC temperature range:** 40–80°C.
- ✅ **Molten salt storage:** 290°C → 565°C, $c_p = 1.55$ kJ/kgK.
- ✅ **Typical $F_R$:** 0.7–0.9.
- ✅ **Typical $U_L$:** 4–8 W/m²K.
- ✅ **Typical $(\tau\alpha)$:** 0.75 (double-glazed) to 0.85 (single-glazed).

### 🔑 Collector Comparison Points (for 7-mark answers)
- ✅ **FPC:** No tracking, water heating, $C = 1$.
- ✅ **Trough:** Single-axis, oil HTF, Rankine cycle, 300–400°C.
- ✅ **Dish:** Two-axis, Stirling engine, 500–800°C, highest efficiency.
- ✅ **Tower:** Heliostat field, molten salt, utility-scale, 500–1000°C.

### 🔑 TES Quick-Reference
- ✅ **Sensible:** $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ (e.g., water, molten salt, concrete).
- ✅ **Latent:** $Q = mL$ (e.g., paraffin PCM, salt hydrates).
- ✅ **Thermochemical:** $Q = m \Delta H_{rxn}$ (e.g., CaO/Ca(OH)₂).

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps
- ⚠️ Always use **$T_{in}$**, not $T_{out}$, in the Bliss equation.
- ⚠️ Always convert $G_T$ to W/m² and $U_L$ to W/m²K for unit consistency.
- ⚠️ Molten salt mass: $m = Q_{TES} / [c_p (T_{hot} - T_{cold})]$.
- ⚠️ Stagnation temperature: $T_{stag} = T_a + G_T (\tau\alpha) / U_L$.
- ⚠️ Concentration ratio for **FPC = 1** (not 2, not 1.5).

### 🔑 High-Yield 2-Mark Conceptual Questions
1. **Distinguish between PV and CSP.**
2. **Why is a selective surface used in FPCs?**
3. **Why are 2-axis trackers needed for dishes but not troughs?**
4. **What is the role of the glazing in an FPC?**
5. **Why is thermal energy storage essential for round-the-clock solar power?**
6. **What is a heliostat?**
7. **List two examples each for sensible, latent, and thermochemical TES.**

### 🔑 KTU 2024 Module 1 Must-Know Topics (Priority Order)
1. 🥇 **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** (derivation + numerical) — guaranteed 7–14 marks.
2. 🥈 **Concentration ratio** (definition + comparison among collector types) — 3–7 marks.
3. 🥉 **Thermal Energy Storage** (sensible/latent/thermochemical + molten salt numerical) — 3–7 marks.
4. 🏅 **Solar Air Heater** (types + applications) — 3 marks.
5. 🏅 **Solar Concentrators** (trough/dish/tower comparison) — 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible Heat Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensible Heat Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** is a method of thermal energy storage in which the heat energy is stored by raising the temperature of a storage medium, without any change in its phase (i.e., without melting, vaporizing, or undergoing any chemical transformation). The term *sensible* originates from the Latin *sensibilis*, meaning "that which can be felt or measured by the senses" — referring specifically to the fact that the temperature change can be directly sensed or measured using a thermometer.

Mathematically, the energy stored in a Sensible Heat Storage system is given by the fundamental relation:

$$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $Q_{stored}$ = Sensible heat energy stored (Joules, J)
- $m$ = Mass of the storage medium (kilograms, kg)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat capacity of the medium at constant pressure (J/(kg·K))
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature change (T_final − T_initial) (Kelvin, K or °C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sensible Heat Storage is one of the three principal modes of thermal energy storage (TES) taught in the Renewable Energy Engineering course. The other two are **Latent Heat Storage (LHS)** and **Thermochemical Storage (TCS)**. For KTU examinations, students must clearly distinguish SHS by emphasizing the *absence of phase change* during the entire charging and discharging cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a large drum of water placed on a gas stove. As the flame heats the water, you can place your hand near the drum and *feel* the drum getting progressively hotter. The temperature of the water is rising continuously — this is **sensible** heating because you can directly sense the change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Analogy — The Hot Water Bottle:**
> A traditional rubber hot water bottle is one of the simplest examples of a Sensible Heat Storage device. Hot water (the storage medium) at, say, 80 °C is poured into the bottle. As the water cools to room temperature (~25 °C), it releases its stored sensible heat gradually, keeping you warm. The water remains in the *liquid phase* throughout — no phase change occurs. The amount of warmth stored depends on three things: **how much water** (mass), **how hot it was to begin with** (temperature), and **the inherent ability of water to hold heat** (specific heat capacity).

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance in SHS |
|----------|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Specific Heat Capacity | $c_p$ | **J/(kg·K)** | Inherent property of the storage medium |
| Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | Determines volumetric energy density |
| Mass | $m$ | **kg** | Quantity of medium |
| Temperature Change | $\Delta T$ | **K or °C** | Operational temperature swing |
| Stored Energy | $Q$ | **J (or kWh)** | Useful energy extracted per cycle |
| Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v$ | **kJ/m³** or **kWh/m³** | Energy stored per unit volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Reminder:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$. This is the standard unit used in the electrical and renewable energy industries, while the SI unit **Joule (J)** is the academic standard.

### 1.4 Why Sensible Heat Storage Matters in Renewable Energy Systems

SHS plays a **critical bridging role** in the renewable energy transition because most renewable sources (solar thermal, geothermal, and waste heat recovery systems) are inherently *intermittent* or *time-mismatched* with demand. Sensible Heat Storage provides a low-cost, high-reliability, and technologically mature solution for time-shifting thermal energy. For example, parabolic trough Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants routinely use molten salt SHS tanks to store 4–15 hours of thermal energy, enabling electricity generation even after sunset.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operational cycle of a Sensible Heat Storage system can be broken down into three discrete stages, each governed by well-defined heat transfer principles:

**Stage 1 — Charging (Heat Input Phase):**
- The storage medium (e.g., water, molten salt, rocks) is held inside an insulated containment vessel.
- A heat transfer fluid (HTF) or a direct heat source delivers thermal energy to the medium.
- The internal energy of the medium increases, which manifests as a rise in measurable temperature.
- Energy is transferred by **conduction** within the solid medium and by **convection** in liquid/gaseous media.

**Stage 2 — Storage (Holding Phase):**
- The charging inlet is closed. The medium is now thermally isolated (ideally).
- Heat losses occur through the vessel walls, top, and bottom; these are minimized using high-quality thermal insulation (e.g., mineral wool, vacuum insulation panels, aerogels).
- The temperature remains nearly constant, so the stored energy is preserved.

**Stage 3 — Discharging (Heat Output Phase):**
- A cold heat transfer fluid passes through the vessel, extracting heat from the medium.
- The medium's temperature falls, releasing the stored sensible heat.
- The extracted heat is delivered to the end-use application (e.g., steam generation, space heating, industrial process heat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind SHS Efficiency:** The efficiency of an SHS system is fundamentally limited by two factors: (1) the **thermodynamic irreversibilities** of heat transfer (always accompanied by entropy generation), and (2) the **standing heat losses** from the storage vessel to the surroundings. Both factors are minimized by excellent insulation and by choosing storage media with high $c_p$ and large $\Delta T$ capability.

### 2.2 Classification of Sensible Heat Storage Media

SHS media are broadly classified into three families based on their physical state:

| Media Class | Common Examples | Typical $c_p$ (J/(kg·K)) | Typical Operating Range | Key Application |
|-------------|------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Liquid Media** | Water, Thermal oils, Molten salts (e.g., Solar Salt — 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) | 4186 (water), 1520 (Solar Salt) | 0 – 100 °C (water), 220 – 565 °C (molten salt) | Domestic hot water, CSP, district heating |
| **Solid Media** | Rocks, Sand, Concrete, Cast iron, Ceramics | 800 – 1100 (concrete), 920 (sand) | Up to 600 – 1000 °C | Packed-bed storage, industrial furnaces |
| **Gaseous Media** | Air, Steam, Flue gases | 1005 (air at 300 K) | Very wide | Air-based solar collectors, regenerators |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on Sensible Heat Storage. Use $\vert$ (or the LaTeX $\vert$ command) for absolute value brackets inside table cells to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables and Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Sensible Heat (Basic) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ (J), $m$ (kg), $c_p$ (J/(kg·K)), $\Delta T$ (K) | Foundational energy storage equation |
| 2 | Mass-based Form | $m = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Same as above | Calculate required mass of medium |
| 3 | Volume-based Form | $Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\rho$ (kg/m³), $V$ (m³) | Account for storage volume |
| 4 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $E_v$ (J/m³) | Compare storage media compactness |
| 5 | Rate of Heat Transfer | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $\dot{m}$ (kg/s), $T$ in K | Charging/discharging power |
| 6 | Heat Loss (Conduction through wall) | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})}{L}$ | $k$ (W/(m·K)), $A$ (m²), $L$ (m) | Insulation thickness design |
| 7 | Packed-Bed Effectiveness (1st-order) | $\eta = 1 - e^{-NTU}$ | $NTU$ dimensionless | Approximate bed performance |
| 8 | Cylindrical Tank Volume | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$ (m), $h$ (m) | Tank sizing |
| 9 | Spherical Tank Volume | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | $r$ (m) | High-pressure storage geometry |
| 10 | Charging Time | $t_c = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{\dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{loss}}$ | $t_c$ (s) | Time to fully charge the system |
| 11 | Storage Efficiency | $\eta_{storage} = \dfrac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) | Quantifies standby heat loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem gives energy in **kWh** and asks for mass, first convert kWh → J using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J, then apply the formula. A common error is to use $c_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) but leave $Q$ in J, leading to off-by-1000 errors.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP):** The Andasol Solar Power Station (Spain) uses two molten-salt SHS tanks of 28,500 tonnes capacity each, allowing the plant to generate electricity for 7.5 hours after sunset.
- **Building Heating/Cooling:** Underfloor thermal mass storage in concrete slabs uses SHS principles.
- **Industrial Process Heat:** Steel, cement, and chemical industries use regenerator-type SHS to recover waste heat from flue gases.
- **District Heating Networks:** Large insulated water tanks (often called *accumulators* or *thermal storage tanks*) provide buffer storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sensible Heat Equation from First Principles

The Sensible Heat equation can be rigorously derived from the definition of specific heat capacity at constant pressure.

**Starting Definition (Specific Heat Capacity):**

By definition, the specific heat capacity at constant pressure is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **one kilogram** of a substance by **one Kelvin**, under constant-pressure conditions:

$$c_p = \left( \frac{\partial Q}{\partial T} \right)_p \quad \text{(per unit mass)}$$

Rearranging this for an infinitesimal amount of heat $dQ$:

$$dQ = m \cdot c_p \cdot dT$$

**Integration over a Finite Temperature Range:**

To find the total heat stored when the medium is heated from an initial temperature $T_i$ to a final temperature $T_f$, we integrate the differential expression above over the temperature range. We treat $c_p$ as approximately constant over the temperature range (a valid engineering assumption for moderate $\Delta T \le 100$ K):

$$\int_{Q=0}^{Q=Q} dQ = m \cdot c_p \cdot \int_{T_i}^{T_f} dT$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \bigl[ T \bigr]_{T_i}^{T_f}$$

This yields the canonical Sensible Heat Storage equation:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_i) = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The derivation shows that sensible heat is a **state function of temperature difference**, meaning it depends only on the initial and final temperatures, not on the heating path. This is a direct consequence of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed system with no phase change and no chemical reaction.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Volume-Based Storage Equation

In practical engineering, the **volume** $V$ occupied by the storage medium is often a more important design parameter than the mass, because the physical size of the storage tank must fit within available plant footprint.

We start with the mass-volume relationship:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

Substituting this into the basic SHS equation $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$:

$$Q = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Expanding the product to clearly identify each variable:

$$Q = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot V$$

We can define a composite property called the **Volumetric Heat Capacity** $\bigl( \rho \cdot c_p \bigr)$:

$$C_{vol} = \rho \cdot c_p \quad \text{(units: J/(m³·K))}$$

Thus, the storage equation can be written compactly as:

$$Q = C_{vol} \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** $C_{vol}$ is the *key selection metric* when the storage volume is constrained (e.g., rooftop tanks, in-building storage). For example, water has $C_{vol} \approx 4.18 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K), while molten salt has $C_{vol} \approx 2.6 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K). Although water wins on a volumetric basis at low temperatures, molten salts operate at much higher $\Delta T$, giving superior total energy density in CSP applications.

### 3.3 Derivation of Required Storage Volume (Design Equation)

To **design** an SHS system, we usually know three things: the energy $Q$ to be stored, the available temperature swing $\Delta T$, and the chosen storage medium (which fixes $c_p$ and $\rho$). The required storage volume is found by rearranging the volume-based equation.

Starting from:

$$Q = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot V$$

Dividing both sides by $\bigl( \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \bigr)$:

$$\frac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} = V$$

Therefore:

$$V = \frac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

This design equation is the most frequently used formula in SHS system sizing, and it has **direct relevance to KTU Part B (14-mark) problems**.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem Statement:**
> A solar thermal plant requires storing 500 kWh of thermal energy per day using a water-based sensible heat storage tank. The water is heated from 30 °C (initial) to 90 °C (final). Calculate:
> **(a)** The mass of water required.
> **(b)** The volume of the storage tank.
> **(c)** The discharge time if heat is withdrawn at a constant rate of 50 kW.
>
> Given: $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4186$ J/(kg·K); $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³.

#### Part (a) — Mass of Water Required

**Step 1: Convert energy to SI units (Joules).**

$$Q = 500 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kWh}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ J}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = T_f - T_i = 90 \, °C - 30 \, °C = 60 \, K$$

Note: For temperature *differences*, the numerical value is identical in K and in °C, so $\Delta T = 60$ K.

**Step 3: Apply the sensible heat equation and solve for mass.**

Starting from the governing equation:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Rearranging to isolate $m$:

$$m = \frac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$m = \frac{1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ J}}{4186 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 60 \text{ K}}$$

Performing the multiplication in the denominator:

$$4186 \times 60 = 251{,}160 \text{ J/kg}$$

Therefore:

$$m = \frac{1.8 \times 10^9}{251{,}160}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 7168.5 \text{ kg} \approx 7.17 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}}$$

#### Part (b) — Volume of the Storage Tank

**Step 1: Use the mass-density relationship.**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$V = \frac{7168.5 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 7.17 \text{ m}^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** A volume of ~7.17 m³ corresponds to a cylindrical tank of, for example, 1.5 m diameter and ~4 m height, which is well within practical residential/commercial scale.

#### Part (c) — Discharge Time at 50 kW

**Step 1: Convert power to energy-per-time units consistent with $Q$.**

$$P = 50 \text{ kW} = 50 \text{ kJ/s} = 50{,}000 \text{ J/s}$$

**Step 2: Use the energy-time-power relation.**

$$t = \frac{Q}{P}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$t = \frac{1.8 \times 10^9 \text{ J}}{50{,}000 \text{ J/s}}$$

Performing the division:

$$t = 36{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to hours (more intuitive).**

$$t = \frac{36{,}000}{3600} \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t = 10 \text{ hours}}$$

This means the system can deliver 50 kW of thermal power for **10 hours**, which is sufficient to cover an entire overnight heating demand — a typical design target for solar thermal SHS systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sensible Heat Storage System

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic SHS system, showing the interactions between the heat source, storage tank, heat transfer fluid loop, and end-use application. This is the **recommended KTU drawing style** for 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HSRC[Heat Source\nSolar Collector or Industrial Waste Heat] --> CHX1[Charging Heat Exchanger]
    HTFC1[Charging HTF Loop] --> CHX1
    CHX1 --> STANK[Insulated Storage Tank\nContains Storage Medium]
    STANK --> DHX1[Discharging Heat Exchanger]
    HTFD1[Discharging HTF Loop] --> DHX1
    DHX1 --> LOAD[End Use Application\nSteam Generation or Space Heating]
    STANK --> STLOSS[Standby Heat Loss to Ambient\nMinimized by Insulation]
    CTRL[Control and Monitoring System] -.-> CHX1
    CTRL -.-> DHX1
    CTRL -.-> STANK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **solid arrows** represent the primary flow of thermal energy.
> - The **dashed arrows** represent control signals from the monitoring system.
> - The arrow from the storage tank pointing to *Standby Heat Loss* is intentional, indicating that no real-world SHS system is perfectly adiabatic; the goal of insulation is to *minimize* this loss.

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Packed-Bed Solid Sensible Heat Storage

The packed-bed configuration is the most widely used geometry for **solid-media SHS** (e.g., rocks, sand, ceramic pebbles). The storage vessel is filled with the solid medium, and a heat transfer fluid (typically air) flows through the voids between the particles during charging and discharging.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Plenum\nAir Distributor] --> B[Hot Airflow Upward\nDuring Charging Phase]
    B --> C[Packed Bed of Solid Particles\nRocks or Ceramic Spheres]
    C --> D[Outlet Plenum\nCollected Hot Air]
    D --> E[End Use or Storage in Secondary Tank]
    F[Cold Air Inlet\nDuring Discharging Phase] --> C
    C --> G[Heated Air Outlet\nDelivered to Load]
    H[Thermocouples at Multiple Bed Heights\nMonitor Temperature Profile Thermocline] -.-> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Thermocline Concept:** During charging, a steep temperature gradient (the **thermocline**) develops in the packed bed, separating the hot zone at the top from the cold zone at the bottom. The thermocline moves through the bed as charging progresses. A sharp, well-defined thermocline indicates **high storage efficiency**; a diffuse thermocline indicates **excessive mixing or heat conduction**, which degrades performance.

### 4.3 Functional Comparison of Common SHS Media

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SHS[Classification of SHS Media]
    SHS --> LIQ[Liquid Media]
    SHS --> SOL[Solid Media]
    SHS --> GAS[Gaseous Media]
    LIQ --> W[Water\ncp 4186 J per kg K\nLow Temperature]
    LIQ --> O[Thermal Oils\ncp 2000 to 2500 J per kg K\nMedium Temperature]
    LIQ --> M[Molten Salts\ncp 1500 to 1600 J per kg K\nHigh Temperature CSP]
    SOL --> R[Rocks and Gravel\ncp 800 to 900 J per kg K\nPacked Beds]
    SOL --> C[Concrete and Cast Iron\ncp 500 to 1000 J per kg K\nModular Storage]
    SOL --> CR[Refractory Ceramics\ncp 800 to 1100 J per kg K\nHigh Temperature]
    GAS --> AIR[Air\ncp 1005 J per kg K\nLow Energy Density]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway from the Diagram:** Although water has by far the highest $c_p$ on a per-mass basis, its operating range is limited to 0–100 °C (at atmospheric pressure). For high-temperature applications (e.g., CSP above 400 °C), molten salts and ceramics are the only viable SHS media, despite their lower $c_p$, because they allow much larger $\Delta T$ values.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define Sensible Heat Storage. With the help of a suitable example, explain how it differs from Latent Heat Storage.*

**Model Answer:**

Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) is a method of storing thermal energy in a material by raising its temperature, **without any phase change** occurring in the storage medium. The amount of heat stored is given by $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$, where $m$ is mass, $c_p$ is specific heat capacity, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change.

**Example:** Heating water from 30 °C to 80 °C in an insulated tank. The water remains in the liquid phase throughout.

**Difference from Latent Heat Storage:** In Latent Heat Storage (LHS), heat is stored and released during a **phase change** (e.g., solid ↔ liquid) of a phase-change material (PCM) such as paraffin or salt hydrates. The temperature remains nearly constant during the phase change in LHS, whereas in SHS the temperature varies continuously with the state of charge. SHS is simpler and cheaper; LHS offers higher energy density and isothermal operation.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *List any four desirable properties of a Sensible Heat Storage medium. Justify the use of water as a low-temperature SHS medium.*

**Model Answer:**

**Four desirable properties of an SHS medium:**

1. **High specific heat capacity ($c_p$):** Stores more energy per unit mass per °C.
2. **High density ($\rho$):** High volumetric energy density, reducing tank size.
3. **Wide operating temperature range:** Allows large $\Delta T$ for high energy storage.
4. **Chemical stability, non-toxicity, low cost, and high availability.**

**Justification for water:**
Water has an exceptionally high specific heat capacity of **$c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K)** — among the highest of all common liquids — and a high density of **1000 kg/m³**. These give it the highest volumetric heat capacity ($\rho \cdot c_p \approx 4.18$ MJ/(m³·K)) of any low-cost medium. It is non-toxic, non-flammable, inexpensive, and readily available, making it the **default choice for low-temperature SHS** (typically below 95 °C) in domestic, commercial, and district heating applications. However, it is unsuitable above 100 °C at atmospheric pressure due to vaporization.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the energy stored in a Sensible Heat Storage system, starting from the definition of specific heat capacity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2 m³ water tank is used for sensible heat storage in a solar water heating system. The water temperature rises from 25 °C to 75 °C during the charging period. Given $c_p = 4186$ J/(kg·K) and $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, calculate: **(i)** the mass of water in the tank, **(ii)** the energy stored, and **(iii)** the time required to fully charge the tank if the heater delivers 3 kW of thermal power (neglect heat losses). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The derivation is provided in full detail in Section 3.1 of these notes. The examiner-expected sequence is:

1. Start with the definition $c_p = (\partial Q / \partial T)_p$ per unit mass.
2. Rearrange: $dQ = m \cdot c_p \cdot dT$.
3. Integrate: $Q = m \cdot c_p \int_{T_i}^{T_f} dT = m \cdot c_p (T_f - T_i)$.
4. State the final result: $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
5. **Key remark (1 mark):** "Sensible heat depends only on temperature difference, not on the heating path, because it is a state function under the First Law of Thermodynamics, with no phase change or chemical reaction involved."

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of water:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 2 \text{ m}^3 = 2000 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Energy stored:**

$$\Delta T = 75 - 25 = 50 \text{ K}$$

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2000 \times 4186 \times 50$$

$$Q = 418{,}600{,}000 \text{ J} = 4.186 \times 10^8 \text{ J}$$

Converting to kWh:

$$Q = \frac{4.186 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 116.3 \text{ kWh}$$

**(iii) Charging time at 3 kW:**

$$t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{4.186 \times 10^8 \text{ J}}{3000 \text{ W}}$$

$$t = 139{,}533 \text{ s} \approx 38.76 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **(a)** Compare Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) and Latent Heat Storage (LHS) in detail, listing at least six comparison parameters. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1000 kg rock-bed storage system uses granite stones of $c_p = 900$ J/(kg·K) and is heated from 30 °C to 500 °C. Calculate: **(i)** the energy stored, **(ii)** the volumetric energy density if the bulk density of the packed bed is 1600 kg/m³, and **(iii)** the volume of the storage vessel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — students should draw a table):**

| Parameter | Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) | Latent Heat Storage (LHS) |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Storage mechanism | Temperature change (no phase change) | Phase change (solid-liquid) |
| Energy equation | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q = m \cdot L$ |
| Temperature during discharge | Varies continuously | Nearly constant (at phase change temperature) |
| Energy density | Low to moderate | High (typically 5–10× higher) |
| Common media | Water, rocks, molten salts, concrete | Paraffin wax, salt hydrates, fatty acids |
| Cost | Lower, mature technology | Higher, developing technology |
| Complexity | Simple tank + insulation | Requires PCM encapsulation, careful thermal cycling |
| Typical applications | CSP, district heating, waste heat | Building thermal comfort, electronics cooling, solar cookers |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Energy stored:**

$$\Delta T = 500 - 30 = 470 \text{ K}$$

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \times 900 \times 470$$

$$Q = 4.23 \times 10^8 \text{ J} = 423 \text{ MJ}$$

**(ii) Volumetric energy density:**

Volume of bed:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{1600 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.625 \text{ m}^3$$

Volumetric energy density:

$$E_v = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{4.23 \times 10^8 \text{ J}}{0.625 \text{ m}^3} = 6.768 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^3$$

Converting to kWh/m³:

$$E_v = \frac{6.768 \times 10^8}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 188 \text{ kWh/m}^3$$

**(iii) Volume of storage vessel:**

$$\boxed{V = 0.625 \text{ m}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert kWh to Joules (multiply by $3.6 \times 10^6$). A 3-mark penalty is commonly applied for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing mass density vs. bulk density:** In packed-bed problems, the *bulk density* (which includes void spaces) is used to compute volume, not the *true material density* of the solid.
> 3. **Phase-change error:** Students sometimes write $Q = m \cdot L$ (latent heat formula) instead of $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ for SHS problems. Examiners deduct 2 marks and circle the error in red — this is a very common slip.
> 4. **Sign convention for $\Delta T$:** Always take $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$. A negative $\Delta T$ should be flagged in the answer; the student should interpret it as a discharge process.
> 5. **Skipping the integration step in derivations:** The 7-mark derivation questions expect you to show $\int dQ$ on both sides and evaluate the temperature integral. A derivation that jumps directly to $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ without integration loses 2–3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sensible Heat Storage:**

- **Core Definition:** SHS stores heat via *temperature change* without any phase change.
- **Master Equation:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. Memorize it and its three rearranged forms.
- **Volume form:** $Q = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot V = C_{vol} \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Three SHS media classes:** Liquid (water, oils, molten salts), Solid (rocks, concrete, ceramics), Gaseous (air, steam).
- **Water's $c_p$:** **4186 J/(kg·K)** — highest among common liquids; cornerstone of low-T SHS.
- **Volumetric heat capacity** $\rho \cdot c_p$ is the key design metric when storage volume is constrained.
- **Unit conversion to remember:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
- **SHS vs. LHS distinction:** SHS = temperature varies; LHS = phase change, temperature nearly constant.
- **Storage efficiency** $\eta_{storage} = Q_{discharged}/Q_{charged}$ is degraded by **standby heat losses** through tank walls, top, and bottom.
- **Insulation matters:** Mineral wool, vacuum panels, aerogels are common insulation choices.
- **Packed-bed geometry** is preferred for solid media; the **thermocline** is the key performance indicator.
- **Industrial examples:** Molten-salt tanks in CSP (Andasol, Spain), water tanks in district heating, regenerators in steel/glass industries.
- **Sign convention:** $\Delta T > 0$ during charging; $\Delta T < 0$ during discharging.
- **Always state the energy in Joules internally** when chaining with W or kW for time/flow rate calculations.
- **First-Law origin:** Sensible heat is a *state function* of temperature difference, derived from $c_p = (\partial Q/\partial T)_p$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latent Heat Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latent Heat Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Latent Heat Storage (LHS)**, also known as **Phase Change Energy Storage (PCES)**, is a thermal energy storage technique that harnesses the **latent heat** absorbed or released by a material when it undergoes a reversible **phase transition** (most commonly solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid) at a constant temperature (or within a narrow temperature band).

The working substance used is called a **Phase Change Material (PCM)**. Energy is stored during the **charging cycle** (PCM melts, absorbing latent heat of fusion) and recovered during the **discharging cycle** (PCM solidifies, releasing latent heat of fusion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The latent heat of fusion is the dominant energy carrier here. A small mass of PCM can store orders of magnitude more energy than an equivalent mass of water, rock, or oil over the same temperature swing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle to make tea.
- While the water is warming from $25^\circ C$ to $100^\circ C$, you can feel the temperature rising — this is **sensible heat** (you can "sense" the change).
- But once it reaches $100^\circ C$, the water stops getting hotter. It just sits there bubbling and turning into steam, yet the kettle is still consuming huge amounts of energy. That energy is "hidden" because the temperature is not changing — this is **latent heat**.

Now reverse the process. If you pour that steam into a cold block of ice, the steam will dump enormous energy into the ice to melt it, while the temperature of the ice stays stuck at $0^\circ C$ the whole time. That **constant-temperature, high-energy** exchange is precisely what **Latent Heat Storage** exploits in engineering systems.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Latent Heat of Fusion of Water (Ice):** $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at $0^\circ C$)
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water:** $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at $100^\circ C$)
- **Specific Heat of Water:** $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$
- **Standard Energy Density Benchmark for LHS:** $200$–$500 \text{ kJ/L}$ (far higher than sensible storage)
- **Typical Operating Temperature Ranges:** $-20^\circ C$ to $+200^\circ C$ depending on PCM family

> [!NOTE]
> Compare: To store the same energy in sensible form using water heating from $20^\circ C$ to $80^\circ C$, you need a temperature swing of $60 \text{ K}$ and the energy is only $c_p \cdot \Delta T = 4.186 \times 60 = 251 \text{ kJ/kg}$. By contrast, melting ice stores $333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at a *single* temperature!

## 1.4 Classification of LHS by Phase Change Type

| Type | Common Engineering Use | Latent Heat Magnitude |
|------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Solid $\leftrightarrow$ Liquid** | Solar water heaters, building walls, TES tanks | High (preferred) |
| **Solid $\leftrightarrow$ Gas** | Hypothetical/rare | Very High |
| **Liquid $\leftrightarrow$ Gas** | Steam accumulators | Very High but large volume change |
| **Solid $\leftrightarrow$ Solid** | Compact electronic cooling | Moderate |

The **solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid** transition dominates the LHS literature because it offers a good balance between high latent heat and manageable volume change.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of an LHS System

An LHS system is governed by **three sequential energy regimes** as the PCM is heated (charging) from an initial solid state to a final liquid state:

1. **Sensible Heating of the Solid** — PCM temperature rises from $T_i$ to the melting point $T_m$. Energy stored: $Q_1 = m \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i)$.
2. **Phase Change (Latent)** — PCM melts at (nearly) constant $T_m$. Energy stored: $Q_2 = m \cdot L_f$.
3. **Sensible Heating of the Liquid** — PCM temperature rises from $T_m$ to the final liquid temperature $T_f$. Energy stored: $Q_3 = m \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m)$.

The **discharging cycle** is the exact reverse: liquid cools, solidifies at $T_m$, and finally the solid cools back to $T_i$.

## 2.2 Why Latent Heat Storage is "High-Density"

The latent heat of fusion $L_f$ for most practical PCMs is typically **50–100 times** the sensible heat capacity over a $1 \text{ K}$ band. This means:

> A $10 \text{ kg}$ paraffin wax block melting from $60^\circ C$ to $61^\circ C$ in sensible form would store only $\sim 21 \text{ kJ}$. The same $10 \text{ kg}$ block, melting entirely at $60^\circ C$, stores $\sim 2000 \text{ kJ}$ — nearly **100× more** in the same temperature window.

This is the **engineering value proposition** of LHS: compact, near-isothermal storage.

## 2.3 Selection Criteria for a Good PCM

A KTU examiner expects you to know the **selection rules** for an ideal PCM:

- **Thermophysical:** High latent heat, suitable phase-change temperature, high thermal conductivity, small volume change on melting, no supercooling.
- **Chemical:** Long-term chemical stability, non-corrosive, non-toxic, non-flammable.
- **Economic:** Abundant, low cost, easily available.

## 2.4 Categories of PCMs

1. **Organic PCMs** — Paraffins (e.g., $n$-eicosane, $L_f \approx 247 \text{ kJ/kg}$), fatty acids, esters.
2. **Inorganic PCMs** — Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \cdot 10\text{H}_2\text{O}$), metallic alloys.
3. **Eutectic Mixtures** — Combinations of two or more PCMs to tune the melting point.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is your **exam-day formula sheet** for any numerical on Latent Heat Storage. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot \left[ c_{p,s} (T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l} (T_f - T_m) \right]$ | Total energy stored across all 3 regimes | J or kJ |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$ | Pure latent heat portion | J or kJ |
| 3 | $E_v = \rho \cdot L_f$ | Volumetric energy storage density | $\text{kJ/m}^3$ |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat portion | J or kJ |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}} \cdot A$ | Heat transfer during charging (HTF side) | W |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{\text{lm}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_i - \Delta T_f}{\ln \left( \Delta T_i / \Delta T_f \right)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) | K |
| 7 | $t_{\text{charge}} = \dfrac{m \cdot L_f}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}}}$ | Approximate charging time (latent phase) | s |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{k_{\text{PCM}}}{\delta} \cdot A \cdot (T_{\text{HTF}} - T_m)$ | Conduction-limited heat flux in PCM | W |
| 9 | $\text{Ste} = \dfrac{c_p \cdot \Delta T}{L_f}$ | Stefan number (ratio of sensible to latent) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_R = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\min} \cdot c_{p,\min}}{\dot{m}_{\max} \cdot c_{p,\max}}$ | Heat capacity rate ratio (for LMTD use) | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $m$ = mass of PCM (kg)
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $c_{p,s}, c_{p,l}$ = specific heats of solid / liquid PCM (J/(kg·K))
- $\rho$ = density of PCM (kg/m³)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/(m²·K))
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $k_{\text{PCM}}$ = thermal conductivity of PCM (W/(m·K))
- $\delta$ = PCM container thickness (m)
- $\Delta T_i, \Delta T_f$ = temperature differences at inlet and outlet of HTF (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions in KTU problems:** Most LHS numericals assume (a) constant PCM properties, (b) negligible supercooling, (c) HTF inlet temperature $T_{\text{HTF,in}} > T_m$ during charging, and (d) $T_{\text{HTF,out}} > T_m$ until all PCM has melted.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Thermal Power Plants** — LHS stores solar energy for nighttime electricity generation (e.g., nitrate salt mixtures at $\sim 220^\circ C$).
- **Building Energy Conservation** — PCM-impregnated wallboards and ceilings absorb daytime heat and release it at night.
- **Electronic Cooling** — Solid-solid PCMs absorb transient heat loads in CPU/LED thermal management.
- **Cold Chain & HVAC** — Ice-based LHS for air-conditioning off-peak storage.
- **Electric Vehicle Battery Packs** — Paraffin-based PCMs buffer rapid charge/discharge thermal spikes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Energy Stored in a Solid $\leftrightarrow$ Liquid LHS Unit

We consider a PCM mass $m$ initially solid at temperature $T_i$. It is heated (charging) until it becomes fully liquid at $T_f > T_m$, where $T_m$ is the melting temperature.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat absorbed by the solid (from $T_i$ to $T_m$):**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i)$$

*Logic:* Energy = mass × specific heat × temperature change. Here, the specific heat of the *solid* phase is used.

**Step 2 — Latent heat absorbed during the solid-to-liquid phase change (at $T_m$):**

$$Q_2 = m \cdot L_f$$

*Logic:* During the phase change, temperature remains constant at $T_m$, but a large quantity of energy equal to the latent heat of fusion per kg, multiplied by the total mass, is absorbed to break the molecular bonds of the crystalline lattice.

**Step 3 — Sensible heat absorbed by the liquid (from $T_m$ to $T_f$):**

$$Q_3 = m \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m)$$

*Logic:* Now the PCM is fully liquid; further heating raises its temperature using the *liquid-phase* specific heat.

**Step 4 — Summing all three contributions:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot \left[ c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m) \right]}$$

*Logic:* The total energy stored is the algebraic sum of the three sequential heat regimes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Charging Time for the Latent (Phase-Change) Portion

During the constant-temperature phase change, the heat-transfer rate from the Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) to the PCM is governed by:

$$\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}}$$

The **Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** for a phase-change process is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{lm}} = \frac{\Delta T_i - \Delta T_f}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_i}{\Delta T_f}\right)}$$

where $\Delta T_i = T_{\text{HTF,in}} - T_m$ and $\Delta T_f = T_{\text{HTF,out}} - T_m$.

**Step 1 — Energy required to fully melt the PCM:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$$

**Step 2 — Equate this energy to the rate integral over the charging time:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = \dot{Q} \cdot t_{\text{charge}} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}} \cdot t_{\text{charge}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t_{\text{charge}}$:**

$$\boxed{t_{\text{charge}} = \frac{m \cdot L_f}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}}}}$$

*Logic:* Charging time scales linearly with the PCM mass and latent heat, and inversely with the heat-transfer coefficient, area, and LMTD. Doubling the HTF flow or surface area roughly halves the charging time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Storage Density

For a design engineer choosing between sensible and latent storage, the **storage density per unit volume** is the key metric.

**Step 1 — Mass in volume $V$:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the latent energy expression:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot L_f$$

**Step 3 — Energy per unit volume:**

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{Q_{\text{latent}}}{V} = \rho \cdot L_f}$$

*Logic:* This is the figure of merit KTU examiners use to compare PCMs. A high-density PCM (high $\rho$ and high $L_f$) wins. For example, ice ($\rho = 917 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$) gives $E_v = 917 \times 333.55 = 305{,}865 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \approx 306 \text{ MJ/m}^3$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style, 7-mark standard)

**Problem:** A solar thermal LHS unit contains $m = 50 \text{ kg}$ of a paraffin PCM with the following properties:
- $c_{p,s} = 2.0 \text{ kJ/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$
- $c_{p,l} = 2.5 \text{ kJ/(kg}\cdot\text{K)}$
- $L_f = 200 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $T_i = 40^\circ C$ (initial solid)
- $T_m = 60^\circ C$ (melting point)
- $T_f = 80^\circ C$ (final liquid)

**Find:** (a) Total energy stored (kJ), and (b) the percentage contribution of the latent portion.

**Solution (a):**

$$Q_1 = 50 \times 2.0 \times (60 - 40) = 50 \times 2.0 \times 20 = 2000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_2 = 50 \times 200 = 10{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_3 = 50 \times 2.5 \times (80 - 60) = 50 \times 2.5 \times 20 = 2500 \text{ kJ}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 2000 + 10{,}000 + 2500 = \boxed{14{,}500 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\% \text{Latent} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{10{,}000}{14{,}500} \times 100 \approx 68.97\%$$

The latent heat of fusion contributes **~69%** of the total stored energy — confirming the dominance of the phase-change term in LHS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LHS System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["HEAT SOURCE - Solar Collector or Waste Heat"]
        A1["Sunlight / Flue Gas"]
    end

    subgraph HTF["HEAT TRANSFER FLUID LOOP"]
        B1["HTF Inlet - Thigh"]
        B2["Pump / Blower"]
        B3["HTF Outlet - Tlow"]
    end

    subgraph PCM["LATENT HEAT STORAGE UNIT"]
        C1["Container Shell"]
        C2["Phase Change Material - PCM"]
        C3["Fins / Matrix - Enhance h"]
    end

    subgraph LOAD["END USE - Demand Side"]
        D1["Building HVAC"]
        D2["Domestic Hot Water"]
        D3["Industrial Process"]
    end

    A1 -- "Q_in" --> B1
    B1 -- "Hot HTF" --> C1
    C1 -- "Charges" --> C2
    C2 <--> C3
    C2 -- "Releases Q" --> B3
    B3 -- "Discharged HTF" --> D1
    B3 --> D2
    B3 --> D3
    B2 -. "Drives" .-> B1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Charging vs Discharging of PCM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SolidBelowTm: Initial State - T less than Tm
    SolidBelowTm --> SolidAtTm: Sensible Heating - Q1
    SolidAtTm --> LiquidAtTm: Phase Change - Q2 - Latent
    LiquidAtTm --> LiquidAboveTm: Sensible Heating - Q3
    LiquidAboveTm --> LiquidAtTm: Sensible Cooling - Q3
    LiquidAtTm --> SolidAtTm: Solidification - Q2 - Latent
    SolidAtTm --> SolidBelowTm: Sensible Cooling - Q1
    SolidBelowTm --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree: PCM Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Phase Change Materials - PCM"]
    P --> ORG["Organic PCMs"]
    P --> INO["Inorganic PCMs"]
    P --> EUT["Eutectic Mixtures"]

    ORG --> ORG1["Paraffins - CnH2n+2"]
    ORG --> ORG2["Fatty Acids - CH3 COOH family"]
    ORG --> ORG3["Esters and Alcohols"]

    INO --> INO1["Salt Hydrates - Na2SO4 10H2O"]
    INO --> INO2["Metallic Alloys - Low-melt solders"]

    EUT --> EUT1["Organic-Organic blends"]
    EUT --> EUT2["Organic-Inorganic blends"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above model the **functional architecture** of an LHS system — the physical construction (PCM container, HTF loop, fin enhancements, end-use loads) and the thermodynamic state transitions of the PCM itself. KTU examiners reward labeled, structured flow diagrams in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define latent heat storage. List any four desirable properties of an ideal PCM.**

**Model Answer:**
Latent heat storage is a thermal energy storage method that uses the latent heat absorbed or released by a **Phase Change Material (PCM)** during its reversible phase transition, typically solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid, at a nearly constant temperature.

Four desirable properties of an ideal PCM:
1. High latent heat of fusion per unit mass/volume.
2. Phase-change temperature matching the application.
3. Chemical stability over many thermal cycles.
4. Non-corrosive, non-toxic, non-flammable, and inexpensive.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for properties, or 1 each if listed.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between sensible heat storage and latent heat storage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensible Heat Storage | Latent Heat Storage |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Mechanism | Temperature change of storage medium | Phase change at (nearly) constant $T$ |
| Energy equation | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | $Q = m L_f$ |
| Operating profile | Temperature varies during discharge | Near-isothermal discharge |
| Storage density | Low ($\sim 50$–$100 \text{ kJ/L}$) | High ($200$–$500 \text{ kJ/L}$) |
| Example | Water tank, rock-bed | Ice storage, paraffin PCM |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with at least 3 distinguishing points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 
**(a)** Derive the expression for the total amount of heat stored in a solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid latent heat storage system, clearly identifying the sensible and latent contributions. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A solar LHS unit contains $40 \text{ kg}$ of a salt-hydrate PCM with $L_f = 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $c_{p,s} = 1.8 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, and $c_{p,l} = 2.6 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. The PCM is heated from $20^\circ C$ (initial solid) to a final liquid temperature of $70^\circ C$, with melting occurring at $50^\circ C$. Calculate the total energy stored and the percentage contribution of each regime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The derivation proceeds through three sequential energy regimes. Please refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–4** for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final boxed result is:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot \left[ c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m) \right]$$

The three terms correspond to: (i) sensible heating of the solid, (ii) latent heat during phase change, and (iii) sensible heating of the liquid. *(7 marks: 2 for setup + 3 for derivation + 2 for physical interpretation)*

**(b) Given:** $m = 40 \text{ kg}$, $L_f = 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $c_{p,s} = 1.8 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_{p,l} = 2.6 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_i = 20^\circ C$, $T_m = 50^\circ C$, $T_f = 70^\circ C$.

**Sensible heating of solid (Q₁):**

$$Q_1 = 40 \times 1.8 \times (50 - 20) = 40 \times 1.8 \times 30 = 2160 \text{ kJ}$$

**Latent heat during melting (Q₂):**

$$Q_2 = 40 \times 250 = 10{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Sensible heating of liquid (Q₃):**

$$Q_3 = 40 \times 2.6 \times (70 - 50) = 40 \times 2.6 \times 20 = 2080 \text{ kJ}$$

**Total energy stored:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 2160 + 10{,}000 + 2080 = 14{,}240 \text{ kJ}$$

**Percentage contributions:**

$$\% Q_1 = \frac{2160}{14{,}240} \times 100 \approx 15.17\%$$

$$\% Q_2 = \frac{10{,}000}{14{,}240} \times 100 \approx 70.22\%$$

$$\% Q_3 = \frac{2080}{14{,}240} \times 100 \approx 14.61\%$$

*(7 marks: 1 each for Q₁, Q₂, Q₃ + 1 for total + 3 for percentages)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** 
**(a)** Explain the selection criteria for a Phase Change Material (PCM) used in latent heat storage systems. Classify PCMs into three major categories with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A building-integrated LHS wallboard uses $m = 200 \text{ kg}$ of paraffin wax with $L_f = 220 \text{ kJ/kg}$. If the wallboard melts completely every day to absorb solar heat, calculate: (i) the daily latent energy absorbed, and (ii) the equivalent volume of water (in litres) that would store the same energy as sensible heat over a $20 \text{ K}$ temperature swing ($c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Selection Criteria for PCM (Thermophysical, Chemical, Economic):**
- *Thermophysical:* High $L_f$, suitable $T_m$, high thermal conductivity, small volume change, no supercooling.
- *Chemical:* Long-term stability, non-corrosive, non-toxic, non-flammable, reversible phase change.
- *Economic:* Cheap, abundant, easy to encapsulate.

**Classification of PCMs:**

1. **Organic PCMs** — Paraffins (e.g., $n$-eicosane, $T_m \approx 36^\circ C$, $L_f \approx 247 \text{ kJ/kg}$), fatty acids (e.g., capric acid, $T_m \approx 32^\circ C$).
2. **Inorganic PCMs** — Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \cdot 10\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $T_m \approx 32^\circ C$), metallic alloys.
3. **Eutectic Mixtures** — Combinations of two or more PCMs to obtain a desired melting point (e.g., salt-hydrate eutectics for sub-zero applications).

*(7 marks: 3 for criteria + 4 for classification with examples)*

**(b) (i) Daily latent energy absorbed:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f = 200 \times 220 = 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**(b) (ii) Equivalent water volume:**

Energy stored by water over $\Delta T = 20 \text{ K}$:

$$Q = m_{\text{water}} \cdot c_{p,\text{water}} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$44{,}000 = m_{\text{water}} \times 4.186 \times 20$$

$$m_{\text{water}} = \frac{44{,}000}{83.72} \approx 525.6 \text{ kg}$$

Equivalent volume:

$$V_{\text{water}} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{525.6}{1000} = 0.5256 \text{ m}^3 = 525.6 \text{ litres}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for part (i) + 5 for part (ii) including unit conversions)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LHS Numericals:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sensible contributions** — Many students compute only $Q = m \cdot L_f$ and miss the $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ terms. Full marks require **all three** components.
> 2. **Mixing up solid and liquid specific heats** — Use $c_{p,s}$ for $T_i \to T_m$ and $c_{p,l}$ for $T_m \to T_f$. Swapping them is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — If $L_f$ is in kJ/kg, give the final answer in kJ. Do not mix kJ and J mid-calculation without conversion.
> 4. **LMTD misuse** — LMTD applies to sensible heat exchangers, not to the constant-$T$ phase-change process. For latent charging, use the simplified $t = m L_f / (h A \Delta T_{\text{lm}})$ only if the problem states HTF outlet temperature explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring supercooling** — Real salt-hydrate PCMs exhibit supercooling; examiners may dock a mark if you claim "no supercooling" without justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** LHS = thermal energy storage via **phase change** (typically solid $\leftrightarrow$ liquid) of a **PCM** at near-constant temperature.
- **Core equation:** $Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot [c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m)]$ — three terms, three regimes.
- **Latent-only shortcut:** $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$ — used when sensible contributions are negligible.
- **Volumetric energy density:** $E_v = \rho \cdot L_f$ — key PCM comparison metric.
- **Charging time (latent phase):** $t_{\text{charge}} = m L_f / (h A \Delta T_{\text{lm}})$.
- **PCM families:** Organic (paraffins, fatty acids), Inorganic (salt hydrates, metals), Eutectics.
- **Selection criteria:** Thermophysical, chemical, economic — memorize at least **4 properties**.
- **Comparison vs sensible storage:** LHS offers **higher energy density** and **near-isothermal** discharge.
- **Reference benchmark:** Ice $L_f = 333.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$; water $L_v = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
- **Dominant term:** In practical LHS designs, the latent portion contributes **60–80%** of the total stored energy.
- **Real-world applications:** Solar thermal plants, building HVAC, electronic cooling, EV batteries, cold-chain logistics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermochemical Storage (TCS)** is an advanced thermal energy storage (TES) technology that stores energy in the form of **chemical bond energy** through **reversible endothermic/exothermic chemical reactions**. The system absorbs thermal energy during an endothermic *charging* reaction, retains the chemical products indefinitely at near-ambient conditions with negligible energy loss, and releases the stored energy on demand via an exothermic *discharging* reaction.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), thermochemical storage is positioned as a **third-generation thermal storage** technique — succeeding sensible heat storage (SHS) and latent heat storage (LHS).

## 1.2 Classification of Thermochemical Storage

Thermochemical storage is broadly divided into two main sub-classes:

1. **Chemical Reaction Storage (Thermochemical Heat Storage – THS)**
   - Energy is stored in the form of completely broken and reformed chemical bonds.
   - Examples:
     - $\text{CaO}/\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ (Calcium oxide/hydroxide)
     - $\text{CaCO}_3/\text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$ (Carbonation/Calcination)
     - $\text{MgH}_2/\text{Mg}$ (Metal hydrides)
     - $\text{NH}_3/\text{N}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (Ammonia synthesis/cracking)
     - Sabatier reaction: $\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

2. **Sorption Storage**
   - Sub-divided into **Absorption** (uptake into bulk of a liquid/solid) and **Adsorption** (uptake onto a surface).
   - Examples: Silica gel, Zeolites, salt hydrates (e.g., $\text{SrBr}_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — Salt Hydrates vs. Pure Reactions:**
> Salt hydrate decomposition reactions such as $\text{SrBr}_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{SrBr}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} + 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ are sometimes classified separately as "thermochemical" because the released water vapor itself acts as the working fluid, but they share the van't Hoff equilibrium behavior of true chemical systems.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Heat Battery"**
> Imagine a **rechargeable battery**, but instead of storing electricity, it stores **heat** as chemical energy:
> - **Charging** = Push heat *into* the material → it transforms into a "spent fuel" (e.g., CaO).
> - **Storage (Idle)** = The "spent fuel" sits in a drum at room temperature for *months* without losing energy (like a charged battery on a shelf).
> - **Discharging** = Add water vapor (or a catalyst) → the "spent fuel" recombines → heat comes *out* on demand.

Just as a lithium-ion battery stores ~**0.5 MJ/kg** electrochemically, a thermochemical pair like $\text{CaO}/\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ can store ~**1.4 MJ/kg thermally** — a remarkable energy density.

## 1.4 Why Thermochemical Storage? — Salient Features

| Feature | Value/Comment |
|---|---|
| Volumetric energy density | **500 – 3000 kJ/kg** of storage material |
| Storage duration | **Seasonal (months/years)** with negligible loss |
| Transportability | Stored chemicals are **transportable** over long distances |
| Operating temperature range | **100 °C – 1200 °C** (system dependent) |
| Round-trip efficiency | **70 – 95 %** (system dependent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasises *CaO/Ca(OH)₂*, *CaCO₃/CaO*, and *metal hydride* systems in Module 1, and questions frequently test *the van't Hoff equilibrium relation* and *energy density calculations*.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of a TCS System

A thermochemical storage cycle operates in three distinct thermodynamic phases. Let us denote a generic reversible reaction as:

$$A + B \;\rightleftharpoons\; AB + \Delta H_{rxn}$$

**Phase 1 — CHARGING (Endothermic, Heat INPUT)**
- Reactants $A$ and $B$ are combined (or $AB$ is decomposed) at elevated temperature $T_c$.
- Heat from a renewable source (concentrated solar, waste heat, etc.) drives the reaction in the endothermic direction.
- Products of charging are stored separately.

**Phase 2 — STORAGE (Quasi-Isobaric & Isothermal Idle)**
- The "charged" products sit in insulated vessels at near-ambient conditions.
- Because energy is locked in **chemical bonds**, losses are **negligible** (unlike sensible heat, which bleeds out through tank walls).

**Phase 3 — DISCHARGING (Exothermic, Heat OUTPUT)**
- Stored products are brought together (or reacted) to release heat at temperature $T_d$.
- Useful heat is extracted for end-use (space heating, power generation, industrial process).

## 2.2 Governing Thermodynamic Equations

### (a) Van't Hoff Equation (Equilibrium Relation)

For a reversible reaction at equilibrium, the change in Gibbs free energy is zero: $\Delta G = 0$.

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln(K_p)$$

At equilibrium, also $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$. Equating:

$$\boxed{\;\ln(K_p) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} \;+\; \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}\;}$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the **master equation** for designing any TCS system. It tells the designer at what temperature $T$ the reaction will occur at a given pressure $p$ (or vice-versa). A straight line of $\ln K_p$ vs $1/T$ has slope $-\Delta H^\circ/R$ and intercept $\Delta S^\circ/R$.

### (b) Specific Energy Storage Capacity (Most Tested Formula)

For a storage material of molar mass $M$ undergoing a reaction with enthalpy change $\Delta H$:

$$\boxed{\;E_s \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{M_{storage}}\;}$$

Where:
- $E_s$ = specific energy storage capacity (kJ/kg of storage material)
- $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = absolute value of reaction enthalpy (kJ/mol)
- $M_{storage}$ = molar mass of the storage compound (kg/mol)

### (c) Volumetric Energy Density

$$\boxed{\;E_v \;=\; E_s \cdot \rho\;\;[\text{kJ/m}^3]\;}$$

Where $\rho$ is the bulk density (kg/m³) of the storage material in the charged state.

### (d) Round-Trip Storage Efficiency

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{RT} \;=\; \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{Q_{loss,storage} + Q_{loss,recovery}}{Q_{charged}}\;}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\ln K_p = -\Delta H^\circ/(RT) + \Delta S^\circ/R$ | Van't Hoff equilibrium line | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $E_s = \Delta H_{rxn}/M$ | Gravimetric energy density | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $E_v = E_s \cdot \rho$ | Volumetric energy density | kJ/m³ or MJ/m³ |
| 4 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | Reaction spontaneity check | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $K_p = p_{H_2O}$ (for $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ dehydration) | Equilibrium pressure of vapour | Pa or bar |
| 6 | $T_{eq} = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$ | Decomposition temperature (at $K_p=1$) | K |
| 7 | $\eta_{RT} = 1 - Q_{loss}/Q_{charged}$ | Round-trip efficiency | % |
| 8 | $p_{eq} = p^\circ \exp\!\left(-\Delta H^\circ/RT + \Delta S^\circ/R\right)$ | Equilibrium pressure of gas | Pa or bar |

> **Engineering Utility:** The van't Hoff equation is the *design backbone* for solar thermochemical plants, seasonal storage for district heating, and high-temperature industrial heat recovery (cement, steel, ammonia industries).

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Working Principle (CaO/Ca(OH)₂ System)

Consider the most-studied reaction for moderate-to-high temperature storage:

**Charging (Dehydration at ~500 °C):**

$$\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{heat at }\sim 500^\circ\text{C}\;}\; \text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \quad \Delta H = +104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Storage:**
- Solid CaO stored in one silo; H₂O vapour condensed and stored as liquid water in another.

**Discharging (Hydration at ~500 °C):**

$$\text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) + \text{Heat} \quad \Delta H = -104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why CaO/Ca(OH)₂ is pedagogically important:** It is the *canonical example* in almost every KTU-endorsed textbook, has a single gas product (simplifies equilibrium), is non-toxic, inexpensive, and reversible over thousands of cycles. The KTU module explicitly includes this reaction.
