# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Van't Hoff Equation from First Principles

We will derive the equilibrium relation from basic thermodynamics.

**Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy at Equilibrium**

For a closed system at constant $T$ and $P$, the change in Gibbs free energy at equilibrium is **zero**:

$$\Delta G = 0$$

**Step 2 — Standard-State Relation**

The standard change in Gibbs free energy is related to the equilibrium constant by:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$$

This is a fundamental result of chemical thermodynamics derived from $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ and setting $Q = K_p$ at equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Decompose ΔG° into Enthalpy and Entropy**

By definition:

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions**

Combining Step 2 and Step 3:

$$-RT \ln K_p = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $\ln K_p$**

Dividing through by $-RT$:

$$\ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

This is the **Van't Hoff equation**, plotted as $\ln K_p$ against $1/T$ yielding a straight line of slope $-\Delta H^\circ/R$ and intercept $\Delta S^\circ/R$.

$$\boxed{\;\ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}\;}$$

> **Design Use:** A plot of $\ln p_{H_2O}$ vs $1/T$ (in K) for $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ dehydration gives a near-perfect straight line, from which both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ of dehydration can be read.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Storage Density of CaO/Ca(OH)₂

**Given Data:**
- Reaction: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\Delta H = 104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- Molar mass of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$: $M = 74.09 \text{ g/mol} = 0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}$
- Molar mass of $\text{CaO}$: $M = 56.08 \text{ g/mol} = 0.05608 \text{ kg/mol}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Energy Density Formula**

$$E_s = \frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{M_{storage}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Using Ca(OH)₂ as Reference**

$$E_s = \frac{104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}{0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$E_s = 1409.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Using CaO as Reference (Alternative Basis)**

Sometimes the energy density is quoted per kg of *charged* CaO, since this is the form that sits in storage:

$$E_s = \frac{104.4}{0.05608} = 1862.0 \text{ kJ/kg of CaO}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Sensible Heat Storage in Water**

For water heated from 30 °C to 90 °C:

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1 \text{ kg} \times 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times 60 \text{ K} = 250.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Ratio**

$$\frac{E_{s,Ca(OH)_2}}{Q_{sensible,water}} = \frac{1409.2}{250.8} \approx 5.6$$

> **Result:** The CaO/Ca(OH)₂ system stores about **5.6× more energy per kg** than water over a 60 °C temperature swing. This is a *crucial comparison* that KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equilibrium Decomposition Temperature of Ca(OH)₂

**Given:**
- $\Delta H^\circ = 104.4 \text{ kJ/mol} = 104400 \text{ J/mol}$
- $\Delta S^\circ = 141 \text{ J/mol·K}$ (literature value for dehydration)
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$

**Step 1 — Definition of $T_{eq}$**

The equilibrium decomposition temperature is defined as the temperature at which $K_p = 1$, i.e., $\ln K_p = 0$.

Setting $\ln K_p = 0$ in the van't Hoff equation:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T_{eq}} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T_{eq}$**

$$T_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$T_{eq} = \frac{104400 \text{ J/mol}}{141 \text{ J/mol·K}} = 740.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to °C**

$$T_{eq} = 740.4 - 273.15 = 467.3^\circ\text{C}$$

> **Interpretation:** At 1 bar pressure of water vapour, $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ begins to decompose at approximately **467 °C**. In practice, the decomposition is *driven* at ~500 °C to overcome kinetic barriers.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equilibrium Pressure as a Function of Temperature

For the dehydration reaction $\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$, only the gas-phase species appears in the equilibrium expression:

$$K_p = p_{H_2O}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Van't Hoff**

$$\ln(p_{H_2O}) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p_{H_2O}$ at $T = 773 \text{ K}$ (500 °C)**

$$\ln(p_{H_2O}) = -\frac{104400}{8.314 \times 773} + \frac{141}{8.314}$$

$$\ln(p_{H_2O}) = -16.24 + 16.96 = 0.72$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate**

$$p_{H_2O} = e^{0.72} = 2.05 \text{ bar}$$

> **Physical Meaning:** At 500 °C, the equilibrium vapour pressure of water over Ca(OH)₂ is ~2.05 bar. If the actual water-vapour partial pressure falls below 2.05 bar, the reaction proceeds forward (dehydration); if it is above, hydration occurs.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major TCS Systems

| System | Reaction | $\Delta H$ (kJ/mol) | $E_s$ (kJ/kg) | $T_{op}$ (°C) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{CaO}/\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ | $\text{CaO}+\text{H}_2\text{O}\rightleftharpoons\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ | 104.4 | 1409 | 400–600 | Cheap, non-toxic | Slow kinetics |
| $\text{CaCO}_3/\text{CaO}$ | $\text{CaCO}_3\rightleftharpoons\text{CaO}+\text{CO}_2$ | 178.3 | 1781 | 800–900 | Very high $E_s$ | Requires $T>800$ °C |
| $\text{MgH}_2/\text{Mg}$ | $\text{MgH}_2\rightleftharpoons\text{Mg}+\text{H}_2$ | 75.0 | 1117 | 300–400 | Compact | Mg is expensive |
| $\text{NH}_3/\text{N}_2+\text{H}_2$ | $\text{NH}_3\rightleftharpoons\tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2+\tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2$ | 46.0 | 2704 | 400–600 | Already industrial | Toxic, catalyst cost |
| Salt hydrates | $\text{SrBr}_2\cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}\rightleftharpoons\text{SrBr}_2\cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}+5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | ~70 | 250 | 60–90 | Low temp | Deliquescence |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermochemical Storage Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HS[Heat Source] -->|Q_in| CHG[Charging Reactor\nDehydration]
    CHG -->|Solid Product| SS1[Storage Silo A\nSpent Reactant]
    CHG -->|Gaseous Product| SS2[Storage Silo B\nVapour Condensate]
    SS1 -->|Held at ambient| DS[Discharging Reactor\nHydration]
    SS2 -->|Vapour fed| DS
    DS -->|Q_out usable heat| HU[Heat User / Power Block]
    DS -->|Solid product| REG[Regeneration Loop\nOptional]
    REG -->|Heat recover| CHG
```

## 4.2 State Machine — Three-Phase Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charging
    Charging: Phase 1 Charging\nEndothermic Reaction\nT rises to T_charge
    Charging --> Storage: Heat captured\nReactants separated
    Storage: Phase 2 Storage\nQuasi-Infinite duration\nNegligible losses
    Storage --> Discharging: On demand\nReactants reunited
    Discharging: Phase 3 Discharging\nExothermic Reaction\nT rises to T_discharge
    Discharging --> [*]: Heat delivered
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CaO/Ca(OH)₂ Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Side
        S1[Concentrated Solar Receiver] -->|Heat Q| F1[Charging Furnace]
    end
    subgraph Charging
        F1 --> R1[Reactor CaOH2 to CaO plus H2O]
        R1 -->|CaO solid| T1[Hopper CaO]
        R1 -->|H2O vapour| C1[Condenser]
        C1 -->|Liquid H2O| T2[Tank Liquid Water]
    end
    subgraph Storage
        T1 -->|Months| ST1[Silo CaO insulated]
        T2 -->|Months| ST2[Tank Water insulated]
    end
    subgraph Discharging
        ST1 --> H1[Hydrator Reactor]
        ST2 -->|Vapour fed| H1
        H1 -->|Heat Q_out| U1[End user heat exchanger]
        H1 -->|CaOH2 solid| T3[Hopper CaOH2 optional recycle]
        T3 -.->|Re-dehydrate| F1
    end
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy of Thermal Energy Storage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TES[Thermal Energy Storage]
    TES --> SHS[Sensible Heat Storage\nWater, Molten Salts, Rocks]
    TES --> LHS[Latent Heat Storage\nPhase Change Materials PCM]
    TES --> TCS[Thermochemical Storage\nReversible Reactions]
    SHS -->|Low Energy Density| C1[100 to 500 kJ per kg]
    LHS -->|Medium Energy Density| C2[200 to 600 kJ per kg]
    TCS -->|High Energy Density| C3[500 to 3000 kJ per kg]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define thermochemical energy storage. List any two advantages it has over sensible and latent heat storage methods.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermochemical energy storage (TCS) is a method of storing thermal energy in the form of **chemical bond energy** of products formed by a reversible endothermic chemical reaction. Heat is supplied to drive the charging reaction; on demand, the reverse exothermic reaction releases the stored energy.

> **Advantages (1 mark for any two):**
> 1. **Higher energy density** (500 – 3000 kJ/kg) compared to sensible (100 – 500 kJ/kg) and latent (200 – 600 kJ/kg) storage.
> 2. **Long-duration, lossless storage** at ambient temperature — heat can be stored for months/years with negligible degradation, unlike sensible/latent systems which suffer standing losses.
> 3. **Transportability** — the charged chemicals can be transported over long distances, decoupling source and demand locations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between sorption storage and chemical reaction storage with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sorption Storage (1½ marks):** Energy is stored by reversible *physical attachment* (adsorption) or *dissolution* (absorption) of a working fluid into a solid/liquid matrix. No permanent chemical bond is formed. **Example:** Water vapour adsorbed onto zeolite 13X releases heat on adsorption.

> **Chemical Reaction Storage (Thermochemical Reaction) (1½ marks):** Energy is stored by *breaking and reforming* actual chemical bonds in a fully reversible reaction. **Example:** $\text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca(OH)}_2(s)$, $\Delta H = -104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$.

> **Key distinction:** Sorption involves *physical* interaction (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding); chemical reaction storage involves *covalent/ionic* bond rearrangement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully!**
> - Students often write only the *reaction equation* and skip the **van't Hoff plot interpretation** (5 marks lost).
> - Numerical questions **require units in every step**; missing units = 2 marks deducted.
> - In comparison-type answers, students forget to mention **energy density in both kJ/kg AND MJ/m³** — at least one volumetric value is expected.
> - Always state the **assumed reference state** (per kg of CaO or per kg of Ca(OH)₂) in derivation questions.

---

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of the CaO/Ca(OH)₂ thermochemical energy storage system with the help of balanced chemical equations. Discuss the charging, storage, and discharging phases.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Working Principle (7 marks distributed as 2+2+2+1):**
>
> The CaO/Ca(OH)₂ system is a *closed-loop, gas-solid* thermochemical storage system based on the reversible hydration/dehydration of calcium hydroxide. It is one of the most mature and economically attractive TCS options for temperatures in the range of **400 – 600 °C**.

> **Phase 1 — Charging (Dehydration, 2 marks):**
> The system is *charged* by heating calcium hydroxide to ~500 °C, which decomposes it into calcium oxide and water vapour:
> $$\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \;\xrightarrow{\;\Delta\;(500^\circ\text{C})\;}\; \text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$
> The reaction is **endothermic** ($\Delta H = +104.4$ kJ/mol), absorbing heat from a renewable source such as concentrated solar power or industrial waste heat.

> **Phase 2 — Storage (2 marks):**
> The solid CaO is stored in an insulated silo, and the water vapour is condensed and stored as liquid water in a separate tank. Both products can be held at **ambient temperature** for *months to years* with virtually zero thermal losses — this is the key advantage of TCS over SHS and LHS.

> **Phase 3 — Discharging (Hydration, 2 marks):**
> When heat is required, water vapour is fed over the stored CaO at ~500 °C, triggering the reverse exothermic reaction:
> $$\text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) + 104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
> The released heat can be used for space heating, process steam, or driving a power block.

> **Round-trip and Cyclability (1 mark):**
> The system can theoretically be cycled > 1000 times with minimal capacity loss (although real-world systems show ~5–10 % degradation per 100 cycles due to sintering of CaO). The total storage time is limited only by the integrity of the storage vessels.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the energy storage density of the CaO/Ca(OH)₂ system in kJ/kg of Ca(OH)₂. Also compute and compare it with sensible heat storage in water heated from 30 °C to 90 °C. Discuss the significance of the comparison.**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given Data:**
> - $\Delta H_{rxn} = 104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$
> - Molar mass of $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 = 74.09 \text{ g/mol} = 0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}$
> - $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
> - $\Delta T = 60 \text{ K}$

> **Step 1 (2 marks): Energy Storage Density of Ca(OH)₂**
> $$E_s = \frac{\Delta H}{M} = \frac{104.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}{0.07409 \text{ kg/mol}} = 1409.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Step 2 (2 marks): Sensible Heat Storage of Water**
> $$Q_{\text{water}} = c_p \cdot \Delta T = 4.18 \times 60 = 250.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Step 3 (2 marks): Comparison**
> $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{1409.2}{250.8} \approx 5.6$$
> The thermochemical system stores **5.6 times** more energy per kg than water over a 60 °C range.

> **Step 4 (1 mark): Significance**
> - Compact storage: 1 tonne of Ca(OH)₂ ≈ 5.6 tonnes of hot water in storage capacity.
> - Critical for space-constrained applications (e.g., district heating, solar thermal power).
> - Long-term storage: CaO can be stored at room temperature indefinitely, whereas hot water loses heat rapidly through tank walls.

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Starting from the Gibbs free energy criterion, derive the van't Hoff equation. Explain how this equation is used to determine the equilibrium temperature and pressure for a thermochemical storage reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Derivation (4 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Equilibrium condition:** At constant $T$ and $P$, a closed system is at equilibrium when:
> $$\Delta G = 0$$
>
> **Step 2 — Standard-state relation:** The standard-state Gibbs free energy change is related to the equilibrium constant:
> $$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$$
>
> **Step 3 — Decomposition of $\Delta G^\circ$:**
> $$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$
>
> **Step 4 — Equate and rearrange:**
> $$-RT \ln K_p = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$$
> $$\ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$

> **Use in Determining $T_{eq}$ (2 marks):**
> Setting $\ln K_p = 0$ (i.e., $K_p = 1$):
> $$T_{eq} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ}$$
> This is the temperature at which the reaction is in equilibrium at standard pressure.

> **Use in Determining Equilibrium Pressure (1 mark):**
> For solid-gas reactions like $\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, $K_p = p_{H_2O}$, so:
> $$p_{H_2O} = \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}\right)$$
> This allows designers to choose the right operating temperature for a given vapour pressure (e.g., atmospheric).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the CaCO₃/CaO+CO₂ system as a high-temperature thermochemical storage option. Include the reaction, enthalpy, energy density, and at least three practical challenges.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reaction and Enthalpy (2 marks):**
> $$\text{CaCO}_3(s) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g), \quad \Delta H = +178.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
> This is the *calcination/carbonation* cycle, well-known from cement and lime industries.

> **Energy Density Calculation (2 marks):**
> - Molar mass of $\text{CaCO}_3 = 100.09 \text{ g/mol} = 0.10009 \text{ kg/mol}$
> - $E_s = 178.3 / 0.10009 = 1781 \text{ kJ/kg}$ — one of the highest among all TCS systems.

> **Operating Conditions (1 mark):**
> - Calcination temperature: ~900 °C at 1 bar CO₂ (or ~700 °C in pure vacuum).
> - Carbonation temperature: ~700 °C with pure CO₂ at 1 bar.

> **Practical Challenges (2 marks — any three):**
> 1. **High operating temperature** requires concentrated solar or industrial-grade heat; otherwise round-trip efficiency is poor.
> 2. **Sintering of CaO** over repeated cycles reduces surface area and hence reactivity (typically 30–40 % capacity drop after 100 cycles).
> 3. **CO₂ management** — closed-loop operation requires separation and re-pressurization of CO₂, increasing auxiliary energy.
> 4. **Solid handling** at high temperature (abrasion, agglomeration) complicates reactor design.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Before the Exam — Glance Through This Summary**

- **Core definition:** TCS stores energy in chemical bonds via reversible reactions.
- **Three phases:** Charging (endothermic) → Storage (ambient) → Discharging (exothermic).
- **Two categories:** Chemical reactions (CaO/Ca(OH)₂, CaCO₃, MgH₂, NH₃) and Sorption (zeolites, salt hydrates).
- **Van't Hoff equation** is the *master relation*: $\ln K_p = -\Delta H^\circ/(RT) + \Delta S^\circ/R$.
- **Equilibrium decomposition temperature** at $K_p = 1$: $T_{eq} = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$.
- **Energy density formula:** $E_s = \Delta H_{rxn} / M_{storage}$ (always quote in **kJ/kg**, and in volumetric form for full marks).
- **CaO/Ca(OH)₂ canonical values to memorise:** $\Delta H = 104.4$ kJ/mol; $E_s \approx 1409$ kJ/kg; $T_{eq} \approx 467$ °C.
- **CaCO₃/CaO values:** $\Delta H = 178.3$ kJ/mol; $E_s \approx 1781$ kJ/kg; $T_{op} \approx 900$ °C.
- **Energy density order (low to high):** Water SHS (250 kJ/kg) < Salt hydrates (250–600) < PCM (200–600) < CaO/Ca(OH)₂ (1409) < CaCO₃ (1781) < NH₃ (2704).
- **Biggest advantage over SHS/LHS:** Lossless, *seasonal* storage with **5–10×** higher energy density.
- **Major challenge:** Slow reaction kinetics and sintering of solids over many cycles.
- **Always show units** in numericals; KTU examiners explicitly check this.
- **Always state reference basis** — per kg of which compound — when computing energy density.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic System for Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photovoltaic System for Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photovoltaic (PV) System for Power Generation:** A solid-state, semiconductor-based direct energy conversion device that exploits the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Edmond Becquerel (1839), in which incident photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy) striking a suitably doped p–n junction liberate electron–hole pairs, which are spatially separated by the built-in electric field of the depletion region, thereby generating a usable DC electromotive force across the external terminals of the cell.

A complete **PV power system** is the integrated assembly of one or more PV modules, power-conditioning electronics (MPPT charge controllers, inverters, transformers), balance-of-system (BOS) components (wiring, protection, mounting), and energy storage (batteries) or grid-interfacing equipment that converts solar irradiance into reliable electrical power for stand-alone or grid-connected applications.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a PV cell as a "one-way valve" for sunshine:**
> Imagine sunlight as a stream of tiny energy-bullets (photons) raining down on a specially designed sandwich of two materials. The top layer is "thirsty" for negative charges (electrons, n-type), and the bottom layer is "thirsty" for positive charges (holes, p-type). At the junction between them, a tiny invisible "electric hill" exists. When a photon strikes with enough energy, it knocks an electron free from its atom. The electric hill at the junction pushes the electron to one side and the hole to the other—much like a bouncer directing people into different rooms of a club. Once separated, the electron wants to flow back to the hole, but if you connect a wire, it must travel *through your appliance* (a fan, a light, a phone charger) to reunite. That forced journey is **electricity**. Sunlight, directly converted, with no moving parts.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Spectra

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used Throughout PV Engineering (KTU Board-Standard Values):**
> - **Solar Constant (I₀) = 1367 W/m²** — the extraterrestrial irradiance perpendicular to the sun's rays at 1 AU.
> - **Air Mass 1.5 (AM1.5) Reference Spectrum = 1000 W/m²** — the standardized terrestrial irradiance used for rating all commercial PV modules (Standard Test Conditions, STC).
> - **Bandgap of crystalline Silicon (c-Si) ≈ 1.12 eV** at 300 K.
> - **Boltzmann constant k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - **Electron charge q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **Standard Test Temperature T_stc = 25 °C = 298.15 K**

## 1.4 Classification of PV Systems (Syllabus Snapshot)

KTU 2024 categorises PV systems into three top-level families:

1. **Stand-Alone (Off-Grid) PV System** — supplies a dedicated load; typically battery-backed.
2. **Grid-Connected (On-Grid / Utility-Interactive) PV System** — operates in parallel with the utility network; usually without batteries.
3. **Hybrid PV System** — PV combined with another energy source (wind, diesel genset, micro-hydro) and storage for higher reliability.

## 1.5 Historical & Industrial Context

- **1954** — Bell Labs (Chapin, Fuller, Pearson) produced the first practical crystalline-silicon solar cell (~6 % efficiency).
- **Modern record cell efficiencies (NREL 2024 data family):**
  - Monocrystalline Si: ~26.1 %
  - Multi-crystalline Si: ~23.3 %
  - Thin-film CdTe: ~22.1 %
  - Perovskite/Silicon tandem: ~33.9 %
- **Global cumulative installed PV capacity surpassed ~1.6 TW by end of 2024**, making solar PV the fastest-growing electricity-generation technology in human history.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photovoltaic Effect — Step-by-Step Physics

The conversion of sunlight into electricity inside a PV cell proceeds through **five sequential physical events**:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy $h\nu$ is absorbed in the semiconductor if $h\nu \geq E_g$. The photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, promoting it to the conduction band and creating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
2. **Thermalisation of Excess Energy:** If $h\nu > E_g$, the surplus energy $(h\nu - E_g)$ is converted to lattice heat rather than electrical energy—this is the **dominant loss mechanism** in single-junction cells (~30 % of incoming energy lost this way).
3. **Carrier Separation:** EHPs generated within or near the **depletion region** of the p–n junction are swept apart by the built-in electric field before they can recombine.
4. **Carrier Collection:** Minority carriers diffuse to the depletion region edge and are collected at the respective n-side (electrons) and p-side (holes).
5. **External Circuit Delivery:** The accumulated photo-generated current flows through the external load, doing useful work.

## 2.2 The Ideal Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit

The **single-diode model** is the standard analytical representation used across the PV industry and in KTU numerical problems.

```
        I_L (photocurrent)  ↓
   ─────┬──────┬──[ diode ]──┬──────┐
        │      │   I_D       │      │
        │      │             │      │
       (I_L)  (I₀)         (R_s)   (R_sh)
        │      │             │      │
   ─────┴──────┴─────────────┴──────┘
                        +
                        │
                       (V)
                        │
                       LOAD
                        │
                        −
```

- **I_L** = Light-generated current (proportional to irradiance G)
- **I₀** = Diode reverse saturation current (depends on temperature)
- **R_s** = Series resistance (ideally ≈ 0)
- **R_sh** = Shunt resistance (ideally → ∞)
- **n** = Ideality factor (1 ≤ n ≤ 2; typically 1.3 for c-Si)
- **V, I** = Terminal voltage and current

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examination — Memorise These.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Single-diode model I–V equation (implicit) | For $R_s \to 0, R_{sh} \to \infty$ reduces to ideal form |
| 2 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | **Ideal solar cell** I–V equation | Used unless stated otherwise in KTU problems |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (at $I=0$) | Typically 0.55–0.65 V for c-Si cell |
| 4 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Short-circuit current (at $V=0$) | Linear in irradiance $G$ |
| 5 | $I_{mp} = I_L \left[1 - \dfrac{nkT}{qV_{oc}}\right]$ | Current at Maximum Power Point (MPP) | Useful in derivation problems |
| 6 | $V_{mp} = V_{oc} - \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$ | Voltage at MPP (transcendental) | Solved iteratively or graphically |
| 7 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}$ | **Fill Factor** (figure of merit of cell shape) | Typically 0.70–0.85 for good c-Si |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}\,FF}{G \cdot A_c}$ | **Conversion Efficiency** | $G = 1000$ W/m² at STC |
| 9 | $\dfrac{I_{sc,2}}{I_{sc,1}} = \dfrac{G_2}{G_1}$ | Isc scales linearly with irradiance | Negligible spectral correction |
| 10 | $P_{in} = G \cdot A_c$ | Incident solar power on cell/module area $A_c$ | $A_c$ in m² |
| 11 | $V_{module} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | Module voltage = series cells × cell voltage | For $N_s$ cells in series |
| 12 | $I_{module} = N_p \cdot I_{cell}$ | Module current = parallel strings × cell current | For $N_p$ strings in parallel |

> **Note on the I–V equation:** Because the equation is **implicit in I**, KTU typically asks you to evaluate it in two limiting cases (open-circuit and short-circuit) where it simplifies to closed-form expressions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Electrification:** Stand-alone PV systems light millions of off-grid homes in Kerala (e.g., KSEB's "Soura" rooftop scheme) and across the developing world.
- **Grid-Scale Solar Farms:** India's Bhadla Solar Park (2.25 GW) demonstrates utility-scale PV economics.
- **Space Applications:** PV powers every satellite, the ISS, and the Mars rovers.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Solar-powered calculators, watches, and IoT sensors.
- **Pumping & Agriculture:** Solar PV water-pumps (PM-KUSUM scheme in India) replace diesel pumps for irrigation.

## 2.5 Effect of Irradiance and Temperature (Board-Favourite Topic)

- **I_sc ∝ G** (linear): halving irradiance halves I_sc.
- **V_oc ∝ ln(G)** (logarithmic): V_oc drops only slightly with reduced G.
- **Temperature Coefficients (c-Si, per °C above 25 °C):**
  - V_oc decreases by ≈ −2.3 mV/°C per cell
  - I_sc increases by ≈ +0.05 %/°C
  - η decreases by ≈ −0.4 to −0.5 %/°C (absolute)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot panels make less power.** This counter-intuitive fact is a common KTU question. Although higher T *increases* I_sc marginally, the *exponential* drop in V_oc dominates, reducing net output. That is why PV modules in tropical Kerala lose ~10–15 % output at noon compared to STC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Ideal Solar Cell I–V Equation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Start with the Shockley diode equation for a p–n junction in the dark:**

$$
I_{dark} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Superpose the light-generated current $I_L$ (this is the photovoltaic action).**

In the dark, the diode carries current $I_{dark}$ for a forward bias $V$. Under illumination, every absorbed photon that produces an EHP effectively acts as an **additional current source** of magnitude $I_L$ flowing in the opposite direction (from n to p) inside the cell. By the **principle of superposition** (valid when R_sh → ∞ and R_s → 0):

$$
I = I_{dark} - I_L
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and define directions.** The terminal current $I$ delivered to the external load is the net current flowing out of the p-contact:

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

This is the **fundamental ideal solar cell equation** — equation (2) of the formula sheet. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Step 1 — At open circuit**, no external current flows, so $I = 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $I = 0$ into the ideal I–V equation:**

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**

$$
I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] = I_L
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $I_0$ and add 1 to both sides:**

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 = \frac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1
$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

Since $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$ for a typical cell ($\sim 10^8$), the "+1" is often dropped in order-of-magnitude estimates. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Short-Circuit Current $I_{sc}$

**Step 1 — At short circuit**, terminal voltage is zero, so $V = 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $V = 0$ into the ideal I–V equation:**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp(0) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify $\exp(0) = 1$, so the bracket vanishes:**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 [\,1 - 1\,] = I_L
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$
I_{sc} = I_L
$$

This is the rationale behind formula (4) in the cheat sheet. The short-circuit current is the *entire* photo-generated current, with no diode loss. ∎

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Power Point Conditions

**Step 1 — Power delivered to the load:**

$$
P = V \cdot I = V \left\{ I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] \right\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the maximum-power condition $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $I$ with respect to $V$:**

$$
\frac{dI}{dV} = -I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the MPP condition:**

$$
I - V \cdot I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — At the MPP, substitute $I = I_{mp}$ and $V = V_{mp}$:**

$$
I_{mp} = V_{mp}\, I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{nkT} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Recall the ideal diode law at $V_{mp}$:**

$$
I_{mp} = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

Equating the two expressions and simplifying (skipping the algebra, which KTU never demands end-to-end) yields the closed-form approximation:

$$
I_{mp} \approx I_L \left[ 1 - \frac{nkT}{q V_{oc}} \right]
$$

This reproduces formula (5) of the cheat sheet. ∎

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Fill Factor and Efficiency

**Step 1 — The fill factor $FF$ is defined as the ratio of the maximum power rectangle to the $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ rectangle on the I–V curve:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\, I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 2 — A widely used empirical approximation (Green, 1981) for $FF$ in terms of the open-circuit voltage $v_{oc} = qV_{oc}/(nkT)$:**

$$
FF = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}
$$

**Step 3 — The conversion efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of electrical power output $P_{max}$ to incident solar power $P_{in}$:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}\, FF}{G \cdot A_c}
$$

This is formula (8) of the cheat sheet. ∎

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Format)

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** A silicon solar cell has $I_L = 3.2$ A, $I_0 = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $n = 1.3$, and is at $T = 300$ K. The cell area is $A_c = 100$ cm², and incident irradiance is $G = 1000$ W/m². Compute $V_{oc}$ and $\eta$ (take $V_{mp} = 0.52$ V, $I_{mp} = 2.95$ A).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal voltage scaled by ideality factor:**

$$
V_T = \frac{nkT}{q} = \frac{1.3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 0.0336 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{oc}$ using the derived formula:**

$$
V_{oc} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) = 0.0336 \ln\!\left(\frac{3.2}{2 \times 10^{-9}} + 1\right)
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.0336 \times \ln(1.6 \times 10^{9}) = 0.0336 \times 21.19 \approx 0.712 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P_{max}$:**

$$
P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 0.52 \times 2.95 = 1.534 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute incident power:**

$$
P_{in} = G \times A_c = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 10 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.534}{10} = 0.1534 = 15.34\,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $FF$ (optional):**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\, I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}} = \frac{0.52 \times 2.95}{0.712 \times 3.2} \approx 0.673
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{oc} = 0.712$ V, $\eta = 15.34\,\%$, $FF = 0.673$. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stand-Alone PV System (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block-renders the **functional architecture and power/control signal flow** of a typical off-grid PV system. Alphanumeric node IDs are used to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SOURCE: SUN] -->|Solar Irradiance G| B1[PV ARRAY]
    B1 -->|DC Power at MPP| C1[CHARGE CONTROLLER WITH MPPT]
    C1 -->|Regulated DC| D1[BATTERY BANK]
    C1 -->|DC Bus| E1[DC LOAD BUS]
    D1 -->|DC during night| E1
    E1 -->|DC Input| F1[POWER INVERTER]
    F1 -->|230V AC 50Hz| G1[AC LOAD BUS]
    G1 --> H1[AC LOADS LIGHT FAN TV]
    D1 --> I1[BATTERY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM BMS]
    I1 -->|SOC and Protection Feedback| C1
    I1 -->|Low Voltage Disconnect| D1
    F1 --> J1[GRID BACKUP OPTIONAL DIESEL GEN]
    J1 --> G1
    K1[ENVIRONMENTAL SENSOR: IRRADIANCE and TEMP] -->|Reference Signal| C1
    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF
    style C1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF
    style H1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Grid-Connected PV System (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[SUN] -->|Irradiance| B2[PV ARRAY]
    B2 -->|DC at MPP| C2[STRING INVERTER with MPPT]
    C2 -->|AC 230V| D2[AC DISTRIBUTION PANEL]
    D2 -->|Local Loads| E2[HOUSEHOLD AC LOADS]
    D2 -->|Net Excess| F2[BI-DIRECTIONAL METER]
    F2 -->|Export| G2[UTILITY GRID]
    G2 -->|Import at Night| F2
    C2 -->|Anti-Islanding Protection| H2[GRID SYNC RELAY]
    H2 --> G2
    I2[DC DISCONNECT SWITCH] --- B2
    J2[AC DISCONNECT SWITCH] --- C2
    style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF
    style C2 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style G2 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 I–V and P–V Characteristics of a Solar Cell (Conceptual Sketch in Mermaid)

The I–V curve is the single most important graph in the entire PV module. We render its **four canonical landmark points** as a Mermaid schematic that the student should mentally visualise before any exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point 1: Short Circuit ISC at V=0] --> P2[Point 2: Knee Region]
    P2 --> P3[Point 3: Maximum Power Point Vmp Imp]
    P3 --> P4[Point 4: Open Circuit VOC at I=0]
    Q1[Power Curve P=VI] -.Peaks at.-> P3
    Q2[I-V Curve] --> P1
    Q2 --> P2
    Q2 --> P3
    Q2 --> P4
    style P3 fill:#00FF00,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    style P4 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF
```

> **Reading the graph (Key for KTU):**
> - At $V=0$, current is maximum: $I = I_{sc}$.
> - At $I=0$, voltage is maximum: $V = V_{oc}$.
> - In between lies the "knee" — the point $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ where the power $P=VI$ reaches its maximum.
> - The four points $V_{oc}, I_{sc}, V_{mp}, I_{mp}$ define the entire performance envelope of the cell.

## 4.4 Series–Parallel Configuration of Cells in a Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph STRING1[Series String of 36 Cells]
        S1[Cell 1] --> S2[Cell 2] --> S3[Cell ...] --> S4[Cell 36]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL_GROUP[Parallel String of 2 Strings]
        STRING1
        STRING2[Series String of 36 Cells] -.- STRING1
    end
    PARALLEL_GROUP --> MOD[MODULE OUTPUT TERMINALS]
    style S1 fill:#1E90FF,color:#FFF
    style S4 fill:#1E90FF,color:#FFF
    style STRING1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4
    style MOD fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
```

> **Engineering note:** A typical 36-cell "12 V nominal" module has $V_{mp} \approx 18$ V (to charge a 12 V battery after diode and cable losses) and a $V_{oc} \approx 22$ V. Modern modules use 60 or 72 cells in series for grid-tie applications.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms: (i) Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, (ii) Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$, and (iii) Fill Factor $FF$ of a solar cell. State the typical range of $FF$ for a good-quality crystalline-silicon cell.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Open-circuit voltage ($V_{oc}$):** The terminal voltage of an illuminated solar cell measured when the external circuit is open (i.e., no current is drawn, $I = 0$). It is the maximum voltage the cell can deliver. Mathematically:

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

For a c-Si cell under STC, $V_{oc}$ is typically **0.55 – 0.72 V**.

**(ii) Short-circuit current ($I_{sc}$):** The terminal current of an illuminated solar cell when the external terminals are shorted (i.e., $V = 0$). It is the maximum current the cell can deliver and is approximately equal to the light-generated current $I_L$. For STC, $I_{sc} \approx 3 – 4$ A per c-Si cell of area 100 cm².

**(iii) Fill Factor ($FF$):** The ratio of the maximum power that can be extracted from the cell to the product $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$:

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\, I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}}
$$

A higher $FF$ means a more "square" I–V curve and a higher-quality cell. **Typical range for a good c-Si cell: 0.70 – 0.85.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a stand-alone PV system. Mention the function of the MPPT charge controller.**

**Model Answer:**

A **stand-alone PV system** (off-grid) supplies electrical loads independently of the utility grid. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) consists of the following blocks in power-flow order:

1. **PV Array** — Converts sunlight into DC electricity at the cell's I–V operating point.
2. **MPPT Charge Controller** — Continuously tracks the Maximum Power Point of the array (where $dP/dV = 0$) and adjusts the operating voltage to harvest maximum energy under varying irradiance and temperature.
3. **Battery Bank** — Stores surplus energy for use during night-time or cloudy periods.
4. **Power Inverter** — Converts DC from the battery/array into 230 V, 50 Hz AC for conventional AC appliances.
5. **AC and DC Loads** — The end-users of electricity.

**Function of MPPT Charge Controller:** It (a) regulates charging to prevent battery over-charge, (b) extracts the **maximum available power** from the array under all conditions (an ordinary PWM controller can lose 20–30 % energy), and (c) provides low-voltage disconnect to protect the battery from deep discharge. Typical MPPT algorithms include **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE 14-Mark Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $V_{oc}$ derivation** — the $\dfrac{nkT}{q}$ term has units of volts; do not drop the "V" or "mV" in the final answer.
> 2. **Mixing up series and parallel scaling** — modules in **series** add voltage, modules in **parallel** add current. Confusing these is the #1 cause of full-mark loss in numericals.
> 3. **Assuming linear efficiency with temperature** — it is a *percentage point* drop per °C, not a linear efficiency scaling.
> 4. **Using $I_L$ and $I_{sc}$ interchangeably without stating the approximation** — always clarify $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ holds only for the ideal model with $R_{sh} \to \infty$.

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ of an ideal solar cell from the diode I–V equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at STC: $I_L = 3.0$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.2$, cell temperature $T = 300$ K, and cell area $A_c = 100$ cm². The cell delivers maximum power at $V_{mp} = 0.50$ V, $I_{mp} = 2.70$ A. Calculate: (i) $V_{oc}$, (ii) Fill Factor $FF$, (iii) Conversion efficiency $\eta$ under irradiance $G = 1000$ W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Derivation of $V_{oc}$:**

**Step 1:** The ideal solar cell I–V equation is:

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Apply open-circuit boundary condition $I = 0$:

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$
I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] = I_L
$$

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1
$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm and isolate $V_{oc}$:

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

(For $I_L \gg I_0$, the "+1" may be dropped: $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$.) ∎ **(7 marks awarded for full derivation with boundary-condition statement.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Evaluation:**

**(i) $V_{oc}$:** First compute the thermal voltage:

$$
V_T = \frac{nkT}{q} = \frac{1.2 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0310 \text{ V}
$$

Now substitute:

$$
V_{oc} = 0.0310 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{3.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} + 1\right) = 0.0310 \times \ln(2.0 \times 10^9)
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.0310 \times 21.42 \approx \mathbf{0.664 \text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Fill Factor:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\, I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}} = \frac{0.50 \times 2.70}{0.664 \times 3.0} = \frac{1.350}{1.992} \approx \mathbf{0.678}
$$

**(iii) Efficiency:** Incident power on $A_c = 100$ cm² $= 0.01$ m²:

$$
P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ W}
$$

Maximum output power:

$$
P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 0.50 \times 2.70 = 1.35 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.35}{10} = \mathbf{13.5\,\%}
$$

**Final Answers (Question A):** $V_{oc} = 0.664$ V, $FF = 0.678$, $\eta = 13.5\,\%$. ∎

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a single-diode equivalent circuit, derive the **ideal solar cell I–V characteristic equation** and label all components. Explain in two sentences why $I_{sc} \approx I_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **series resistance $R_s$** and **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** on the I–V curve and the fill factor. What are the ideal limits for $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$? A 36-cell module is connected to a 12 V battery bank through a charge controller. Each cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V. Calculate the **maximum open-circuit voltage** of the module and comment on whether the system can charge a 12 V battery safely. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Derivation of the I–V equation:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit (refer to Section 2.2):** A light-generated current source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode (reverse saturation current $I_0$, ideality factor $n$), with series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the top node:** The current leaving the node splits into the diode current $I_D$, the shunt current $I_{sh}$, and the external load current $I$:

$$
I_L = I_D + I_{sh} + I
$$

**Step 3 — Write $I_D$ via Shockley equation (voltage across diode is $V + IR_s$):**

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Write $I_{sh}$ via Ohm's law:**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Ideal-case simplification** (set $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Why $I_{sc} \approx I_L$:** At $V = 0$, the exponential term in the ideal equation reduces to 1, making the diode contribution zero, and so all the photo-generated current $I_L$ flows out of the terminals as $I_{sc}$. ∎

---

**(b) Effect of $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$:**

**Series resistance $R_s$:** Represents ohmic losses in the metal contacts, semiconductor bulk, and interconnecting ribbons. It causes the I–V curve to "tilt" away from the ideal in the **first quadrant near $V_{oc}$**, reducing $V_{oc}$ slightly and (more importantly) drastically reducing $FF$. A high $R_s$ creates a spurious voltage drop $I \cdot R_s$ that is wasted as heat. **Ideal limit:** $R_s \to 0$.

**Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$:** Represents leakage current paths around the p–n junction (e.g., at cell edges or through pinholes). It causes the I–V curve to "sag" near $I_{sc}$, reducing $I_{sc}$ slightly and lowering $FF$. **Ideal limit:** $R_{sh} \to \infty$.

**Combined effect:** Both losses *reduce the Fill Factor*, thereby reducing efficiency. $R_s$ is more damaging in the voltage-source region; $R_{sh}$ is more damaging in the current-source region.

**Numerical calculation — Module $V_{oc}$:**

For a 36-cell module with cells in **series**, voltages add:

$$
V_{oc,\,module} = N_s \times V_{oc,\,cell} = 36 \times 0.65 = \mathbf{23.4 \text{ V}}
$$

**Comment on battery charging safety:** A 12 V lead-acid battery needs a charging voltage of approximately **13.8 – 14.4 V** (float) to **14.8 – 15.0 V** (absorption/boost). The module's $V_{mp}$ (≈ 80 % of $V_{oc}$) is around $0.8 \times 23.4 = 18.7$ V, which is more than enough to charge a 12 V battery even after cable and diode drops (~0.7 V). Therefore, the system is **safe and effective** for charging a 12 V battery bank. The standard "12 V nominal" PV module is in fact designed with 36 cells precisely for this purpose. ∎

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Photovoltaic effect** = direct conversion of photon energy to electrical energy via EHP generation at a p–n junction. No moving parts, no thermodynamic cycle.
- **Five canonical events:** absorption → thermalisation → separation → collection → delivery.
- **Single-diode model I–V:** $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ (ideal form). Be ready to write it from memory.
- **Two boundary conditions are non-negotiable:** $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ (at $V=0$) and $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ (at $I=0$).
- **Fill Factor $FF$** is the cell-shape quality factor (0.70–0.85 for c-Si). Always: $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$.
- **Efficiency formula** at STC: $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G \cdot A_c}$, with $G = 1000$ W/m².
- **AM1.5 / STC = 1000 W/m² at 25 °C** is the global standard. Cell area is in **m²**, not cm².
- **Temperature hurts voltage, helps current (marginally), kills net efficiency** — −0.4 to −0.5 %/°C absolute for c-Si.
- **Irradiance scales $I_{sc}$ linearly and $V_{oc}$ logarithmically.**
- **Series connection → adds voltage; parallel connection → adds current.** This is the most-tested KTU board rule.
- **Stand-alone system** = PV + charge controller (with MPPT) + battery + inverter + loads.
- **Grid-connected system** = PV + string/central inverter + bi-directional meter + anti-islanding protection. No batteries needed (usually).
- **MPPT algorithms** worth knowing: Perturb & Observe (P&O) and Incremental Conductance (IncCond).
- **Anti-islanding** is a mandatory protection in grid-tie inverters — the inverter must disconnect within ~2 seconds of a grid outage to protect line workers.
- **Typical KTU value-memorisation:** $kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K; $n=1.3$ typical for c-Si.
- **Module configuration:** 36 cells → "12 V" module; 60 or 72 cells → grid-tie module.
- **Industrial milestones:** 1954 Bell Labs first cell; 2024 cumulative global PV ≈ 1.6 TW; record cell efficiency ~33.9 % (tandem perovskite/Si).
- **Schemes relevant to Kerala/India:** PM-KUSUM (solar pumps), Soura (rooftop), PM-Surya Ghar Yojana (2024 launch).
- **Three system topologies:** Stand-alone, Grid-connected, Hybrid.
- **Ideal cell limit:** $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says "a solar cell is illuminated…", first identify the model (ideal / single-diode with $R_s, R_{sh}$ / two-diode), then apply the two boundary conditions ($V = 0$ for $I_{sc}$, $I = 0$ for $V_{oc}$) — these will give you ~70 % of the marks even on a question you haven't seen before.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Pond - Solar Cells - Types of Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Pond - Solar Cells - Types of Solar Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Pond — Definition & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Solar Pond** is an artificial brine pool that uses the **salinity-gradient principle** to absorb, store, and retain solar thermal energy. The dissolved salt creates a density gradient that suppresses natural convection, allowing the lower zones to reach temperatures of **60 °C – 100 °C**, suitable for direct industrial process heat, electricity generation (via Organic Rankine Cycle), and desalination.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Transparent Thermos Flask"
Imagine a glass of hot water left in the sun. The hot water at the bottom rises (convection), cools at the surface, and your water never gets hot. Now imagine you filled the glass with a layered cocktail — fresh water on top, salty dense water at the bottom. The salty bottom, even when heated by the sun, **cannot rise** because the lighter fresh water sits on top like a transparent lid. The bottom keeps heating up, just like a **thermos flask laid horizontally in the sun**. That trapped heat is harvestable energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "magic" is not the salt — it is the **gradient of salt concentration** that creates a stable, transparent insulating layer.

### Layers of a Salinity-Gradient Solar Pond (SGSP)

| Layer | Name | Salinity | Temperature | Function |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|----------|
| Top | Upper Convective Zone (UCZ) | Low (≈ 1–3 %) | Ambient | Wind mixing, light entry, evaporation shield |
| Middle | Non-Convective Zone (NCZ) | Gradient (3 % → 25 %) | ~ 30–60 °C | Transparent insulating barrier |
| Bottom | Lower Convective Zone (LCZ) | High (≈ 25–30 %, saturated) | 60 – 100 °C | **Heat storage & extraction** |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** The NCZ thickness is typically **1.0 – 1.5 m**; total pond depth is **2 – 3 m**; surface area is **1000 – 10,000 m²** for commercial systems.

---

## 1.2 Solar Cell — Definition & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Solar Cell** (or Photovoltaic Cell) is a semiconductor p–n junction device that converts incident photons directly into electrical energy via the **photovoltaic effect**, first observed by Becquerel (1839) and physically explained by Einstein (1905, Nobel Prize).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The One-Way Staircase"
Think of a p–n junction as a **staircase that only goes up**: photons (light packets) hit the surface, knock electrons loose, and a built-in **electric field at the junction** acts as a one-way turnstile. Electrons are pushed to the n-side, holes to the p-side, and a wire connecting the two sides gives them a path back — that flow of electrons **is** your electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Photovoltaic Effect** = generation of a voltage / electric current in a material upon exposure to electromagnetic radiation (photons).

### Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU)

- Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Electron charge: **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Boltzmann constant: **k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Band-gap of crystalline Si: **E_g = 1.12 eV**
- Standard test condition: **AM 1.5, 1000 W/m², 25 °C**

---

## 1.3 Types of Solar Cells — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification (Three Generations):**

| Generation | Type | Typical Efficiency | Examples |
|------------|------|--------------------|----------|
| **1st Gen** | Crystalline Silicon (wafer-based) | 15 – 25 % | Monocrystalline Si, Polycrystalline Si |
| **2nd Gen** | Thin-Film (deposited) | 10 – 18 % | Amorphous Si (a-Si), CdTe, CIGS |
| **3rd Gen** | Emerging / Multi-junction | 20 – 47 % | Multi-junction (GaInP/GaAs/Ge), Perovskite, DSSC, Organic PV, Quantum Dot |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** A "type" is identified by the **active absorber material**, not by physical form. Always classify by generation + active material.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Pond — Theory of Operation

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Solar incidence:** Sunlight passes through the transparent UCZ and NCZ (both optically clear, attenuation coefficient < 0.3 m⁻¹).
2. **Absorption in LCZ:** ~ 20 – 30 % of incident solar radiation is absorbed in the bottom dark layer.
3. **Natural convection attempt:** Heated bottom water tries to rise.
4. **Gradient suppression:** The saltier (denser) bottom cannot rise through fresher (lighter) water above because the density gradient exceeds the thermal expansion effect. **Rayleigh number < critical value (Ra_cr ≈ 1000).**
5. **Storage:** Heat is stored as **sensible heat** in the LCZ (water has specific heat ≈ 4200 J/kg·K).
6. **Extraction:** Hot brine is pumped through an external **heat exchanger**; cooled brine is re-injected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the gradient prevent convection?**
> Hot salty water *expands* (less dense) but is still **denser** than the cooler, fresher water above it, so the column is **statically stable** (negative density gradient vs. depth).

### Governing Equations

The **thermal efficiency** of a solar pond collector is:

$$
\eta_{pond} = \frac{Q_{extracted}}{Q_{incident}} = \frac{\dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in})}{G_T \cdot A_{pond}}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of brine (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of brine (≈ 3200 – 4200 J/kg·K)
- $G_T$ = global solar irradiance (W/m²)
- $A_{pond}$ = effective pond area (m²)

The **Heat Loss** from the LCZ to NCZ by conduction:

$$
Q_{loss} = k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_{LCZ} - T_{UCZ})}{d_{NCZ}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Israel (Ein Bokek, 1979 — world's first operational SGSP, 5 MW), USA, India (Bhavnagar, IICT Hyderabad pilot), China (Hebei, Tibet). Used for:
> - Power generation via **Kalina Cycle** or ORC
> - Greenhouse heating (Tibet pilot — 40,000 m² pond)
> - Seawater desalination (multi-effect distillation)
> - Space heating in cold desert regions

---

## 2.2 Solar Cell — Theory of Operation

### Step-by-Step Photovoltaic Conversion

1. **Photon absorption:** Photon energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed in the semiconductor, creating an **electron–hole pair**.
2. **Carrier separation:** The built-in field at the p–n junction sweeps electrons → n-side, holes → p-side.
3. **Collection:** Metal contacts collect the carriers → external current $I$.
4. **Recombination losses:** Not all generated carriers are collected; characterized by **reverse saturation current $I_0$**.

### Equivalent Circuit Model

A solar cell is modelled as a **current source in parallel with a diode**, with series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shockley Diode Equation with Photogeneration:**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

For an **ideal cell** ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$):

$$
\boxed{I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = terminal current (A)
- $I_L$ = light-generated current (A)
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (≈ 10⁻¹² A for Si)
- $V$ = terminal voltage (V)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 – 2)
- $T$ = cell temperature (K)
- $q/kT$ = thermal voltage ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ideal solar cell I–V relation | A |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage | V |
| 3 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Short-circuit current (ideal) | A |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (0.6 – 0.85) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{P_{in}}$ | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{max} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF$ | Maximum power output | W |
| 7 | $E_g = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_c}$ | Cut-off wavelength (band-gap) | m |
| 8 | $I_{ph} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Photogenerated current (photon flux) | A |
| 9 | $\eta_{pond} = \dfrac{\dot m c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}{G_T A_{pond}}$ | Solar pond thermal efficiency | % |
| 10 | $Q_{loss} = k_{eff} A \dfrac{T_{LCZ} - T_{UCZ}}{d_{NCZ}}$ | Pond conductive heat loss | W |
| 11 | $Q_{stored} = m \, c_p \, (T_{final} - T_{initial})$ | Sensible heat stored in pond | J |
| 12 | $\lambda_c (\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g (eV)}$ | Quick cut-off wavelength | μm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Marks-Loser Trap:** In equation 12, mixing units gives a wrong answer. If $E_g$ is in **eV**, then $\lambda_c$ comes in **μm** (because $hc/q \approx 1.24$ eV·μm). If $E_g$ is in **Joules**, use $hc$ with metres.

---

## 2.4 Types of Solar Cells — Comparative Analysis

### First Generation (Crystalline Silicon)

#### (a) Monocrystalline Silicon (mono-Si)
- **Process:** Czochralski pulling of single-crystal ingot → wafers (180 – 300 μm thick).
- **Efficiency:** 17 – 25 % (lab record 26.7 %, cell — UNSW PERL).
- **Colour/look:** Uniform dark blue/black, rounded pseudo-square wafers.
- **Pros:** Highest commercial efficiency, mature technology, 25–30 yr lifespan.
- **Cons:** High cost (energy-intensive Si purification), kerf loss during sawing.

#### (b) Polycrystalline Silicon (poly-Si)
- **Process:** Cast ingot of molten Si, lower purity, multi-grain structure.
- **Efficiency:** 13 – 18 %.
- **Look:** Speckled blue surface (grain boundaries visible).
- **Pros:** Cheaper than mono, less wastage.
- **Cons:** Lower efficiency due to grain-boundary recombination.

### Second Generation (Thin-Film)

#### (c) Amorphous Silicon (a-Si)
- **Process:** Vapour deposition of Si:H on glass/plastic (1 – 2 μm thick, 100× thinner than c-Si).
- **Efficiency:** 6 – 10 % (initial); degrades by ~ 15 % in first 6 months (Staebler–Wronski effect).
- **Pros:** Flexible, low cost, works in low light.
- **Cons:** Low efficiency, light-induced degradation.

#### (d) Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)
- **Process:** Thin film of CdTe on glass with CdS window layer.
- **Efficiency:** 18 – 22 % (First Solar commercial record 22.1 %).
- **Pros:** Cheapest $/W; ideal band-gap 1.45 eV.
- **Cons:** Toxicity of Cd; Te scarcity.

#### (e) Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS)
- **Efficiency:** 18 – 23 % (lab record 23.35 %).
- **Pros:** High efficiency in thin film, flexible substrates possible.
- **Cons:** Indium scarcity, complex deposition.

### Third Generation (Emerging / Advanced)

#### (f) Multi-Junction (MJ) Cells
- **Concept:** Stack 2 or 3 sub-cells with decreasing band-gaps (e.g., GaInP 1.86 eV / GaAs 1.42 eV / Ge 0.67 eV) to absorb different parts of the spectrum.
- **Efficiency:** 35 – 47 % (concentrator cells, lab record 47.6 %, NREL 2023).
- **Application:** Space satellites, concentrated PV (CPV, 500 – 1000 suns).

#### (g) Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC / Grätzel Cell)
- **Structure:** Nanoporous TiO₂ + dye + liquid electrolyte (I⁻/I₃⁻ redox).
- **Efficiency:** 12 – 15 %.
- **Pros:** Cheap, semi-transparent, works in diffuse light.
- **Cons:** Liquid electrolyte leakage, low stability.

#### (h) Perovskite Solar Cells
- **Material:** Methylammonium lead halide (CH₃NH₃PbI₃) etc.
- **Efficiency:** Skyrocketed from 3.8 % (2009) → **26.1 %** (2024, lab) — fastest growth in PV history.
- **Pros:** Cheap solution-processable, high absorption.
- **Cons:** Lead toxicity, moisture sensitivity, long-term stability.

#### (i) Organic Photovoltaics (OPV)
- **Material:** Conjugated polymers (P3HT, PCBM blends).
- **Efficiency:** 8 – 18 %.
- **Pros:** Roll-to-roll printing, flexible, semi-transparent.
- **Cons:** Very low lifetime, rapid degradation in air.

#### (j) Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSC)
- **Concept:** Use semiconductor nanocrystals (PbS, CdSe) — tunable band-gap by particle size.
- **Efficiency:** 18 – 19 %.
- **Pros:** Multiple exciton generation (MEG) → theoretical efficiency > 44 %.
- **Cons:** Toxicity, stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Solar Cell I–V Equation (Shockley)

### Step 1: Start with the p–n Junction Diode Equation

The current through a p–n junction diode (dark condition) is governed by the **Shockley diode equation**:

$$
I_{dark} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Explanation of constants:**
- $I_0$ = reverse saturation current (A) — depends on doping and material
- $V$ = applied voltage across the junction (V)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 = ideal diffusion, 2 = recombination-dominated)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

---

### Step 2: Introduce Photogeneration Current $I_L$

When light strikes the cell, **additional electron–hole pairs** are generated. The photogenerated current flows in the **opposite direction** to the diode forward current. By the **Superposition Principle** (valid for low injection):

$$
I_{cell} = I_{dark} - I_L
$$

Where $I_L$ is the **light-generated current**, proportional to incident light intensity $G$:

$$
I_L \approx I_{sc} \approx K \cdot G
$$

(K = responsivity constant)

---

### Step 3: Apply Superposition

Substituting the dark diode current into the cell equation:

$$
I = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - I_L
$$

Rearranging (reversing sign convention so $I$ is positive when delivered to the load):

$$
\boxed{I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]}
$$

This is the **fundamental solar cell equation** — note the sign convention: short-circuit current is **positive**.

---

### Step 4: Boundary Conditions

**Open-circuit condition** ($I = 0$):

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$
\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)}
$$

**Short-circuit condition** ($V = 0$):

$$
\boxed{I_{sc} = I_L}
$$

(because the exponential term becomes $e^0 - 1 = 0$).

---

### Step 5: Maximum Power Point (MPP) — Fill Factor

The power output is $P = V \cdot I$. To find the maximum, differentiate and set to zero: $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.

At the maximum power point $(V_m, I_m)$:

$$
V_m \approx V_{oc} - \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{V_m}{nkT/q} + 1\right)
$$

(The transcendental equation is solved numerically.)

The **Fill Factor (FF)** quantifies the "squareness" of the I–V curve:

$$
\boxed{FF = \frac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}}
$$

Empirical approximation (Green, 1981):

$$
FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}
$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ is the **normalized open-circuit voltage**.

---

### Step 6: Conversion Efficiency

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G_T \cdot A_{cell}}}
$$

Where $G_T \cdot A_{cell} = P_{in}$ is the total incident solar power.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Solar Cell (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A silicon solar cell has $I_L = 3.0$ A, $I_0 = 1 \times 10^{-10}$ A, $n = 1.2$, $T = 300$ K. Compute $V_{oc}$ and the conversion efficiency if $I_{sc} = 2.95$ A, $FF = 0.78$, and incident power is $P_{in} = 60$ W. Also find the cut-off wavelength if $E_g = 1.12$ eV.

**Step 1: Thermal voltage**

$$
V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Open-circuit voltage**

$$
V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\frac{I_L}{I_0} = \frac{3.0}{1 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.0 \times 10^{10}
$$

Compute the logarithm:

$$
\ln(3.0 \times 10^{10}) = \ln(3.0) + 10 \ln(10) = 1.0986 + 23.0259 = 24.1245
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{oc} = 1.2 \times 0.02585 \times 24.1245 = 0.7484 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum power**

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \times I_{sc} \times FF = 0.7484 \times 2.95 \times 0.78 = 1.722 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.722}{60} = 0.0287 = 2.87 \%
$$

**Step 5: Cut-off wavelength**

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107 \text{ μm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cell absorbs all photons with wavelength $\lambda \leq 1.107\ \mu\text{m}$ (i.e., visible and near-IR up to ~1100 nm). Photons above this wavelength (lower energy) pass through unabsorbed.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Solar Pond (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 1500 m² solar pond receives average daily solar radiation of $G_T = 5.5$ kWh/m²/day. The LCZ stores 7 × 10⁵ kg of brine ($c_p = 3500$ J/kg·K). The pond heats from 25 °C to 75 °C in 24 hours. Heat extraction load is $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T = 2.0 \times 10^8$ J/day. Compute the daily heat stored, the thermal efficiency, and the NCZ heat loss (assume $k_{eff} = 0.6$ W/m·K, $d_{NCZ} = 1.2$ m, $T_{LCZ} - T_{UCZ} = 50$ K).

**Step 1: Solar energy input**

$$
Q_{in} = G_T \times A_{pond} = 5.5 \times 1500 = 8250 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Convert to Joules:

$$
Q_{in} = 8250 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 2.97 \times 10^{10} \text{ J/day}
$$

**Step 2: Heat stored in LCZ**

$$
Q_{stored} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 7 \times 10^5 \times 3500 \times 50
$$

$$
Q_{stored} = 1.225 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
$$

(Notice: the stored heat can exceed one day's input because the pond is a **seasonal** storage device.)

**Step 3: Heat loss through NCZ (per day)**

$$
Q_{loss} = k_{eff} A \dfrac{\Delta T}{d_{NCZ}} \times 86400 \text{ s}
$$

$$
= 0.6 \times 1500 \times \dfrac{50}{1.2} \times 86400
$$

$$
= 0.6 \times 1500 \times 41.67 \times 86400
$$

$$
= 3.24 \times 10^9 \text{ J/day}
$$

**Step 4: Thermal efficiency (instantaneous)**

$$
\eta_{pond} = \dfrac{\dot m c_p \Delta T}{G_T A_{pond}} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^8}{2.97 \times 10^{10}} = 0.00673 = 0.67 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is pond efficiency low?** Most incident solar energy is **re-radiated, reflected, or lost** to evaporation. The pond's role is **storage**, not peak conversion. Annual efficiency is 15 – 25 %.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
