# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define a solar pond. Name its three zones with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **solar pond** is a salinity-gradient brine pool that absorbs, stores, and converts solar thermal energy by suppressing natural convection through a vertical salt concentration gradient. The three zones are:
- **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ):** Low-salinity surface layer; allows solar transmission and protects NCZ from wind mixing.
- **Non-Convective Zone (NCZ):** Middle transparent gradient zone; acts as a transparent insulating barrier preventing convective heat loss from the hot LCZ.
- **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ):** Bottom saturated brine layer where solar energy is absorbed and stored as sensible heat; from which heat is extracted.

*(1 mark each: definition + 3 zones, with 1 mark for explaining the function of any one zone.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three differences between monocrystalline and polycrystalline silicon solar cells.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabulated form expected):**

| Property | Monocrystalline Si | Polycrystalline Si |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Crystal structure | Single, continuous | Multiple grains |
| Efficiency | 17 – 25 % | 13 – 18 % |
| Cost | High (Czochralski process) | Moderate (cast ingot) |
| Appearance | Uniform dark black/blue | Speckled blue |
| Grain boundary losses | None | Significant |

*(Any 3 valid differences = 1 mark each.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and the salinity-gradient mechanism of a **solar pond**. Discuss its main applications and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Derive the **open-circuit voltage** of a solar cell from the Shockley diode equation. A silicon solar cell has $I_L = 2.5$ A, $I_0 = 10^{-10}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.3$, and operates at 300 K. Calculate $V_{oc}$. Take $kT/q = 0.0259$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Q. A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**
The pond is a shallow artificial pool (depth 2–3 m, area 1000–10,000 m²) constructed with:
- **Black HDPE liner** at the bottom for absorption and waterproofing.
- **Three fluid layers** (UCZ, NCZ, LCZ) filled with brine (NaCl or MgCl₂).
- **Inlet/outlet piping** for brine circulation through a heat exchanger.
- **Optional wind break fence** to reduce surface mixing.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Solar radiation penetrates the transparent UCZ and NCZ with little absorption (water extinction < 0.3 m⁻¹).
2. The black bottom and dark LCZ absorb ~ 20–30 % of incident radiation.
3. Heat normally would rise by convection, but the **salinity gradient** increases density with depth, making the water column **statically stable**.
4. The bottom water temperature rises to **60 – 100 °C** while the surface remains near ambient.
5. Hot brine is pumped from the LCZ, passed through a **heat exchanger**, and returned. Useful heat is delivered to a turbine (ORC/Kalina), distillation unit, or building heating loop.

**Step 3 — Salinity-Gradient Mechanism (1 mark):**
The NCZ is established by carefully layering brine of decreasing concentration from bottom to top. The criterion for stability is:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} \geq \beta \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}
$$

where $\beta$ is the thermal expansion coefficient. This guarantees **Rayleigh number Ra < Ra_critical ≈ 1000**, suppressing convection.

**Step 4 — Applications (1 mark):**
- Power generation via low-temperature Rankine/Kalina cycles
- Greenhouse heating in cold regions
- Multi-effect distillation desalination
- Aquaculture + space heating integration

**Step 5 — Limitations:** Land-intensive, low power density (~ 10–20 W/m²), susceptible to surface dilution from rain, requires maintenance of gradient zone.

---

### Solution to Q. A (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the ideal solar cell I–V equation:

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Apply **open-circuit condition** $I = 0$:

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Rearrange:

$$
I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] = I_L
$$

Divide by $I_0$:

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 = \frac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

Add 1 to both sides:

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1
$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$
\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{nkT}{q}$:

$$
\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given: $I_L = 2.5$ A, $I_0 = 10^{-10}$ A, $n = 1.3$, $kT/q = 0.0259$ V.

Step 1 — Ratio:

$$
\frac{I_L}{I_0} = \frac{2.5}{10^{-10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{10}
$$

Step 2 — Natural log:

$$
\ln(2.5 \times 10^{10}) = \ln(2.5) + 10 \ln(10) = 0.9163 + 23.0259 = 23.9422
$$

Step 3 — Compute $V_{oc}$:

$$
V_{oc} = 1.3 \times 0.0259 \times 23.9422
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.03367 \times 23.9422 = 0.806 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{oc} = 0.806$ V (approx **0.81 V**). This is realistic for a single-junction silicon cell.

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks

> **Q. (a)** Explain the **photovoltaic effect** with a clear diagram. Derive the **ideal I–V characteristic equation** of a solar cell starting from the Shockley diode equation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Classify solar cells into three generations. Compare monocrystalline, amorphous silicon, and perovskite solar cells on the basis of efficiency, cost, and stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Q. B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Photovoltaic Effect (2 marks):**
When photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike a semiconductor p–n junction, they transfer their energy to valence-band electrons, which then jump to the conduction band, leaving behind holes. The **built-in depletion-region electric field** of the p–n junction sweeps the photo-generated electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side. Connecting an external circuit allows these carriers to flow, producing a **photocurrent** in the opposite direction to the forward diode current.

**Step 2 — Derivation of I–V (5 marks):**

Start with the **Shockley diode equation** (dark current):

$$
I_{dark} = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Under illumination, an additional current $I_L$ is generated, flowing in the **opposite direction** to $I_{dark}$. Apply the **superposition principle**:

$$
I_{cell} = I_{dark} - I_L
$$

Substituting:

$$
I = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - I_L
$$

By convention (load current positive), reverse the sign:

$$
\boxed{I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]}
$$

At short circuit ($V = 0$): $I = I_L = I_{sc}$.
At open circuit ($I = 0$): $V = V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$.

---

### Solution to Q. B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Three Generations (2 marks):**

1. **First Generation:** Crystalline silicon wafer-based cells (mono-Si, poly-Si).
2. **Second Generation:** Thin-film deposited cells (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS).
3. **Third Generation:** Emerging technologies (multi-junction, perovskite, DSSC, OPV, quantum dot).

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Monocrystalline Si | Amorphous Si (a-Si) | Perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃) |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Generation | 1st | 2nd | 3rd |
| Module efficiency | 18 – 22 % | 6 – 10 % | 18 – 26 % (lab) |
| Cost ($/W) | 0.30 – 0.50 | 0.20 – 0.35 | 0.10 – 0.25 (projected) |
| Stability / Lifetime | Excellent (25–30 yr) | Moderate (10–15 yr; Staebler–Wronski degradation) | Poor (moisture, heat; < 5 yr) |
| Toxicity | None | None | Lead-containing |
| Manufacturing | Energy-intensive (Czochralski) | Low-temp vapour deposition | Solution-processable (printing) |
| Best application | Rooftop, utility | Calculators, low-power electronics | Research, building-integrated PV |

**Step 3 — Inference (1 mark):**
Monocrystalline Si remains the **industrial workhorse**; a-Si is niche for low-power flexible devices; perovskite is **promising** but commercial deployment awaits stability breakthroughs (encapsulation, lead-free formulations).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion** in the diode equation: Writing $I = I_0 \exp(\cdot) - I_L$ instead of $I = I_L - I_0 \exp(\cdot)$ → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the "-1"** inside the exponential → invalid boundary conditions.
> 3. **Confusing thermal voltage $kT/q$** (≈ 25.85 mV) with band-gap voltage $E_g/q$ (1.12 V for Si).
> 4. In solar pond answers, **not mentioning all three zones** explicitly → loses 1 mark.
> 5. In the classification question, **mixing up generation and type** (e.g., calling CdTe "first generation" is wrong — it is second generation) → loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Unit errors in $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$** — must specify the equation requires $E_g$ in eV to get $\lambda_c$ in μm.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Solar pond = 3 zones** (UCZ top, NCZ middle gradient, LCZ bottom hot storage). Function of NCZ is to suppress convection.
- [ ] **Storage medium = sensible heat** in dense brine; $Q = m c_p \Delta T$.
- [ ] **Pond efficiency** is low (daily ~ 1 %, annual 15 – 25 %) because its primary role is **storage**, not peak conversion.
- [ ] **Photovoltaic effect** = photon-induced generation of electron–hole pairs separated by the p–n junction field.
- [ ] **Key cell equation:** $I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ — memorize signs and "-1".
- [ ] **$V_{oc}$** depends on $\ln(I_L/I_0)$; **$I_{sc}$** depends linearly on light intensity.
- [ ] **FF + efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{P_{in}}$. FF typically 0.6–0.85.
- [ ] **Band-gap cut-off:** $\lambda_c(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$. Si (1.12 eV) → ~ 1.11 μm.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV. Memorize this.
- [ ] **First-generation cells** = crystalline Si (mono + poly). **Second-generation** = thin films (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS). **Third-generation** = emerging (perovskite, DSSC, multi-junction, OPV, QD).
- [ ] **Perovskite efficiency** has grown fastest — 3.8 % (2009) → 26.1 % (2024). But **stability is the bottleneck**.
- [ ] **Multi-junction cells** = highest lab efficiency (47.6 % under concentration), used in **space** and **CPV**.
- [ ] **AM 1.5 G, 1000 W/m², 25 °C** = the universal standard test condition (STC) for rating solar cells.
- [ ] For numerical problems, **always state the boundary condition used** (open circuit / short circuit / MPP) — examiners award marks for this.
- [ ] **Equivalent circuit:** current source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode and $R_{sh}$, in series with $R_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Working and Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Working and Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Cell — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A Solar Photovoltaic Cell is a semiconductor-based solid-state **p-n junction device** that directly converts incident solar photon energy into usable direct current (DC) electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect** — the generation of a potential difference at the junction upon absorption of photons whose energy is greater than or equal to the semiconductor bandgap energy $E_g$.

The associated **Performance Characteristics** refer to the graphical and analytical relationships between the cell's electrical output parameters — specifically the **Current–Voltage (I-V)** and **Power–Voltage (P-V)** curves — under defined conditions of irradiance ($G$), temperature ($T$), and air mass (AM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> The principle of operation is centered on three pillars: **(i) Photon absorption**, **(ii) Electron-hole pair generation**, and **(iii) Charge separation by the built-in junction electric field**. Performance is quantitatively expressed using **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$), Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$), Fill Factor (FF), Maximum Power Point ($P_{max}$), and Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **two-storey water tank system** where water naturally wants to flow from the upper tank to the lower one:

- The **upper tank (P-side)** represents holes (positive charge carriers).
- The **lower tank (N-side)** represents electrons (negative charge carriers).
- A **one-way valve (the p-n junction's depletion region)** prevents charges from re-mixing once separated.
- **Sunlight photons** act like an external **water pump**, forcing electrons "uphill" from the N-side to the P-side by exciting them across the bandgap.
- Once the pump (photon) has lifted the charges, they flow through the **external load** (e.g., a bulb or motor), delivering useful work — just like water flowing out of the tank through a tap.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **A-G-S-C** → **A**bsorption → **G**eneration of EHP → **S**eparation by junction → **C**ollection at terminals.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Electron Charge | $q$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| Thermal Voltage at 300 K | $V_T = kT/q$ | **$\approx 25.85$ mV** |
| Standard Test Irradiance | $G_{STC}$ | **$1000 \text{ W/m}^2$** |
| Standard Test Temperature | $T_{STC}$ | **$25^\circ\text{C} \; (298.15\text{ K})$** |
| Air Mass Reference | AM | **1.5 G** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Six Logical Steps

The photovoltaic conversion process is decomposed as follows:

1. **Photon Incidence**: Solar irradiance $G$ strikes the anti-reflective coated front surface of the cell. Photons with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ are transmitted into the absorber material (typically crystalline silicon, $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV).
2. **Band-to-Band Absorption**: Each absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, elevating it into the conduction band. This creates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)** — the fundamental "currency" of photovoltaic current.
3. **Thermalization Loss**: Photons with $h\nu > E_g$ lose the excess energy as lattice heat (phonons). This is the *primary* thermodynamic loss mechanism, capping single-junction efficiency below the **Shockley–Queisser limit of $\approx 33.7\%$**.
4. **Drift Across Depletion Region**: The built-in electric field $\mathcal{E}$ of the p-n junction depletion region sweeps the photo-generated electrons toward the N-side and holes toward the P-side *before* they recombine.
5. **Collection at Ohmic Contacts**: Front (grid) and rear (continuous) metallic contacts collect the separated carriers. The terminal voltage $V$ appears between the contacts, equal to the quasi-Fermi level splitting.
6. **External Delivery**: The photocurrent $I_L$ drives the connected load. The terminal current is reduced by the **diode (dark) recombination current** $I_D$, giving the net output $I = I_L - I_D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this engineering-critical?** Understanding each loss step allows engineers to optimize **bandgap selection** (multi-junction cells), **passivation** (PERC, HIT cells), **light trapping** (textured surfaces), and **contact design** (multi-busbars) — all hot topics in KTU semiconductor electives and modern industry practice.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers $\ge 80\%$ of numerical problems on this topic in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ | **Ideal Solar Cell I-V Equation** (single exponential model); $n$ = ideality factor ($1 \le n \le 2$). |
| 2 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Short-circuit current when $V = 0$; dominated by **photogeneration** — proportional to irradiance $G$. |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage when $I = 0$; logarithmically dependent on $I_L$, strongly affected by **temperature**. |
| 4 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Instantaneous electrical power output of the cell. |
| 5 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Power at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** on the P-V curve. |
| 6 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}$ | **Fill Factor** — "squareness" of the I-V curve; higher $FF$ = higher quality cell. |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | **Conversion Efficiency**; $A$ = cell area in $\text{m}^2$. |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg|_{V=V_{mp}} = 0$ | Condition defining the Maximum Power Point. |
| 9 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage ($\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K). |
| 10 | $\eta_{SQ} \approx 33.7\%$ | **Shockley–Queisser detailed-balance limit** for a single-junction cell under AM1.5G. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Caution:** In handwritten KTU scripts and LaTeX tables, never confuse the **ideality factor $n$** with the **diode ideality constant**. KTU valuators *will* deduct marks for ambiguous notation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Ideal Solar Cell I-V Equation

We model the illuminated p-n junction as a **photocurrent source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode** carrying dark current $I_D$, plus a series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node of the equivalent circuit:**

$$
I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the diode current using the Shockley diode equation:**

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_D}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Express the shunt current via Ohm's law across $R_{sh}$:**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V_D}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I_D$ and $I_{sh}$ into the KCL equation (ideal case: $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$, so $I_{sh} \to 0$ and $V_D \approx V$):**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Define the thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q$ to simplify notation:**

$$
\boxed{\;I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]\;}
$$

> This is the **single-diode model** of a solar cell, the foundational equation for all subsequent derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Step 1 — At open-circuit terminals, the external current $I = 0$. Apply this boundary condition:**

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the exponential term:**

$$
I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] = I_L
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the bracket:**

$$
I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - I_0 = I_L
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange so the exponential stands alone:**

$$
I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) = I_L + I_0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the natural logarithm to both sides (since $I_L \gg I_0$ in well-illuminated cells):**

$$
\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T} = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L + I_0}{I_0}\right) \approx \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**

$$
\boxed{\;V_{oc} = nV_T \,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \frac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)\;}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Because $V_{oc}$ depends on the **ratio $I_L/I_0$**, any reduction in saturation current (better passivation, higher wafer quality) exponentially increases $V_{oc}$ — a key lever in modern cell design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Power Point Condition

The instantaneous power delivered to the load is:

$$
P(V) = V \cdot I(V) = V \left\{I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]\right\}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the power expression:**

$$
P(V) = V I_L - V I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right) + V I_0
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $P(V)$ with respect to $V$ (product rule applied to the second term):**

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I_L - I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right) - V I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{nV_T} \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right) + I_0
$$

**Step 3 — Group the exponential terms together:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I_L + I_0 - I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V}{nV_T}\right)\left[1 + \frac{V}{nV_T}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the MPP condition $\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg|_{V=V_{mp}} = 0$:**

$$
I_L + I_0 = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mp}}{nV_T}\right)\left[1 + \frac{V_{mp}}{nV_T}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $I_L \gg I_0$ under normal operation:**

$$
\boxed{\;I_L \approx I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{mp}}{nV_T}\right)\!\left[1 + \frac{V_{mp}}{nV_T}\right]\;}
$$

> This **transcendental equation** has no closed-form solution for $V_{mp}$ and is solved numerically (Newton–Raphson) in PV simulators such as **PVsyst, SAM, or MATLAB/Simulink**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

**Step 1 — Write the definition of efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $P_{max} = V_{mp} I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} I_{sc}$ (by definition of Fill Factor):**

$$
\eta = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express input power as irradiance times cell area $A$:**

$$
P_{in} = G \cdot A
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form expression:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}\;}
$$

> **Numerical sanity check (KTU-style):** A commercial mono-Si cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A, $FF = 0.78$, $A = 0.0156$ m² ($156 \times 156$ mm pseudo-square). Compute:
> $$\eta = \frac{0.78 \times 0.65 \times 3.5}{1000 \times 0.0156} = \frac{1.7745}{15.6} \approx 0.1138 = 11.38\%.$$
> This matches first-generation commercial modules (pre-PERC era). Modern PERC cells exceed 22%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PV Cell Operating Principle

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sunlight Photons hv] --> B[Anti Reflection Coating]
    B --> C[Photon Absorption in Si]
    C --> D[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    D --> E[PN Junction Depletion Field]
    E --> F[Electrons Drift to N Side]
    E --> G[Holes Drift to P Side]
    F --> H[Front Contact Grid]
    G --> I[Back Contact Layer]
    H --> J[External Load]
    I --> J
    J --> K[DC Electrical Power Output]
```

**Reading guide:** Each arrow represents a sequential energy-conversion step. Photons flow top-down; charges separate laterally into P and N streams before recombining through the load.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Photocurrent Source IL] --> N1((Node A))
    N1 --> D[Diode with Dark Current I0]
    N1 --> Rsh[Shunt Resistance Rsh]
    Rsh --> N2((Node B))
    D --> N2
    N1 --> Rs[Series Resistance Rs]
    Rs --> T[Output Terminals]
    N2 --> T
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** A single-line equivalent-circuit diagram in your answer script — even hand-drawn — earns at least **2 of the 7 marks** allocated to the working principle.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph P1[I V Characteristic Curve]
        V1[Open Circuit Voltage Voc] --> K1[Knee Region]
        K1 --> I1[Short Circuit Current Isc]
    end

    subgraph P2[P V Characteristic Curve]
        V2[Open Circuit Voltage Voc] --> M1[Maximum Power Point MPP]
        M1 --> Pmax[Peak Power Pmax]
        Pmax --> I2[Short Circuit Current Isc]
    end

    P1 -.x-axis Voltage.-> P2
    P1 -.y-axis Current.-> P2
    P2 -.y-axis Power.-> P2
```

**Reading guide:**
- The **I-V curve** slopes downward from $I_{sc}$ (at $V = 0$) to $V_{oc}$ (at $I = 0$).
- The **P-V curve** rises from zero, peaks at the **MPP**, then falls back to zero at $V_{oc}$.
- The rectangle formed by $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$ encloses the *theoretical maximum*; the inscribed rectangle $V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ encloses the *actual maximum* power. Their ratio is the **Fill Factor**.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Complete PV System

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[PV Array Module] --> CC[Charge Controller MPPT]
    CC --> B[Battery Bank Storage]
    CC --> I[Inverter DC to AC]
    I --> L[AC Load]
    B --> I
```

> **KTU Application Context:** This is the canonical architecture for **standalone (off-grid) PV systems** for rural electrification in Kerala — a frequent KTU case-study question topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the photovoltaic effect. List any three semiconductor materials commonly used in solar cells.**

**Model Answer:**
The **photovoltaic effect** is the phenomenon of generating a voltage and current in a semiconductor material upon exposure to light, resulting from the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs by the built-in electric field of a p-n junction.

Three commonly used semiconductor materials:
1. **Monocrystalline Silicon (mono-Si)** — efficiency 18–22%, high cost, uniform dark appearance.
2. **Polycrystalline Silicon (poly-Si)** — efficiency 15–18%, lower cost, blue-speckled appearance.
3. **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)** — thin-film technology, efficiency 18–20%, low manufacturing cost.

*(Any other valid: GaAs, CIGS, perovskite — also accepted)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**What is Fill Factor? Mention its typical range for a good-quality silicon solar cell.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Fill Factor (FF)** is defined as the ratio of the actual maximum obtainable power to the theoretical product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}
$$

It is a dimensionless number that quantifies the "squareness" of the I-V curve. For a high-quality crystalline silicon solar cell, FF typically lies in the range **0.70 to 0.85**. A higher FF indicates lower parasitic resistive losses and better junction quality.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of working of a solar PV cell** with a neat labelled diagram and the necessary equations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **open-circuit voltage** of a solar cell. Discuss how $V_{oc}$ varies with temperature and irradiance. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

1. **Diagram** — Draw the layered structure: anti-reflection coating → n-type emitter → p-type base → back contact. Add photon arrow and load connection. *(2 marks)*
2. **Photon absorption** — Photons with $h\nu \ge E_g$ are absorbed in the semiconductor. *(1 mark)*
3. **EHP generation** — Valence electrons jump to conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs. *(1 mark)*
4. **Charge separation** — The depletion-region electric field sweeps electrons to n-side and holes to p-side. *(1 mark)*
5. **Current delivery** — Photo-generated carriers flow through the external load; net photocurrent $I_L$ drives the circuit. *(1 mark)*
6. **Key equation** — $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$ *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1:** Apply the boundary condition $I = 0$ at the open-circuited terminals.

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V_{oc}$:

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)
$$

*(3 marks for full derivation)*

**Step 3: Variation with irradiance** — $I_L \propto G$, so $V_{oc}$ increases *logarithmically* with irradiance (weak dependence). Doubling $G$ raises $V_{oc}$ by only $\approx 30$–$40$ mV for a Si cell. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Variation with temperature** — As $T$ rises, $I_0$ increases exponentially (because $I_0 \propto T^3 \exp(-E_g/kT)$) while the thermal voltage $V_T$ grows only linearly. Net effect: $V_{oc}$ **decreases** by roughly **2 mV/°C** for silicon cells. *(2 marks)*

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **performance characteristics (I-V and P-V curves)** of a solar cell. Explain the significance of each region of the curve. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **conversion efficiency** and **fill factor** of a solar cell. A silicon solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A, $FF = 0.76$, and active area $A = 0.015$ m². Calculate its efficiency under AM1.5G, $G = 1000$ W/m². *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

1. **Axes definition** — I-V curve: $I$ on Y-axis, $V$ on X-axis. P-V curve: $P$ on Y-axis, $V$ on X-axis. *(1 mark)*
2. **Three key points** — $I_{sc}$ (y-intercept), $V_{oc}$ (x-intercept), and $V_{mp}, I_{mp}$ (Maximum Power Point, "knee"). *(2 marks)*
3. **Region 1 — Near $I_{sc}$** (low $V$): Current is nearly constant; behaves as a constant-current source. Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ dominates. *(1 mark)*
4. **Region 2 — Knee region**: Diode begins to conduct; exponential rise in current. Determines $V_{mp}$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Region 3 — Near $V_{oc}$** (high $V$): Current collapses rapidly; series resistance $R_s$ dominates. *(1 mark)*
6. **P-V curve** — Single-peaked curve; $P_{max}$ occurs at the "knee" of the I-V curve. Area under the rectangle $V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ must be maximized. *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — State definitions:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}
$$

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\eta = \frac{0.76 \times 0.62 \times 3.2}{1000 \times 0.015}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.76 \times 0.62 \times 3.2 = 0.76 \times 1.984 = 1.50784 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1000 \times 0.015 = 15 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1.50784}{15} = 0.1005 = \mathbf{10.05\%}
$$

*(2 marks for numerical substitution; 1 mark for correct final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_{sc}$:** $I_L$ is the *light-generated* (internal) current; $I_{sc}$ is the *terminal* current at $V = 0$ and equals $I_L$ only when $R_s = 0$. Writing "$I_L = I_{sc}$" without the ideal-circuit caveat costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "-1" in the diode equation:** $\exp(qV/kT) - 1$ — not just $\exp(qV/kT)$. This loses **half a mark** consistently.
> 3. **Unit mismatches in efficiency:** Always convert area to **m²** and irradiance to **W/m²**. Mixing cm² and m² is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers:** KTU 2024 rubric mandates a labelled diagram for full marks in principle-of-working questions — omission forfeits **at least 2 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PV cell** = semiconductor p-n junction device exploiting the **photovoltaic effect**.
- ✅ **Working principle**: Photon absorption $\rightarrow$ EHP generation $\rightarrow$ charge separation by junction field $\rightarrow$ collection at terminals.
- ✅ **Master I-V equation**: $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$.
- ✅ **Key output parameters**: $I_{sc}, V_{oc}, V_{mp}, I_{mp}, P_{max}, FF, \eta$.
- ✅ **Open-circuit voltage formula**: $V_{oc} = (nkT/q) \ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$.
- ✅ **Fill Factor**: $FF = (V_{mp}I_{mp})/(V_{oc}I_{sc})$, typical range **0.70–0.85** for Si.
- ✅ **Efficiency formula**: $\eta = (FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})/(G \cdot A)$.
- ✅ **Temperature effect**: $V_{oc}$ **decreases** by $\approx 2$ mV/°C; $I_{sc}$ slightly **increases** with $T$.
- ✅ **Irradiance effect**: $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear); $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$ (logarithmic, weak).
- ✅ **MPP condition**: $\dfrac{dP}{dV}\bigg|_{V_{mp}} = 0$ — solved numerically (Newton–Raphson).
- ✅ **Shockley–Queisser limit**: $\approx 33.7\%$ for single-junction cells under AM1.5G.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled diagram** and quote **standard test conditions (STC)** in any KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Process - Block Diagram Only Applications - Solar Space Heating and Cooling of Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Production Process - Block Diagram Only Applications - Solar Space Heating and Cooling of Buildings # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Solar Energy — The Production Process (Block Diagram Perspective) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > The *Solar Energy Production Process* refers to the engineered sequence of energy conversion stages that capture, concentrate, store, and deliver usable thermal (or electrical) energy from incident solar radiation for end-use applications such as space heating, space cooling, water heating, and power generation. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this process is best represented as a **functional block diagram** showing the four universal stages of any solar energy system: $$\text{Solar Resource} \rightarrow \text{Collector} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Distribution} \rightarrow \text{Load}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The Production Process is to be studied as a **Block Diagram Only** — meaning students are expected to *draw, label, and explain* the functional blocks (not derive the heat-transfer equations of every individual block). Equations are reserved for the *Applications* portion (heating/cooling). ## 1.2 Applications — Solar Space Heating and Cooling of Buildings **Space Heating:** The use of solar energy to raise the indoor air temperature of a building, either passively (through architectural design — south-facing glazing, Trombe walls, sunspaces) or actively (using collectors, pumps, and ducts). **Space Cooling:** The use of solar energy *as the driving heat source* for thermally-activated cooling cycles — primarily **absorption refrigeration** and **desiccant evaporative cooling** — to lower indoor air temperature. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** > Think of the sun as a giant free "fuel tanker" parked 150 million km away. A solar space-heating/cooling system is simply a set of **pipes, tanks, and valves** that catches some of that free fuel, stores it in a *thermal battery* (water tank, pebble bed, PCM), and delivers it — as **hot water in winter** or **cool air in summer** — exactly when the building needs it. > - The *collector* is the fuel nozzle (catches the sun). > - The *storage tank* is the fuel tank (saves it for night/cloudy days). > - The *distribution system* is the pipeline (pumps, fans, ducts). > - The *building* is the engine that finally uses the fuel. ## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Use in this Topic | |---|---|---|---| | **Solar Constant (Outside Atmosphere)** | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** | Baseline extraterrestrial irradiance | | Air Mass (AM) at sea level, sun overhead | AM 1.0 | $G_{sc} \cdot 0.7 \approx 1000$ W/m² | Peak terrestrial solar input | | Global horizontal irradiance (design) | GHI | 4–7 kWh/m²/day (Kerala) | Sizing collectors | | Specific heat of water | $c_{pw}$ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** | Sensible heat storage sizing | | Latent heat of fusion of water/ice | $h_{sf}$ | 333.4 kJ/kg | PCM / phase-change storage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Kerala receives an average daily solar insolation of **~5.0–5.5 kWh/m²/day** (MNRE, NIWE data) — making it highly suitable for both solar thermal heating and solar cooling applications despite its tropical climate. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Universal Production Process — Four Functional Blocks A solar energy production chain, irrespective of end-use, contains these four functional blocks. KTU expects students to **draw and annotate** each block clearly in the exam. **Block 1 — Solar Resource (Input):** - Source: Direct (beam), Diffuse, and Reflected solar radiation. - Quantity characterized by: Insolation (kWh/m²/day) or Irradiance (W/m²). - *Why it matters:* The availability and variability of this block dictate the *size of every downstream block*. **Block 2 — Collector (Conversion):** - Device that intercepts solar radiation and converts it to thermal (or electrical) energy. - *Thermal types:* Flat-Plate Collector (FPC), Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC), Concentrating Collector (parabolic trough, dish). - *Efficiency (FPC):* $\eta_c = a - b \cdot \dfrac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$ (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation — refer Section 3 for derivation logic). **Block 3 — Storage (Buffering):** - Compensates for the day/night mismatch between solar supply and building demand. - *Sensible storage:* Water tanks, pebble-bed, concrete slabs. - *Latent storage:* Phase Change Materials (paraffin, salt hydrates). - *Thermochemical storage:* Reversible chemical reactions (research stage). **Block 4 — Distribution & End-Use (Delivery):** - Pumps, fans, ducts, control valves. - For **heating:** delivers hot water/air to the building interior. - For **cooling:** delivers chilled water/air via absorption/desiccant chiller. > [!NOTE] > **Why each block exists — The Engineering Logic:** > 1. **Collector** *captures* the energy. > 2. **Storage** *time-shifts* it (you need heat at midnight, but the sun shines at noon). > 3. **Distribution** *transports* it to where the humans are. > 4. **End-use device** *converts* it into the desired form (warm air, cool air, hot water). ## 2.2 Application 1 — Solar Space Heating of Buildings ### Classification (KTU 2024 Module-1 Mandate) | Type | Mode | Key Subsystems | |---|---|---| | **Passive Heating** | No mechanical/electrical input | Direct gain, Indirect gain (Trombe wall), Isolated gain (Sunspace/Greenhouse) | | **Active Heating** | Uses pumps/fans | Liquid-based, Air-based, Thermosiphon | | **Hybrid** | Combination | Most modern installations | ### 2.2.1 Passive Solar Heating — The Three Classic Configurations 1. **Direct Gain System:** - South-facing glazed windows admit sunlight directly into the living space. - Thermal mass (concrete floor, masonry wall) absorbs and re-radiates heat. - Simplest, lowest cost, most common in KTU exam questions. 2. **Indirect Gain System (Trombe Wall):** - A thick masonry wall painted black sits behind a south-facing glass pane. - Sun heats wall → wall radiates/convects heat indoors *with a time delay* (6–10 hours). - Vents at top and bottom of wall allow controlled convective heat release. 3. **Isolated Gain System (Sunspace / Attached Greenhouse):** - A separate glazed enclosure is attached to the south wall. - Acts as a buffer zone; heat is transferred to the main building via fans or vents. ### 2.2.2 Active Solar Heating — Liquid and Air Systems **Liquid (Hydronic) System Block Logic:** > Collector → Pump → Storage Tank → Heat Exchanger → Building (radiators/floor heating) **Air System Block Logic:** > Collector → Fan → Rock-Bed Storage → Ducts → Building > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why prefer air systems in Kerala?** Air systems avoid freezing problems (irrelevant in Kerala) and the rock-bed storage is cheaper than insulated water tanks for large institutional buildings. ## 2.3 Application 2 — Solar Space Cooling of Buildings Cooling is *counterintuitive* — you use **solar heat to produce cold**. The two KTU-relevant technologies are: ### 2.3.1 Solar Absorption Cooling - Uses a working pair: **LiBr–H₂O** (chilled water to ~5 °C) or **H₂O–NH₃** (chilled water to ~0 °C). - **Solar heat** drives the **generator**, which separates refrigerant from absorbent. - **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** $\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen}+W_{pump}}$ > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — Absorption Chiller as a "Heat Pump in Reverse":** > A normal AC uses *electricity* to push heat from cold (inside) to hot (outside). An absorption chiller uses *heat* (from a flame, or from the sun via collectors) to do the same job. So the sun is literally powering your air conditioner! ### 2.3.2 Solar Desiccant Evaporative Cooling - A desiccant (e.g., silica gel, lithium chloride) **dries** the incoming hot humid air. - The dried air is then **evaporatively cooled** (adiabatic saturation) → very cool, dry air delivered to the building. - The desiccant is **regenerated** using low-grade solar heat (60–90 °C). ### 2.3.3 Why Solar Cooling is Synergistic in Kerala - Peak **cooling demand** occurs when solar radiation is highest (March–May). - Hence supply and demand are *naturally matched* — unlike solar heating (where peak demand is winter). - This is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question** angle. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All high-yield equations for Module-1 (Production Process & Applications):** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$ | Solar constant (extraterrestrial) | W/m² | | 2 | $G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on day *n* | W/m² | | 3 | $\eta_c = a - b \cdot \dfrac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$ | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss collector efficiency | — | | 4 | $Q_u = A_c \cdot G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - A_c \cdot U_L (T_i - T_a)$ | Useful heat gain from collector | W | | 5 | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage capacity | kJ | | 6 | $V_{st} = \dfrac{Q_{load} \cdot t}{ \rho \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required storage tank volume | m³ | | 7 | $R_{se} = 1/h_o, \quad R_{si} = 1/h_i$ | External / internal surface resistances | m²K/W | | 8 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Building heat loss / gain | W | | 9 | $\text{Load} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{design}}$ | Heating/cooling design load | W | | 10 | $\text{SSF} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Solar Gain}}{\text{Building Heat Loss}}$ | Solar Savings Fraction (0 to 1) | — | | 11 | $\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | Absorption chiller performance | — | | 12 | $\text{SPF} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Heat Delivered}}{\text{Auxiliary Energy In}}$ | Seasonal Performance Factor | — | **Boundary conditions / notes for exam use:** - *For FPC efficiency:* $a \in [0.7, 0.8]$, $b \in [3, 6]$ W/m²·K (typical ranges). - *Storage ΔT:* Use 5–15 °C for water tanks; 10–25 °C for pebble beds. - *Design ΔT for Kerala:* Indoor 24 °C DB, Outdoor 35 °C DB (summer), 21 °C (winter rare). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Full derivations are not required for the *block diagram* portion, but **are mandatory** for the *applications* portion (heating load, collector heat gain, SSF, storage sizing, COP). The following derivations are the most frequently asked. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Useful Heat Gain from a Flat-Plate Collector (Hottel-Whillier-Bliss) **Starting physical principle:** Energy balance on the absorber plate. The absorber plate receives solar radiation, loses some by reflection, absorbs the rest, and loses heat to the surroundings (top + bottom + edges) while gaining heat transferred to the working fluid. **Step 1:** Net absorbed solar radiation on the plate: $$q_{abs} = G_T \cdot (\tau \alpha)$$ where $\tau$ = transmittance of glass cover, $\alpha$ = absorptance of the selective coating, $G_T$ = total incident radiation on tilted plane (W/m²). **Step 2:** Heat loss from the plate to surroundings (top + bottom + sides combined) is proportional to $(T_p - T_a)$: $$q_{loss} = U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$ where $U_L$ = overall loss coefficient (W/m²·K) and $T_p$ = plate temperature (K or °C), $T_a$ = ambient temperature. **Step 3:** Useful heat transferred to the fluid per unit collector area: $$q_u = q_{abs} - q_{loss} = G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_p - T_a)$$ **Step 4:** For design purposes, the fluid inlet temperature $T_i$ is used in place of $T_p$ (since $T_p > T_i$): $$q_u = G_T \cdot (\tau\alpha) - U_L \cdot (T_i - T_a)$$ **Step 5:** Total useful heat from a collector of area $A_c$: $$Q_u = A_c \cdot \left[ G_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_i - T_a) \right]$$ **Step 6:** Dividing by incident solar power $G_T$, the instantaneous thermal efficiency: $$\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot G_T} = (\tau\alpha) - \dfrac{U_L (T_i - T_a)}{G_T}$$ This is the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation. Conventionally written with the slope and intercept parameters $a$ and $b$: $$\eta_c = a - b \cdot \dfrac{(T_i - T_a)}{G_T}, \quad \text{where } a = \tau\alpha, \; b = U_L$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam tip — KTU 2024:** A common 7-mark question gives values of $a$, $b$, $G_T$, $T_i$, $T_a$ and asks to compute $\eta_c$ and $Q_u$. Memorize Step 6. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Tank Sizing for Solar Heating **Starting principle:** Energy balance — useful solar energy supplied over a storage period must equal the stored energy capacity. **Step 1:** Energy demand to be met by storage (assume no auxiliary backup for a day): $$Q_{demand} = L \cdot t \quad [\text{kJ}]$$ where $L$ = design heating load (kW) and $t$ = storage duration (seconds or hours — keep units consistent). **Step 2:** Sensible heat storage capacity of water mass *m* heated through temperature rise $\Delta T$: $$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T = \rho_w \cdot V \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 3:** Equating supply and demand (assuming 100% storage efficiency, $\eta_{st} = 1$): $$L \cdot t = \rho_w \cdot V \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 4:** Solving for the required storage volume $V$: $$V = \dfrac{L \cdot t}{\rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T}$$ **Step 5:** For practical use, include a storage efficiency factor $\eta_{st} \approx 0.8$–$0.9$: $$\boxed{V = \dfrac{L \cdot t}{\rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T \cdot \eta_{st}}}$$ **Step 6 (Numerical Illustration):** A 5 kW heating load must be supplied for 8 hours using water storage (ΔT = 10 °C, $\eta_{st} = 0.9$): $$V = \dfrac{5 \text{ kW} \cdot 8 \text{ h}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \cdot 10 \text{ K} \cdot 0.9} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 8 \cdot 3600 \text{ kJ}}{37674 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$ $$V = \dfrac{144000}{37674} = 3.82 \text{ m}^3 \approx 4000 \text{ litres}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solar Savings Fraction (SSF) **Starting principle:** SSF = fraction of the total heating load met by *solar* (vs. auxiliary fuel/electricity) over a period (typically one heating season). **Step 1:** Define auxiliary energy supplied over the period: $$E_{aux} = \int_{season} L_{aux}(t) \, dt \quad [\text{kWh}]$$ **Step 2:** Define total heating load (solar + auxiliary): $$E_{total} = E_{solar} + E_{aux}$$ **Step 3:** By definition: $$\text{SSF} = \dfrac{E_{solar}}{E_{total}} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{aux}}{E_{total}}$$ **Step 4 (Practical form, monthly):** $$\text{SSF} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_i (E_{aux,i})}{\sum_i (L_i)}$$ where $L_i$ is the monthly heating load and $E_{aux,i}$ the monthly auxiliary energy used. > [!NOTE] > **Why SSF matters for KTU exams:** It is the single most important *performance metric* for any active solar heating system. Typical KTU answer expects: SSF = 0 (no solar) to 1 (100% solar). Economically attractive designs target **SSF ≥ 0.5**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Building Heating/Cooling Load (Simplified) **Starting principle:** Steady-state heat transfer through the building envelope. **Step 1:** Heat loss by conduction through walls, roof, floor, windows: $$Q_{cond} = \sum_{j} (U_j \cdot A_j \cdot \Delta T_j)$$ where $U_j$ = U-value of building element *j* (W/m²·K), $A_j$ = area, $\Delta T_j$ = indoor-outdoor temperature difference. **Step 2:** Heat loss/gain by ventilation/infiltration: $$Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 3:** Internal heat gains (lights, occupants, equipment — reduce net heating load, increase cooling load): $$Q_{int} = \sum \text{(appliance, occupant, lighting powers)}$$ **Step 4:** Net design heating load: $$\boxed{L_{heat} = Q_{cond} + Q_{vent} - Q_{solar,passive} - Q_{int}}$$ **Step 5:** Net design cooling load: $$\boxed{L_{cool} = Q_{cond} + Q_{vent} + Q_{solar,passive} + Q_{int}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common student error (KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfall):** Sign convention confusion! For *heating*, the building needs heat → losses positive, gains negative. For *cooling*, the building needs heat removal → all terms positive. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — COP of Solar Absorption Chiller **Starting principle:** First-law energy balance on the absorption cycle (steady state). **Step 1:** Energy balance on the *generator* (heat input from solar collector): $$Q_g + Q_{evap} = Q_{cond} + Q_{abs}$$ **Step 2:** Energy balance on the *evaporator* (cooling effect delivered to building): $$Q_e = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot h_{fg,ref}$$ **Step 3:** Work input is negligible (only a small solution pump, often omitted in KTU problems): $$W_p \approx 0$$ **Step 4:** Define COP: $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g}}$$ **Step 5 (Typical value):** Single-effect LiBr–H₂O chiller → $\text{COP} \approx 0.7$ – $0.8$. > [!NOTE] > **Insight — KTU favourite exam point:** Even though the COP is < 1, the *primary energy* used is **solar heat** (free), so the *primary energy ratio* is much higher than a vapour-compression AC (COP ≈ 3 but uses grid electricity with 30–40% power-plant efficiency). --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Solar Heating vs. Solar Cooling | Parameter | Solar Space Heating | Solar Space Cooling | |---|---|---| | **Primary energy flow** | Sun → Collector → Heat to building | Sun → Collector → Heat *drives chiller* → Cool to building | | **Peak demand timing** | Winter (low sun) | Summer (high sun) | | **Solar-resource match** | Poor (mismatch) | Excellent (synergy) | | **Best technology for Kerala** | Thermosiphon water heater; passive Trombe wall | LiBr–H₂O absorption chiller; desiccant system | | **Storage** | Sensible (water tank) | Cool storage (chilled water/ice) | | **Typical COP / efficiency** | $\eta_{system} \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ | $\text{COP}_{cooling} \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ | | **KTU exam weightage** | High (passive + active) | High (absorption + desiccant) | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-1 Mandate:** Students must draw **labelled block diagrams** for the production process and for each application. Use dotted lines for *control signals* and *auxiliary backup*. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Compliance (ESE — End Semester Examination):** > - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions (answer in ~60–90 words). > - Part B: 14-mark long-answer with *internal choice* (a + b sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks). > - All questions below are mapped to **CO1 / CO2** and Revised Bloom's levels (L1–L6). --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** *List and explain the four functional blocks of a solar energy production process with a neat block diagram. Why is a storage block always necessary between the collector and the load?* **Model Answer (≈80 words):** The four functional blocks are: **1) Solar Resource** (input — beam + diffuse radiation), **2) Collector** (converts solar radiation to thermal energy), **3) Storage** (temporarily stores the thermal energy), and **4) Distribution & End-Use** (delivers conditioned energy to the building). A block diagram is drawn as: > Solar Resource → Collector → Storage → Distribution → Load **Storage is necessary** because the sun shines during the day (peak supply) while heating/cooling demand often peaks at night or on cloudy days. The storage block acts as a *thermal buffer* that time-shifts the energy from the supply period to the demand period, ensuring continuous service. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** *With a sketch, explain the working principle of a Trombe wall for passive solar space heating.* **Model Answer (≈85 words):** A **Trombe wall** is a passive solar heating element consisting of a **thick masonry wall** (typically 200–400 mm) painted black on its sun-facing side, covered with a single or double glass pane leaving a narrow air gap. Sunlight passes through the glass, heats the wall, and the wall re-radiates long-wave heat *slowly* into the room (delayed by 6–10 hours). Optional **vents** at the top and bottom of the wall allow controlled convective heat transfer to the room during daytime. It is a classic *indirect-gain* passive heating system with no moving parts. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Model]** *Solar space heating and cooling are both feasible in tropical climates like Kerala. Justify.* #### (a) *Discuss, with block diagrams, the working of a flat-plate collector-based active solar space heating system for a residential building in Kerala. Include the role of auxiliary backup. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** 1. **System Selection (1 mark):** Kerala's ambient temperature rarely falls below 21 °C, so a *low-temperature* active solar heating system using a flat-plate collector (FPC) is suitable. We select a **liquid-based (hydronic) thermosiphon system** for simplicity, or a pumped system for larger buildings. 2. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Sun → [FPC, A_c, η_c] → [Hot water storage tank, V_st] → [Distribution: pump + pipes] → [Building radiators / underfloor coil] → [Return water] → Tank → Collector loop. Auxiliary electric immersion heater is connected in parallel to the storage tank for cloudy days. 3. **Component Role (2 marks):** - **FPC (A_c ≈ 4–6 m²):** Captures solar radiation, heats water/glycol mixture to 50–70 °C. - **Storage tank (≈ 200–500 L):** Stores 1–2 days' worth of energy; insulated with 50 mm PUF. - **Pump & control:** Activates when $T_{collector} > T_{tank} + 5°C$ (ΔT control). - **Auxiliary heater:** Kicks in automatically when tank temperature falls below set-point (e.g., 40 °C). 4. **Performance (1 mark):** A typical Kerala installation achieves **SSF ≈ 0.4–0.6** (i.e., 40–60% of the annual heating demand is met by solar). #### (b) *A building in Kerala has a winter design heating load of 8 kW. A solar heating system uses an FPC with a = 0.78, b = 4.5 W/m²·K. Design conditions: G_T = 800 W/m², T_i = 50 °C, T_a = 22 °C. Calculate: (i) collector efficiency, (ii) useful heat gain per m², (iii) required collector area for full-load operation, and (iv) the volume of water storage if the system must supply full load for 6 hours (ΔT_storage = 10 °C, η_st = 0.9). (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Collector efficiency (1 mark):** $$\eta_c = a - b \cdot \dfrac{T_i - T_a}{G_T} = 0.78 - 4.5 \cdot \dfrac{50 - 22}{800}$$ $$\eta_c = 0.78 - 4.5 \cdot \dfrac{28}{800} = 0.78 - 4.5 \cdot 0.035 = 0.78 - 0.1575 = 0.6225$$ $$\boxed{\eta_c \approx 0.62 \; \text{or} \; 62.25\%}$$ **(ii) Useful heat per m² (1.5 marks):** $$q_u = G_T \cdot \eta_c = 800 \cdot 0.6225 = 498 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q_u \approx 498 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **(iii) Required collector area (1.5 marks):** For 8 kW load with $q_u = 498$ W/m²: $$A_c = \dfrac{L}{q_u} = \dfrac{8000}{498} = 16.06 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\boxed{A_c \approx 16.1 \text{ m}^2}$$ **(iv) Storage tank volume (3 marks):** $$V = \dfrac{L \cdot t}{\rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T \cdot \eta_{st}}$$ Convert units: $L = 8$ kW, $t = 6$ h $= 6 \times 3600 = 21600$ s, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K $= 4186$ J/kg·K, $\Delta T = 10$ K, $\eta_{st} = 0.9$. $$V = \dfrac{8 \cdot 21600}{1000 \cdot 4186 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.9} = \dfrac{172800}{37674000} = 4.59 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 4.6 \text{ litres}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Such a small storage is sufficient because Kerala winter loads are mild. The 16.1 m² collector area is the dominant cost driver. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *Explain, with block diagrams, the working of solar absorption and desiccant cooling systems. Compare their suitability for tropical buildings.* #### (a) *Draw the block diagram of a single-effect LiBr–H₂O absorption chiller driven by solar heat. Explain the function of each block and define the COP. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** 1. **Block Diagram (3 marks)** — Refer to Diagram D in Section 4. The four key blocks are: **Generator, Condenser, Evaporator, Absorber**, connected by the solution pump, expansion valve, and refrigerant/solution flow lines. Heat input $Q_g$ comes from a solar collector loop delivering hot water at 80–100 °C. 2. **Function of each block (3 marks):** - **Generator:** Solar heat boils off refrigerant (water) vapour from the LiBr solution, concentrating the solution. - **Condenser:** The high-pressure refrigerant vapour rejects heat $Q_c$ to the outdoor ambient (via a cooling tower). - **Evaporator:** Low-pressure refrigerant evaporates, absorbing $Q_e$ from the chilled-water loop (5–7 °C chilled water) → this is the **useful cooling**. - **Absorber:** The refrigerant vapour is reabsorbed by the weak LiBr solution (released from generator), reconcentrating it via exothermic reaction. Heat $Q_a$ must be removed. - **Solution pump:** Pumps concentrated solution back to the generator (consumes negligible work $W_p$). 3. **COP definition (1 mark):** $$\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_e \text{ (useful cooling)}}{Q_g \text{ (solar heat input) + } W_p \text{ (pump work)}}$$ For a single-effect LiBr–H₂O chiller, typical COP ≈ **0.7–0.8**. #### (b) *Compare solar absorption cooling with solar desiccant evaporative cooling for a tropical building like in Kerala. State, with reasons, which is more suitable for: (i) a 5-star hotel, (ii) a small rural household. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** 1. **Tabular Comparison (3 marks):** | Feature | Absorption (LiBr–H₂O) | Desiccant Evaporative | |---|---|---| | Working fluid | Water (refrigerant) | Silica gel / LiCl wheel | | Driving temperature | 80–100 °C | 60–80 °C | | COP / EER | 0.6–0.8 (cooling) | 1.0–1.4 (latent + sensible) | | Chilled water temp | 5–7 °C (good for AC) | 15–18 °C (indirect, air-side only) | | Best for | High sensible load, low humidity | High latent load, humid climate | | Cost | High (vacuum-tight equipment) | Moderate (rotary wheels) | | Maintenance | Skilled | Simple | | Suitability — high humidity | Poor (condenser struggles) | Excellent (handles humidity directly) | 2. **(i) 5-star hotel in Kerala (2 marks):** A 5-star hotel needs **precise temperature and humidity control** in guest rooms, kitchens, server rooms. Kerala's coastal humidity (often > 80% RH) is high. While absorption can provide the low chilled-water temperature required for FCUs, the desiccant system is **more suitable for the latent (humidity) load** that dominates tropical comfort. A **hybrid** is recommended: absorption chiller for sensible cooling + desiccant for latent load. For the sake of a single answer: **Hybrid (absorption primary + desiccant polishing)** is the KTU-preferred answer. 3. **(ii) Small rural household in Kerala (2 marks):** A rural household has **modest budget**, no skilled maintenance crew, and a high latent load (humidity). A **solar desiccant evaporative cooler** is far more suitable: it is cheaper, uses lower-grade heat (compatible with low-cost ETC), and directly addresses the humidity discomfort. *The absorption chiller is over-specified and uneconomical for a single household.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark-Deduction Triggers):** > 1. **Missing the *auxiliary backup* in block diagrams** — Examiners specifically look for a dotted line to the "Auxiliary Heater/Grid" block. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 2. **Sign error in heating vs. cooling load equations** — Solar gains are *negative* for heating load, *positive* for cooling load. **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > 3. **Unit inconsistency in storage tank sizing** — Mixing kW·h with J/kg·K without converting. Always convert to J or kJ consistently. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 4. **Confusing COP with EER** — COP is dimensionless (W/W); EER is in BTU/h·W. KTU expects **COP**. **Loss: 0.5 mark.** > 5. **Forgetting to label the *control system* in the block diagram** — A complete production-process block diagram has 4–5 blocks; missing the control system is considered an incomplete answer. > 6. **Writing "solar cooling" without specifying *absorption* or *desiccant*** — Vague answers lose 1–2 marks. Always name the technology. > 7. **Not drawing arrows correctly** — Arrows must show *energy flow direction*. Reversed arrows (e.g., "load → collector") is a 0.5-mark deduction. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Print This Before Exam:** ### A. Production Process (Block Diagram Only) - **Universal 4-block chain:** Solar Resource → Collector → Storage → Distribution → Load (+ Auxiliary + Control). - Each block has a **function**, a **key variable**, and a **typical efficiency**. - KTU exam: *Draw, label, annotate in 2–3 minutes, max 4 marks.* ### B. Solar Space Heating - **Three passive strategies:** Direct gain (windows), Indirect gain (Trombe wall), Isolated gain (sunspace). - **Two active strategies:** Liquid (hydronic) and Air systems. - **Trombe wall** = 200–400 mm masonry + glass + air gap + (optional) vents. Delay = 6–10 h. - **FPC efficiency:** $\eta_c = a - b \dfrac{T_i - T_a}{G_T}$ — *most-tested equation in this module.* - **SSF** = fraction of load met by solar. Target ≥ 0.5 for economic viability. ### C. Solar Space Cooling - **Two technologies only:** Absorption (LiBr–H₂O or H₂O–NH₃) and Desiccant Evaporative. - **Absorption COP ≈ 0.7**; **Desiccant EER ≈ 1.0–1.4**. - **Four blocks of absorption chiller:** Generator, Condenser, Evaporator, Absorber. - **Solar heat drives the generator** (80–100 °C for LiBr–H₂O; 60–80 °C for desiccant). - **Kerala synergy:** Peak cooling demand ↔ peak solar radiation (March–May). ### D. Key Numerical Cans (Use in Any 7-Mark Numerical) - $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²; $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K; $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³. - FPC typical: $a = 0.78$, $b = 4.5$ W/m²·K. - Storage ΔT: water 5–15 °C, pebble bed 10–25 °C. - Kerala design: indoor 24 °C, summer 35 °C, winter 21 °C. ### E. High-Yield One-Liners (Quote in Answers for Bonus Marks) - "Storage time-shifts the mismatch between solar supply and building demand." - "Solar cooling is a demand–supply matched technology in the tropics." - "COP of absorption chiller is low (<1) but uses free solar heat instead of expensive grid electricity." - "Trombe wall is an indirect-gain passive system with a 6–10 hour thermal lag." ### F. Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid - ✗ Drawing block diagrams without arrows. - ✗ Confusing absorption with vapour-compression. - ✗ Using W/m²·K instead of kJ/kg·K in storage calculations. - ✗ Writing the SSF as > 1 (impossible — always 0 ≤ SSF ≤ 1). - ✗ Forgetting auxiliary backup in the production-process block. --- **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — PRODUCTION PROCESS & APPLICATIONS: SOLAR HEATING/COOLING** *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 / OBE Standards*

topics.title: Solar Pumping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Pumping # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Solar Photovoltaic Water Pumping System (SPVWPS)** is an integrated renewable-energy-driven electromechanical assembly that converts incident solar irradiance directly into electrical energy using photovoltaic (PV) modules, which then drives a motor–pump unit to lift and deliver water from a source (well, bore well, river, reservoir) to a delivery point (field, tank, reservoir) without requiring any grid electricity or conventional diesel fuel. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the system is classified as an *off-grid, distributed, solar-electric prime mover* and is governed by the performance equation: $$\eta_{system} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{hydraulic}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Solar Pumping is positioned under "Solar Energy Conversion & Applications." Examiners expect students to demonstrate competence in **(i) component identification, (ii) system sizing, (iii) head-discharge calculations, and (iv) comparative evaluation of motor-pump topologies.** --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket on a Conveyor Belt" Mental Model Imagine a construction site where workers carry buckets of water from a deep pit up to a tank on the roof. They climb stairs (lifting energy) and walk along the roof (transportation energy). Now replace the workers with: | Real System Component | Bucket Analogy | |---|---| | **PV Array** | A rechargeable battery that fills itself from sunlight | | **Controller / MPPT** | A valve that controls how fast the workers move | | **Motor (DC/AC)** | The legs of the worker — provides mechanical power | | **Pump (Centrifugal / Positive Displacement)** | The bucket + scoop mechanism | | **Pipe Network** | The staircase + corridor | The *amount* of water delivered per day depends on three things: **(1)** how much sunlight hits the panels, **(2)** how deep the pit is, and **(3)** how efficient the worker is at carrying. This single mental model covers every solar pumping problem you will face. --- ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants) - **Standard Test Condition Irradiance (G₀):** **1000 W/m²** - **AM 1.5 Spectrum, Cell Temperature:** **25 °C (298.15 K)** - **Water Density (ρ):** **1000 kg/m³** (at 4 °C reference) - **Acceleration due to Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** - **Solar Pump Hydraulic Conversion Constant:** **102 (for Q in L/s → kW)** and **75 (for Q in L/s → HP)** - **Maximum Practical Suction Lift (Centrifugal Pumps):** **6 to 7 m** (limited by atmospheric pressure) > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter in KTU exam:** Any numerical problem on solar pumping begins by checking the total head, daily water requirement, and peak solar irradiance at the site. Memorizing these constants is the difference between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer. --- ## 1.4 System Block Topology (Textual Preview) A solar pumping system is **not** just "panel + pump." The full architecture includes: 1. **PV Array** — Series/parallel configured modules 2. **DC–DC Converter with MPPT** — Extracts maximum power under varying irradiance 3. **Motor Drive** — VFD for AC pumps; direct coupling for DC pumps 4. **Motor** — DC brushless (BLDC), AC induction, or synchronous reluctance 5. **Pump** — Centrifugal (high flow / low head) **or** Positive Displacement (low flow / high head) 6. **Hydraulic Network** — Suction pipe, foot valve, delivery pipe, non-return valve 7. **Storage** — Water tank (overhead) **or** battery bank (electrical storage) The detailed block diagram is provided in SECTION 4. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Solar Pumps (KTU Exam Favourite) Solar pumps are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: ### Axis 1 — By Electrical Drive | Type | Configuration | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **DC Motor Pump Set** | PV array → MPPT → DC motor → Pump | Small irrigation (≤ 5 HP) | | **AC Motor Pump Set** | PV array → MPPT → VFD → AC induction motor → Pump | Medium/large irrigation (5–50 HP) | ### Axis 2 — By Pump Hydraulic Principle | Type | Flow Characteristic | Head Range | Efficiency | |---|---|---|---| | **Centrifugal Pump** | Flow ∝ Speed (Q ∝ N) | Low to medium head (≤ 50 m) | 60–80 % | | **Submersible Centrifugal** | Multistage for high head | 50–250 m | 65–80 % | | **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Flow ≈ Constant (Q independent of H) | High head (up to 300 m) | 70–85 % | | **Diaphragm / Piston PD** | Pulsating low flow | Very high head, low flow | 50–70 % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Rule:** For **low head + high discharge** (e.g., river-fed farm), use **centrifugal**. For **high head + low discharge** (e.g., deep borewell), use **PD or multistage submersible**. ### Axis 3 — By Storage Strategy | Type | Description | Cost Implication | |---|---|---| | **Direct-coupled (No storage)** | Pump runs when sun shines; excess water stored in tank | Lowest cost | | **Battery-buffered** | Batteries store electrical energy | Highest cost, rarely recommended | | **Float-tank buffered** | Water tank provides buffering | Standard recommended approach | --- ## 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Photovoltaic Conversion:** PV cells generate DC electricity proportional to incident irradiance **G** (W/m²). 2. **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT):** Controller continuously adjusts the operating voltage to extract peak power from the array (typically at **Vmpp ≈ 0.8 × Voc**). 3. **Motor Drive Conversion:** Either directly drives a DC motor or feeds a VFD for an AC motor. 4. **Mechanical-to-Hydraulic Conversion:** Pump impeller or piston converts rotational/linear motion into pressurized water flow. 5. **Hydraulic Delivery:** Water is lifted through the suction pipe, pressurized by the pump, and discharged against the total head **H**. 6. **Storage or Use:** Water is either used immediately (drip irrigation) or stored in an overhead tank for night-time use. --- ## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Table — Print This for the Exam Hall.** All units are explicitly stated to avoid the #1 KTU valuation pitfall: unit mismatch. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic power output | Watts (W) | | 2 | $P_{hyd,kW} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{102}$ | Hyd. power (quick formula) | kW, with Q in L/s, H in m | | 3 | $P_{hyd,HP} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{75}$ | Hyd. power in horsepower | HP, with Q in L/s, H in m | | 4 | $P_{PV,electrical} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G$ | PV array electrical output | W, with A in m², G in W/m² | | 5 | $N_{modules} = \dfrac{P_{pump,electrical}}{P_{module,peak}}$ | Number of PV modules | Dimensionless | | 6 | $H_{total} = H_{static} + h_{friction} + h_{minor} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Energy equation for piping | m of water column | | 7 | $h_{friction} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction loss | m | | 8 | $H_{a} \leq P_{atm}/\rho g - NPSH_{r} - h_{f,suc}$ | Maximum suction head | m | | 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{pump}$ | System efficiency chain | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 10 | $Q_{daily} = A_{field} \cdot d_{irrigation}$ | Daily water volume required | m³/day | | 11 | $V_{tank} = Q_{pump} \cdot t_{sun}$ | Required tank storage volume | m³ | | 12 | $\dot{Q} \propto \omega$ (centrifugal) | Affinity law 1 | — | | 13 | $H \propto \omega^{2}$ (centrifugal) | Affinity law 2 | — | | 14 | $P \propto \omega^{3}$ (centrifugal) | Affinity law 3 (Pump power ∝ speed³) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Affinity Laws are exclusively valid for centrifugal pumps** — DO NOT apply them to positive displacement pumps. This is a frequent KTU trick question. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Solar pumping is the **single largest off-grid solar PV application worldwide** by installed capacity. Three primary use cases dominate: - **Agricultural irrigation** in arid/semi-arid regions (MNRE India PM-KUSUM scheme targets 3.5 lakh solar pumps) - **Drinking water supply** in rural villages (replacing diesel pumps in remote Africa, Rajasthan, Ladakh) - **Livestock watering** and **aquaculture pond aeration** The critical engineering advantage is **energy independence** — once installed, the operating cost is zero, and the levelized cost of water (LCOW) becomes substantially lower than diesel pumping within 3–4 years. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Hydraulic Power Delivered by the Pump **Starting Concept:** Work done in lifting a volume of water against gravity equals the force multiplied by the distance lifted. **Step 1 — Define discharge Q.** Discharge Q is the volume of water delivered per unit time. If a volume V is delivered in time t: $$Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$$ **Step 2 — Mass flow rate.** Mass of water flowing per second is density times volume flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$$ **Step 3 — Force required to lift water.** The weight of the water per second (which equals the force to support it continuously) is: $$F = \dot{m} \cdot g = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g$$ **Step 4 — Work done per second (Power).** Power is force times velocity (or force times vertical lift per unit time). The lift per unit time corresponds to total head H: $$P_{hydraulic} = F \cdot H = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H$$ **Step 5 — Practical unit conversion.** Substituting ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², Q in L/s (= 10⁻³ m³/s), H in meters: $$P_{hydraulic} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \dfrac{Q_{L/s}}{1000} \cdot H$$ $$P_{hydraulic} = 9.81 \cdot Q_{L/s} \cdot H \quad \text{(Watts)}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to kilowatts:** $$P_{hyd,kW} = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot Q_{L/s} \cdot H}{1000} = \dfrac{Q_{L/s} \cdot H}{102}$$ This is the famous "**divide by 102**" rule that KTU examiners love. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Head Computation (Energy Equation) Applying Bernoulli's equation between the water surface in the well (point 1) and the discharge point (point 2), with pump input: $$H_{pump} = \left( z_{2} - z_{1} \right) + \dfrac{P_{2} - P_{1}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + h_{L}$$ **Step 1 — Identify terms.** - $z_{2} - z_{1}$ = Static lift (vertical difference in water levels) - $\dfrac{P_{2} - P_{1}}{\rho g}$ = Pressure head difference (often zero if both at atmospheric) - $\dfrac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2g}$ = Velocity head difference - $h_{L}$ = Total head loss (friction + minor losses) **Step 2 — Simplify for typical solar pump case.** Both surfaces at atmospheric pressure (P₁ = P₂ = Pₐₜₘ), well water essentially stationary (v₁ ≈ 0): $$H_{total} = H_{static} + h_{friction} + h_{minor} + \dfrac{v_{discharge}^{2}}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Expand friction loss.** Using Darcy-Weisbach: $$h_{friction} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ where **f** is the Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.02 for smooth commercial steel pipe, ≈ 0.04 for galvanized iron). **Step 4 — Expand minor losses.** $$h_{minor} = \sum K_{L} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ where K_L values: foot valve ≈ 1.5, 90° bend ≈ 0.9, gate valve half-open ≈ 2.1, non-return valve ≈ 2.0. **Final Energy Equation:** $$H_{total} = H_{static} + \left( f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} + \sum K_{L} \right) \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Number of PV Modules Required **Step 1 — Compute hydraulic power needed.** From Section 3.1: $$P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(W)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute electrical power input to pump.** Working backward through the efficiency chain: $$P_{electrical,input} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute PV array peak power required.** Accounting for inverter/converter and MPPT losses: $$P_{PV,peak} = \dfrac{P_{electrical,input}}{\eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{wiring}}$$ **Step 4 — Module count.** If each PV module has peak rating **P_mod** (typically 330 W to 550 W in 2024 modules): $$N_{modules} = \dfrac{P_{PV,peak}}{P_{mod}}$$ **Step 5 — Apply sun-hours correction.** In India, average peak sun hours (PSH) range from **4.5 to 6.5 hours/day**. Daily water delivered: $$V_{daily} = Q \cdot PSH \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/day)}$$ If daily requirement is specified: $$Q_{design} = \dfrac{V_{required}}{PSH}$$ This Q is then fed back into Step 1 — completing the design loop. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern) > **Problem Statement:** A farmer in Palakkad requires **50,000 litres/day** of irrigation water. The well is **25 m deep**, the static water level is **8 m** below ground, and the overhead tank is **5 m** above ground. Total pipe length is **60 m** of 75 mm diameter GI pipe (f = 0.04). Foot valve + non-return valve losses are equivalent to K_L = 4.0. Assume PSH = 5 h/day, η_pump = 0.65, η_motor = 0.85, η_MPPT = 0.95. PV module rating = 350 Wp. Compute the number of modules required. ### **Solution:** **Step 1 — Daily volume to discharge flow rate.** $$Q = \dfrac{50{,}000 \text{ L}}{5 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h}} = \dfrac{50}{18{,}000} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.778 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute static head.** $$H_{static} = \text{static water level below GL} + \text{tank height above GL} = 8 + 5 = 13 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute velocity in pipe.** $$v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \cdot (0.075)^2 / 4} = \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.629 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute velocity head.** $$\dfrac{v^2}{2g} = \dfrac{(0.629)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} = \dfrac{0.396}{19.62} = 0.0202 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Compute friction head loss.** $$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g} = 0.04 \cdot \dfrac{60}{0.075} \cdot 0.0202 = 0.04 \cdot 800 \cdot 0.0202 = 0.646 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Compute minor head loss.** $$h_{minor} = \sum K_L \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g} = 4.0 \cdot 0.0202 = 0.081 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 — Total head.** $$H_{total} = 13 + 0.646 + 0.081 = 13.73 \text{ m}$$ **Step 8 — Hydraulic power.** $$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{102} = \dfrac{2.778 \cdot 13.73}{102} = 0.374 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 9 — Electrical input to motor.** $$P_{motor,input} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{0.374}{0.65 \cdot 0.85} = 0.677 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 10 — PV array peak power.** $$P_{PV} = \dfrac{0.677}{0.95} = 0.713 \text{ kW} = 713 \text{ W}$$ **Step 11 — Number of modules.** $$N = \dfrac{713}{350} = 2.04 \approx 3 \text{ modules (rounded up for safety)}$$ ### **Final Answer:** 3 PV modules of 350 Wp rating. --- ## 3.5 Critical Performance Insight — Variable Irradiance Behavior A solar pump does **not** deliver constant flow. Because PV power output scales linearly with irradiance: $$P_{PV}(t) = \eta \cdot A \cdot G(t)$$ the flow rate and head delivered also vary with time of day. This produces a characteristic **bell-shaped flow curve** from sunrise to sunset. KTU examiners frequently ask students to plot or describe this curve — the answer is **Gaussian-like** with peak around solar noon. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid) The following block diagram maps the energy flow and component interactions of a complete solar pumping system. This is a **functional architecture flow**, not a physical schematic. ```mermaid flowchart LR sunNode[Sun
G 1000 W per m2] --> pvArr[PV Array
DC Voltage Vmpp] pvArr --> mpptCtrl[MPPT Controller
