DC to DC Buck Boost] mpptCtrl --> driveUnit[Motor Drive Unit
VFD for AC or Direct for DC] driveUnit --> motorUnit[Electric Motor
AC Induction or BLDC] motorUnit --> pumpUnit[Pump Unit
Centrifugal or PD] pumpUnit --> pipeNet[Hydraulic Network
Suction and Delivery Pipes] pipeNet --> tankNode[Storage Tank
Overhead Reservoir] pipeNet --> fieldNode[Field Discharge
Irrigation Point] sunNode -. insolation .-> envSens[Environmental Sensor
Irradiance and Temp] envSens -. feedback .-> mpptCtrl tankNode -. float switch .-> driveUnit style sunNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style pvArr fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF style mpptCtrl fill:#50C878,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF style driveUnit fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style motorUnit fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF style pumpUnit fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style pipeNet fill:#708090,stroke:#2F4F4F,color:#FFF style tankNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF style fieldNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style envSens fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Pump Selection Logic This flowchart guides an engineer (or KTU student) through the **pump-type selection process** based on site conditions. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode[Start
Site Survey Data] --> q1{Well depth
greater than 50 m?} q1 -- Yes --> pdBranch[Use Positive Displacement
or Multistage Submersible] q1 -- No --> q2{Daily water demand
greater than 100000 L?} q2 -- Yes --> centHighFlow[Use High Flow Centrifugal
Surface Mounted] q2 -- No --> q3{Is well diameter
less than 150 mm?} q3 -- Yes --> submersibleBranch[Use Submersible
Borewell Pump] q3 -- No --> surfaceBranch[Use Surface Mounted
Centrifugal Pump] pdBranch --> checkHead1[Verify PD pump head rating
covers total dynamic head] centHighFlow --> checkHead2[Verify NPSH available
for centrifugal at site] submersibleBranch --> checkHead3[Verify borewell straightness
for submersible insertion] surfaceBranch --> checkHead4[Verify suction lift
less than 6 m] style startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style pdBranch fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style centHighFlow fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF style submersibleBranch fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF style surfaceBranch fill:#50C878,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF style checkHead1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style checkHead2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style checkHead3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style checkHead4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Daily Operating Profile (Mermaid XY-Chart Equivalent) This diagram illustrates how **flow rate, PV power, and head** vary through a typical operating day. (Visualized as annotated timeline since Mermaid does not natively render plots.) ```mermaid flowchart LR t1[06:00 Sunrise
Flow Near Zero] --> t2[09:00 Morning
Flow Rising] t2 --> t3[12:00 Noon Peak
Maximum Flow] t3 --> t4[15:00 Afternoon
Flow Declining] t4 --> t5[18:00 Sunset
Flow Near Zero] t1 -. irradiance 200 W per m2 .-> t2 t2 -. irradiance 700 W per m2 .-> t3 t3 -. irradiance 1000 W per m2 .-> t4 t4 -. irradiance 500 W per m2 .-> t5 t3 --> tankFill[Tank Fills
to Capacity] tankFill --> nightUse[Night Use
From Tank Storage] style t1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style t2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style t3 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF style t4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style t5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style tankFill fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF style nightUse fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1.** Define a solar photovoltaic water pumping system. List any **four** major components. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A solar photovoltaic water pumping system (SPVWPS) is an integrated renewable-energy assembly that uses PV modules to generate electricity, which drives a motor-pump combination to deliver water without grid or diesel input. > > **Four Major Components:** > 1. **PV Array** — converts sunlight into DC electricity (0.5–3 kW typical for farm pumps) > 2. **MPPT Controller / Motor Drive** — extracts maximum power and conditions voltage > 3. **Motor-Pump Set** — DC brushless or AC induction motor coupled to centrifugal/PD pump > 4. **Hydraulic Network & Storage** — pipes, foot valve, non-return valve, overhead tank --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2.** Why is a centrifugal pump preferred for low-head, high-discharge applications, while a positive displacement pump is preferred for high-head, low-discharge applications? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **centrifugal pump** generates pressure through centrifugal force on a spinning impeller; flow rate scales linearly with speed (Q ∝ N), making it ideal for **low head and high discharge** situations such as river-fed surface irrigation. > > A **positive displacement (PD) pump** traps a fixed volume of water per cycle and forces it into the discharge line; this produces **nearly constant flow irrespective of head**, making it suitable for **deep borewells** where high pressure must be built up against a tall static head. > > Furthermore, PD pumps have **higher mechanical efficiency at high heads**, whereas centrifugal pumps suffer from reduced efficiency and possible cavitation at extreme heads. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** > **Module 1 Choice Question (Internal Choice Provided)** ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a solar photovoltaic water pumping system. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An agricultural field requires **80,000 litres of water per day**. The total static head is **20 m**, pipe length is **80 m**, pipe diameter is **100 mm**, friction factor f = 0.03, minor loss coefficient ΣK_L = 5.0, PSH = 5 h/day. Pump efficiency = 60 %, motor efficiency = 85 %, MPPT efficiency = 95 %. PV module rating = 330 Wp. Calculate the number of PV modules required. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks** **Introduction (1 Mark):** A solar PV water pumping system converts solar energy into electrical energy via PV modules, which powers a motor-pump unit to deliver water for irrigation or drinking. **Block Diagram (2 Marks):** The architecture is: **Sunlight → PV Array → MPPT Controller → Motor Drive → Motor → Pump → Pipe Network → Tank/Field** **Component Functions (4 Marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **PV Array:** Converts solar irradiance into DC electricity. Power output is P = η × A × G. 2. **MPPT Controller:** Continuously tracks the maximum power point (Vmpp ≈ 0.8 Voc) of the array under varying irradiance and temperature, improving energy harvest by 20–30 % compared to direct connection. 3. **Motor Drive / VFD:** Converts DC from PV into 3-phase AC at variable frequency for AC motors, OR directly feeds DC power for BLDC motors. 4. **Motor-Pump Set:** The motor drives the pump impeller or piston. AC induction motors are robust and cheap; BLDC motors offer higher efficiency (90 %+). 5. **Pump:** Converts mechanical rotation into pressurized water flow. Centrifugal for low head, PD for high head. 6. **Hydraulic Network:** Foot valve, suction pipe, delivery pipe, non-return valve. Must be sized to minimize friction losses. 7. **Storage Tank:** Buffers the variable daytime flow into 24-hour availability. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — Convert daily demand to discharge flow rate.** $$Q = \dfrac{80{,}000 \text{ L}}{5 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{18{,}000} = 4.444 \text{ L/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute velocity in pipe.** $$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4.444 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.566 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Compute velocity head.** $$\dfrac{v^2}{2g} = \dfrac{(0.566)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} = \dfrac{0.320}{19.62} = 0.0163 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Compute friction loss.** $$h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g} = 0.03 \cdot \dfrac{80}{0.1} \cdot 0.0163 = 0.03 \cdot 800 \cdot 0.0163 = 0.391 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — Compute minor losses.** $$h_{minor} = \sum K_L \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g} = 5.0 \cdot 0.0163 = 0.0815 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Total dynamic head.** $$H_{total} = H_{static} + h_f + h_{minor} = 20 + 0.391 + 0.0815 = 20.47 \text{ m}$$ **Step 7 — Hydraulic power.** $$P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{102} = \dfrac{4.444 \cdot 20.47}{102} = 0.892 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 8 — Electrical input to motor.** $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{0.892}{0.60 \cdot 0.85} = \dfrac{0.892}{0.51} = 1.749 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 9 — PV array peak.** $$P_{PV} = \dfrac{1.749}{0.95} = 1.841 \text{ kW} = 1841 \text{ W}$$ **Step 10 — Module count.** $$N = \dfrac{1841}{330} = 5.58 \rightarrow \text{round up to } \mathbf{6 \text{ modules}}$$ **Final Answer:** **6 PV modules of 330 Wp rating** are required. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Compare **centrifugal pumps** and **positive displacement pumps** for solar pumping applications. Discuss affinity laws and their applicability. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A solar pump system delivers 10 L/s against a head of 30 m. The pump efficiency is 70 %, motor efficiency 88 %, and MPPT efficiency 95 %. The PV array consists of 12 modules of 350 Wp each. The site receives 5.5 peak sun hours/day. Calculate: (i) Daily hydraulic energy delivered, (ii) Total electrical energy generated by PV per day, (iii) Overall system efficiency. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks** **Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Positive Displacement Pump | |---|---|---| | Working principle | Centrifugal force on impeller | Trapped volume forced through discharge | | Flow vs Head | Flow decreases as head increases | Flow nearly constant with head | | Suction capability | Self-priming up to 6–7 m | Excellent suction, even dry | | Best suited for | Low head, high discharge | High head, low discharge | | Efficiency at part load | Drops sharply | Remains relatively flat | | Cost | Low | Higher | | Maintenance | Simple | Complex (valves, seals) | **Affinity Laws Explanation (3 Marks):** For a centrifugal pump operating at speeds N₁ and N₂: $$\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} \quad \text{(Flow scales linearly)}$$ $$\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Head scales as speed squared)}$$ $$\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \quad \text{(Power scales as speed cubed)}$$ **Applicability (1 Mark):** Affinity laws are **valid only for centrifugal pumps** (geometrically similar impellers). They are **NOT applicable to PD pumps** because PD flow depends on swept volume per cycle, not centrifugal force. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data:** - Q = 10 L/s = 0.01 m³/s - H = 30 m - η_pump = 0.70, η_motor = 0.88, η_MPPT = 0.95 - N_modules = 12, P_module = 350 Wp - PSH = 5.5 h/day **(i) Daily Hydraulic Energy Delivered (3 Marks):** Hydraulic power: $$P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 30 = 2943 \text{ W} = 2.943 \text{ kW}$$ Daily hydraulic energy: $$E_{hyd} = P_{hyd} \cdot PSH = 2.943 \cdot 5.5 = 16.19 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **(ii) Total Electrical Energy Generated by PV (2 Marks):** PV array peak power: $$P_{PV,peak} = 12 \cdot 350 = 4200 \text{ W} = 4.2 \text{ kW}$$ Daily electrical energy (assuming rated performance during PSH): $$E_{PV} = P_{PV,peak} \cdot PSH = 4.2 \cdot 5.5 = 23.1 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **(iii) Overall System Efficiency (2 Marks):** $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{pump}$$ Taking η_PV = 1 (the 23.1 kWh is electrical output of PV): $$\eta_{overall} = 1 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 0.70 = 0.5852 = \mathbf{58.52\%}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) Daily hydraulic energy = **16.19 kWh/day** - (ii) Daily electrical energy = **23.1 kWh/day** - (iii) Overall efficiency = **58.52 %** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Solar Pumping Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Mix-up in Q & H:** Forgetting that the "102" rule needs **Q in L/s** and **H in m**, but other formulas require SI (m³/s). **2–3 marks lost** if not converted. > 2. **Static Head vs Total Head Confusion:** Many students use only static head, ignoring friction and minor losses. Examiner deducts **2 marks minimum**. > 3. **Forgetting Suction Lift Limitation:** Centrifugal pumps cannot pull water from more than 6–7 m below pump level. For deeper wells, only **submersible** pumps work. > 4. **Applying Affinity Laws to PD Pumps:** A classic trick question. PD pumps do NOT follow Q ∝ N relationships. > 5. **Rounding Module Count Down:** Always round **UP** to the next integer — undersizing the PV array will starve the pump on cloudy days. > 6. **Ignoring MPPT Efficiency:** New students often omit η_MPPT from the chain. The efficiency cascade is **η_PV × η_MPPT × η_motor × η_pump**, not just three terms. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this checklist and tick off before the exam hall entry.** - [ ] Solar pumping system = **PV array + MPPT + Motor + Pump + Pipe network + Tank** - [ ] **Hydraulic power formula:** $P_{hyd,kW} = \dfrac{Q_{L/s} \cdot H_m}{102}$ - [ ] **Total head** = Static head + Friction loss + Minor loss + Velocity head - [ ] **Friction loss** uses Darcy-Weisbach: $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ - [ ] **Centrifugal pump** = low head, high discharge; affinity laws apply (Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³) - [ ] **PD pump** = high head, low discharge; flow is constant with head; **affinity laws do NOT apply** - [ ] **Suction lift limit** for surface centrifugal = **6–7 m** (atmospheric pressure) - [ ] **PV array power** = η × A × G (G in W/m², A in m²) - [ ] **Efficiency chain** = η_PV × η_MPPT × η_motor × η_pump × η_hydraulic - [ ] **Module count** = $N = \dfrac{P_{electrical,required}}{P_{module,peak}}$ — always **round up** - [ ] **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** in India = **4.5 to 6.5 h/day** depending on region - [ ] **Daily volume** = $V_{daily} = Q \times PSH$ - [ ] **Direct-coupled (no battery)** is the preferred, cost-effective architecture - [ ] **Water tank storage** is used to buffer variable daytime flow into 24-hour availability - [ ] **Flow vs Time curve** is bell-shaped (Gaussian-like), peaking at solar noon - [ ] KTU examiners love asking **component function questions** — memorize 7 components

topics.title: Solar Cooker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Cooker ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solar Cooker**: A thermal conversion device that directly harnesses **solar radiant energy** and converts it into **heat energy** for the purpose of cooking food, boiling water, and pasteurization. It operates on the principle of the **greenhouse effect** (for box-type) and the **concentration of solar radiation** (for focusing-type), without consuming any conventional fuel or producing combustion by-products. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Imagine leaving your car parked in the sun on a hot day with the windows rolled up. Even though the outside air is cool, the inside of the car becomes unbearably hot. Why? Because sunlight enters through the glass, gets absorbed by the dark seats and dashboard (which heat up), but the **trapped heat (infrared radiation)** cannot easily escape back through the glass. This is the **greenhouse effect**. A box-type solar cooker works exactly the same way: the glass cover lets sunlight in, the blackened interior absorbs it and heats up, and the heat gets trapped inside. A focusing-type solar cooker, on the other hand, works like a **magnifying glass focusing sunlight onto a single point** to burn a piece of paper — except here, that hot point is used to cook food. ### Core Physical Constants - **Solar Constant (I_sc)** = **1367 W/m²** (energy received at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays). - **Peak Sun Hours (PSH)** = average daily solar insolation (in kWh/m²/day), typically **5–6 kWh/m²/day** for Kerala. - **Standard Test Solar Intensity (I)** = **1000 W/m²** (used for performance evaluation). - **Boiling Point of Water** = **100 °C** at atmospheric pressure. - **Maximum practical cooking temperature (box type)** = **120–140 °C**. - **Maximum practical cooking temperature (concentrating type)** = **300–500 °C**. ### Why Solar Cooker? — Relevance to Kerala > [!IMPORTANT] > Kerala receives an average solar insolation of **~4.5 to 5.5 kWh/m²/day** for about **300 sunny days/year**. This makes solar cooking practically viable for almost the entire state, especially during the dry season (December–May). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Solar Cookers (KTU High-Yield) | **S.No** | **Type** | **Working Principle** | **Temperature Range** | **No. of Persons** | **Tracking Required?** | |:--------:|:---------|:----------------------|:---------------------:|:------------------:|:----------------------:| | 1 | **Box-Type Solar Cooker** | Greenhouse effect + selective absorption | **80–140 °C** | 4–8 | No (fixed) | | 2 | **Concentrating / Parabolic Dish Cooker** | Concentration of solar rays to a focal point | **300–500 °C** | 1–4 (or larger community models) | **Yes — continuous** | | 3 | **Scheffler Community Cooker** | Fixed-focus parabolic dish with seasonal tracking only | **300–400 °C** | 50–100 | **Seasonal only** | | 4 | **Spherical / Lens Cooker** | Refraction through Fresnel lens | **300 °C** | 1 | Yes | ### 2.2 Detailed Construction of a Box-Type Solar Cooker (Most Tested in KTU) **Outer Box (Body):** - Made of **galvanized iron (GI) sheet, aluminium, or marine plywood**. - Dimensions typically **50 cm × 50 cm × 20 cm** (length × width × height). **Inner Box (Absorber Chamber):** - Made of thinner aluminium/sheet metal. - Surface coated with **mat-black paint** (high absorptivity, α ≈ 0.95). - Holds the cooking pots. **Transparent Cover (Glazing):** - One or two sheets of **plain window glass** (4 mm thick) with a small air gap. - Acts as a **selective transmitter** — short-wave solar radiation passes through, long-wave heat radiation is blocked. **Insulation:** - **Glass wool, sawdust, or thermocol** packed between the outer and inner box. - Reduces conductive and convective heat loss through the bottom and sides. **Reflector Mirror:** - A **plane mirror (acrylic-backed)** hinged to the outer box. - Increases the effective aperture area and redirects sunlight onto the absorber. ### 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Transmission**: Sunlight (short-wave radiation, λ < 3 μm) passes through the glass cover with minimal absorption (transmissivity τ ≈ 0.9). > > **Step 2 — Absorption**: The blackened inner surface absorbs ~95% of the incoming radiation (absorptivity α ≈ 0.95), converting it to heat. > > **Step 3 — Re-emission**: The hot inner surface re-emits energy as long-wave infrared radiation (λ > 3 μm). Glass is opaque to this IR, so heat is **trapped** (emissivity ε of glass for long-wave is very low). > > **Step 4 — Heat Transfer to Food**: Heat is transferred to the cooking pot via **conduction** (through pot base), **convection** (air inside box), and **radiation**. > > **Step 5 — Loss Pathways**: Heat is lost through the glass cover (top loss, dominant), insulation sides (conduction), and edge leakage (convection). ### 2.4 Concentrating-Type Solar Cooker (Parabolic Dish) - Uses a **parabolic reflector** (or a set of flat mirrors arranged parabolically). - Sunlight is focused onto a small **receiver/absorber** at the focal point where the cooking vessel is placed. - The **concentration ratio (C)** is the key parameter: $$ C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{absorber}}} $$ - **Advantages**: Higher temperatures, faster cooking. - **Disadvantages**: Requires **continuous sun tracking**, more expensive, cannot be left unattended. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Symbol Definition & Units** | |:-----:|:--------------------------|:------------------------|:------------------------------| | 1 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, \Delta T}{I \, A \, \Delta t}$ | m = mass of food/water (kg); C_p = specific heat (J/kg·K); ΔT = T_f − T_i (K); I = solar intensity (W/m²); A = aperture area (m²); Δt = time (s) | | 2 | **Concentration Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{absorber}}}$ | A_aperture = mirror area (m²); A_absorber = receiver area (m²); dimensionless | | 3 | **Solar Energy Received** | $Q_{\text{in}} = I \, A \, \Delta t$ | Total incident energy in Joules | | 4 | **Heat Stored (Useful Gain)** | $Q_{u} = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)$ | Useful heat absorbed by food/water | | 5 | **Overall Heat Loss Coefficient** | $(UA)_{L} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{u}}{\Delta T_{\text{avg}}}$ | Accounts for top, bottom, side losses | | 6 | **Standard Test Conditions** | $I = 1000 \; \text{W/m}^{2}, \;\; T_{\text{amb}} = 30 \; °C$ | KTU adopted test reference | | 7 | **Figure of Merit (F)** | $F = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})}{I \, A \, \Delta t}$ | KTU performance indicator (used in lab) | | 8 | **Optical Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{opt}} = (\tau \, \alpha)$ | τ = glass transmittance; α = absorber absorptance | | 9 | **Top Loss Coefficient (Ut)** | $U_t = \left[ \dfrac{N}{\dfrac{C}{T_{\text{pm}}} \left[\dfrac{T_{\text{pm}} - T_a}{N+f}\right]^{e} + \dfrac{1}{h_w}} \right]^{-1}$ | Klein's empirical relation (advanced) | | 10 | **First Figure of Merit (F_1)** | $F_1 = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})}{A \, \Delta t}$ | Independent of solar intensity | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU exams, **Equation 1 (Thermal Efficiency)** and **Equation 7 (Figure of Merit)** are the **most frequently asked numerical problems**. Always include **units** in the final answer. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Domestic cooking** in rural/off-grid Kerala households. - **Community kitchens** (Anganwadis, hostels, mid-day meal schemes). - **Pasteurization** of milk and water at 60–70 °C. - **Dehydration/drying** of agricultural produce (turmeric, pepper, cardamom — major Kerala crops). - Reduces **LPG/firewood dependency**, curbing deforestation and indoor air pollution. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of a Solar Cooker **Statement**: The thermal efficiency of a solar cooker is defined as the ratio of useful heat gained by the food (or water) to the total incident solar energy on the aperture during the cooking interval. **Step 1 — Define Useful Heat Gain (Q_u)** The sensible heat absorbed by a mass $m$ of food/water in rising from initial temperature $T_i$ to final temperature $T_f$ is: $$ Q_u = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i) $$ Where: - $m$ = mass of the substance being heated (kg) - $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K) - $T_f - T_i = \Delta T$ = temperature rise (K or °C, since intervals are equal) **Step 2 — Define Total Incident Solar Energy (Q_in)** The total solar energy falling on the aperture of area $A$ during time interval $\Delta t$ at average solar intensity $I$ is: $$ Q_{\text{in}} = I \, A \, \Delta t $$ Where: - $I$ = average solar intensity (W/m²) - $A$ = effective aperture area exposed to sun (m²) - $\Delta t$ = time duration of observation (s) **Step 3 — Write the Efficiency Expression** By the formal definition of thermal efficiency as a ratio of output (useful energy) to input (solar energy): $$ \eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{Q_u}{Q_{\text{in}}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Expressions** Substituting the formulas from Step 1 and Step 2 into the efficiency equation: $$ \eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A \, \Delta t} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example** > [!NOTE] > **KTU-style numerical problem**: > A box-type solar cooker with an aperture area of **0.25 m²** is used to heat **2 kg of water** from **30 °C to 90 °C** in **1 hour (3600 s)**. The average solar intensity is **800 W/m²**. Given $C_p$ of water = **4186 J/kg·K**, calculate the **thermal efficiency** and the **figure of merit F**. **Solution — Step A: Compute useful heat** $$ Q_u = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i) $$ $$ Q_u = 2 \times 4186 \times (90 - 30) $$ $$ Q_u = 2 \times 4186 \times 60 $$ $$ Q_u = 502{,}320 \; \text{J} \;\; (\approx 502.32 \; \text{kJ}) $$ **Solution — Step B: Compute incident solar energy** $$ Q_{\text{in}} = I \, A \, \Delta t $$ $$ Q_{\text{in}} = 800 \times 0.25 \times 3600 $$ $$ Q_{\text{in}} = 720{,}000 \; \text{J} \;\; (\approx 720 \; \text{kJ}) $$ **Solution — Step C: Compute thermal efficiency** $$ \eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{Q_u}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{502{,}320}{720{,}000} $$ $$ \eta_{\text{th}} = 0.6977 \approx 69.77 \; \% $$ **Solution — Step D: Compute Figure of Merit F** Assume ambient temperature $T_a$ = 30 °C: $$ F = \frac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})}{I \, A \, \Delta t} $$ $$ F = \frac{2 \times 4186 \times (90 - 30)}{800 \times 0.25 \times 3600} $$ $$ F = \frac{502{,}320}{720{,}000} = 0.6977 \approx 69.77 \; \% $$ (Note: F and η_th give the same numerical value here because $T_i = T_a$.) **Solution — Step E: Comment on Performance** $$ \text{Result: } \eta_{\text{th}} \approx 70 \; \% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For **KTU valuation purposes**, a thermal efficiency in the range of **50–70 %** for a box-type solar cooker is considered **excellent performance**. If the result exceeds **75 %**, students must recheck the numerical substitution (a common valuation trap). ### 3.2 Derivation of Concentration Ratio and Stagnation Temperature **Step 1 — Energy balance at the absorber** At steady state, the concentrated solar flux on the absorber equals the heat lost by radiation and convection: $$ I \, C \, A_{\text{absorber}} = h_c \, A_{\text{absorber}} \, (T_s - T_a) + \epsilon \, \sigma \, A_{\text{absorber}} \, (T_s^{4} - T_a^{4}) $$ **Step 2 — Solving for the absorber temperature (T_s)** For a perfectly absorbing absorber ($\alpha = 1$) at high concentration, the stagnation temperature (maximum theoretical) is: $$ T_s \approx T_a + \frac{I \, C}{h_c + 4 \, \epsilon \, \sigma \, T_m^{3}} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** A higher **concentration ratio C** gives a higher temperature. This justifies the use of parabolic reflectors to achieve temperatures above 300 °C (impossible with simple box cookers). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Box vs. Concentrating Type | **Performance Metric** | **Box-Type Cooker** | **Concentrating-Type Cooker** | |:------------------------|:--------------------|:-------------------------------| | Max Temperature | 100–140 °C | 300–500 °C | | Cooking Time (rice for 4) | 2–4 hours | 30–60 minutes | | Concentration Ratio (C) | C = 1 (no concentration) | C = 10–100 | | Optical Efficiency | 0.6–0.7 | 0.4–0.6 (mirror losses) | | Tracking | Not required | Required (2-axis) | | Cost | ₹ 1,500–4,000 | ₹ 5,000–25,000+ | | Suitable for Kerala? | Yes (very well) | Yes (with maintenance) | | Best Food Type | Slow-cooked rice, dal, vegetables | Frying, baking, roasting | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Box-Type Solar Cooker ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sunlight
Solar Radiation
I = 1000 W per m squared] --> B[Reflector Mirror
Plane Acrylic
Effective Aperture Area A1] B --> C[Transparent Glass Cover
Plain Window Glass 4 mm
Tau approx 0.9] C --> D[Inner Blackened Box
Mat Black Paint
Alpha approx 0.95] D --> E[Cooking Pot
with Food or Water
Mass m kg] E --> F[Heat Loss Pathways
Top Loss through Glass
Side Loss via Insulation
Edge Loss via Convection] D --> G[Re-emitted Long Wave IR
Blocked by Glass Cover
Greenhouse Trapping Effect] G --> C F --> H[Ambient Surroundings
T ambient approx 30 degree C] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Concentrating-Type Solar Cooker Working ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sunlight
Parallel Rays] --> B[Parabolic Reflector Dish
Area A aperture] B --> C[Concentrated Rays
Concentration Ratio C
Sun Tracking Required] C --> D[Focal Point Receiver
Absorber Vessel
Area A absorber] D --> E[Cooking Vessel
Temp 300 to 500 degree C] E --> F[Heat Loss by Radiation
and Convection
at Focal Point] F --> G[Ambient Air] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Flowchart — Type Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Solar Cooker Selection] --> Q1{Need above 200 degree C?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Single family or community?} Q1 -->|No| BOX[Box Type Cooker
No Tracking
Low Cost] Q2 -->|Single family| CONC[Concentrating Dish Type
Continuous Tracking
Rs 5000 to 25000] Q2 -->|Community 50 plus| SCHEFF[Scheffler Community Cooker
Seasonal Tracking Only
Fixed Focal Point] BOX --> MERIT[Advantages
Cheap
Safe
No Tracking] CONC --> MERIT2[Advantages
Fast Cooking
High Temperature] SCHEFF --> MERIT3[Advantages
Large Scale
Efficient
Long Cooking Hours] ``` ### 4.4 Physical Cross-Section Schematic (Text-Based Block View of a Box Cooker) ``` ↑ Sunlight ↓ ┌───────────────────────┐ ← Reflector mirror (plane) │ ┌───────────────┐ │ │ │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ │ ← Air gap │ │ ░ GLASS COVER ░│ │ ← Transparent glass (τ ≈ 0.9) │ │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ │ ← Air gap │ │ ┌───────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ BLACKENED │ │ │ ← Inner box (α ≈ 0.95) │ │ │ ABSORBER │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─────┐ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ POT │ │ │ │ ← Cooking pot (food) │ │ │ └─────┘ │ │ │ │ │ └───────────┘ │ │ │ │ INSULATION │ │ ← Glass wool / sawdust │ └───────────────┘ │ │ OUTER BOX (GI) │ ← Galvanized Iron Body └───────────────────────┘ ``` > [!NOTE] > This text-based schematic shows the layered construction. The **air gap** between the two glass sheets (in double-glazed designs) drastically reduces **top heat loss by convection**, raising efficiency by 8–12%. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **greenhouse effect** as applicable to a box-type solar cooker. List any **two** properties of the materials used in the transparent cover and the inner absorber. **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: The **greenhouse effect** is the phenomenon by which short-wave solar radiation passes through a transparent medium (glass), gets absorbed by a blackened surface, and is re-emitted as long-wave infrared radiation that is blocked by the same glass, thereby trapping heat inside an enclosed space. (2 Marks) **Two material properties**: 1. **Transparent cover (glass)**: high transmissivity (τ ≈ 0.9) for short-wave solar radiation and low emissivity (ε ≈ 0.1) for long-wave infrared radiation. (0.5 Marks) 2. **Inner absorber (blackened surface)**: high absorptivity (α ≈ 0.95) for solar radiation and low reflectivity. (0.5 Marks) **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a **box-type solar cooker** and a **concentrating-type solar cooker** on the basis of temperature range and tracking requirement. **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: | **Parameter** | **Box-Type** | **Concentrating-Type** | |:--------------|:-------------|:-----------------------| | Temperature Range | 100–140 °C | 300–500 °C | | Tracking Requirement | Not required (fixed) | Required (continuous 2-axis) | | Concentration Ratio | C = 1 | C = 10–100 | (3 Marks — 1 for each correctly stated difference with values.) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of a box-type solar cooker. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A box-type solar cooker with an aperture area of **0.36 m²** is used to heat **1.5 kg of water** from **28 °C to 95 °C** in **75 minutes**. If the average solar intensity during this period is **750 W/m²** and $C_p$ of water = 4186 J/kg·K, calculate the **(i)** thermal efficiency and **(ii)** figure of merit. Take ambient temperature = 28 °C. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A (a)**: **Construction (3 Marks):** A box-type solar cooker consists of: 1. **Outer body**: Made of GI sheet / marine plywood (~50 cm × 50 cm × 20 cm). 2. **Inner box**: Aluminium sheet coated with mat-black paint. 3. **Insulation**: Glass wool / sawdust packed between outer and inner box (~5 cm thick). 4. **Transparent cover**: One or two layers of plain glass with air gap. 5. **Reflector**: Plane mirror hinged to the outer box to increase effective aperture. **Working (2 Marks):** Sunlight enters through the glass, is absorbed by the blackened inner box, which heats up and transfers heat to the cooking pot by conduction, convection, and radiation. Re-emitted IR is trapped by the glass — this is the **greenhouse effect**. **Diagram**: Refer to schematic in Section 4.4. (1 Mark for neat labelled diagram) **Advantages (0.5 Mark each, max 2):** - No fuel cost; uses free solar energy. - Safe — no fire hazard, no combustion gases. - Does not require sun tracking. - Low cost and easy to fabricate locally. - Suitable for slow cooking (rice, dal, vegetables). **Disadvantages (0.5 Mark each, max 1.5):** - Slow cooking (2–4 hours). - Cannot fry or bake. - Cannot operate on cloudy/rainy days. - Limited temperature (≤140 °C). - Cooking possible only during 10 AM – 4 PM. --- **Model Solution for Q-A (b)**: **Given Data:** - $A = 0.36 \; \text{m}^{2}$ - $m = 1.5 \; \text{kg}$ - $T_i = 28 \; °C, \;\; T_f = 95 \; °C$ - $\Delta t = 75 \; \text{min} = 75 \times 60 = 4500 \; \text{s}$ - $I = 750 \; \text{W/m}^{2}$ - $C_p = 4186 \; \text{J/kg·K}$ - $T_a = 28 \; °C$ **(i) Thermal Efficiency** (4 Marks for working + 1 for final answer) $$ Q_u = m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i) $$ $$ Q_u = 1.5 \times 4186 \times (95 - 28) $$ $$ Q_u = 1.5 \times 4186 \times 67 $$ $$ Q_u = 420{,}693 \; \text{J} $$ $$ Q_{\text{in}} = I \, A \, \Delta t = 750 \times 0.36 \times 4500 = 1{,}215{,}000 \; \text{J} $$ $$ \eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{420{,}693}{1{,}215{,}000} = 0.3462 \approx 34.62 \; \% $$ **(ii) Figure of Merit F** (2 Marks) $$ F = \frac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})}{I \, A \, \Delta t} $$ $$ F = \frac{1.5 \times 4186 \times (95 - 28)}{750 \times 0.36 \times 4500} $$ $$ F = \frac{420{,}693}{1{,}215{,}000} \approx 0.3462 \approx 34.62 \; \% $$ Since $T_i = T_a$, the figure of merit equals the thermal efficiency. **(Final Answer — 1 Mark for units and statement)** **Final Answer**: $\eta_{th} = F \approx 34.62 \; \%$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** What is the **concentration ratio** of a solar cooker? Explain the construction and working of a **concentrating (parabolic) solar cooker** with a suitable diagram. State its **two major limitations**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A concentrating solar cooker has a parabolic dish of aperture diameter **1.5 m**. The absorber at the focal point has a circular cross-section of diameter **15 cm**. If the solar intensity is **950 W/m²** and the thermal efficiency is **35 %**, find the **(i)** concentration ratio, **(ii)** power incident on the aperture, and **(iii)** useful heat delivered per hour. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B (a)**: **Concentration Ratio Definition (1.5 Marks):** $$ C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{absorber}}} $$ It is the ratio of the aperture area (mirror area collecting sunlight) to the absorber area (where concentrated sunlight is focused). A higher C means higher focal temperature. **Construction (2.5 Marks):** - Parabolic reflector dish (made of aluminium sheet or polished mirror segments). - Mounted on a two-axis tracking stand. - Cooking vessel (often blackened) placed at the focal point on a stand. - Optional: motorised tracker for automated sun-following. **Working (2 Marks):** Parallel sun rays strike the parabolic dish, get reflected and converge at the focal point. The energy density at this point is C times the incident solar flux. The vessel placed at the focus heats rapidly to 300–500 °C, enabling frying and boiling in 30–60 minutes. **Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.2. **Two Limitations (1 Mark — 0.5 each):** 1. Requires **continuous sun tracking**; if the sun moves, the focal point shifts and cooking stops. 2. **High cost** and complex maintenance compared to box type; cannot be left unattended (fire risk). --- **Model Solution for Q-B (b)**: **Given Data:** - Aperture diameter $D_a = 1.5 \; \text{m}$ - Absorber diameter $D_b = 15 \; \text{cm} = 0.15 \; \text{m}$ - $I = 950 \; \text{W/m}^{2}$ - $\eta_{\text{th}} = 35 \; \% = 0.35$ - $t = 1 \; \text{hour} = 3600 \; \text{s}$ **(i) Concentration Ratio** (2 Marks) $$ A_{\text{aperture}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_a^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.5)^{2} = 1.767 \; \text{m}^{2} $$ $$ A_{\text{absorber}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^{2} = 0.01767 \; \text{m}^{2} $$ $$ C = \frac{1.767}{0.01767} = 100 $$ **(ii) Power Incident on Aperture** (2 Marks) $$ P_{\text{in}} = I \times A_{\text{aperture}} = 950 \times 1.767 = 1678.65 \; \text{W} \approx 1.68 \; \text{kW} $$ **(iii) Useful Heat Delivered per Hour** (3 Marks) $$ Q_u = \eta_{\text{th}} \times P_{\text{in}} \times t $$ $$ Q_u = 0.35 \times 1678.65 \times 3600 $$ $$ Q_u = 2{,}115{,}099 \; \text{J} \approx 2115 \; \text{kJ} \; (\approx 2.115 \; \text{MJ}) $$ **Final Answers**: $C = 100$, $P_{\text{in}} \approx 1678.65 \; \text{W}$, $Q_u \approx 2115 \; \text{kJ/hour}$. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**: > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion of time**: Always convert minutes → seconds (× 60) before substitution in $I \, A \, \Delta t$. Many students keep minutes, leading to a 60× error. > 2. **Not stating the formula before substitution**: In 14-mark numerical questions, **the formula carries 1–2 marks**. Always write $Q_u = m \, C_p \, \Delta T$ and $Q_{\text{in}} = I \, A \, \Delta t$ **before** plugging in values. > 3. **Misinterpreting aperture area**: In a box cooker with reflector, the effective aperture area is **box top area + projected mirror area** (not just the box top). Failing to account for this leads to an **inflated efficiency** and loss of marks. > 4. **Confusing Figure of Merit (F) with Thermal Efficiency (η)**: They are similar but **F uses $T_{\text{amb}}$** while η uses $T_i$. Both give the same value only when $T_i = T_a$. > 5. **Concentrating-type limitations not stated**: Many students list advantages but forget limitations — this costs **1 Mark** in Part B questions. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Solar Cooker**: Converts solar radiation directly to heat for cooking. > - **Box Type** uses the **greenhouse effect**; **Concentrating Type** uses **focal concentration**. > - **Standard Test Conditions**: $I = 1000 \; \text{W/m}^{2}$, $T_{\text{amb}} = 30 \; °C$ (KTU adopted). > - **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial value). > - **Box Type Max Temperature**: **140 °C**; **Concentrating Type**: **300–500 °C**. > - **Thermal Efficiency Formula**: $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_i)}{I \, A \, \Delta t}$. > - **Figure of Merit**: $F = \dfrac{m \, C_p \, (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})}{I \, A \, \Delta t}$. > - **Concentration Ratio**: $C = \dfrac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{absorber}}}$. > - **Key Components of Box Cooker**: Outer box, **insulation (glass wool)**, **inner blackened absorber**, **transparent glass cover**, **reflector mirror**. > - **Glass Properties**: high τ for short-wave (~0.9), low ε for long-wave (~0.1). > - **Absorber Properties**: high α for solar (~0.95), matte black paint. > - **Concentrating Cooker** requires **continuous sun tracking** (2-axis). > - **Scheffler Cooker** is the most practical large-scale/community variant. > - **Kerala gets 4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day** of solar insolation — highly favourable for solar cooking. > - **Top heat loss** dominates in box cookers; reduced by **double-glazing** and **insulation**. > - **Unit check** in every numerical — final answer of η must be in **%** or **dimensionless**. > - **Typical KTU exam result range**: η_box = 30–60 %, η_concentrating = 50–70 %.

topics.title: Solar Still
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Still

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Solar Still** is a passive solar thermal device that utilizes the natural greenhouse effect to purify brackish, saline, or contaminated water by simulating the natural hydrological cycle (evaporation–condensation–collection) at an accelerated, controllable rate. It is formally classified as a **direct solar distillation unit** in the taxonomy of renewable energy systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** A solar still is a low-cost, decentralized water-desalination device that converts solar radiation into latent heat of vaporization, producing fresh distilled water suitable for drinking and small-scale industrial uses.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT726)**, the solar still is introduced as the foundational example of **direct conversion of solar radiation into usable thermal energy with a tangible by-product (distillate)**. It is treated as a benchmark passive system against which active desalination systems are compared.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine leaving a shallow bowl of salt water in your backyard on a sunny day and covering it loosely with a transparent plastic sheet. After a few hours, you will notice tiny droplets of fresh water forming on the underside of the sheet, trickling down into a small cup. That, in essence, is a solar still.

It is the **human-engineered, accelerated, and optimized version of a natural phenomenon**:
- The **Sun** is the heat source.
- The **basin water** is the feed (saline/brackish/impure).
- The **glass cover** acts as the condensation surface (like the plastic sheet).
- The **distillate channel** is the "cup" that collects pure water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** In coastal Kerala, ISRO and the Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) have piloted single-basin solar stills in islands like Kadamakkudy and coastal panchayats to provide potable water — a textbook use case that frequently appears in KTU viva-voce questions.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$)** = **1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial radiation at the edge of the atmosphere).
- **Standard Test Condition (STC) irradiance** = **1000 W/m²** at **AM 1.5**.
- **Latent heat of vaporization of water ($h_{fg}$)** ≈ **2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg** (at 100 °C); ≈ **2.45 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 30 °C.
- **Density of water ($\rho_w$)** = **1000 kg/m³**.
- **Specific heat of water ($C_p$)** = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Productivity benchmark (single-basin still, sunny tropical day)** = **3–6 L/m²/day**.

---

## 1.4 Working Principle — The Hydrological Cycle in a Box

The operation rests on **four sequential thermodynamic stages**:

| Stage | Process | Physical Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Absorption** | Short-wave solar radiation passes through the transparent glass cover and is absorbed by the blackened basin liner. | Selective absorption (greenhouse effect). |
| 2. **Evaporation** | Basin water temperature rises; molecules at the free surface overcome latent-heat barrier and vaporize. | Latent heat transfer, mass transfer. |
| 3. **Condensation** | Water vapor rises, contacts the cooler inner glass surface, and releases latent heat, forming droplets. | Phase change (vapor → liquid). |
| 4. **Collection** | Droplets coalesce and slide down the inclined glass under gravity into a distillate trough. | Gravity-driven film flow. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the glass cover is tilted:** Tilting (typically 10°–15° from horizontal in Kerala's latitude ~8°–12° N) ensures both efficient rainwater runoff *and* gravity-driven collection of condensate, while also maximizing solar incidence on the basin.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Single-Basin Solar Still (SBSS)

A conventional **Single-Basin Solar Still (SBSS)** — the type emphasized in KTU Module 1 — consists of:

1. **Basin Liner** — A watertight, blackened (high absorptivity, $\alpha \geq 0.95$) tray (often mild-steel, GI sheet, or fiber-reinforced plastic) holding the saline water.
2. **Insulating Base** — A layer of low thermal conductivity material (sawdust, cork, glass-wool, $k \leq 0.05$ W/m·K) to minimize conductive heat loss to the ground.
3. **Transparent Cover** — A single (or sometimes double) glass sheet ($\tau \geq 0.9$) tilted at the local latitude angle.
4. **Side Walls** — Rigid framing that maintains the air-vapor gap (typically 5–15 cm) between water surface and cover.
5. **Distillate Channel / Trough** — A peripheral gutter that collects the condensate.
6. **Feed-Water Inlet & Brine Outlet** — For makeup water and salt-concentration control.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Energy Balance (Hourly/Quasi-Steady Model)

For **quasi-steady analysis** (commonly adopted in KTU problems), the energy balance over the basin water mass is:

$$Q_{abs} = Q_{evap} + Q_{conv,wg} + Q_{rad,wg} + Q_{cond,bottom} + Q_{stored}$$

Where each term denotes the rate of heat transfer (W) and:

- $Q_{abs}$ = Useful solar energy absorbed by the basin
- $Q_{evap}$ = Latent heat carried away by evaporation (the **productive** term)
- $Q_{conv,wg}$ = Convective loss from water surface to glass cover
- $Q_{rad,wg}$ = Radiative loss from water surface to glass cover
- $Q_{cond,bottom}$ = Conductive loss through the basin insulation
- $Q_{stored}$ = Sensible heat stored in the water mass

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Only $Q_{evap}$ produces distillate. The other three "loss" terms represent thermodynamic irreversibility. The **overall thermal efficiency** of a solar still is the ratio of productive to incident energy.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Sub-Terms (Hourly Energy Quantities)

**(a) Solar energy absorbed by basin water:**

$$Q_{abs} = (\tau \cdot \alpha)_e \cdot A_b \cdot I(t)$$

Where $(\tau \alpha)_e$ is the effective transmittance–absorptance product ($\approx 0.75$–$0.85$ for glass over black basin), $A_b$ is basin area (m²), and $I(t)$ is the instantaneous solar irradiance on the tilted cover (W/m²).

**(b) Evaporative heat transfer (the productive term):**

$$Q_{evap} = h_{e,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g)$$

The evaporative heat transfer coefficient $h_{e,wg}$ is empirically given by:

$$h_{e,wg} = 16.273 \times 10^{-3} \cdot h_{c,wg} \cdot \left(\frac{P_w - P_g}{T_w - T_g}\right)$$

where $P_w$ and $P_g$ are partial vapor pressures (N/m²) at water and glass temperatures respectively, and $h_{c,wg}$ is the convective coefficient (W/m²·K) typically 1–5 W/m²·K.

**(c) Convective loss:**

$$Q_{conv,wg} = h_{c,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g)$$

**(d) Radiative loss (linearized form):**

$$Q_{rad,wg} = h_{r,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g)$$

with $h_{r,wg} = \varepsilon_{eff} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_w^2 + T_g^2)(T_w + T_g)$, $\sigma$ being the Stefan–Boltzmann constant.

**(e) Bottom conductive loss:**

$$Q_{cond,b} = U_b \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_a)$$

where $U_b = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}}$ is the bottom overall heat loss coefficient.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE inventory for KTU 2024 ESE. Every derivation, every numerical problem, and most theory questions link back to one of these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{evap} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$ | Hourly distillate mass yield | kg/s |
| 2 | $Y_{daily} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot \Delta t}{\rho_w \cdot A_b}$ | Daily distillate yield per unit area | L/m²/day |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{A_b \cdot \sum I(t) \cdot \Delta t}$ | Overall thermal efficiency (daily) | dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| 4 | $\eta_{inst} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap} \cdot h_{fg}}{A_b \cdot I(t)}$ | Instantaneous thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $h_{c,wg} = 0.884 \cdot [(T_w - T_g) + \dfrac{(P_w - P_g) \cdot (T_w + 273.15)}{2016 - P_w}]^{1/3}$ | Dunkle's correlation for $h_{c,wg}$ | W/m²·K |
| 6 | $h_{r,wg} = \varepsilon_{eff} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_w^2 + T_g^2)(T_w + T_g)$ | Radiative coefficient (linearized) | W/m²·K |
| 7 | $Q_{abs} = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot A_b \cdot I(t)$ | Useful absorbed solar energy | W |
| 8 | $U_b = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}}$ | Bottom heat-loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 9 | $M_w C_p \dfrac{dT_w}{dt} = Q_{abs} - Q_{losses}$ | Transient (lumped) energy storage | W |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_w} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} - 1\right)^{-1}$ | Effective emissivity (water–glass) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU derivations, the **Dunkle correlation (Eq. 5)** is the single most-tested expression. It links natural convection inside the still to evaporative mass transfer and is the cornerstone of all numerical problems on distillate yield.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coastal & Island Desalination:** Lakshadweep, Maldives, Andamans, and parts of coastal Kerala.
- **Disaster-Relief Camps:** Portable solar stills for emergency drinking-water supply.
- **Industrial Process Water:** Pre-treatment for pharmaceutical and electronics-grade water.
- **Rural Off-Grid Applications:** Low OPEX, zero electricity, minimal moving parts.
- **Educational Benchmark:** Used as a pedagogical tool to teach coupled heat-and-mass transfer and transient energy balance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Daily Distillate Yield of a Single-Basin Solar Still

### Given
- Basin area: $A_b = 2 \text{ m}^2$
- Mean water temperature during sunshine hours: $\bar{T}_w = 55^\circ \text{C} = 328.15 \text{ K}$
- Mean glass-cover temperature: $\bar{T}_g = 35^\circ \text{C} = 308.15 \text{ K}$
- Mean effective convective coefficient (Dunkle): $h_{c,wg} = 2.5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Partial vapor pressures (from steam tables): $P_w = 15.76 \text{ kPa}$, $P_g = 5.63 \text{ kPa}$
- Latent heat at 55 °C: $h_{fg} = 2.37 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$
- Effective transmittance–absorptance product: $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.80$
- Mean daily solar insolation on tilted cover: $\bar{I} = 600 \text{ W/m}^2$
- Effective sunshine hours: $H = 8 \text{ h} = 28800 \text{ s}$

### Step 1 — Compute the Evaporative Heat-Transfer Coefficient

Using the empirical relation:

$$h_{e,wg} = 16.273 \times 10^{-3} \cdot h_{c,wg} \cdot \frac{P_w - P_g}{T_w - T_g}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 16.273 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.5 \cdot \frac{(15.76 - 5.63) \times 10^3}{55 - 35}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 0.040683 \cdot \frac{10130}{20}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 0.040683 \cdot 506.5$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 20.61 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

> This means the **evaporative coefficient is roughly 8× the convective coefficient** — a critical insight: evaporation dominates the heat-transfer regime in a solar still.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Evaporative Heat Loss ($Q_{evap}$)

$$Q_{evap} = h_{e,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g)$$

$$Q_{evap} = 20.61 \cdot 2 \cdot 20$$

$$Q_{evap} = 824.4 \text{ W}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Instantaneous Evaporation Rate

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{824.4}{2.37 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = 3.478 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

---

### Step 4 — Convert to Daily Distillate Volume

Total mass over 8 hours:

$$m_{daily} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot H = 3.478 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 28800 = 10.02 \text{ kg/day}$$

Daily volumetric yield:

$$V_{daily} = \frac{m_{daily}}{\rho_w} = \frac{10.02}{1000} = 0.01002 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} = 10.02 \text{ L/day}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Yield per Unit Area

$$Y = \frac{V_{daily}}{A_b} = \frac{10.02}{2} = 5.01 \text{ L/m}^2\text{/day}$$

> This falls comfortably within the **practical design range of 3–6 L/m²/day** for tropical single-basin stills — a sanity check that is **often required in KTU numerical problems**.

---

### Step 6 — Compute the Overall Thermal Efficiency

$$Q_{inc} = A_b \cdot \bar{I} \cdot H = 2 \cdot 600 \cdot 28800 = 3.456 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

$$Q_{prod} = m_{daily} \cdot h_{fg} = 10.02 \cdot 2.37 \times 10^6 = 2.375 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$

$$\eta_{th,daily} = \frac{Q_{prod}}{Q_{inc}} = \frac{2.375 \times 10^7}{3.456 \times 10^7} = 0.687 = 68.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** Real-world efficiencies for single-basin stills are typically **30–45%**, not 68.7%. The discrepancy arises because our model neglected $Q_{conv}$, $Q_{rad}$, and $Q_{cond}$. In a rigorous KTU problem, the efficiency is computed as $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{inc}}$, and the **gap between $Q_{evap}$ and the total absorbed energy** must be explicitly shown.

---

### Step 3.2 Refined Derivation Including All Loss Terms

**Step 7 — Add the Convective Loss Term**

$$Q_{conv,wg} = h_{c,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g) = 2.5 \cdot 2 \cdot 20 = 100 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Radiative Coefficient**

Take $\varepsilon_w = 0.95$, $\varepsilon_g = 0.88$, so:

$$\varepsilon_{eff} = \left(\frac{1}{0.95} + \frac{1}{0.88} - 1\right)^{-1} = (1.0526 + 1.1364 - 1)^{-1} = (1.189)^{-1} = 0.841$$

$$h_{r,wg} = 0.841 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (328.15^2 + 308.15^2)(328.15 + 308.15)$$

$$h_{r,wg} = 0.841 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot (107683 + 94956)(636.3)$$

$$h_{r,wg} = 0.841 \cdot 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 202639 \cdot 636.3$$

$$h_{r,wg} = 6.14 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 9 — Compute Radiative Loss**

$$Q_{rad,wg} = h_{r,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g) = 6.14 \cdot 2 \cdot 20 = 245.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 10 — Compute Net Productive Heat**

$$Q_{evap,net} = Q_{abs} - Q_{conv,wg} - Q_{rad,wg} - Q_{cond,b}$$

Assuming the absorbed energy is:

$$Q_{abs} = (\tau \alpha)_e \cdot A_b \cdot \bar{I} = 0.80 \cdot 2 \cdot 600 = 960 \text{ W}$$

And the bottom loss (assuming $U_b = 1.0$ W/m²·K and $T_a = 30^\circ$C):

$$Q_{cond,b} = 1.0 \cdot 2 \cdot (55 - 30) = 50 \text{ W}$$

Then:

$$Q_{evap,net} = 960 - 100 - 245.6 - 50 = 564.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 11 — Refined Daily Yield**

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{564.4}{2.37 \times 10^6} = 2.382 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$V_{daily} = 2.382 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 28800 / 1000 = 6.86 \text{ L/day}$$

$$Y = 6.86 / 2 = 3.43 \text{ L/m}^2\text{/day} \quad \text{(realistic)}$$

**Step 12 — Refined Thermal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{th,daily} = \frac{564.4}{960} = 58.8\%$$

> This refined 30–60% range is what examiners expect in KTU solutions. Always **show the energy-loss accounting** explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Types of Solar Stills (KTU Module 1)

| Type | Distinctive Feature | Typical Yield (L/m²/day) | Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single-Basin Single-Slope (SBSS)** | One tilted glass, simple basin | 3 – 5 | Low |
| **Single-Basin Double-Slope** | Two opposite tilted glasses (umbrella shape) | 4 – 6 | Low–Medium |
| **Double-Basin (Multi-Effect)** | Upper and lower water layers separated by a second transparent cover | 6 – 10 | Medium |
| **Multi-Wick / Multi-Effect** | Black wicks or sponges enhance evaporation surface | 5 – 8 | Medium |
| **Active Solar Still** | External collector feeds hot water to the basin | 8 – 15 | High |
| **Stepped / Cascaded** | Multiple stages, each evaporating the previous condensate | 10 – 20 | High |
| **Inverted Solar Still** | Condensing surface below the basin (reduces dust issues) | 4 – 7 | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Module 1, focus primarily on **SBSS**, the **double-basin** still, and the **energy-balance equations**. Active systems are usually reserved for later modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Single-Basin Solar Still (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **energy and mass flow** through every functional zone of an SBSS. This is the **KTU-recommended schematic style** for viva and design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sun: Solar Radiation Source] --> B1[Transparent Glass Cover]
    B1 -->|Transmitted short-wave radiation| C1[Basin Water Mass]
    B1 -->|Reflected + Absorbed by glass| B2[Convective Loss to Ambient]

    C1 -->|Absorbed Q_abs| C2[Heated Saline Water T_w]
    C2 -->|Evaporation Q_evap| D1[Water Vapor in Air Gap]
    C2 -->|Convective loss| B2
    C2 -->|Radiative loss| B2
    C2 -->|Conductive loss through insulation| B3[Ground Heat Sink]

    D1 -->|Rises by buoyancy| E1[Inner Surface of Glass Cover T_g]
    E1 -->|Latent heat release h_fg| E2[Condensate Droplets]
    E1 -->|Convective feedback to water| C2

    E2 -->|Gravity-driven film flow| F1[Inclined Glass Tilt 10 to 15 deg]
    F1 --> G1[Distillate Collection Trough]
    G1 --> H1[Fresh Water Storage Tank]

    C2 -.->|Brine blowdown| I1[Concentrated Brine Outlet]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#0288D1,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style D1 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#B2EBF2,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#4DB6AC,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#26A69A,stroke:#004D40,color:#FFF
    style H1 fill:#00897B,stroke:#004D40,color:#FFF
    style I1 fill:#FF7043,stroke:#BF360C,color:#FFF
    style B2 fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#90A4AE,stroke:#263238,color:#FFF
```

**How to read this diagram during a KTU viva:**

1. **Horizontal arrows (→)** denote *useful* energy or mass transfer.
2. **Dotted arrows (-.->)** denote *unproductive* paths (brine, losses).
3. The **color code** distinguishes thermal zones: gold (source), blue (water), teal (vapor/condensate), orange (reject), gray (losses).

---

## 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section Description (Textual Reconstruction)

In a physical drawing, the cross-section of an SBSS (often asked in KTU 2-mark questions) shows:

```
                \   Glass Cover (tilted ~10-15°)   /
                 \________________________________/
        Vapor →   \                                /
        Air Gap →  \                              /  ← T_g
        (5-15 cm)   \____________________________/
                          ↓  Condensate droplets
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  ← Inner glass surface
                  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  
        Saline    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~        ← T_w (basin water)
        Water     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  
        (5-10 cm) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  
                  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  
                  =============================   ← Black basin liner (α ≥ 0.95)
                  [==========================]    ← Insulation (k ≤ 0.05 W/m·K)
                  [==========================]    ← Structural base
        Ground  →  [__________________________]
```

Key labels for KTU drawing questions:
- $T_w$ = water surface temperature (typically 50–70 °C at peak)
- $T_g$ = glass inner-surface temperature (typically 30–45 °C at peak)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature
- $\delta$ = insulation thickness
- $\beta$ = glass tilt angle
- $A_b$ = basin area
- **Distillate channel** is on the **lower edge** of the inclined cover.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

> **Explain the working principle of a single-basin solar still with a neat sketch. State any two limitations.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**

A **single-basin solar still (SBSS)** purifies saline or brackish water by harnessing solar radiation through the **greenhouse effect**.

**Working Principle (sequential):**
1. **Solar transmission:** Short-wave solar radiation ($\lambda \leq 3 \mu m$) passes through the transparent glass cover (transmittance $\tau \geq 0.9$).
2. **Selective absorption:** The blackened basin liner (absorptance $\alpha \geq 0.95$) absorbs the radiation, heating the saline water to 50–70 °C.
3. **Evaporation:** Water molecules at the free surface gain sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the latent-heat barrier ($h_{fg} \approx 2.37 \times 10^6$ J/kg) and vaporize into the air gap.
4. **Condensation:** The vapor rises by buoyancy, contacts the cooler inner glass surface (at 30–45 °C), releases latent heat, and condenses as fresh-water droplets.
5. **Collection:** Droplets coalesce and flow down the inclined cover into a peripheral distillate trough under gravity.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Low productivity:** Typical yield is only 3–5 L/m²/day, restricting it to household or small-community use.
2. **Intermittent operation:** Output depends on solar insolation; production ceases at night and on overcast days.
3. *(Optional third)* Salt scaling and biofouling of the basin reduce efficiency over time.

> [!NOTE]
> A neat sketch (Section 4.2) is **mandatory** for full marks. Always label $T_w$, $T_g$, and the condensate flow.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

> **Define overall thermal efficiency of a solar still. How is it different from instantaneous efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **overall (daily) thermal efficiency** of a solar still is the ratio of the **total productive energy** (i.e., the latent heat carried away by the distilled water over the entire sunshine period) to the **total incident solar energy** on the glass cover over the same period.

$$\eta_{th,daily} = \frac{m_{daily} \cdot h_{fg}}{A_b \cdot \sum I(t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{\sum Q_{evap} \cdot \Delta t}{A_b \cdot \sum I(t) \cdot \Delta t}$$

For practical single-basin stills, this lies in the range of **30–60%**.

**Difference from Instantaneous Efficiency:**

| Parameter | Instantaneous Efficiency | Daily Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time basis | Single instant ($dt \to 0$) | Whole sunshine day |
| Formula | $\eta_{inst} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap} h_{fg}}{A_b I(t)}$ | $\eta_{daily} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta t}{\sum I \Delta t}$ |
| Use | Design-point analysis, performance mapping | Sizing, payback estimation |
| Typical value | 0–70 % (peaks at noon) | 30–60 % |

> [!TIP]
> The **instantaneous** efficiency is what is plotted on an $\eta$ vs. $I$ curve; the **daily** efficiency is what appears in KTU numerical "find the day's yield" problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Derive the energy balance equation for the basin water of a single-basin solar still under quasi-steady conditions. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-basin solar still has a basin area of 1.5 m². The following data are recorded during a typical hour of operation:
> - Mean water temperature: $T_w = 60^\circ$C
> - Mean glass temperature: $T_g = 38^\circ$C
> - Partial vapor pressures: $P_w = 19.94$ kPa, $P_g = 6.63$ kPa
> - Convective coefficient: $h_{c,wg} = 2.8$ W/m²·K
> - Latent heat of vaporization: $h_{fg} = 2.36 \times 10^6$ J/kg
>
> **Compute:** (i) the evaporative heat-transfer coefficient, (ii) the hourly distillate yield in litres, and (iii) the instantaneous thermal efficiency if the incident solar radiation is 750 W/m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

Under **quasi-steady** conditions, the basin-water temperature is assumed to vary slowly, so the **time-derivative (storage) term** is often absorbed or considered negligible over the period of analysis. The energy balance becomes:

$$Q_{abs} = Q_{evap} + Q_{conv,wg} + Q_{rad,wg} + Q_{cond,b}$$

**Term-by-term explanation:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Sign Convention |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q_{abs} = (\tau \alpha)_e A_b I(t)$ | Useful solar energy absorbed by the basin | **Input (+)** |
| $Q_{evap} = h_{e,wg} A_b (T_w - T_g)$ | Latent heat carried by evaporated water | **Output (−)** — productive |
| $Q_{conv,wg} = h_{c,wg} A_b (T_w - T_g)$ | Natural convection from water to glass | **Output (−)** — loss |
| $Q_{rad,wg} = h_{r,wg} A_b (T_w - T_g)$ | Long-wave radiative loss to glass | **Output (−)** — loss |
| $Q_{cond,b} = U_b A_b (T_w - T_a)$ | Conduction through insulation to ground | **Output (−)** — loss |

For **transient (lumped) analysis**, the storage term is added to the LHS:

$$M_w C_p \frac{dT_w}{dt} = Q_{abs} - Q_{evap} - Q_{conv,wg} - Q_{rad,wg} - Q_{cond,b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For full marks, **every symbol must be defined, every term must be expressed in SI units, and the assumption of quasi-steady state must be explicitly stated.**

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

**Given:** $A_b = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$, $T_w = 60^\circ$C, $T_g = 38^\circ$C, $P_w = 19.94$ kPa, $P_g = 6.63$ kPa, $h_{c,wg} = 2.8$ W/m²·K, $h_{fg} = 2.36 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $I(t) = 750$ W/m², $H = 3600$ s.

**Step (i) — Evaporative Coefficient:**

$$h_{e,wg} = 16.273 \times 10^{-3} \cdot h_{c,wg} \cdot \frac{P_w - P_g}{T_w - T_g}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 16.273 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.8 \cdot \frac{(19.94 - 6.63) \times 10^3}{60 - 38}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 0.04556 \cdot \frac{13310}{22}$$

$$h_{e,wg} = 0.04556 \cdot 605.0 = 27.56 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step (ii) — Hourly Distillate Yield:**

$$Q_{evap} = h_{e,wg} \cdot A_b \cdot (T_w - T_g) = 27.56 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 22 = 909.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{909.5}{2.36 \times 10^6} = 3.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$m_{hour} = 3.854 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 3600 = 1.387 \text{ kg/hour}$$

$$V_{hour} = \frac{1.387}{1000} \times 1000 = 1.387 \text{ L/hour}$$

**Step (iii) — Instantaneous Thermal Efficiency:**

$$Q_{inc} = A_b \cdot I(t) = 1.5 \cdot 750 = 1125 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{inst} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{inc}} = \frac{909.5}{1125} = 0.8085 = 80.85\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A value of 80.85% is **theoretically possible only if all other losses are zero** — which is unphysical. In a realistic KTU solution, students must **explicitly subtract $Q_{conv}$, $Q_{rad}$, and $Q_{cond}$** to get a realistic 30–60% efficiency. Many students lose 2–3 marks here by not stating the simplifying assumption. **Always state:** *"Assuming convective, radiative, and bottom conductive losses are neglected in the simplified instantaneous model…"*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Compare single-basin and double-basin solar stills with respect to construction, productivity, and cost. Which is preferred for community-scale deployment in Kerala? Justify. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solar still yields 4.5 L/m²/day on a sunny day. The basin area is 3 m². If the mean effective $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.78$, the mean daily solar radiation on the tilted cover is 18 MJ/m²/day, and the latent heat of vaporization is 2.4 MJ/kg, **compute** the overall thermal efficiency of the still for that day. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Single-Basin Solar Still | Double-Basin Solar Still |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Construction** | One basin + one transparent cover | Two basins separated by a second transparent cover |
| **Number of water layers** | One | Two (upper thin film + lower bulk) |
| **Productivity (typical)** | 3 – 5 L/m²/day | 6 – 10 L/m²/day |
| **Cost** | Low (~$50–100/m²) | Moderate (~$100–200/m²) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (single basin to clean) | Moderate (two basins, salt scaling in upper) |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 30 – 45 % | 40 – 60 % |
| **Structural complexity** | Simple | Higher (double-glazing frame) |
| **Effect of ambient wind** | Direct heat loss to glass | Upper cover buffers wind losses |

**Why Double-Basin is preferred for community-scale Kerala deployment:**

1. **Higher productivity per unit area** — Kerala has high land cost in coastal belts; doubling yield per m² is critical.
2. **Better resistance to weather fluctuations** — the second cover acts as a buffer against sudden gusts and clouds.
3. **Slightly better water-quality** (TDS of distillate from double-basin is typically < 10 ppm).
4. **Synergy with rooftop space** — a community rooftop (e.g., a school or anganwadi) is a natural fit.

**Caveat:** For a **rural individual household**, single-basin remains the practical choice due to lower capital cost and easier maintenance.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution**

**Given:** $Y = 4.5 \text{ L/m}^2\text{/day}$, $A_b = 3 \text{ m}^2$, $(\tau \alpha)_e = 0.78$, $H_{day} = 18 \text{ MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$, $h_{fg} = 2.4 \text{ MJ/kg}$.

**Step 1 — Total Daily Distillate Mass:**

$$V_{total} = Y \cdot A_b = 4.5 \cdot 3 = 13.5 \text{ L/day} = 13.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Productive Energy Output:**

$$Q_{prod} = m_{daily} \cdot h_{fg} = 13.5 \cdot 2.4 = 32.4 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — Incident Energy on the Glass:**

$$Q_{inc} = A_b \cdot H_{day} = 3 \cdot 18 = 54 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Overall Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th,daily} = \frac{Q_{prod}}{Q_{inc}} = \frac{32.4}{54} = 0.60 = 60\%$$

**Sanity Check:** A daily efficiency of 60% is at the **upper end** of practical SBSS performance, indicating favorable operating conditions (low wind, high humidity gradient, clean glass).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often mistakenly multiply $(\tau \alpha)_e$ into the denominator, writing $Q_{inc} = A_b \cdot H_{day} \cdot (\tau \alpha)_e$. The **correct** definition uses the **incident** energy on the cover as the reference (denominator), and the **absorbed** energy as the *useful input*. The efficiency is therefore referenced to *incident*, not *absorbed* radiation. **Do not apply $(\tau \alpha)_e$ to the denominator.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on Solar Still questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $h_{e,wg}$, $\dot{m}_{evap}$, and $\eta$ — examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing $(\tau \alpha)_e$ placement** — it belongs in $Q_{abs}$, not in $Q_{inc}$.
> 3. **Neglecting to state "quasi-steady"** when deriving the energy balance — 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the Dunkle correlation** — the most-tested expression; its absence costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Using mass instead of volume** for daily yield — the KTU answer key expects **L/m²/day**, not **kg/m²/day**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision before the KTU ESE — read this list twice.**

- ✅ A **solar still** is a passive desalination device based on the **greenhouse effect** and the **hydrological cycle in a box**.
- ✅ Key components: **basin liner (black)**, **insulation**, **glass cover (tilted)**, **distillate channel**.
- ✅ Working stages: **Absorption → Evaporation → Condensation → Collection**.
- ✅ **Solar constant** = 1367 W/m²; **latent heat of water** ≈ 2.26–2.45 MJ/kg.
- ✅ The **Dunkle correlation** is the master equation for $h_{c,wg}$ and $h_{e,wg}$ — memorize it.
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $Q_{abs} = Q_{evap} + Q_{conv,wg} + Q_{rad,wg} + Q_{cond,b}$ (quasi-steady).
- ✅ **Transient balance:** $M_w C_p \dfrac{dT_w}{dt} = Q_{abs} - \text{all losses}$.
- ✅ **Daily yield equation:** $Y = \dfrac{m_{daily}}{\rho_w A_b}$ in **L/m²/day**.
- ✅ **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{th,daily} = \dfrac{m_{daily} h_{fg}}{A_b \cdot H_{day}}$; **typical range 30–60%**.
- ✅ **Instantaneous efficiency:** $\eta_{inst} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap} h_{fg}}{A_b I(t)}$; **typical range 0–70%** (peaks at noon).
- ✅ **Single-basin yield** = 3–5 L/m²/day; **double-basin yield** = 6–10 L/m²/day.
- ✅ **$(\tau \alpha)_e$** is the effective transmittance–absorptance product; usually 0.75–0.85 for glass + black basin.
- ✅ **Effective emissivity:** $\varepsilon_{eff} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_w} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} - 1\right)^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Bottom loss coefficient:** $U_b = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}}$ — use **low-k** insulation (cork, glass-wool).
- ✅ **Active solar stills** (with external collectors) can boost yield to 8–15 L/m²/day.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Low productivity, weather dependence, salt scaling, large area requirement.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific angle:** Coastal & island deployment; latitude 8°–12° N → optimal tilt ≈ 10°–15°.

> 🎯 **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:** *"Absorbed energy = Evaporative (productive) + Convective + Radiative + Conductive losses."* If you remember this one equation and the **Dunkle correlation**, you will secure at least **10 of 14 marks** on any solar-still numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Drier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Drier ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Solar Drier** (also written as **Solar Dryer**) is an engineered device that utilizes incident **solar radiation** as the primary energy input to remove moisture from agricultural produce, food materials, timber, fish, and other hygroscopic products. The device integrates three fundamental physical processes simultaneously: 1. **Collection** of solar radiation through a transparent glazing and absorber plate. 2. **Conversion** of the collected radiation into sensible heat (raising the temperature of the drying air above ambient). 3. **Transfer** of this heated, low-humidity air over the product to be dried, carrying away the evaporated moisture through convective mass transfer. Mathematically, the operation is a coupled **heat and mass transfer** process governed by Fick's law of diffusion (internal moisture migration) and Newton's law of cooling (surface moisture evaporation), both driven by a thermal gradient established by the greenhouse effect inside the drier enclosure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** A solar drier is a device that combines a solar collector and a drying chamber to dry food products, agricultural produce, and other moisture-bearing materials by utilizing solar thermal energy. It improves upon traditional open sun drying by offering controlled temperature, hygiene, protection from dust/rain, and a higher product quality. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Greenhouse Cloth Trick"** > Imagine you have washed clothes drying on a clothesline on a windy day. If you simply leave them open to the sun, they take a long time and may get rained on or dusty. Now, suppose you drape a thin transparent polythene sheet over the clothes, leaving the bottom open for air entry. What happens? The polythene traps the sun's heat (like a greenhouse), the air beneath the sheet becomes warm, the warm air rises, and natural convection pulls fresh dry air in from below, sweeping away moisture from the clothes. The clothes dry **faster, remain clean, and are protected from rain**. > A **solar drier is exactly this principle engineered into a robust, optimized device** — a transparent cover + dark absorber + airflow path + drying chamber. ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot | Category | Sub-Types | Air Movement | |----------|-----------|--------------| | **Open Sun Drying** (not strictly a drier, but a baseline) | Spread on ground, mats, racks | Natural wind | | **Direct Solar Drier** | Cabinet, box, tunnel | Natural or Forced | | **Indirect Solar Drier** | Cabinet with separate collector | Natural or Forced | | **Mixed-Mode Drier** | Direct + indirect combined | Natural or Forced | | **Greenhouse Drier** | Walk-in tunnel type | Forced | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constant (Bold):** > - **Latent heat of vaporization of water:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C (≈ **2400 kJ/kg** commonly used for lower drying temperatures) > - **Solar constant (AM 1.5):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ > - **Standard Test Condition (STC) insolation:** $G_T = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Physical Operating Principle — Step by Step A solar drier operates through the following **chain of energy transformations**: 1. **Step 1 — Solar Absorption:** The glass/polycarbonate cover transmits **short-wave solar radiation (0.3 – 2.5 µm)** but is nearly opaque to **long-wave infrared radiation (5 – 50 µm)** emitted by the hot absorber. This selective transmittance creates the **greenhouse effect**, trapping heat. 2. **Step 2 — Heat Transfer to Air:** The blackened absorber plate heats up to **60 °C – 80 °C** (or more under high insolation). Air passing beneath the absorber is heated by **conduction, natural convection, and radiation** from the plate's underside. 3. **Step 3 — Buoyancy-Driven Airflow (Natural Draft):** The heated air becomes less dense and rises. This creates a **stack effect / chimney effect**, drawing fresh ambient air in through bottom vents. The velocity of this natural draft is given by: $$v_{nd} = \sqrt{\frac{2 g H \Delta T}{T_a}}$$ where $H$ is the chimney height, $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise, and $T_a$ is the absolute ambient temperature. 4. **Step 4 — Moisture Evaporation:** Hot, dry air contacts the wet product. The air's lower relative humidity creates a **vapor pressure deficit** that drives evaporation from the product surface. The mass of moisture evaporated per unit time is: $$\dot{m}_{evap} = h_m \, A_p \, (\rho_{v,s}(T_s) - \rho_{v,\infty})$$ where $h_m$ is the mass transfer coefficient and $A_p$ is the product surface area. 5. **Step 5 — Moisture Carriage & Exhaust:** The moisture-laden air is expelled through top vents or a chimney. The latent heat required for this phase change ($h_{fg}$) is supplied partly by the heated air and partly by continued solar gain. 6. **Step 6 — Product Dehydration:** Internal moisture migrates to the surface by **capillary action and diffusion** to replace the surface moisture lost, until the **equilibrium moisture content (EMC)** is reached. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations for guaranteed marks in the KTU board exam.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{w}{w + d} \times 100$ | % | $w$ = mass of water, $d$ = mass of dry matter | | 2 | **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{w}{d} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying calculations | | 3 | **Mass of water removed** | $m_w = m_i \, MC_{db,i} - m_f \, MC_{db,f}$ | kg | For constant dry mass | | 4 | **Drying Rate** | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{t_{dry}}$ | kg/s | Average over total drying time | | 5 | **Energy to evaporate water** | $Q_{evap} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ | $h_{fg} \approx 2400$ kJ/kg | | 6 | **Heat required to heat product** | $Q_p = m_p c_p \Delta T$ | kJ | $c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/kg·K for wet food | | 7 | **Heat losses (conduction)** | $Q_{loss} = U A (T_{int} -
