T_a)$ | W | $U$ = overall heat loss coefficient | | 8 | **Solar Energy input to drier** | $Q_{solar} = A_c \, G_T \, \eta_c$ | W | $\eta_c$ = collector efficiency | | 9 | **Drying Efficiency** | $\eta_d = \dfrac{m_w \, h_{fg}}{A_c \, G_T \, t}$ | — | Ratio of useful energy to incident solar energy | | 10 | **Collector Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{G_T / \dot{m}_a c_{p,a}}$ | — | Or use Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation | | 11 | **Mass flow rate of air** | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \, A_c \, v$ | kg/s | $v$ = air velocity at collector inlet | | 12 | **Pickup Efficiency** | $\eta_p = \dfrac{T_{out,dc} - T_{in,dc}}{T_{in,dc} - T_a}$ | — | Measures air heating efficiency in collector | | 13 | **Air heating (sensible)** | $Q_{air} = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a} \Delta T$ | W | Heat transferred to drying air | | 14 | **Vapor pressure deficit** | $VPD = e_s(T) - e_a$ | Pa | Drives evaporation | | 15 | **Specific energy consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{m_w}$ | kJ/kg | Lower is better | > **Notation Note:** For absolute value/conditional expressions, use $\vert x \vert$ (e.g., $\vert T_{out} - T_{in} \vert$) — never the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside table cells. --- ### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why this matters in the field > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Post-Harvest Loss Reduction:** Up to **30–40%** of agricultural produce in developing regions is lost due to improper drying. Solar driers cut this to **<5%**. > - **Food Safety:** Controlled temperatures in solar driers inhibit aflatoxin growth (Aspergillus flavus) compared to open sun drying. > - **Off-Grid Industrial Use:** Dehydrated products (herbs, fruits, fish, spices) command **3× – 5× higher market price** than fresh equivalents. > - **Carbon Footprint:** Each kg of moisture removed by a solar drier saves roughly **0.6 – 1.0 kWh** of electrical energy that would otherwise be used in a mechanical dryer. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture Content **Given:** $$MC_{wb} = \frac{w}{w + d} \quad ; \quad MC_{db} = \frac{w}{d}$$ **To Prove:** The conversion relationship between the two. **Step 1:** From the wet basis definition, isolate $w$: $$w = MC_{wb} \cdot (w + d)$$ **Step 2:** Expand and rearrange: $$w = MC_{wb} \cdot w + MC_{wb} \cdot d$$ $$w - MC_{wb} \cdot w = MC_{wb} \cdot d$$ $$w (1 - MC_{wb}) = MC_{wb} \cdot d$$ **Step 3:** Solve for the ratio $w/d$ (which equals $MC_{db}$): $$\frac{w}{d} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$$ $$\boxed{MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}}$$ **Step 4:** Reverse conversion (algebraic inversion of the above): $$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}$$ **Verification:** If $MC_{wb} = 0.8$ (80% wet basis, very wet product), then $MC_{db} = 0.8 / 0.2 = 4.0$ (i.e., 400% dry basis) — consistent with intuition. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass of Moisture Removed During Drying **Given:** - Initial mass of product: $m_i$ - Initial moisture content (dry basis): $MC_{db,i}$ - Final mass of product: $m_f$ - Final moisture content (dry basis): $MC_{db,f}$ **Step 1:** Express the mass of **dry matter** (which is invariant during drying): $$m_d = m_i \, (1 - MC_{wb,i}) = m_f \, (1 - MC_{wb,f})$$ **Step 2:** Express initial and final moisture masses using dry basis: $$m_{w,i} = m_d \cdot MC_{db,i} = m_i \cdot \frac{MC_{wb,i}}{1} \cdot \frac{1 - MC_{wb,i}}{1 - MC_{wb,i}}$$ **Step 3:** Working with the simpler dry-basis form directly: $$m_{w,i} = \frac{m_i \cdot MC_{wb,i}}{1} = m_i \cdot MC_{wb,i} \quad \text{(since } w = MC_{wb} \cdot m_{total} \text{)}$$ **Step 4:** The mass of water **removed** is the difference: $$m_w = m_{w,i} - m_{w,f} = \frac{m_i \cdot MC_{wb,i} - m_f \cdot MC_{wb,f}}{1}$$ **Step 5:** Since dry mass is constant, an elegant alternate form uses the dry mass: $$m_w = m_d (MC_{db,i} - MC_{db,f})$$ $$\boxed{m_w = m_d \, (MC_{db,i} - MC_{db,f}) = m_i \cdot MC_{wb,i} - m_f \cdot MC_{wb,f}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Drying Efficiency **Given:** A solar drier with collector area $A_c$, receiving solar insolation $G_T$ for time $t$, removing moisture mass $m_w$. **Step 1:** Total solar energy incident on the collector during drying: $$Q_{in} = A_c \cdot G_T \cdot t \quad \text{(units: J or kJ)}$$ **Step 2:** Useful energy = energy used to evaporate moisture: $$Q_{useful} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$$ **Step 3:** Drying efficiency is the ratio: $$\eta_d = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{m_w \cdot h_{fg}}{A_c \cdot G_T \cdot t}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_d = \frac{m_w \, h_{fg}}{A_c \, G_T \, t} \times 100 \, \%}$$ > **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** > A solar drier with $A_c = 2 \text{ m}^2$ operates for $t = 6 \text{ h} = 21600 \text{ s}$ under $G_T = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$, removing $m_w = 4 \text{ kg}$ of water. Find $\eta_d$. **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$Q_{in} = 2 \times 800 \times 21600 = 3.456 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 34560 \text{ kJ}$$ $$Q_{useful} = 4 \times 2400 = 9600 \text{ kJ}$$ $$\eta_d = \frac{9600}{34560} = 0.2778 = \mathbf{27.78\%}$$ > **Physical interpretation:** Only about **28%** of incident solar energy is used for moisture removal; the rest is lost through glazing, conduction, and exhaust. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Air Mass Flow Rate Through Collector (Natural Draft Velocity) **Step 1:** Buoyancy force per unit volume of air column in a chimney of height $H$: $$\Delta P = \rho_a \cdot g \cdot H \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_a}$$ **Step 2:** Equate this pressure to the dynamic pressure of the rising air: $$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a v^2$$ **Step 3:** Solve for velocity (chimi ney draught velocity): $$\frac{1}{2} \rho_a v^2 = \rho_a g H \frac{\Delta T}{T_a}$$ $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 g H \Delta T}{T_a}}$$ $$\boxed{v_{chimney} = \sqrt{2 g H \left(\frac{T_{out} - T_a}{T_a}\right)}}$$ > **Note:** Temperatures are in **Kelvin (absolute)**. If using °C differences, the ratio $\Delta T / T_a$ is small, so use Kelvin for $T_a$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drying Performance Summary | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value for Solar Drier | Effect of Increase | |-----------|--------|-------------------------------|---------------------| | Drying temperature | $T_{dry}$ | **50 – 80 °C** | Higher = faster drying, but may damage product | | Air velocity | $v$ | **0.5 – 2.0 m/s** (natural) | Higher = better convective mass transfer | | Initial moisture content | $MC_i$ | **70 – 85% wb** | Higher = more energy, longer drying time | | Final moisture content | $MC_f$ | **10 – 15% wb** (safe storage) | Lower = longer drying, more energy | | Relative humidity of drying air | $RH$ | **10 – 40%** | Lower = faster drying | | Solar collector efficiency | $\eta_c$ | **30 – 60%** | Higher = less collector area needed | | Drying efficiency | $\eta_d$ | **15 – 35%** | Indicates overall system performance | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mixed-Mode Solar Drier The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture, energy flow, and mass flow interactions in a **mixed-mode solar drying system** — a common KTU examination diagram type. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sun
Solar Source] -->|Shortwave Radiation| B[Transparent Glazing
Glass Polycarbonate] B -->|Transmitted Solar| C[Absorber Plate
Black Painted Metal] C -->|Conduction Convection| D[Air Plenum
Heated Air Channel] D -->|Hot Dry Air| E[Drying Chamber
Product Trays] E -->|Moisture Laden Air| F[Chimney
Exhaust Vent] F -->|Hot Humid Air Out| G[Atmosphere] H[Ambient Air In] -->|Bottom Inlet Vents| D E -->|Direct Solar Through Side Glazing| E I[Backup Heater
Optional] -->|Aux Heat| D J[Fan
Optional Forced Draft] -->|Boosts Flow| D style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#1A1A1A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style D fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Forced-Convection Indirect Solar Drier ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sunlight] --> GL[Transparent Cover] GL --> AB[Absorber Plate] AB -->|Heat Transfer| AIR[Air Stream] AMB[Fresh Ambient Air] --> FAN[DC Fan PV Powered] FAN --> AIR AIR -->|Heated Dry Air| CH[Drying Chamber with Trays] CH -->|Moisture Pickup| EX[Exhaust Chimney] EX --> OUT[Moist Air to Atmosphere] PRO[Wet Product] --> CH CH --> DPRO[Dried Product Out] style S1 fill:#FFD700 style GL fill:#87CEEB style AB fill:#000000,color:#FFFFFF style FAN fill:#DDA0DD style CH fill:#90EE90 style EX fill:#A9A9A9 ``` --- ### 4.3 Schematic Component Cross-Section Description (Textual Diagram) For KTU board exams, the following labelled cross-section is **frequently asked**. Reconstruct it mentally: ``` ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ Hot, moist air exits | | | | | | | | | ~~~ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ ~~~ ←── 1. Transparent Glazing ──────────────────────────────────── ████████████████████████████████████ ←── 2. Absorber Plate (black) ↓ ↓ Air channel (heated) ↓ ↓ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ ←── 3. Drying Chamber │ ═══ Product Tray 1 ═══ │ │ ═══ Product Tray 2 ═══ │ │ ═══ Product Tray 3 ═══ │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ ↑ ↑ ↑ Cool air enters here ↑ (Bottom Inlet Vents) (Chimney on top for draft) ``` **Component Labels for KTU Answer Sheets:** 1. Transparent Glazing — allows shortwave solar radiation in, traps longwave IR. 2. Absorber Plate — black-painted metal sheet that converts solar to heat. 3. Drying Chamber — holds the product trays; receives hot air. 4. Chimney — creates stack effect for natural convection. 5. Inlet Vents — admit fresh dry air. 6. Insulation (back/sides) — minimize conductive losses. --- ### 4.4 Comparative Architecture of Drier Types (Block Matrix) | Feature | Direct Drier | Indirect Drier | Mixed-Mode Drier | Tunnel Drier | |---------|--------------|----------------|-------------------|--------------| | Glazing on top | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Glazing on sides | ✅ (around product) | ❌ (closed) | ✅ (partial) | ✅ | | Separate collector | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Product directly heated by sun | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (partial) | ❌ | | Better for color-sensitive products | ❌ (may fade) | ✅ | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ | | Best drying quality | Moderate | High | High | High (industrial) | --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OESFT726]** **Define a solar drier. List any four advantages of solar drying over conventional open sun drying.** **Model Answer:** A **solar drier** is a device that utilizes solar thermal energy to remove moisture from agricultural and food products by combining a solar collector and a drying chamber, where heated air absorbs moisture from the product and is exhausted to the atmosphere. **Advantages over open sun drying:** 1. **Faster drying** due to higher air temperature (50–80 °C) inside the chamber. 2. **Protection from dust, rain, insects, birds, and animals.** 3. **Hygienic and superior product quality** with no fungal/aflatoxin contamination. 4. **Higher market value** due to uniform color, texture, and moisture content. 5. **Reduces post-harvest losses** significantly. 6. **Eco-friendly** — uses renewable solar energy, zero carbon emission. *(Any 4 well-explained points = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OESFT726]** **Distinguish between a direct and an indirect solar drier with examples.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Direct Solar Drier | Indirect Solar Drier | |--------|---------------------|-----------------------| | **Sunlight on product** | Direct sunlight falls on the product | Product is shielded from direct sun | | **Heating mechanism** | Greenhouse effect directly on the chamber | Separate solar collector heats air; air then enters chamber | | **Suitable for** | Grains, herbs (color-tolerant) | Fruits, vegetables, medicinal plants (color-sensitive) | | **Example** | Solar cabinet dryer, box dryer | Solar tunnel dryer, solar air heater + drying chamber | | **Product quality** | May fade or over-dry on top | Uniform, color-preserved | | **Efficiency** | Lower (~15–25%) | Higher (~25–40%) | *(Tabular answer = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern > **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer either OR. Below are TWO independent alternatives. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, OESFT726]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle and main components of a **mixed-mode solar tunnel dryer** for drying agricultural produce. **Model Solution:** A **mixed-mode solar tunnel dryer** is a semicircular or Quonset-shaped structure covered with a transparent UV-stabilized polyethylene sheet, used for bulk drying of fruits, vegetables, and fish. **Working Principle:** - Solar radiation passes through the transparent sheet and falls on: 1. The absorber floor (indirect heating of incoming air). 2. The product placed on trays inside the tunnel (direct heating). - Air is heated by **both** modes — hence "mixed-mode". - A **fan** (forced convection) drives the heated air through the product bed. - Moist air exits from the far end of the tunnel through a chimney. **Main Components:** 1. **Tunnel structure** — semicircular frame, GI pipe or wooden arches. 2. **Transparent cover** — 150–200 µm UV-stabilized polyethylene. 3. **Absorber floor** — black-painted concrete or metal sheet. 4. **Product trays** — perforated mesh trays. 5. **Fan (DC, PV-powered)** — 12–24 V, 50–100 W. 6. **Inlet and outlet vents** — controlled by dampers. 7. **Insulation** — at the base, 50 mm glass wool. **Sketch (mark this on your answer sheet):** A long tunnel shape with arrows showing airflow direction (inlet → product → outlet), sun rays striking from above, and a fan on the inlet side. *(Working principle 3 marks + components list 2 marks + sketch 2 marks = 7 marks)* --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A solar cabinet dryer has a collector area of **1.8 m²** and receives an average solar insolation of **750 W/m²** for **8 hours**. If **3.2 kg** of moisture is evaporated during this period, calculate: - (i) The drying efficiency. - (ii) The pickup efficiency if the air enters the collector at 32 °C and leaves at 58 °C, while the ambient is 30 °C. Take $h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $A_c = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$, $G_T = 750 \text{ W/m}^2$, $t = 8 \text{ h} = 28800 \text{ s}$, $m_w = 3.2 \text{ kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $T_{in,c} = 32 °C$, $T_{out,c} = 58 °C$, $T_a = 30 °C$. **Step 1: Total solar energy input** $$Q_{in} = A_c \cdot G_T \cdot t$$ $$Q_{in} = 1.8 \times 750 \times 28800 = 3.888 \times 10^7 \text{ J}$$ $$Q_{in} = 38880 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 2: Useful energy used for moisture evaporation** $$Q_{useful} = m_w \cdot h_{fg} = 3.2 \times 2400 = 7680 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3: Drying efficiency** $$\eta_d = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{7680}{38880} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta_d = 19.75\%}$$ **Step 4: Pickup efficiency of the collector** $$\eta_p = \frac{T_{out,c} - T_{in,c}}{T_{in,c} - T_a} = \frac{58 - 32}{32 - 30} = \frac{26}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_p = 13}$$ > **Note:** A pickup efficiency > 1 indicates the air was heated **substantially above** its inlet temperature relative to the small ambient-inlet difference. In practical dryers, this is achievable and indicates good collector design. *(Drying efficiency 4 marks + pickup efficiency 3 marks = 7 marks)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, OESFT726]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **different types of solar driers** based on the mode of heat transfer and airflow. State two suitable applications for each. **Model Solution:** **Classification of Solar Driers:** **1. Direct Solar Drier (Natural Convection)** - Sunlight falls directly on the product. - Heat is transferred to the product by **radiation and natural convection**. - Example: Solar cabinet dryer, box-type dryer. - *Applications:* Drying of grains, chillies, turmeric, coriander, herbs. **2. Indirect Solar Drier (Forced Convection)** - Separate solar air heater collector; heated air is ducted to the drying chamber. - Product is shielded from direct sunlight; heat is transferred by **convection only**. - Example: Solar tunnel dryer, solar air heater + chamber. - *Applications:* Drying of grapes (raisins), mango slices, banana, pineapple. **3. Mixed-Mode Drier** - Combines direct radiation on the product and indirect heating via a collector. - Example: Hohenheim-type dryer, integrated tunnel dryers. - *Applications:* Fish drying, large-scale fruit drying, medicinal plants. **4. Greenhouse Drier (Walk-in type)** - Large walk-in enclosure with transparent cover; product on racks. - Air moves by natural or forced draft. - *Applications:* Bulk drying in commercial operations, coffee beans, cocoa. **5. Hybrid Drier** - Solar energy supplemented by biomass, electric, or fossil-fuel heater. - *Applications:* Continuous industrial drying where solar energy is intermittent. *(5 types × ~1 mark each with applications = 7 marks)* --- **(b) [7 Marks]** **5 kg of fresh grapes** are to be dried to **1.5 kg** in a solar drier. The initial moisture content is **80% (wet basis)** and the final moisture content is **15% (wet basis)**. Calculate: - (i) The mass of moisture to be removed. - (ii) The total energy required for evaporation, taking $h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$. - (iii) The required collector area if the average solar insolation is **600 W/m²** for **10 hours/day** and the drying efficiency is **20%**. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $m_i = 5 \text{ kg}$, $m_f = 1.5 \text{ kg}$, $MC_{wb,i} = 0.80$, $MC_{wb,f} = 0.15$, $h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $G_T = 600 \text{ W/m}^2$, $t = 10 \text{ h} = 36000 \text{ s}$, $\eta_d = 0.20$. **Step 1: Mass of moisture removed** Method A (direct from mass balance): $$m_w = m_i - m_f = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ kg}$$ Verification using dry mass: $$m_d = m_i(1 - MC_{wb,i}) = 5 \times 0.20 = 1.0 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_d = m_f(1 - MC_{wb,f}) = 1.5 \times 0.85 = 1.275 \text{ kg}$$ *(Inconsistency indicates given data is approximate; using the mass balance directly: $m_w = 3.5$ kg.)* $$\boxed{m_w = 3.5 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 2: Energy for evaporation** $$Q_{useful} = m_w \cdot h_{fg} = 3.5 \times 2400$$ $$\boxed{Q_{useful} = 8400 \text{ kJ} = 8.4 \times 10^6 \text{ J}}$$ **Step 3: Required collector area** From $\eta_d = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{A_c \cdot G_T \cdot t}$: $$A_c = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta_d \cdot G_T \cdot t} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^6}{0.20 \times 600 \times 36000}$$ $$A_c = \frac{8.4 \times 10^6}{4.32 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{A_c \approx 1.944 \text{ m}^2 \approx 2 \text{ m}^2}$$ *(Part i 2 marks + Part ii 2 marks + Part iii 3 marks = 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert hours → seconds** in solar energy calculations ($1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s}$). A frequent 1–2 mark error. > 2. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content** without conversion. The KTU paper often gives one and asks in the other. > 3. **Using $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg** (at 100 °C) when the question specifies a lower temperature; use **$h_{fg} = 2400$ kJ/kg** as the standard textbook value for drying problems. > 4. **Skipping the labelled sketch** in Part B (a) — KTU deducts up to 2 marks for missing diagrams. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** in numerical problems (e.g., "neglecting heat losses to surroundings" or "assuming constant solar insolation"). > 6. **Confusing drying efficiency with collector efficiency** — they are NOT the same. Drying efficiency is the system-level metric; collector efficiency is component-level. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam** - ✅ A **solar drier** uses solar energy to evaporate moisture from products; combines a collector + drying chamber. - ✅ **Three heat transfer modes operate:** conduction, convection, radiation. - ✅ **Latent heat of vaporization of water:** $h_{fg} = 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (standard for drying problems). - ✅ **Moisture content relations:** $MC_{db} = MC_{wb} / (1 - MC_{wb})$; reverse: $MC_{wb} = MC_{db} / (1 + MC_{db})$. - ✅ **Mass of water removed:** $m_w = m_i MC_{wb,i} - m_f MC_{wb,f} = m_d (MC_{db,i} - MC_{db,f})$. - ✅ **Drying efficiency formula:** $\eta_d = \dfrac{m_w h_{fg}}{A_c G_T t}$ — must convert time to **seconds**. - ✅ **Pickup efficiency formula:** $\eta_p = (T_{out} - T_{in}) / (T_{in} - T_{amb})$. - ✅ **Four main types:** Direct, Indirect, Mixed-Mode, Greenhouse (with Tunnel and Hybrid as sub-categories). - ✅ **Natural draft velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2 g H \Delta T / T_a}$ (T in Kelvin). - ✅ **Indirect dryers are best** for color-sensitive products (fruits, medicinal plants). - ✅ **Direct dryers** are simpler and cheaper but may cause fading/over-drying on the top layer. - ✅ **Drying efficiency range:** 15–35% for well-designed systems. - ✅ **Collector efficiency range:** 30–60% depending on design and insulation. - ✅ **Post-harvest losses** can be reduced from 30–40% to under 5% using solar driers. - ✅ **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** is the lowest moisture a product can reach under given air conditions. - ✅ **Glass transmits shortwave but blocks longwave** — the basis of the greenhouse effect inside driers. - ✅ **Always draw a labelled diagram** for descriptive answers (sketch + labels = 2 marks minimum). - ✅ **For numerical answers in KTU:** show all steps, units, and box the final answer. ---

topics.title: Heliostat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heliostat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heliostat** is a precisely actuated, dual-axis (azimuth-elevation) tracking mirror assembly that continuously orients itself to reflect solar irradiance from a moving sun toward a **fixed receiver** or **fixed focal target**. Unlike a heliostat's simpler cousin (the heliostat field array of a solar power tower), the device is a *single* optical-mechanical unit whose fundamental function is the **decoupling of the optical collection axis from the thermal conversion axis**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Module 1 — *Solar Radiation and Concentrating Collectors*), a heliostat is defined as:

> A computer-controlled, motor-driven flat (or slightly curved) reflector whose instantaneous angular position is computed so that the **bisector of the angle subtended between the incoming sunbeam and the outgoing target line is the surface normal of the mirror**.

This geometric condition — known as the **"equal-angle reflection law"** or **Snell's law applied to specular reflection** — is what makes the heliostat work. The incident ray, the surface normal, and the reflected ray must always lie in the same plane, and the angle of incidence must equal the angle of reflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> Heliostats are covered under *Concentrating Solar Power (CSP) Systems* and *Solar Field Optics*. The student is expected to understand the kinematic geometry, the sun-tracking algorithms, the cos² optical losses, the *aiming strategy* (on-axis vs. off-axis), and the blocking/shadowing constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **hand mirror** while standing in a sunny garden, and your friend is standing at a fixed spot. As the sun moves across the sky, you constantly twist and tilt the mirror so that the sun's reflection *always* lands on your friend's face, even though your friend is not moving. You are, in effect, a **biological heliostat**.

- The **sun** = the moving source.
- Your **friend** = the fixed receiver (e.g., boiler atop a solar tower).
- The **mirror** = the heliostat reflector.
- The **angle calculations you do in your head** = the heliostat's tracking algorithm.

Now scale this up: instead of one mirror, a *solar power plant* uses **hundreds to thousands** of these mirrors arranged in a *heliostat field* around a central tower, all computer-coordinated to bounce sunlight onto a single receiver perched at the top of the tower (typically 50–200 m tall). The collective temperature at the receiver can exceed **1000 °C**, which drives a steam turbine or a molten-salt heat exchanger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Scale Check:**
> The Ivanpah Solar Power Facility (California, USA) — one of the world's largest — uses approximately **173,500 heliostats**, each with a reflective area of ~14 m², for a total reflective surface of about **2.1 million m²**. The solar flux at the receiver can exceed **1 MW/m²**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard values are *essential* for any heliostat-related KTU numerical problem:

- **Solar Declination (δ):** −23.45° ≤ δ ≤ +23.45° (varies seasonally)
- **Solar Constant (Iₛc):** **1361 W/m²** (TSI — Total Solar Irradiance at 1 AU)
- **Air Mass (AM):** AM0 = 0 (space), AM1 (sea level, sun overhead), AM1.5 (standard terrestrial test condition)
- **Standard Reflectivity (ρ) of silvered glass:** **0.88 – 0.94**
- **Solar Tower Receiver Temperature Range:** **500 °C – 1400 °C** depending on receiver type
- **Heliostat Surface Area (commercial):** **1 m² – 150 m²** per unit
- **Tracking Accuracy Required:** **< 1 mrad** (≈ 0.057°)
- **Solar Tower Height Range:** **50 m – 250 m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The heliostat is *not* a passive device — it is a **closed-loop servo-controlled mechatronic system**. The mirror position is updated every **0.01 – 0.1 seconds** in modern digital systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

The heliostat operates on a precise optical-geometric law. Here is the structured logic of its function:

1. **Sun Position Determination:**
   - Compute the **solar hour angle (ω)**, **declination (δ)**, and **latitude (φ)**.
   - Derive the **solar altitude (α)** and **solar azimuth (γₛ)** at the current time.
   - This gives the unit vector **ŝ** pointing from the heliostat toward the sun.

2. **Target Vector Establishment:**
   - Define a fixed unit vector **t̂** pointing from the heliostat's pivot to the receiver (e.g., the top of the solar tower).
   - This vector is **time-invariant** because the receiver is stationary.

3. **Mirror Normal Calculation (the core heliostat equation):**
   - For specular reflection, the mirror's surface normal **n̂** must bisect the angle between the *negative* sun vector (−ŝ) and the target vector (t̂):

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{(-\hat{s} + \hat{t})}{\vert\, -\hat{s} + \hat{t}\,\vert}
$$

   - This is the **fundamental heliostat aiming equation** and is derivable directly from vector Snell's law.

4. **Actuation:**
   - The mirror is rotated about its **two orthogonal axes** (typically altitude and azimuth) using precision gear motors until the computed **n̂** aligns with the mirror's physical normal.
   - Encoders provide feedback in a closed PID loop.

5. **Cosine Loss Penalty:**
   - Because the reflected ray must travel a non-vertical path to reach the elevated receiver, the **effective area** of the heliostat as seen by the receiver is reduced by the **cosine of the reflection angle (θᵣ)**.
   - For a tower-top receiver, this introduces a **cos²(θᵣ)** factor in total collected flux — hence the famous **"cos² effect"** of heliostat fields.

6. **Attenuation & Blocking Losses:**
   - Atmospheric attenuation along the slant path from heliostat to receiver.
   - **Blocking** (one heliostat's ray shadowed by another) and **shadowing** (one heliostat casting a shadow on another) — these scale with the **solar field packing density**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every formula** a KTU student needs to solve heliostat-related numerical or descriptive problems. Memorize this table before attempting Module 1 numericals.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\hat{n} = \dfrac{(-\hat{s} + \hat{t})}{\vert\, -\hat{s} + \hat{t}\,\vert}$ | Mirror normal vector (vector Snell's law) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\cos\theta_i = -\hat{n}\cdot\hat{s}$ | Cosine of angle of incidence | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\cos\theta_r = \hat{n}\cdot\hat{t}$ | Cosine of angle of reflection | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\theta_i = \theta_r$ (Snell's reflection law) | Specular reflection condition | degrees or radians |
| 5 | $A_{\text{eff}} = A_m \cdot \cos\theta_r$ | Effective mirror area as seen from receiver | m² |
| 6 | $\eta_{\cos^2} = \cos^2\theta_r$ | Cosine-square loss factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{received}} = I_b \cdot A_m \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{\cos^2} \cdot \tau_a$ | Power delivered to receiver | W |
| 8 | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Solar altitude equation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sin\gamma_s = \dfrac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ | Solar azimuth (using convention) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta = 23.45°\sin\!\left[\dfrac{360°}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | Spencer's declination formula | degrees |
| 11 | $\omega = 15°(t_{\text{solar}} - 12)$ | Hour angle | degrees |
| 12 | $d_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{H_t^2 + R^2}$ | Slant distance heliostat → receiver | m |
| 13 | $\tau_a = e^{-k \cdot d_{\text{slant}}}$ | Atmospheric transmittance (Beer-Lambert) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $I_{\text{DNI}} = I_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033\cos\dfrac{360n}{365}\right)e^{-\tau_a/\sin\alpha}$ | Direct Normal Irradiance on a clear day | W/m² |
| 15 | $P_{\text{peak, plant}} = N_h \cdot A_m \cdot \eta_{\text{overall}}$ | Plant peak power | W |

> **Notation key:** $A_m$ = mirror area, $\rho$ = mirror reflectivity, $\tau_a$ = atmospheric transmittance, $k$ = extinction coefficient (~0.0001–0.001 m⁻¹), $H_t$ = tower height, $R$ = radial distance of heliostat from tower base, $I_b$ = beam irradiance, $N_h$ = number of heliostats.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Power Towers (Central Receiver Systems):** Heliostat fields are the defining component of CSP plants like Ivanpah (USA), Noor-III (Morocco), DEWA CSP (UAE), and Gemasolar (Spain).
- **Solar Furnaces:** Research furnaces (e.g., Odeillo, France) use a single large heliostat (≈ 40 m × 40 m = 1800 m²) to focus sunlight onto a 1 MW thermal test station.
- **Daylighting:** Architectural heliostats redirect sunlight into building interiors, reducing daytime electrical lighting demand.
- **Solar Chemistry / Hydrogen Production:** High-flux solar concentrators based on heliostat arrays are used to drive thermochemical cycles (e.g., ZnO/Zn, CeO₂/CeO₂₋δ) for green H₂.
- **High-Temperature Materials Testing:** Solar furnaces serve as clean, non-contaminating heat sources for testing spacecraft heat shields and refractory materials.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mirror Normal Vector Equation

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU. It directly follows from the law of reflection expressed in vector form.

**Starting point:** Specular reflection demands that the incident ray, the surface normal, and the reflected ray are *coplanar*, and that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

**Step 1: Define the vectors.**
Let:
- $\hat{s}$ = unit vector from the heliostat to the sun (incoming ray direction).
- $\hat{t}$ = unit vector from the heliostat to the target (the desired outgoing ray direction).
- $\hat{n}$ = unit normal to the mirror surface (pointing away from the sun, i.e., into the half-space where the heliostat sits).

**Step 2: Write Snell's law in vector form.**
Snell's reflection law in vector form states that the incoming ray is reflected about the normal:

$$
\hat{r} = \hat{s} - 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n}
$$

where $\hat{r}$ is the reflected ray direction.

**Step 3: Impose the geometric bisector condition.**
For a mirror to send the sun's ray *specifically* toward the target, we require $\hat{r} = \hat{t}$. Substituting:

$$
\hat{t} = \hat{s} - 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\hat{n}$.**
Rearranging:

$$
\hat{s} - \hat{t} = 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n}
$$

Recognizing that the vector on the right is parallel to $\hat{n}$, and taking magnitudes:

$$
\vert \hat{s} - \hat{t} \vert = 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})
$$

So:

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{s} - \hat{t}}{2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the bisector property.**
Now use the geometric fact that the **bisector of the two vectors** (specifically, the *external* bisector of $-\hat{s}$ and $\hat{t}$) gives the normal direction directly:

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{t} - \hat{s}}{\vert \hat{t} - \hat{s} \vert}
$$

**Step 6: Final canonical form.**
Equivalently, this can be written as:

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{t} + (-\hat{s})}{\vert \hat{t} + (-\hat{s}) \vert} = \frac{\hat{t} - \hat{s}}{\vert \hat{t} - \hat{s} \vert}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Note:** Some textbooks (e.g., Stine & Harrigan, *Solar Energy Fundamentals and Modeling*) use $\hat{s}$ pointing *from* the sun *toward* the mirror, in which case the formula becomes $\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{s} + \hat{t}}{\vert \hat{s} + \hat{t} \vert}$. Always check the convention used in your KTU study material.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cos² Loss Factor

**Step 1:** When the sun's rays hit the mirror at angle $\theta_i$ from the normal, the *projected area* of the mirror as seen from the sun is:

$$
A_{\text{seen by sun}} = A_m \cos\theta_i
$$

**Step 2:** Similarly, when the reflected ray departs at angle $\theta_r$ from the normal (and by reflection law $\theta_r = \theta_i$), the *projected area* of the mirror as seen from the receiver is:

$$
A_{\text{seen by receiver}} = A_m \cos\theta_r
$$

**Step 3:** Combining both effects, the **bidirectional** effective area for the energy transfer is:

$$
A_{\text{eff}} = A_m \cos\theta_i \cos\theta_r
$$

**Step 4:** Since $\theta_i = \theta_r$ (the reflection law), we get:

$$
A_{\text{eff}} = A_m \cos^2\theta_r
$$

This is the **cos² effect**, and it represents one of the largest optical losses in heliostat fields — sometimes reducing intercepted power by 30–60% near sunrise/sunset or for far-field heliostats.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2-mark / 3-mark format):**
A heliostat of area **25 m²** is positioned **150 m** horizontally from the base of a solar tower of height **80 m**. The sun's rays arrive at an angle $\theta_i = 30°$ from the vertical. The mirror reflectivity is **0.90** and the atmospheric transmittance along the slant path is **0.95**. The beam irradiance is **850 W/m²**. Calculate:
(a) The slant path length from heliostat to receiver.
(b) The effective mirror area.
(c) The power delivered to the receiver.

**Solution:**

**(a) Slant path length:**
Using the Pythagorean relation:

$$
d_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{H_t^2 + R^2} = \sqrt{80^2 + 150^2}
$$

$$
d_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{6400 + 22500} = \sqrt{28900}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\text{slant}} = 170\,\text{m}\,}
$$

**(b) Effective mirror area:**
The reflection angle equals the incidence angle: $\theta_r = \theta_i = 30°$.

$$
A_{\text{eff}} = A_m \cos^2\theta_r = 25 \times \cos^2(30°)
$$

$$
A_{\text{eff}} = 25 \times (0.866)^2 = 25 \times 0.75
$$

$$
\boxed{\,A_{\text{eff}} = 18.75\,\text{m}^2\,}
$$

**(c) Power delivered:**
Using the comprehensive formula:

$$
Q_{\text{received}} = I_b \cdot A_m \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{\cos^2} \cdot \tau_a
$$

$$
Q_{\text{received}} = 850 \times 25 \times 0.90 \times 0.75 \times 0.95
$$

$$
Q_{\text{received}} = 850 \times 25 \times 0.90 \times 0.7125
$$

$$
Q_{\text{received}} = 850 \times 25 \times 0.64125
$$

$$
Q_{\text{received}} = 850 \times 16.03125
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\text{received}} \approx 13{,}627\,\text{W} \approx 13.63\,\text{kW}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Heliostat vs. Other Solar Trackers

| Parameter | Heliostat | Parabolic Trough | Solar Dish (Stirling) | PV Sun-Tracker |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Optical Concentration | 500–2000× | 30–80× | 2000–10,000× | 1× (no conc.) |
| Working Temperature | 500–1400 °C | 150–400 °C | 650–850 °C | ~25 °C (cell) |
| Receiver Type | Fixed (tower top) | Fixed (focal line) | Fixed (dish focus) | Fixed (module) |
| Tracking Type | 2-axis (mirrors) | 1-axis (parabola) | 2-axis (dish) | 1- or 2-axis |
| Power Output | 10–200 MW | 50–400 MW | 5–50 kW | 1 kW–100+ MW |
| Cosine Loss | Heavy (cos²) | Moderate (cos) | Heavy (cos²) | Moderate (cos) |
| Energy Storage Compatibility | Excellent (molten salt) | Good (thermal oil) | Limited (battery) | None inherent |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single Heliostat Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUN: Apparent Daily Motion] --> B[Sun Position Sensor or Astronomical Calculator]
    B --> C[Tracking Control Unit - Microcontroller / PLC]
    D[Receiver Coordinates: Tower XYZ - Fixed Input] --> C
    C --> E[Mirror Normal Vector Calculator - Eq. n = t-s/|t-s|]
    E --> F[Servo Drive Command Generator]
    F --> G[Azimuth Motor - Gear Reducer - Encoder]
    F --> H[Elevation Motor - Gear Reducer - Encoder]
    G --> I[Mirror Mounting Pedestal]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Reflective Glass Facets]
    J --> K[Sunlight Incident]
    K --> L[Specular Reflection off Curved Plane]
    L --> M[Fixed Receiver atop Solar Tower]
    M --> N[Heat Transfer Fluid: Steam / Molten Salt / Air]
    N --> O[Thermal Storage and Power Block]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solar irradiance (left) is geometrically processed by a heliostat (center blocks), which redirects the energy to a stationary receiver (right). The two feedback loops from the encoders (dashed in the real system) close the servo control.

### 4.2 Heliostat Field Layout Around a Central Tower

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SF [Heliostat Field - Concentric Ring Layout]
        direction TB
        nodeRing1[Inner Ring: Closest Heliostats - Low cos^2 Loss, High Blocking Risk]
        nodeRing2[Middle Ring: Optimum Optical-Economic Trade-off Zone]
        nodeRing3[Outer Ring: Far Heliostats - High cos^2 Loss, Low Blocking]
        nodeRing1 --> nodeRing2
        nodeRing2 --> nodeRing3
    end
    SF --> TOWER[CENTRAL TOWER - 50 to 250 m Tall]
    TOWER --> REC[Central Receiver - Cavity or External Type]
    REC --> HX[Heat Exchanger / Steam Generator]
    HX --> TURB[Steam Turbine - Rankine Cycle]
    TURB --> GEN[Electrical Generator]
    GEN --> GRID[Power Grid - AC Output]
    HX --> STORE[Thermal Energy Storage - Molten Salt Tanks]
    STORE --> HX
```

### 4.3 Vector Geometry of the Heliostat Aiming Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sun Vector: s-hat, direction to Sun] --- N[Mirror Surface Normal: n-hat - Bisector]
    N --- T[Target Vector: t-hat, direction to Tower Receiver]
    INC[Angle of Incidence: theta_i] -. between -S and N .-> N
    REF[Angle of Reflection: theta_r] -. between N and T .-> N
    LAW[Snells Law: theta_i = theta_r] -. constrains .-> N
```

### 4.4 Optical Loss Mechanism Flowchart (Why the Cos² Effect Matters)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Incident Beam on Heliostat] --> P2[Mirror Reflectivity Loss: 6-12 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Cosine Loss 1: Mirror effective area from sun = Am cos theta_i]
    P3 --> P4[Cosine Loss 2: Mirror effective area to receiver = Am cos theta_r]
    P4 --> P5[Atmospheric Attenuation: Beer-Lambert along slant distance]
    P5 --> P6[Blocking Loss: Heliostat-to-Heliostat Interception]
    P6 --> P7[Shadowing Loss: Cast Shadows in Early Morning and Late Afternoon]
    P7 --> P8[Soiling Loss: Dust and Particulate Deposition 1-5 percent]
    P8 --> P9[Receiver Intercept Efficiency: Geometric - Receiver misses some rays]
    P9 --> FINAL[Net Flux at Receiver: Typically 40-70 percent of Geometric Maximum]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define a heliostat. With the aid of the relevant vector equation, explain how the mirror orientation is determined.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **heliostat** is a two-axis, computer-controlled flat or slightly curved mirror that tracks the sun and reflects its rays onto a stationary target (typically a receiver at the top of a solar tower).

The mirror's surface normal $\hat{n}$ is determined using the **vector form of Snell's reflection law**:

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{t} - \hat{s}}{\vert \hat{t} - \hat{s} \vert}
$$

where $\hat{s}$ is the unit vector from the mirror to the sun, and $\hat{t}$ is the unit vector from the mirror to the fixed target. This ensures the angles of incidence and reflection are equal and coplanar. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
What is the **cos² effect** in a heliostat field? Why is it important in the design of solar power towers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cos² effect** arises because the effective area of a heliostat is reduced by the cosine of the angle of incidence *and* the cosine of the angle of reflection. Since the reflection law forces $\theta_i = \theta_r$, the effective area is $A_{\text{eff}} = A_m \cos^2\theta_r$.

**Importance:** It significantly reduces the power delivered to the receiver, especially for heliostats far from the tower base and during early/late hours. Designers mitigate this by optimizing the **radial spacing** of heliostats and selecting appropriate field layouts (radial, spiral, or "del Carmen" staggered). *(3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Heliostat Optical Geometry and Field Design**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper, Q1(a)+Q1(b) Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the vector equation used to compute the surface normal of a heliostat. Clearly define all vectors used and state the underlying physical law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar power tower of height **100 m** is surrounded by heliostats in a circular field of radius ranging from **80 m to 300 m**. A specific heliostat is located **200 m** from the tower base. The local solar altitude is **45°** and the solar azimuth places the sun directly along the line joining the heliostat to the tower. The mirror area is **40 m²**, reflectivity is **0.92**, and the atmospheric extinction coefficient is $k = 0.0001$ m⁻¹. Beam irradiance is **900 W/m²**.

Calculate:
- (i) The slant path length from the heliostat to the receiver. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The atmospheric transmittance. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The power delivered to the receiver, accounting for all optical losses. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We start with **Snell's law in vector form** for specular reflection:

$$
\hat{r} = \hat{s} - 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

where:
- $\hat{s}$ = unit vector along the incoming ray (from the sun).
- $\hat{r}$ = unit vector along the reflected ray.
- $\hat{n}$ = unit normal to the mirror surface.

The geometric requirement is that the reflected ray $\hat{r}$ must point along the target direction $\hat{t}$:

$$
\hat{r} = \hat{t} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Equating (i) and (ii):

$$
\hat{t} = \hat{s} - 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n}
$$

Rearranging to isolate $\hat{n}$:

$$
\hat{s} - \hat{t} = 2(\hat{s}\cdot\hat{n})\hat{n}
$$

Since the right-hand side is parallel to $\hat{n}$, we take unit magnitudes and identify the direction:

$$
\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{s} - \hat{t}}{\vert \hat{s} - \hat{t} \vert}
$$

This is the canonical mirror-normal equation. **Physical Law:** Law of reflection — incident ray, reflected ray, and normal are coplanar, with $\theta_i = \theta_r$.

*Mark allocation:*
- Vector form of Snell's law: 2 marks
- Geometric bisector condition: 2 marks
- Algebraic manipulation: 2 marks
- Final equation + physical law statement: 1 mark

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Slant path length (2 marks):**

$$
d_{\text{slant}} = \sqrt{H_t^2 + R^2} = \sqrt{100^2 + 200^2} = \sqrt{10000 + 40000} = \sqrt{50000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\text{slant}} \approx 223.6\,\text{m}\,}
$$

**(ii) Atmospheric transmittance (2 marks):**

$$
\tau_a = e^{-k \cdot d_{\text{slant}}} = e^{-0.0001 \times 223.6} = e^{-0.02236}
$$

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$
\tau_a \approx 1 - 0.02236 = 0.9776
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_a \approx 0.978\,}
$$

**(iii) Power delivered (3 marks):**

First, determine the reflection angle. Since the sun is along the heliostat-tower line, the sun is at altitude 45°, so the sun's elevation vector makes an angle with the horizontal. The reflection angle from the normal equals the angle between the heliostat-to-sun direction and the heliostat-to-tower direction. Geometrically, with the geometry given:

- Horizontal distance from heliostat to tower: $R = 200$ m.
- Vertical distance (tower height): $H_t = 100$ m.
- The line of sight to the receiver makes an angle $\arctan(H_t/R) = \arctan(0.5) \approx 26.57°$ above the horizontal.
- The sun is at altitude 45° above the horizontal, in the same azimuthal plane.

The reflection angle $\theta_r$ from the mirror's normal is half of the angle between the incoming ray and the outgoing ray, but since the mirror normal bisects these two directions, $\theta_r$ equals the angle between the mirror's normal and the line of sight to the receiver. By symmetry, with the sun "directly along the line joining the heliostat to the tower," the geometry simplifies so that $\theta_r = (45° - 26.57°)/2 \approx 9.22°$... 

However, a more direct approach: **total internal computation** uses the simple formula:

$$
Q = I_b \cdot A_m \cdot \rho \cdot \tau_a \cdot \cos^2\theta_r
$$

Assuming the standard $\cos^2\theta_r$ factor is computed with $\theta_r$ as the angle between the normal and the receiver direction (≈ 26.57° for a worst-case tower-top reflection):

$$
\cos^2\theta_r = \cos^2(26.57°) = (0.894)^2 \approx 0.800
$$

$$
Q = 900 \times 40 \times 0.92 \times 0.978 \times 0.800
$$

$$
Q = 900 \times 40 \times 0.92 \times 0.7824
$$

$$
Q = 900 \times 40 \times 0.7198
$$

$$
Q = 900 \times 28.79
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 25{,}914\,\text{W} \approx 25.9\,\text{kW}\,}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Heliostat Field Losses and Plant Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** With neat schematic reasoning, explain the **aiming strategies** for heliostats: on-axis and off-axis. State their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 MWe solar tower plant uses heliostats of area **20 m²** each, with an overall plant efficiency of **20%** and solar irradiance of **900 W/m²** at the site.
- (i) Estimate the **total number of heliostats** required in the field. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) If the field covers a circular area of radius **600 m** around the tower, calculate the **solar field packing density** in m² of mirror per m² of land. Comment on the result. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Aiming Strategies (7 marks):**

**On-axis aiming (also called "target-image-plane" aiming):**
The heliostat is aimed so that the reflected solar image is formed *on* the receiver aperture. This requires the mirror normal to be computed such that the receiver, mirror, and sun are coplanar. It is geometrically simple but assumes the receiver is infinitesimally small.

**Off-axis aiming (also called "spillage-optimized" or "cavity-receiver" aiming):**
The mirror normal is biased slightly so that the reflection lands on a *specific sub-region* of a larger receiver. This compensates for the fact that real receivers have finite area and non-uniform heat flux distributions, allowing a more even thermal load.

*Tabular comparison:*

| Aspect | On-Axis | Off-Axis |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Optical Accuracy | High for small receiver | High for large receiver |
| Computational Complexity | Low | Higher |
| Thermal Uniformity | Poor (concentrated hot spot) | Excellent |
| Best For | Cavity receivers, solar furnaces | External flat-plate receivers |
| Used in | Research, small systems | Commercial plants (Ivanpah, etc.) |

*Mark allocation:*
- Definitions: 2 marks
- Geometry/schematic explanation: 2 marks
- Comparison table: 2 marks
- Example application: 1 mark

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of heliostats (3 marks):**

Total thermal power required:

$$
P_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{P_{\text{electrical}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{0.20} = 250 \times 10^6\,\text{W} = 250\,\text{MW}_{\text{th}}
$$

Power per heliostat (assuming ideal $\cos^2 \approx 0.7$ average and $\rho = 0.9$):

$$
P_{\text{per heliostat}} = I_b \cdot A_m \cdot \eta_{\cos^2} \cdot \rho = 900 \times 20 \times 0.7 \times 0.9
$$

$$
P_{\text{per heliostat}} = 900 \times 20 \times 0.63 = 11{,}340\,\text{W} \approx 11.34\,\text{kW}
$$

Number of heliostats:

$$
N_h = \frac{P_{\text{thermal}}}{P_{\text{per heliostat}}} = \frac{250 \times 10^6}{11.34 \times 10^3} \approx 22{,}045
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_h \approx 22{,}000\,\text{heliostats}\,}
$$

**(ii) Packing density (4 marks):**

Total mirror area:

$$
A_{\text{mirror, total}} = N_h \times A_m = 22{,}000 \times 20 = 440{,}000\,\text{m}^2
$$

Total land area (circular field of radius 600 m):

$$
A_{\text{land}} = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 600^2 = \pi \times 360{,}000 \approx 1{,}130{,}973\,\text{m}^2
$$

Packing density:

$$
\sigma = \frac{A_{\text{mirror, total}}}{A_{\text{land}}} = \frac{440{,}000}{1{,}130{,}973} \approx 0.389
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma \approx 0.39\,\text{(i.e., 39\% ground coverage)}\,}
$$

**Comment:** A packing density of ~0.39 is **typical and reasonable** for a heliostat field. Values much above 0.5 lead to excessive blocking and shadowing losses, while values below 0.25 waste land. The actual packing density varies radially — the inner rings are sparser (to avoid blocking) and outer rings can be denser.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Heliostat Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the mirror normal equation.** The most frequent error is writing $\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{s} + \hat{t}}{\vert \hat{s} + \hat{t} \vert}$ when the convention requires $\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{t} - \hat{s}}{\vert \hat{t} - \hat{s} \vert}$. Always draw the geometry first and verify the bisector points in the correct direction.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\cos\theta$ loss with $\cos^2\theta$ loss.** A common mistake is using only **one** cosine factor instead of **two**. Remember: the mirror loses effective area *both* as seen from the sun *and* as seen from the receiver. Both cosines multiply, giving $\cos^2$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting atmospheric attenuation along the slant path.** Students often apply transmittance as if the heliostat were directly overhead, but for a tower receiver, the **slant distance** is the relevant optical path length.
>
> 4. **Mixing up hour-angle convention.** The hour angle $\omega$ is *zero* at solar noon, *negative* in the morning, and *positive* in the afternoon (or vice versa — depends on sign convention). Mixing it up shifts the sun position by 12 hours, which is a 2–3 mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the reflectivity factor $\rho$.** In KTU numericals, $\rho$ is *always* a multiplier. Forgetting it leads to overestimating delivered power by 6–12%.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong value of the solar constant.** Use **1361 W/m²** (modern accepted value) or **1367 W/m²** (older NASA value) — both are accepted in KTU, but **never use 1353** (outdated) or **1000** (irradiance at Earth's surface, not the solar constant).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heliostat = two-axis tracking flat mirror** that decouples the sun-tracker from a **fixed receiver**.
- **Core law:** Vector form of Snell's reflection — $\hat{n} = \frac{\hat{t} - \hat{s}}{\vert \hat{t} - \hat{s} \vert}$.
- **Cos² effect** is the dominant optical loss and scales with the **square** of the reflection angle.
- **Solar tower heliostat fields** consist of 10,000 to 200,000+ units arranged in concentric rings.
- **Packing density** of 30–50% is optimal to balance land use against blocking/shadowing losses.
- **Three main aiming strategies:** on-axis (small receiver), off-axis (large receiver), and **eccentric** (used in some cavity receivers).
- **Atmospheric transmittance** follows Beer-Lambert law: $\tau_a = e^{-k d_{\text{slant}}}$.
- **Receiver temperatures** can reach **1400 °C** in advanced central receiver systems, enabling thermochemical fuel production and supercritical-CO₂ power cycles.
- **Tracking accuracy** must be better than **1 mrad** to keep the focused image on the receiver aperture.
- **Key engineering trade-off:** closer heliostats have lower cosine loss but higher blocking risk; farther heliostats have higher cosine loss but better clearance.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** vector geometry, the cos² derivation, field layout, and plant-level numerical estimation are the **highest-yield** topics for ESE questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Furnace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Furnace ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Solar Furnace:** A solar energy conversion system that uses **optically concentrated solar radiation** to generate extremely high temperatures (typically **1000 °C to 4000 °C**) at a focal point, enabling high-temperature thermal processes such as materials testing, metallurgy, chemical reactions, and clean energy research without combustion of fossil fuels. A solar furnace (also called a *solar concentrator furnace* or *solar thermal concentrator*) is a non-imaging/imaging optical system that collects parallel solar rays over a large aperture area and converges them onto a small receiver/target, thereby multiplying the natural solar flux density from approximately **1000 W/m²** to flux densities exceeding **10⁷ W/m²** at the focal spot. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a small magnifying glass under bright noon sunlight. The lens bends (refracts) all the rays passing through its circular area and forces them to converge into a single bright dot on a piece of paper — and the paper begins to smoke and burn. A **solar furnace is essentially this magnifying glass scaled up to industrial size**, but instead of using glass refraction, it uses **curved mirrors (reflection)** to focus sunlight. There are two principal ways of "magnifying" the sun: 1. **A single big curved mirror (Parabolic Dish)** — like a giant satellite dish, all rays bounce once and meet at one point in front of it. 2. **A field of small flat mirrors (Heliostats) + a tower-mounted target** — like thousands of soldiers using tiny mirrors to redirect sunlight onto a single spot on a castle wall. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction from Solar Cooker / Solar Water Heater:** > A solar furnace operates at **concentration ratios (C) of 1000 to 30000+**, whereas solar cookers operate at C ≈ 10–50. The defining feature of a solar furnace is its ability to reach temperatures **above 2000 °C**, enabling applications impossible in conventional flat-plate or low-concentration collectors. ### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Solar Constant (Extra-terrestrial) | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** | | Direct Normal Irradiance (at surface, clear noon) | $DNI$ | **800–1000 W/m²** | | Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** | | Absolute Temperature of Sun's Surface | $T_{sun}$ | **5778 K** | | Apparent Angular Radius of Sun | $\theta_s$ | **0.00465 rad (≈ 16 arcmin)** | | Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio | $C_{max}$ | **~46200** (for a 3-D point focus) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Solar Furnaces (Operational Logic) Solar furnaces are broadly classified into **two architectural families** based on the number of reflections and the geometry of concentration: #### A. Direct-Focusing (Single Reflection) Solar Furnaces - **Parabolic Dish Furnace** — A single paraboloidal mirror tracks the sun in **two axes** (azimuth + elevation), and the receiver is mounted at the focus *in front* of the dish (front-side focus). - **Spherical / Segmented Mirror Furnaces** — Used historically (e.g., the **Odeillo furnace, France**); a large fixed spherical concentrator reflects sunlight to a secondary focusing mirror, which in turn focuses onto the target. This allows the *target to be stationary*, which is critical for materials-testing experiments. #### B. Indirect-Focusing (Multi-Reflection / Tower) Solar Furnaces - A **field of heliostats** (flat, sun-tracking mirrors) surrounds a tall central tower. - Each heliostat is individually steered to reflect sunlight onto a **furnace target** mounted near the top of the tower. - This architecture is *not* limited by the size of a single mirror casting, so very large thermal powers (**multi-MW**) can be delivered. - The *Solar Tower at the Weizmann Institute (Israel)* and the *PS10 / PS20 plants in Spain* are tower systems in this family. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Solar Ray Collection:** Sun-tracking heliostats (or a paraboloidal dish) intercept direct normal irradiance $DNI$ over a large geometric aperture area $A_a$. 2. **Optical Reflection:** Mirrors (typically aluminized glass or polished aluminum, reflectance $\rho \approx 0.85$–$0.95$) redirect all intercepted rays toward a common target. 3. **Concentration at Focus:** The rays intersect over a small receiver area $A_r$. The ratio $C = A_a / A_r$ is the **geometric concentration ratio**. 4. **Thermal Conversion:** The concentrated flux $q = C \cdot DNI$ is absorbed by the target (graphite, ceramic, or a cavity receiver), which heats up to the **radiative equilibrium temperature**. 5. **Heat Utilization / Extraction:** For a *test furnace*, a small specimen is placed at the focus and subjected to extreme thermal shock. For a *power furnace*, a heat-transfer fluid (molten salt, air, or water/steam) is circulated through the receiver to drive a thermodynamic cycle. ### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Component | Component | Why it is needed | |---|---| | **Two-axis sun tracking** | The sun's apparent position changes ±23.5° seasonally and ±180° daily; concentration only works when mirrors remain normal to sun rays. | | **High-reflectance mirrors** | Every reflection loses 5–15% of the energy. A system with N reflections has total reflectance $\rho^N$; the *Odeillo* design uses 2 reflections and total $\rho \approx 0.7$–$0.8$. | | **Cavity receiver** | A re-entrant cavity reduces convective and re-radiative losses by presenting a small effective aperture to the surroundings. | | **Attenuated atmosphere** (Odeillo) | Water vapor and dust strongly absorb/scatter infrared radiation; placing the furnace at high altitude in a dry climate (Pyrenees) maximizes beam quality. | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the **first three formulas** below — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on solar concentrators. | # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | |---|---|---| | 1 | $C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$ | Geometric concentration ratio (aperture area ÷ receiver area) | | 2 | $C_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s} \approx 46200$ | Maximum theoretical concentration (3-D, point focus) | | 3 | $T_{max}^4 = \dfrac{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}{\sigma}$ | **Radiative equilibrium temperature** (Stefan–Boltzmann balance) | | 4 | $\eta_{opt} = \rho_{avg}^N \cdot \tau_{atm} \cdot \gamma_{intercept}$ | Optical efficiency: mirror reflectance, atmospheric transmittance, intercept factor | | 5 | $q_{focus} = C \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt}$ | Peak flux density at the focal spot (W/m²) | | 6 | $P_{th} = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt}$ | Useful thermal power delivered to the receiver | | 7 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{\sigma T_r^4}{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}$ | Thermal efficiency of the receiver (when only radiative loss is considered) | | 8 | $I_b = 1.353 \cdot (0.7)^{AM^{0.678}}$ | Beam irradiance corrected for air mass $AM$ (Hottel model) | > **Where:** $A_a$ = aperture area (m²), $A_r$ = receiver area (m²), $\theta_s$ = half-angle subtended by sun (rad), $\rho_{avg}$ = average mirror reflectance, $N$ = number of reflections, $I_b$ = beam irradiance (W/m²), $T_r$ = receiver temperature (K), $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Materials Research:** Ultra-high-temperature testing of aerospace re-entry tiles, nuclear-reactor ceramics, and refractory metals — *no contamination* since there is no flame. - **Clean Metallurgy:** Reduction of metal oxides (e.g., ZnO, MgO) using solar-grade carbon to release CO instead of CO₂. - **Solar Chemistry:** Dissociation of water / CO₂ to produce solar fuels (hydrogen, syngas). - **Heliostat Field Calibration Studies:** Used as a benchmark facility for validating optical codes (e.g., Monte Carlo ray tracing of solar tower plants). - **Solar Hydrogen Production:** The famous 1 MW *Solar Hydrogen Demonstrator* at Julich, Germany (closed in 2015), used a solar furnace to drive thermochemical cycles. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Achievable Temperature at the Focus This is the single most important derivation for KTU. We perform an **energy balance** on the receiver, assuming the receiver is a small *black body* suspended in a cold sky and that all absorbed solar energy is re-radiated as thermal radiation. **Given:** - Aperture area: $A_a$ (m²) - Receiver area: $A_r$ (m²) - Beam irradiance on aperture: $I_b$ (W/m²) - Optical efficiency of the concentrator: $\eta_{opt}$ (dimensionless) - Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ **Step 1 — Solar power intercepted by the concentrator:** $$ P_{in} = I_b \cdot A_a $$ This is the total beam solar power captured by the mirror aperture. **Step 2 — Power delivered to the receiver after optical losses:** The concentrator loses a fraction $(1 - \eta_{opt})$ due to mirror reflectance, atmospheric attenuation, and imperfect ray intercept. Hence: $$ P_{abs} = \eta_{opt} \cdot P_{in} = \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b \cdot A_a $$ **Step 3 — Express incident flux on the receiver using the concentration ratio:** By definition, $C = A_a / A_r$, so $A_a = C \cdot A_r$. Substituting: $$ P_{abs} = \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b \cdot C \cdot A_r $$ Dividing both sides by $A_r$ gives the **flux density at the receiver surface**: $$ q_{focus} = \frac{P_{abs}}{A_r} = C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Stefan–Boltzmann law for re-radiation from the receiver:** The receiver, at absolute temperature $T_r$, radiates as a gray/black body: $$ P_{rad} = \sigma \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4 $$ **Step 5 — Steady-state energy balance: $P_{abs} = P_{rad}$:** $$ \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b \cdot C \cdot A_r = \sigma \cdot A_r \cdot T_r^4 $$ The $A_r$ terms cancel, yielding the **focal-spot radiative equilibrium temperature**: $$ \boxed{\,T_r^4 = \frac{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}{\sigma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_r = \left( \frac{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}{\sigma} \right)^{1/4}\,} $$ **Numerical sanity check (KTU-style):** Take $C = 2000$, $\eta_{opt} = 0.70$, $I_b = 900$ W/m². $$ T_r = \left( \frac{2000 \times 0.70 \times 900}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \right)^{1/4} = \left( 2.22 \times 10^{13} \right)^{1/4} \approx 2168 \text{ K} \approx 1895 \text{ °C} $$ This matches the operating temperature of the **Odeillo solar furnace** (≈ 3500 °C peak, ≈ 2500 °C routine). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio (Ries Limit) The sun is *not* a point source — it subtends a finite half-angle $\theta_s \approx 0.00465$ rad. A perfect lens/mirror can therefore never focus the sun's image to a point smaller than the sun's own disc would project through the optics. This sets a hard physical upper limit on $C$. **Step 1 — Geometric statement:** For an *ideal* 3-D concentrator (one with a 2-D focus, like a paraboloid), the maximum concentration is: $$ C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s} $$ **Step 2 — Insert $\theta_s = 0.00465$ rad:** $$ C_{max} = \frac{1}{(0.00465)^2} = \frac{1}{2.162 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 46241 $$ **Step 3 — Rounded KTU value:** $$ \boxed{\,C_{max} \approx 46\,200\,} $$ For a 2-D concentrator (trough, line focus) the limit relaxes to: $$ C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_s} \approx 215 $$ > [!NOTE] > This explains why *trough* collectors cannot exceed ≈ 200× concentration, while *dish* and *tower* systems can exceed 1000× — only the latter are suitable for solar furnace applications. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Efficiency of the Receiver The receiver cannot reach the *theoretical* maximum $T_{max}$ because it re-radiates. The **thermal efficiency** is the fraction of incoming solar energy actually stored as useful heat (rather than re-radiated): **Step 1 — Useful heat = $P_{abs} - P_{rad}$:** $$ P_{use} = \eta_{opt} I_b C A_r - \sigma A_r T_r^4 $$ **Step 2 — Divide by $P_{abs}$:** $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\sigma T_r^4}{\eta_{opt} \cdot I_b \cdot C} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $T_r^4$ from §3.1:** If the receiver is at *exactly* the equilibrium temperature, $T_r^4 = \eta_{opt} I_b C / \sigma$, so: $$ \eta_{th} = 1 - 1 = 0 $$ This is a **triviality**: at radiative equilibrium, all absorbed energy is re-radiated. To extract *useful* heat, we must operate the receiver *below* the equilibrium temperature by extracting a working fluid. The correct design relation is: $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\sigma T_r^4}{\eta_{opt} \cdot I_b \cdot C}\,} $$ This must be evaluated at the **operating** $T_r$, which is below the radiative limit. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Standard Numerical Pattern A typical KTU question gives $A_a$, $A_r$, $I_b$, and asks for $(T_r, P_{th}, \eta_{th})$. The matrix below is the solver's checklist: | Known | Compute | Formula | |---|---|---| | $A_a$, $A_r$ | $C$ | $C = A_a / A_r$ | | $C$, $I_b$, $\eta_{opt}$ | $T_{max}$ | $T_{max} = (C \eta_{opt} I_b / \sigma)^{1/4}$ | | $A_a$, $I_b$, $\eta_{opt}$ | $P_{th}$ | $P_{th} = A_a I_b \eta_{opt}$ | | $T_r$, $C$, $I_b$, $\eta_{opt}$ | $\eta_{th}$ | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \sigma T_r^4 / (C \eta_{opt} I_b)$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture — Direct-Focusing (Parabolic Dish) Solar Furnace ```mermaid flowchart LR Sun((Sun 5778K)) -->|Direct Beam| DishMirror[Paraboloidal Dish Mirror] DishMirror -->|Reflected Cone| FocusNode[Focal Point above Dish] FocusNode -->|Concentrated Flux| Receiver[Target or Cavity Receiver] Receiver -->|Heat Extracted| HTF[Heat Transfer Fluid Loop] HTF -->|Hot Fluid| Process[Industrial Process or Power Block] Tracker1[Two Axis Sun Tracker] -.controls.-> DishMirror Attenuator[Attenuator Shutter] -.modulates.-> FocusNode Receiver -->|Re radiated Loss| Sky((Cold Sky 3K)) ``` ### 4.2 System Architecture — Indirect-Focusing (Heliostat Field + Tower) Solar Furnace ```mermaid flowchart TB Sun((Sun 5778K)) -->|Beam Rays| Helio1[Heliostat 01] Sun -->|Beam Rays| Helio2[Heliostat 02] Sun -->|Beam Rays| Helio3[Heliostat 03] Sun -->|Beam Rays| HelioN[Heliostat NN] Helio1 -->|Reflected Ray 1| TowerTop Helio2 -->|Reflected Ray 2| TowerTop Helio3 -->|Reflected Ray 3| TowerTop HelioN -->|Reflected Ray N| TowerTop[Furnace Target on Tower] TowerTop -->|Concentrated Heat| Cavity[Cavity Receiver] Cavity -->|Steam or Molten Salt| Storage[Thermal Storage Tank] Storage -->|Hot Fluid| Turbine[Steam Turbine and Generator] CentralCtrl[Central Heliostat Controller] -.aims.-> Helio1 CentralCtrl -.aims.-> Helio2 CentralCtrl -.aims.-> Helio3 CentralCtrl -.aims.-> HelioN ``` ### 4.3 Optical Path Diagram — Two-Stage Spherical Concentrator (Odeillo-type) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Parallel Solar Rays] --> B[Primary Spherical Concentrator
63 Heliostats Total Area 2830 sqm] B --> C[Water Cooled Secondary
Concentrator at First Focus] C --> D[Final Focal Point
Inside Test Chamber] D --> E[Specimen Under Test] B -.shutter.-> F[Attenuator for Power Control] ``` ### 4.4 Energy-Flow Functional Block ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Q solar in I times Aa] -->|minus Reflection Loss| Q2[Q after Optics eta opt times Q1] Q2 -->|minus Re radiation| Q3[Q useful to Receiver] Q3 -->|minus Convection| Q4[Q to Working Fluid] Q4 -->|minus Cycle Losses| Q5[Q electrical out] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]** *Define a solar furnace. Mention any two industrial applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **solar furnace** is a high-concentration solar thermal system that focuses direct solar radiation onto a small receiver area to attain extremely high temperatures (typically 1500–3500 °C) using only sunlight as the energy input. **Two applications (1.5 marks each):** 1. **High-temperature materials testing** — exposure of aerospace ceramics, nuclear-fuel cladding, and refractory metals to extreme thermal conditions without flame-induced contamination. 2. **Solar metallurgy / solar chemistry** — carbothermic reduction of metal oxides (ZnO, MgO) and thermochemical splitting of H₂O / CO₂ for clean solar fuel production. > **Mark-split (KTU pattern):** Definition 1.0 mark · Application 1 1.0 mark · Application 2 1.0 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *What is meant by concentration ratio? State its theoretical maximum for a point-focus concentrator.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **concentration ratio (C)** is defined as the ratio of the geometric aperture area $A_a$ of the concentrator to the area of the receiver $A_r$ onto which solar energy is focused. $$ C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} $$ The **theoretical maximum** for a 3-D (point-focus) concentrator is set by the apparent angular radius of the sun ($\theta_s = 0.00465$ rad): $$ C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s} \approx 46\,200 $$ > **Mark-split (KTU pattern):** Definition + formula 1.5 marks · Numerical value + justification 1.5 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** Answer **any ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]** **(a)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, explain the working principle of a **parabolic-dish solar furnace**. State any two advantages of a direct-focusing system over a heliostat-tower system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A parabolic dish of aperture diameter **6 m** concentrates sunlight onto a circular receiver of diameter **30 cm**. The beam irradiance is **900 W/m²** and the optical efficiency of the dish is **0.75**. Calculate the **(i)** concentration ratio, **(ii)** peak flux density at the receiver, and **(iii)** radiative equilibrium temperature at the focal spot. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):** A parabolic-dish solar furnace uses a **single paraboloidal reflector** that is rotated about two axes to track the sun throughout the day (1 mark). Parallel solar rays striking the dish are reflected and converge at the **focal point located in front of the dish** (1 mark). The target/receiver is mounted at this focal point (0.5 mark), where the concentrated solar flux may exceed **1 MW/m²** (0.5 mark), producing temperatures of 2000–3000 °C (0.5 mark). *Block schematic:* **Sun → Dish Mirror → Focal Point → Receiver → Heat Exchanger / Specimen** (0.5 mark). **Two advantages of direct-focusing over heliostat-tower (2 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Higher achievable flux density** — the dish can deliver > 10 MW/m² at focus, while a tower furnace typically delivers ≈ 1–5 MW/m². 2. **Simpler optical path (single reflection)** — gives higher optical efficiency (no cumulative mirror losses) and lower capital cost per kW for small-scale units. --- **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):** **Given:** $d_a = 6$ m, $d_r = 0.30$ m, $I_b = 900$ W/m², $\eta_{opt} = 0.75$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. **(i) Concentration ratio (2 marks):** $$ A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} d_a^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (6)^2 = 28.27 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} d_r^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.30)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{28.27}{0.0707} = 400 $$ **(ii) Peak flux density (2 marks):** $$ q_{focus} = C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b = 400 \times 0.75 \times 900 = 2.7 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **(iii) Equilibrium temperature (3 marks):** $$ T_r^4 = \frac{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}{\sigma} = \frac{400 \times 0.75 \times 900}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} = 4.76 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4 $$ $$ T_r = (4.76 \times 10^{12})^{1/4} \approx 1476 \text{ K} \approx 1203 \text{ °C} $$ > **Final numerical answers:** $C = 400$ · $q_{focus} = 2.7 \times 10^5$ W/m² · $T_r \approx 1476$ K (≈ 1203 °C). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** Differentiate between a **direct-focusing solar furnace** and an **indirect (heliostat-tower) solar furnace**. State any two limitations of solar furnaces. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A heliostat field of total mirror area **2500 m²** is used to focus sunlight on a tower-mounted receiver of area **1.0 m²**. The system has **two reflections** (heliostat → secondary concentrator). Average mirror reflectance is **0.88**, atmospheric transmittance between heliostats and tower is **0.92**, and the beam irradiance is **950 W/m²**. Compute the **(i)** geometric concentration ratio, **(ii)** overall optical efficiency, **(iii)** useful thermal power delivered, and **(iv)** theoretical maximum temperature at the receiver. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):** | Parameter | Direct-Focusing (Dish) | Indirect (Heliostat-Tower) | |---|---|---| | Number of reflections | One (dish only) | Two or more (heliostats + optional secondary) | | Tracking | Whole dish rotates on 2 axes | Each heliostat tracks individually; tower is fixed | | Achievable C | up to ≈ 10 000 | 500 – 3000 (limited by heliostat image size) | | Typical scale | 5 – 100 kW thermal | 1 – 50 MW thermal | | Optical efficiency | High (0.7 – 0.85) | Moderate (0.5 – 0.7) due to multiple reflections | | Sample / target | Moving with the dish | Stationary (mounted on tower) — ideal for materials testing | (3 marks for the table; 1 mark per *meaningful* entry, capping at 3 marks.) **Two limitations of solar furnaces (2 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Direct-normal-irradiance (DNI) dependence** — output drops to zero under cloud cover; thermal storage or hybridization is mandatory for continuous operation. 2. **High capital cost and large land area** — heliostat fields require ≈ 5–10 m² of mirror per kW thermal, and precision sun-tracking drives up civil/electrical costs. (2 additional marks for clarity / neatness / examples.) --- **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):** **Given:** $A_a = 2500$ m², $A_r = 1.0$ m², $N = 2$, $\rho = 0.88$, $\tau_{atm} = 0.92$, $I_b = 950$ W/m². **(i) Geometric concentration ratio (1.5 marks):** $$ C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{2500}{1.0} = 2500 $$ **(ii) Overall optical efficiency (2 marks):** $$ \eta_{opt} = \rho^N \cdot \tau_{atm} = (0.88)^2 \times 0.92 = 0.7744 \times 0.92 \approx 0.712 $$ **(iii) Useful thermal power (1.5 marks):** $$ P_{th} = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt} = 2500 \times 950 \times 0.712 \approx 1.69 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 1.69 \text{ MW} $$ **(iv) Theoretical maximum temperature (2 marks):** $$ T_{max} = \left( \frac{C \cdot \eta_{opt} \cdot I_b}{\sigma} \right)^{1/4} = \left( \frac{2500 \times 0.712 \times 950}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \right)^{1/4} $$ $$ T_{max} = \left( \frac{1.69 \times 10^6}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \right)^{1/4} = (2.98 \times 10^{13})^{1/4} \approx 2340 \text{ K} \approx 2067 \text{ °C} $$ > **Final numerical answers:** $C = 2500$ · $\eta_{opt} = 0.712$ · $P_{th} \approx 1.69$ MW · $T_{max} \approx 2340$ K (≈ 2067 °C). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing aperture with receiver area:** Students often invert the ratio and write $C = A_r / A_a$. This is worth *zero* marks. Memorize: $C = A_a / A_r \geq 1$. > 2. **Forgetting the fourth root in the temperature formula:** Many students square or cube-root the expression, giving absurd temperatures (> 10⁶ K). Always double-check that your final $T$ is physically realistic (< 6000 K). > 3. **Mixing up 2-D vs 3-D concentration limits:** $C_{max} = 46\,200$ is for a *point focus*. For a *line focus* (trough) it is ≈ 215. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you quote 46 200 for a trough. > 4. **Not including optical efficiency in $T_{max}$:** A common error is to write $T = (C I_b / \sigma)^{1/4}$ without $\eta_{opt}$, which overstates the answer by 10–30%. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Solar furnace** = a high-concentration solar thermal device that delivers flux densities > 10⁶ W/m² to a small receiver. - **Two architectural types:** Direct (parabolic dish / spherical) and Indirect (heliostat + tower). - **Concentration ratio** $C = A_a / A_r$ governs everything; **$C_{max} \approx 46\,200$** (3-D) and **≈ 215** (2-D). - **Radiative equilibrium temperature:** $T_r = (C \, \eta_{opt} \, I_b / \sigma)^{1/4}$ — the *single most important* KTU formula. - **Optical efficiency** is multiplicative: $\eta_{opt} = \rho^N \cdot \tau_{atm} \cdot \gamma_{intercept}$. - **Useful thermal power:** $P_{th} = A_a \cdot I_b \cdot \eta_{opt}$ (W). - **Thermal efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \sigma T_r^4 / (C \, \eta_{opt} \, I_b)$ — measure of receiver performance. - **Applications:** Materials testing, solar metallurgy, hydrogen production, thermochemical research. - **Famous example:** **Odeillo Solar Furnace, France** — 1 MW thermal, 63 heliostats, peak temperature ≈ 3500 °C, located in the Pyrenees. - **Limitations:** DNI dependence, large land area, high capital cost, atmospheric attenuation. - **KTU favourite numerical pattern:** Given $A_a$, $A_r$, $I_b$, $\eta_{opt}$ → find $C$, $q_{focus}$, $T_r$, $P_{th}$. - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** must always be carried in SI units: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. - **Beam irradiance** uses the *direct normal* component, not global horizontal — many students confuse the two.

topics.title: Wind Energy - Classification of Wind Turbines and Power Performance Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy - Classification of Wind Turbines and Power Performance Curve
