<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wind Energy — Formal Definition
Wind energy is the **kinetic energy** possessed by air masses in motion due to differential solar heating of the Earth's surface. It is a form of **indirect solar energy** that can be converted into useful mechanical work or electrical energy using devices called **Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)** or **Wind Turbines**.

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

where $m$ is the mass of air (kg) and $v$ is the wind velocity (m/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wind energy is the **fastest-growing renewable energy segment** globally. As per KTU 2024 Module 2, the focus is on the **classification of wind turbines** and understanding the **power performance curve** that defines energy yield.

### 1.2 Wind Turbine — KTU Definition
A **Wind Turbine** is a rotary mechanical device that converts the kinetic energy of wind into rotational mechanical energy, which is then converted into electricity by a coupled **electrical generator**. The conversion process is governed by **aerodynamic principles** (lift and drag) acting on specially shaped blades.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constants to Memorize:**
> - Standard air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Power coefficient theoretical maximum: $C_{p,\max} = 0.593$ (**Betz Limit**)
> - Betz Limit percentage: **59.3 %**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sailboat vs The Paddle Wheel"
Imagine two boats crossing a river:
- A **sailboat** uses the *lift* generated by air flowing over a curved sail — this is exactly how a modern **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** blade works. The blade acts like an aircraft wing.
- A **paddle wheel** boat uses the *push* of water against flat paddles — this is how an old-style **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)** like the Savonius rotor works, using the drag force.

**Just as you would never use a paddle wheel for ocean crossing (too slow), engineers prefer HAWTs for high-efficiency power generation**, while VAWTs are reserved for niche low-wind, urban, or decorative applications.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024
- Direct 7-mark derivations on **Betz Limit** and **power in wind** appear every semester.
- Classification tables are frequently asked as **3-mark short notes**.
- The power curve is essential for **energy yield estimation**, which is the foundation of all wind farm feasibility studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Wind Turbines

Wind turbines are classified based on **five primary criteria** as per KTU 2024 syllabus.

#### A. Classification Based on Axis of Rotation

**(i) Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)**
- Axis of rotation is **parallel to the wind direction** (horizontal).
- Requires a **yaw mechanism** to keep it facing the wind.
- Examples: Modern 3-blade utility-scale turbines (e.g., Vestas V164, Siemens SG-14).
- **High efficiency** ($\eta$ up to 45–50 %), high wind capture, dominant in industry.

**(ii) Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)**
- Axis of rotation is **perpendicular to the wind direction** (vertical).
- **No yaw mechanism** required — accepts wind from any direction.
- Examples: **Darrieus** (lift-based, egg-beater shape) and **Savonius** (drag-based, S-shaped).
- Lower efficiency (15–25 %), used in low-wind urban applications.

#### B. Classification Based on Blade Action

| Type | Principle | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Lift-type** | Lift force > Drag force | Modern 3-blade HAWT, Darrieus |
| **Drag-type** | Drag force dominates | Savonius, traditional windmills |

#### C. Classification Based on Rotor Position

- **Upwind turbine:** Rotor is located **upwind** of the tower (most common, avoids tower shadow).
- **Downwind turbine:** Rotor is located **downwind** of the tower (allows passive yaw, but suffers tower shadow fatigue).

#### D. Classification Based on Number of Blades

- **Single-blade** — Cheapest, highest tip speed, noisy.
- **Two-blade** — Used in early prototypes (e.g., NASA Mod-0).
- **Three-blade** — **Industry standard**; optimum balance of cost, smoothness, and visual appeal.
- **Multi-blade** — Used in traditional water-pumping windmills.

#### E. Classification Based on Speed Control / Regulation

- **Pitch-controlled** — Blade pitch angle adjusted actively (active stall/pitch).
- **Stall-controlled** — Blade aerodynamically stalls at high wind (passive).
- **Active yaw control** — Nacelle rotates to face the wind.

### 2.2 Power in the Wind — The Foundation

Consider a column of air of cross-section $A$ moving with velocity $v$. The **mass flow rate** of air through the swept area is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

The **power available in the wind** (raw kinetic energy flux) is therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

This is the **most important equation in wind energy engineering** — power varies with the **cube of wind speed**, which is why a 10 % increase in wind speed yields a **33 % increase in available power**.

### 2.3 The Power Coefficient (Cp) and Betz Limit

A wind turbine **cannot extract 100 % of the wind's kinetic energy** — if it did, air would have to stop completely behind the rotor, blocking further inflow.

The fraction of wind power extracted is the **power coefficient**:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$$

The **maximum theoretical value of $C_p$** was derived by **Albert Betz in 1919**:

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} = 0.5926 \approx 0.593 \quad (59.3\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Betz Limit = 59.3 %** — No wind turbine, no matter how well designed, can ever exceed this efficiency. Real turbines achieve $C_p$ between 0.35 and 0.45.

### 2.4 Actual Mechanical Power Output

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

### 2.5 Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) — A Critical Design Parameter

The Tip Speed Ratio (TSR or $\lambda$) is the ratio of blade tip speed to free-stream wind speed:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 v}}$$

where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of rotor (rad/s)
- $R$ = rotor radius (m)
- $n$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $v$ = wind speed (m/s)

- **High-speed turbines** (HAWT, 3-blade): $\lambda = 6$ to $8$ (optimised for $C_p$).
- **Low-speed turbines** (multi-blade, Savonius): $\lambda = 1$ to $3$ (high starting torque).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy of air | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Energy calculation |
| 2 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass conservation |
| 3 | Power available in wind | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Site energy estimation |
| 4 | Turbine mechanical power | $P = C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Actual output |
| 5 | Power coefficient | $C_p = P_{turbine} / P_{wind}$ | Efficiency indicator |
| 6 | Betz limit | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 = 0.593$ | Theoretical maximum |
| 7 | Tip speed ratio | $\lambda = \omega R / v = \pi R n / (30 v)$ | Rotor design |
| 8 | Swept area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^2$ | Rotor sizing |
| 9 | Swept area (VAWT, Savonius) | $A = 2 R H$ (approx.) | VAWT sizing |
| 10 | Solidity | $\sigma = (B c) / (2 \pi R)$ | Blade count effect |
| 11 | Annual energy yield | $E = \sum P(t) \cdot \Delta t$ | Wind farm economics |
| 12 | Capacity factor | $C_{F} = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$ | Plant performance |

> **Note on absolute values:** Any $C_p$ values must satisfy $0 \le C_p \le 0.593$ (i.e. $\vert C_p \vert \le 16/27$).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Wind farm site selection** uses $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ to rank potential sites — even a small difference in average wind speed causes huge energy differences.
- **Blade designers** use $\lambda$ and $C_p$ curves (from Blade Element Momentum theory) to optimise airfoil shape and chord.
- **Energy auditors** use the Betz limit to flag over-claimed turbine efficiencies in vendor brochures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power in the Wind

**Given:** A stream tube of air of cross-sectional area $A$ approaches the rotor with free-stream velocity $v_1$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the rotor disc.**
The volume of air crossing the rotor per second is $A v_1$. Multiplying by density $\rho$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_1$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass of air.**

$$KE_{\text{per kg}} = \frac{1}{2} v_1^2$$

**Step 3 — Total power (kinetic energy flux) crossing the rotor disc per second.**

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times KE_{\text{per kg}} = \rho A v_1 \times \frac{1}{2} v_1^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *total* kinetic power in the wind; the turbine can only extract a fraction $C_p$ of this.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Betz Limit (Maximum Cp)

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass.**
Let $v_1$ = upstream wind speed, $v_2$ = downstream wind speed, $v$ = wind speed at the rotor disc.

For an incompressible fluid through a stream tube of varying area:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v = \rho A_2 v_2$$

Therefore, the **mass flow rate is constant**:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law (momentum theorem).**
The force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream:

$$F = \dot{m} (v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 3 — Express the power extracted by the rotor.**
Using $P = F \times v$ (force times velocity at the disc):

$$P = \rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4 — Express wind power available upstream.**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$$

**Step 5 — Define power coefficient $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2)}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3} = \frac{2 v^2 (v_1 - v_2)}{v_1^3}$$

**Step 6 — Use the velocity relation at the disc (actuator disc theory).**
By the energy-momentum balance, the velocity at the disc equals the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the induction factor 'a'.**
Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that:

$$v = v_1 (1 - a)$$

It can also be shown that:

$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into the $C_p$ equation.**

$$C_p = \frac{2 \left[v_1 (1-a)\right]^2 \left[v_1 - v_1(1-2a)\right]}{v_1^3}$$

**Step 9 — Simplify.**

$$C_p = \frac{2 v_1^3 (1-a)^2 (2a)}{v_1^3}$$

$$\boxed{C_p = 4 a (1-a)^2}$$

**Step 10 — Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$.**

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4 \left[ (1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) \right] = 0$$

$$4 (1-a)^2 - 8 a (1-a) = 0$$

$$4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 0$$

$$4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

This gives $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = 1/3$.

**Step 11 — Substitute the optimal induction factor $a = 1/3$ back into $C_p$.**

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{4}{9}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} = 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%}$$

This is the famous **Betz Limit**, derived by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Downstream Velocity at Betz Limit

Using $v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$ and $a = 1/3$:

$$v_2 = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{v_1}{3}$$

So at the Betz limit, the wind speed drops to **one-third of its original value** downstream.

**Velocity at rotor disc:**

$$v = v_1 (1 - a) = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{2 v_1}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The turbine cannot be more efficient than 59.3 % because extracting more energy would require the air to slow down further, eventually blocking the inflow — a phenomenon called **flow choking**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Wind Turbine Types

| Parameter | 3-Blade HAWT | 2-Blade HAWT | Darrieus VAWT | Savonius VAWT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Axis orientation | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical | Vertical |
| Operating principle | Lift | Lift | Lift | Drag |
| Tip speed ratio ($\lambda$) | 6–8 | 5–7 | 4–6 | 0.8–1.2 |
| Power coefficient ($C_p$) | 0.40–0.48 | 0.35–0.45 | 0.25–0.35 | 0.10–0.18 |
| Self-starting | No (needs boost) | No | No | **Yes** |
| Yaw mechanism | Required | Required | Not required | Not required |
| Wind direction sensitivity | High | High | Low | Very low |
| Wind shear tolerance | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Typical application | Utility-scale grid | R&D / small | Rooftop / urban | Water pumping |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A wind turbine with rotor diameter $D = 80$ m operates at a wind speed of $v = 12$ m/s. The air density is $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and the power coefficient is $C_p = 0.40$. Calculate the available wind power, the turbine output power, and the percentage of Betz limit achieved.

**Solution:**

Swept area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

Power in wind:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 12^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 = 5.319 \text{ MW}$$

Turbine output power:

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.319 = 2.128 \text{ MW}$$

Percentage of Betz limit:

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{C_p}{C_{p,\max}} \times 100 = \frac{0.40}{0.593} \times 100 = 67.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The turbine is operating at **67.5 % of the Betz limit** — a typical good performance for a modern utility-scale machine.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Wind Turbines

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wind Turbines] --> B[Axis of Rotation]
    A --> C[Blade Action]
    A --> D[Rotor Position]
    A --> E[Blade Count]
    A --> F[Speed Control]

    B --> B1[HAWT Horizontal Axis]
    B --> B2[VAWT Vertical Axis]

    B1 --> B1a[Upwind Type]
    B1 --> B1b[Downwind Type]
    B2 --> B2a[Darrieus Lift]
    B2 --> B2b[Savonius Drag]

    C --> C1[Lift Type Modern]
    C --> C2[Drag Type Traditional]

    D --> D1[Upwind of Tower]
    D --> D2[Downwind of Tower]

    E --> E1[Single Blade]
    E --> E2[Two Blade]
    E --> E3[Three Blade Industry Standard]
    E --> E4[Multi Blade]

    F --> F1[Pitch Controlled]
    F --> F2[Stall Controlled]
    F --> F3[Active Yaw]
```

### 4.2 Wind Turbine Power Performance Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Wind Speed v ms] --> B{Cut in Speed 3 to 4 ms}
    B -- Below --> Z1[No Power Output]
    B -- Above --> C[Rising Cp Region]
    C --> D[Rated Speed 12 to 14 ms]
    D -- Beyond --> E[Power Limited to Rated]
    E --> F{Cut out Speed 25 ms}
    F -- Beyond --> G[Turbine Shut Down Safety]

    subgraph REGION_1
    B
    C
    end
    subgraph REGION_2
    D
    E
    end
    subgraph REGION_3
    F
    G
    end
```

**Conceptual Power Curve Description (x-axis = wind speed, y-axis = power output):**

- **Region 0 (0 to $v_{cut-in}$):** Output = 0 kW. Insufficient torque to overcome friction.
- **Region 1 ($v_{cut-in}$ to $v_{rated}$):** Power rises with $v^3$ (cubic relationship).
- **Region 2 ($v_{rated}$ to $v_{cut-out}$):** Power held constant at $P_{rated}$ by pitch control.
- **Region 3 (above $v_{cut-out}$):** Turbine is **shut down** (feathered blades) to prevent structural damage.

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[Wind Resource] --> R[Rotor Blades Aerodynamic]
    R --> GB[Gearbox Speed Increaser]
    GB --> SH[Shaft High Speed]
    SH --> G[Generator Induction or PMSG]
    G --> PC[Power Converter AC to DC to AC]
    PC --> TR[Transformer Step Up]
    TR --> GRID[Grid or Load]
    G --> CTL[Controller Yaw and Pitch]
    CTL --> R
    CTL --> G
```

### 4.4 Aerodynamic Forces on a Wind Turbine Blade

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Relative Wind W] --> B[Resolved into Two Components]
    B --> C[Lift Force L Perpendicular to W]
    B --> D[Drag Force D Parallel to W]
    C --> E[Generates Torque on Rotor]
    D --> F[Resists Motion]
    E --> G[Useful Mechanical Power]
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Power Curve:** The area under the power curve, multiplied by 8760 hours, gives the **Annual Energy Production (AEP)** in kWh. This is the key metric in any KTU wind energy problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define the term 'Power Coefficient' in wind energy conversion. State its maximum theoretical value.**

**Model Answer:**
The **power coefficient ($C_p$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual mechanical power extracted by a wind turbine to the total kinetic power available in the wind:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

Its **maximum theoretical value** was derived by Albert Betz in 1919 and is known as the **Betz Limit**:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} = 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%$$

No wind turbine, regardless of design, can extract more than 59.3 % of the kinetic energy of the wind.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Distinguish between Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) in any four aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Aspect | HAWT | VAWT |
|------|--------|------|------|
| 1 | Axis orientation | Parallel to wind | Perpendicular to wind |
| 2 | Yaw mechanism | Required | Not required |
| 3 | Self-starting | Generally needs booster | Savonius type self-starts |
| 4 | Power coefficient | 0.40–0.48 (high) | 0.10–0.35 (lower) |
| 5 | Maintenance | Complex (on tall tower) | Easy (ground level) |
| 6 | Wind direction sensitivity | High | Omni-directional |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — Module 2, Expected Pattern]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the power available in the wind passing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $v$.  
**(b)** A wind turbine has rotor diameter 60 m, air density 1.225 kg/m³, wind speed 10 m/s, and power coefficient 0.45. Calculate the power output. Comment on how it compares with the Betz limit.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of Power in Wind — 7 marks**

Consider a stream tube of cross-sectional area $A$ with wind of density $\rho$ moving at velocity $v$.

*Mass flow rate:*

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

*Kinetic energy of 1 kg of moving air:*

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} v^2$$

*Power (rate of kinetic energy flow):*

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times KE = \rho A v \times \frac{1}{2} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$

This shows that **power in wind varies as the cube of wind velocity** — doubling wind speed increases available power **eight-fold**.

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks**

Given: $D = 60$ m, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $v = 10$ m/s, $C_p = 0.45$.

*Swept area:*

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 60^2}{4} = 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$

*Power in wind:*

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 10^3 = 173180 \text{ W} \approx 173.18 \text{ kW}$$

*Turbine output:*

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.45 \times 173.18 = 77.93 \text{ kW}$$

*Comparison with Betz limit:*

$$\% \text{ Betz} = \frac{0.45}{0.593} \times 100 = 75.9\%$$

The turbine is operating at **75.9 % of the theoretical maximum**, indicating good aerodynamic design.

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — Module 2, Internal Choice]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, classify wind turbines based on axis of rotation and explain the working principle of a Darrieus VAWT.  
**(b)** Derive the Betz limit starting from first principles. State the induction factor at which maximum $C_p$ occurs.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification and Darrieus VAWT — 7 marks**

Wind turbines are classified based on axis of rotation as:

1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT):** Axis parallel to wind direction. Modern 3-blade utility turbines. High $C_p$, requires yaw control.

2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT):** Axis perpendicular to wind direction. Two main types:
   - **Darrieus Rotor** (lift-based, troposkein / egg-beater shape)
   - **Savonius Rotor** (drag-based, S-shape)

**Working of Darrieus VAWT:**
The Darrieus rotor uses **airfoil-shaped curved blades** attached to a vertical shaft. As wind passes the blades, the asymmetric airfoil profile generates **lift force** perpendicular to relative wind, producing torque on the shaft.

- Advantages: Omni-directional, no yaw, ground-level generator.
- Disadvantages: **Not self-starting**, low starting torque, lower $C_p$ (~0.30).

**(b) Derivation of Betz Limit — 7 marks**

Using actuator disc theory:
- Upstream velocity: $v_1$, downstream: $v_2$, at disc: $v$
- By momentum: $F = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$
- Power extracted: $P = F \cdot v = \rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2)$
- Power in wind: $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$

Using induction factor $a$:

$$v = v_1(1-a), \quad v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$$

Substituting:

$$C_p = \frac{2 [v_1(1-a)]^2 [v_1 - v_1(1-2a)]}{v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

Maximising: $\dfrac{dC_p}{da} = 0 \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{1}{3}$

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \boxed{\frac{16}{27} = 0.5926}$$

**Induction factor for maximum $C_p$ is $a = 1/3$.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation):**
> 
> 1. **Betz Limit value:** Students write **0.59** or **59 %** — KTU strict marking awards full marks only for **16/27 or 0.593 or 59.3 %**. Always give the fraction.
> 
> 2. **Cubic power relation:** Forgetting the cube of velocity ($v^3$, not $v^2$) is the #1 calculation error.
> 
> 3. **Swept area formula:** Writing $A = \pi r$ (perimeter) instead of $A = \pi r^2$ (area) — common slip.
> 
> 4. **Induction factor 'a':** Many students forget to specify that **a = 1/3 at Betz limit** — a 2-mark loss.
> 
> 5. **HAWT vs VAWT sketches:** Without labelled diagrams, KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks. Always draw the rotor axis and indicate wind direction with an arrow.
> 
> 6. **Units mismatch:** Mixing kW with MW, or forgetting to convert m/s to m/s correctly. Final answer must carry **correct SI units**.
> 
> 7. **Power curve regions:** Students often miss Region 0 (cut-in) and Region 3 (cut-out). Mention all **four regions** for full credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Wind power formula:** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — *cube of velocity is the most critical point.*
- [x] **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at 15 °C, sea level.
- [x] **Betz Limit = 16/27 = 0.593 = 59.3 %** — must be memorised as a fraction.
- [x] **Induction factor at Betz:** $a = 1/3$; downstream velocity = $v_1/3$.
- [x] **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / v = \pi R n / 30v$.
- [x] **HAWT** has higher $C_p$ and is the industry standard; **VAWT** is omni-directional but less efficient.
- [x] **Power curve has 4 regions:** Below cut-in, ramp-up ($v^3$), rated plateau, cut-out shutdown.
- [x] **Typical cut-in ≈ 3–4 m/s, rated ≈ 12–14 m/s, cut-out ≈ 25 m/s** (memorise ranges).
- [x] **Three-blade rotor** is the industry standard; **multi-blade** for water-pumping.
- [x] **Solidity** $\sigma = Bc / 2\pi R$ — high solidity → high starting torque, low TSR.
- [x] **Capacity factor** of a good wind farm ≈ 25–40 % — important for energy yield problems.
- [x] **Pitch control** = active; **Stall control** = passive; both used to limit power at rated wind speed.
- [x] **Always include labelled diagram** for classification questions (KTU marking).
- [x] **Betz can never be exceeded** — flag any vendor claim of $> 59.3$ % efficiency as physically impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain power curve," always draw it and label **all 4 regions** (cut-in, cubic rise, rated plateau, cut-out) and mention typical values (3 m/s, 12 m/s, 25 m/s). Examiners allocate 1 mark for the figure itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy in Wind
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energy in Wind ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy in Wind** refers to the **kinetic energy** possessed by moving air masses (wind) due to their mass and velocity, which can be converted into useful mechanical or electrical work using wind turbines. The **extractable power** from wind is fundamentally derived from the rate of change of kinetic energy of an air stream passing through a swept area of the turbine rotor. The total **kinetic energy (KE)** of a moving air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $V$ is expressed as: $$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}$$ For wind energy applications, the quantity of interest is the **power** (rate of energy flow) in the wind, not just the static energy contained in a parcel. This distinction is critical because wind energy conversion is a **continuous flow process** rather than a stored energy extraction process. ### 1.2 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Air Density at Standard Conditions (sea level, 15°C):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ - **Air Density Variation Range:** $\rho \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.4 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ (depending on altitude, temperature, pressure) - **Power Coefficient Upper Bound (Betz Limit):** $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (dimensionless, **Betz Coefficient**) - **Standard Swept Area Reference:** for a rotor of diameter $D$, $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \text{ m}^{2}$ - **Wind Power Density:** measured in $\text{W/m}^{2}$ — the power available per unit swept area ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The "River of Air" Analogy:** > Imagine wind not as "air blowing," but as a **horizontal river of air flowing over the landscape** at a certain speed. The faster the river flows, and the larger the "dipper" (turbine blades) you place in it, the more water (energy) you can scoop out per second. Breaking this down into three real-world physical insights: 1. **Doubling wind speed gives 8× the power.** A wind blowing at 10 m/s carries $2^{3} = 8$ times more power than one at 5 m/s. Wind energy is therefore **highly sensitive to velocity** — small changes in wind speed produce dramatic changes in available energy. 2. **Doubling the rotor diameter quadruples the swept area.** Since the blades sweep a circular disk, area scales with $D^{2}$. Larger rotors capture vastly more energy, but at the cost of higher structural and material expenses. 3. **You cannot extract ALL the wind's energy.** A turbine cannot remove 100% of the wind's kinetic energy because the air must keep flowing (otherwise it would pile up behind the rotor). The **maximum theoretical extraction is 59.3%** (Betz limit), and real machines achieve **30% to 45%** typically. ### 1.4 Why "Energy in Wind" Matters in Engineering The study of energy in wind is the **foundational principle** underlying all wind power engineering: - It enables **sizing of wind turbines** for specific site wind regimes. - It allows estimation of **annual energy yield (AEO)** for project economics. - It is the basis for **wind resource assessment** and **power curve generation**. - It is indispensable for **micro-siting** and **capacity factor** calculations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow The energy-in-wind derivation proceeds by considering a **control volume** — a cylindrical tube of air flowing through the rotor swept area. **Step 1: Mass Flow Rate Identification** - The mass of air passing through a cross-section $A$ per unit time is the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$. - $\dot{m} = \rho A V$, where $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area, $V$ = wind velocity. **Step 2: Kinetic Energy Per Unit Mass** - Each kilogram of moving air carries energy $\frac{1}{2} V^{2}$. **Step 3: Power = Energy Flow Rate** - Multiplying energy per unit mass by the mass flow rate gives power: $P = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ **Step 4: Extractable Power (Practical View)** - A real turbine extracts a fraction of this, governed by the **power coefficient** $C_p$: $P_{\text{actual}} = C_{p} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ **Step 5: Integration Over Time for Energy** - Energy is **power integrated over time**: $E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \int_{0}^{T} V(t)^{3} \, dt$ **Step 6: Statistical Estimation Using Wind Speed Distribution** - Using the **Weibull probability density function** $f(V) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{V}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(V/c)^{k}}$: $\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A T \int_{0}^{\infty} V^{3} f(V) \, dV$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is high-yield — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 question.** | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Kinetic Energy of air parcel | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}$ | J (Joules) | Mass-based static energy | | 2 | Mass flow rate through rotor | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | kg/s | $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area | | 3 | **Power in Wind (Fundamental Equation)** | $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | W (Watts) | **Most critical KTU equation** | | 4 | Swept Area of Rotor | $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² | $D$ = rotor diameter | | 5 | Power Extracted (with $C_p$) | $P = C_{p} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | W | $C_p \leq \frac{16}{27}$ (Betz) | | 6 | Betz Limit Coefficient | $C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | dimensionless | Theoretical maximum | | 7 | Maximum Theoretical Power | $P_{\text{Betz}} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V^{3}$ | W | Plug $C_p = 16/27$ into (5) | | 8 | Wind Power Density | $P/A = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^{3}$ | W/m² | Power per unit swept area | | 9 | Specific Energy (per unit area per year) | $E_{\text{spec}} = \int P/A \, dt$ | Wh/m²/year | For site assessment | | 10 | Annual Energy Output (AEO) | $AEO = 8760 \cdot CF \cdot P_{\text{rated}}$ | kWh/year | $CF$ = Capacity Factor | | 11 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \frac{AEO}{8760 \cdot P_{\text{rated}}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.20 to 0.45 | | 12 | Tip-Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 V}$ | dimensionless | $N$ in rpm, $R$ in m | | 13 | Force on Blades (Axial Thrust) | $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{2} \cdot 2a(1-a)$ | N | Momentum theory, $a$ = axial induction | | 14 | Power Coefficient (BEM/Rotor Disk) | $C_p = 4a(1-a)^{2}$ | dimensionless | Simplified single-streamtube | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The energy-in-wind concept is the **backbone of several engineering applications**: - **Wind Farm Energy Yield Forecasting:** Operators use $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ coupled with Weibull wind distributions to predict AEO before construction. - **Turbine Design and Blade Sizing:** Engineers balance $A$ (capital cost) against $V^{3}$ (site wind speed) to optimize Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE). - **Grid Integration and Dispatch Planning:** The cubic dependence on $V$ means output is highly intermittent, requiring storage or backup. - **Wind Resource Mapping:** NREL and KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) use wind power density at hub height to classify sites (Class 1 to Class 7). - **Offshore Wind Assessment:** Higher $\rho$ at sea level (cooler air) gives 5–10% more power than comparable onshore sites. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Power in the Wind **Starting Point:** Consider a wind stream of velocity $V$ passing normally through a circular area $A$ swept by a wind turbine rotor. **Step 1: Mass of air crossing the swept area per second** In one second, a column of air of length equal to the wind velocity $V$ (in meters) passes through the area $A$. $$\text{Volume per second} = A \cdot V \quad (\text{m}^{3}/\text{s})$$ Since air has density $\rho$ (kg/m³), the **mass flow rate** is: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V \quad (\text{kg}/\text{s})$$ **Conversion Logic:** The mass flow rate is density (kg per m³) multiplied by volumetric flow rate (m³ per second), giving kg per second — a fundamental unit-consistency check. **Step 2: Kinetic energy of this mass of air** The kinetic energy associated with a mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is: $$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}$$ **Step 3: Kinetic energy per second (= Power)** Since $\dot{m}$ kilograms of air pass per second, and each kilogram has $\frac{1}{2} V^{2}$ joules of kinetic energy, the **energy flow per second** is: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} V^{2}$$ Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A V$: $$P_{\text{wind}} = (\rho A V) \cdot \frac{1}{2} V^{2}$$ Simplifying the exponent of $V$: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$ **This is the fundamental wind power equation.** ### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Available Over a Time Period To obtain the **total energy** in the wind over a time interval $T$ (e.g., one hour, one day, or one year), we integrate power with respect to time. **Step 1: Write instantaneous power at any time $t$** $$P(t) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V(t)^{3}$$ **Step 2: Integrate over the time interval $[0, T]$** $$E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1}{2} \rho A V(t)^{3} \, dt$$ Pulling out constants: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \int_{0}^{T} V(t)^{3} \, dt$$ **Step 3: Express in terms of average wind speed (cubed) — only valid for constant $V$** If $V$ is constant over the period, then $V(t) = V$ and: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} T$$ For example, with $T = 8760$ hours per year: $$E_{\text{year}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \cdot 8760 \quad \text{(in Wh, if } V \text{ in m/s, } A \text{ in m}^{2}\text{)}$$ **Step 4: Accounting for variable wind speeds using Weibull statistics** In reality, $V$ fluctuates continuously. The expected annual energy uses the Weibull distribution $f(V)$: $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \cdot T \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} V^{3} f(V) \, dV$$ The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} V^{3} f(V) dV = c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$, where $c$ is the Weibull scale parameter, $k$ is the shape parameter, and $\Gamma$ is the gamma function. Thus: $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A T c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$ **Conversion Logic:** The factor $c^{3}\Gamma(1+3/k)$ is the **mean cubed wind speed** $\langle V^{3}\rangle$, which is greater than $\langle V\rangle^{3}$ by Jensen's inequality — this physically reflects that energy production is dominated by high-wind-speed hours. ### 3.3 Derivation: Force on Rotor (Axial Thrust from Momentum Theory) **Step 1: Apply conservation of momentum to an air streamtube** Let $V$ be the upstream wind speed, $V_1$ the speed at the rotor disk, and $V_2$ the downstream wake speed. Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that $V_1 = V(1-a)$. For an ideal actuator disk (no rotational extraction loss): $V_1 = \frac{V + V_2}{2}$, giving $V_2 = V(1-2a)$. **Step 2: Mass flow rate through the rotor** $$\dot{m} = \rho A V_1 = \rho A V(1-a)$$ **Step 3: Thrust force equals rate of change of momentum** $$F = \dot{m}(V - V_2) = \rho A V(1-a) \cdot [V - V(1-2a)]$$ $$F = \rho A V(1-a) \cdot 2aV$$ $$F = 2 \rho A V^{2} a(1-a)$$ **Step 4: Power extracted equals thrust times velocity at the disk** $$P = F \cdot V_1 = 2\rho A V^{2} a(1-a) \cdot V(1-a)$$ $$P = 2 \rho A V^{3} a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 5: Define power coefficient** Comparing with $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} C_p$: $$C_p = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 6: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$** $$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4[(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ This gives $a = 1/3$ (non-trivial solution; $a=1$ gives zero power). **Step 7: Substitute $a = 1/3$** $$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ This is the **Betz Limit** — the maximum fraction of wind power that can ever be captured by an ideal turbine. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. At a hub-height wind speed of 12 m/s, the air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (a) the swept area, (b) the power in the wind, (c) the maximum extractable power (Betz), and (d) the actual power output assuming $C_p = 0.40$. **Solution:** **(a) Swept Area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.5 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **(b) Power in the Wind:** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.5 \cdot (12)^{3}$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.6125 \cdot 5026.5 \cdot 1728$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5{,}321{,}200 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$ **(c) Maximum Extractable Power (Betz Limit):** $$P_{\text{Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.593 \cdot 5.32 \text{ MW} \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$ **(d) Actual Power with $C_p = 0.40$:** $$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.40 \cdot 5.32 \text{ MW} = 2.13 \text{ MW}$$ **Annual Energy Estimate (assuming 8760 h at this speed — physically unrealistic but for calculation):** $$E = 2.13 \text{ MW} \cdot 8760 \text{ h} \approx 18{,}650 \text{ MWh/year}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Wind Turbine System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Wind Stream
Velocity V m per s] --> B[Air Mass Crossing
Rotor Plane] B --> C[Swept Area A
pi D squared over 4] A --> D[Kinetic Energy
per kg equals half V squared] B --> E[Mass Flow Rate
m dot equals rho A V] D --> F[Total Power in Wind
P equals half rho A V cubed] E --> F F --> G[Betz Limit Applied
Cp max equals 16 over 27] G --> H[Extractable Power
P equals Cp times P wind] H --> I[Rotor Blades
Convert KE to Rotation] I --> J[Gearbox
Steps up RPM] J --> K[Generator
Mechanical to Electrical] K --> L[Transformer
Voltage Step Up] L --> M[Grid Connection
Power Export] H --> N[Energy Losses
Mechanical, Electrical, Aerodynamic] N --> O[Net Useful Output
P useful] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wind Power Conversion ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputStage [Energy Input Stage] I1[Atmospheric Kinetic Energy] I2[Air Density rho] I3[Wind Velocity V] end subgraph ConversionStage [Power Conversion Stage] C1[Swept Area A] C2[Power in Wind P wind] C3[Power Coefficient Cp] C4[Useful Power P useful] end subgraph OutputStage [Energy Output Stage] O1[Annual Energy Yield] O2[Capacity Factor CF] O3[Grid Delivered kWh] end I1 --> C2 I2 --> C2 I3 --> C2 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C4 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Control Volume and Force Balance on a Rotor Disk ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Upstream Wind V] --> B[Rotor Disk
Swept Area A] B --> C[Axial Induction
Factor a] C --> D[Rotor Plane Velocity
V1 equals V times 1 minus a] B --> E[Thrust Force
F equals 2 rho A V squared a times 1 minus a] E --> F[Power Extracted
P equals 2 rho A V cubed a times 1 minus a squared] F --> G[Power Coefficient
Cp equals 4 a times 1 minus a squared] G --> H[Maximum Cp
equals 16 over 27
at a equals 1 third] B --> I[Downstream Wake
V2 equals V times 1 minus 2a] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Power in Wind" and state the expression for the same.** **Model Answer:** The "power in wind" is defined as the rate at which kinetic energy of a moving air stream crosses a unit area perpendicular to the wind direction. The mathematical expression for the power available in the wind is: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$ where $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³), $A$ = swept area of the rotor (m²), and $V$ = wind velocity (m/s). The unit is Watts (W). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State the Betz Limit and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** The **Betz Limit** is the maximum theoretical fraction of the kinetic energy in wind that can be captured by an ideal wind turbine. It is given by: $$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ (or 59.3\%)}$$ **Physical Significance:** - It represents the **theoretical upper bound** of aerodynamic energy extraction. - It arises because if 100% of the wind's energy were extracted, the air would stop completely behind the rotor, blocking further inflow — hence extraction cannot be 100%. - Real wind turbines typically achieve $C_p$ values between 0.35 and 0.45 due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a) Derive the expression for the power available in the wind. Also state the factors affecting the power in the wind.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Consider a wind stream of velocity $V$ passing through a circular swept area $A$ of a wind turbine. The volume of air crossing this area per second is $A \cdot V$. Multiplying by air density $\rho$: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V \quad (\text{kg/s})$$ The kinetic energy per unit mass of air is $\frac{1}{2}V^{2}$. Therefore, the kinetic energy per second (which is power) is: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$ **Factors Affecting Power in the Wind:** 1. **Air Density ($\rho$):** Higher density (cool, sea-level air) gives more power. 2. **Swept Area ($A$):** Larger area captures more wind, $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$. 3. **Wind Velocity ($V$):** Power is proportional to $V^{3}$ — the most dominant factor. --- **(b) A wind turbine with rotor diameter of 60 m operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s. The air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate the power in the wind, the maximum power extractable (Betz limit), and the actual power output if $C_p = 0.35$.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Swept Area** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^{2}}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Power in the Wind** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot (10)^{3}$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.6 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot 1000 = 1{,}696{,}464 \text{ W} \approx 1.70 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 3: Maximum Power (Betz Limit)** $$P_{\text{Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.593 \cdot 1.70 \text{ MW} \approx 1.006 \text{ MW} \approx 1.01 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 4: Actual Power Output ($C_p = 0.35$)** $$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.35 \cdot 1.70 \text{ MW} = 0.594 \text{ MW} \approx 594 \text{ kW}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a) Explain the concept of energy in wind. Derive an expression for the total energy in the wind blowing over a time period T, assuming variable wind speeds using the Weibull distribution.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The energy in wind is the kinetic energy of moving air parcels, arising from atmospheric circulation driven by solar heating differentials. For a continuous flow through a turbine: **Power at any instant:** $$P(t) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V(t)^{3}$$ **Total energy over time T:** $$E = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \int_{0}^{T} V(t)^{3} \, dt$$ **Using Weibull Distribution:** The Weibull PDF gives the probability of wind speed $V$ occurring: $$f(V) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{V}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(V/c)^{k}}$$ The expected (mean) energy over time $T$ is: $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A T \int_{0}^{\infty} V^{3} f(V) \, dV$$ Using the standard integral identity $\int_{0}^{\infty} V^{n} f(V) dV = c^{n}\Gamma(1 + n/k)$: $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A T \cdot c^{3} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$ where $c$ = Weibull scale parameter, $k$ = shape parameter, $\Gamma$ = gamma function. --- **(b) The wind at a site follows a Weibull distribution with $c = 8$ m/s and $k = 2$. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³. A turbine with rotor diameter 70 m and $C_p = 0.40$ is installed. Estimate the annual energy output (kWh/year).** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Swept Area** $$A = \frac{\pi (70)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 4900}{4} = 1225\pi \approx 3848.5 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Compute $\Gamma(1 + 3/k)$ for $k = 2$** $$\Gamma(1 + 3/2) = \Gamma(2.5)$$ Using $\Gamma(2.5) = 1.5 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 1.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \Gamma(0.5) = 1.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{\pi} \approx 1.3293$ **Step 3: Mean Cubed Wind Speed** $$\langle V^{3}\rangle = c^{3} \Gamma(1+3/k) = (8)^{3} \cdot 1.3293 = 512 \cdot 1.3293 \approx 680.6 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{3}$$ **Step 4: Power in Wind (mean)** $$P_{\text{wind, mean}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 3848.5 \cdot 680.6 \approx 1{,}604{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 1.604 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 5: Actual Power Output** $$P_{\text{actual}} = 0.40 \cdot 1.604 \text{ MW} = 0.6416 \text{ MW} = 641.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6: Annual Energy Output (T = 8760 hours)** $$AEO = 641.6 \text{ kW} \cdot 8760 \text{ h} \approx 5{,}620{,}416 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 5.62 \text{ GWh/year}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to cube the wind speed** — the most common error. $V$ must be raised to the **third power** in the wind power equation. > 2. **Using radius instead of diameter** in the swept area formula: $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$, not $\pi r^{2}$ (though equal — students sometimes confuse the forms). > 3. **Forgetting to apply $C_p$** in actual output questions — $P_{\text{wind}}$ is the raw available power, but $P_{\text{actual}} = C_p \cdot P_{\text{wind}}$. > 4. **Unit errors:** Mixing W, kW, MW — keep consistent. $1 \text{ MW} = 1000 \text{ kW} = 10^{6} \text{ W}$. > 5. **Betz Limit misapplication:** The Betz limit applies to $C_p$ (coefficient), not directly to power. Always write $P_{\text{Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{\text{wind}}$. > 6. **Annual hours:** Use $T = 8760$ hours for one year. Do not use 365 days directly without converting to hours. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Energy in Wind** - ✅ **The cornerstone equation** is $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ — memorize it cold. - ✅ **Power is proportional to $V^{3}$** — doubling wind speed gives 8× more power. - ✅ **Power is proportional to $A$** — doubling rotor diameter gives 4× more area, hence 4× more power (at constant wind speed). - ✅ **Swept area** $A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ for a horizontal-axis turbine. - ✅ **Air density at standard conditions:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. - ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ — no turbine can exceed this. - ✅ **Maximum theoretical power** $P_{\text{Betz}} = \frac{8}{27}\rho A V^{3}$. - ✅ **Real turbine $C_p$ range:** 0.30 to 0.45 typically; modern multi-MW turbines reach 0.48–0.50. - ✅ **Actual power output:** $P_{\text{actual}} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$. - ✅ **Energy in wind over time:** $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho A \int V^{3}\,dt$; for constant $V$, $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} T$. - ✅ **Weibull energy formulation:** $E = \frac{1}{2}\rho A T c^{3}\Gamma(1+3/k)$. - ✅ **Capacity Factor:** $CF = \frac{AEO}{8760 \cdot P_{\text{rated}}}$; typical range 0.20–0.45. - ✅ **Tip-Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$ — optimal $\lambda$ depends on blade design (3–5 for stall-regulated, 6–8 for pitch-regulated). - ✅ **Axial induction factor:** $a = 1/3$ gives maximum $C_p$ (Betz condition). - ✅ **Annual hours convention:** 1 year = 8760 hours (non-leap year). - ✅ **Power density concept:** $P/A = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{3}$ in W/m² — used for site classification. - ✅ **Cut-in, rated, cut-out speeds:** Typical 3 m/s, 12–14 m/s, 25 m/s — no power below cut-in, rated between rated and cut-out, zero above cut-out. - ✅ **Why cubic dependence matters:** Small site selection errors can compound into massive energy output differences — careful wind resource assessment is critical.

topics.title: Calculation of Energy Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Energy Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Renewable Energy Engineering**, the **Energy Content** (also called **Calorific Value** or **Heating Value**) of a fuel or energy resource is defined as the amount of thermal energy liberated per unit mass (solid/liquid fuels) or per unit volume (gaseous fuels) when the fuel undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, with the products of combustion being cooled back to the initial temperature of the reactants.

Formally, for KTU Board examinations, the term is expressed as:

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Energy Content (E):** The total amount of usable heat released during the complete combustion of a unit quantity of fuel under isothermal and isobaric (steady-flow) conditions, typically measured in **kJ/kg** (solids/liquids) or **kJ/m³** (gases at NTP).

The two principal variants are:

| Variant | Symbol | Definition | Combustion Product Water State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Higher Heating Value** | **HHV** (or GCV – Gross Calorific Value) | Heat released assuming all water vapour in products condenses to liquid, releasing latent heat | **Liquid** |
| **Lower Heating Value** | **LHV** (or NCV – Net Calorific Value) | Heat released assuming water leaves as vapour (latent heat NOT recovered) | **Vapour** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a slice of firewood and you want to know "how much hot shower water can this wood heat up?"

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** is like saying: *"If I capture absolutely every drop of heat — including the hidden heat inside the steam rising from the flames (latent heat of condensation) — how much energy is there?"*
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** is like saying: *"If I let the steam escape uncondensed (like in a car engine or furnace chimney) — how much usable heat is there?"*

The difference between HHV and LHV is precisely the **latent heat carried away by water vapour** produced from the fuel's hydrogen content and any moisture in the fuel.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Key KTU Distinction:** For **boilers and heating systems** → use **HHV** (water condenses, all heat recovered). For **IC engines, gas turbines, and most practical renewable energy conversion devices** → use **LHV** (exhaust gases are hot, water remains as vapour).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used

- Latent heat of vaporisation of water at **25 °C (298 K)**: **h_fg = 2442 kJ/kg** (often taken as 2,260 kJ/kg at 100 °C)
- Universal gas constant: **R_u = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- Standard temperature & pressure (STP/NTP): **T = 273.15 K / 293.15 K**, **P = 101.325 kPa**
- Conversion: **1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ**
- 1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe) = **41.868 GJ**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How Energy Content is Determined

The energy content of a fuel can be determined through **three** primary routes, each carrying a different KTU weightage:

**Route A — Direct Experimental Measurement (Calorimetry)**
- A known mass of fuel is burned inside a **Bomb Calorimeter** (constant-volume) or a **Junker's Calorimeter** (continuous-flow, for gases).
- The temperature rise of a known mass of water (or air) is recorded, and the heat released is computed from:
$$Q = m_w \, c_w \, \Delta T$$
- This gives the **HHV** directly (water condenses inside the bomb).
- LHV is then obtained by subtracting the latent heat of water formed.

**Route B — Empirical Formulae from Ultimate Analysis (C, H, O, N, S, Ash)**
- Used when laboratory calorimetry is unavailable. Empirical correlations link elemental composition to HHV.
- The most cited formula is **Dulong's Formula** (for solid fossil fuels):
$$HHV \; (kJ/kg) = 33800\,C + 144200\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S$$
- For **biomass**, the **Channiwala & Parikh (2002)** correlation is KTU-recommended (more accurate for low-rank fuels):
$$HHV \; (MJ/kg) = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,Ash$$
- All elemental fractions (C, H, O, N, S, Ash) must be expressed on a **dry, ash-free (DAF)** or **dry basis (db)** as specified in the problem.

**Route C — Summation Method for Gaseous Fuels (Biogas, Syngas, Natural Gas)**
- The energy content of a gas mixture is the **volume-fraction-weighted sum** of the calorific values of its combustible constituents:
$$HHV_{mix} = \sum_{i} y_i \cdot HHV_i$$
$$LHV_{mix} = \sum_{i} y_i \cdot LHV_i$$
where $y_i$ is the mole/volume fraction of component $i$ (CH₄, CO, H₂, etc.).

### 2.2 Conversion Between HHV and LHV

The bridge relation used in nearly every KTU numerical:

$$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left( \frac{9H}{100} + M \right)$$

where:
- $H$ = mass fraction of hydrogen in fuel (kg H per kg fuel, on the same basis as HHV)
- $M$ = moisture content of fuel (kg water per kg fuel)
- $9/100$ = stoichiometric mass of water produced per kg of hydrogen burned (since $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation ≈ **2442 kJ/kg** (at 25 °C reference)

### 2.3 Energy Density and Volumetric Energy Content

For transport and storage applications (biofuels, hydrogen, biogas), the **volumetric energy content** is equally important:

$$E_v = \rho \cdot HHV \quad \text{(MJ/m}^3\text{)}$$

For gases at STP, since 1 kmol occupies 22.414 m³:

$$E_v \;(MJ/m^3) = \frac{MW \cdot HHV_{mass}}{22.414}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for any numerical on this topic in the KTU exam.

| # | Formula / Relation | Applicable Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m_w c_w \Delta T$ | Bomb calorimeter energy balance | kJ |
| 2 | Dulong: $HHV = 33800C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9270S$ | Coal, solid fossil fuels | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Channiwala-Parikh: $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211Ash$ | Biomass (KTU preferred) | MJ/kg |
| 4 | $LHV = HHV - 2442 \cdot (9H/100 + M)$ | All H/C/O containing fuels | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $HHV_{mix} = \sum y_i HHV_i$ | Gaseous fuels (biogas, syngas) | kJ/m³ |
| 6 | $E_v = \rho_{gas} \cdot HHV_{mix} = \frac{P \cdot MW}{R_u T} \cdot HHV_{mix}$ | Gaseous fuels at NTP | MJ/m³ |
| 7 | $HHV_{CH_4} = 55.50$ MJ/kg; $LHV_{CH_4} = 50.02$ MJ/kg | Methane reference values | MJ/kg |
| 8 | $HHV_{H_2} = 141.80$ MJ/kg; $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg | Hydrogen reference values | MJ/kg |
| 9 | $HHV_{CO} = 10.10$ MJ/m³ (at STP) | Carbon monoxide in syngas | MJ/m³ |
| 10 | $LHV_{CO} = 12.64$ MJ/kg | CO reference | MJ/kg |
| 11 | Stoichiometric O₂ for CH₄: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | Combustion chemistry | – |
| 12 | Air-Fuel ratio (mass): $A/F = \frac{(32/CH_4) \cdot 4.76 \cdot 29}{16} \approx 17.2$ | Combustion engineering | kg/kg |
| 13 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{m_{fuel} \cdot HHV} \times 100\%$ | System-level efficiency | % |
| 14 | Energy per unit mass of biogas: $E = y_{CH_4} \cdot HHV_{CH_4}$ | Quick biogas problems | MJ/m³ |

> **Important Note on Pipe Symbols:** In the formulas above, all absolute-value / division separators have been written using the explicit form (e.g., `O/8`, `9H/100`) to preserve markdown table integrity. The `vert` or `mid` convention may be used for ambiguous cases.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomass Power Plants:** Energy content of crop residues (rice husk, bagasse) determines boiler sizing and electricity yield (typically 14–17 MJ/kg).
- **Biogas Design:** Methane fraction (CH₄) directly drives digester economics — every 1% rise in CH₄ ≈ +0.36 MJ/m³ of energy content.
- **Hydrogen Economy:** Volumetric energy density is the key bottleneck for hydrogen storage (≈ 10.8 MJ/m³ gas vs. 36,000 MJ/m³ for petrol).
- **Waste-to-Energy:** Mixed municipal solid waste has HHV ≈ 8–12 MJ/kg — used to size incinerators.
- **Bioethanol/Gasoline Comparison:** Ethanol LHV (26.8 MJ/kg) < Gasoline LHV (43.4 MJ/kg) — explains flex-fuel engine derating.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Dulong's Formula Application (Solid Fuel)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, by mass):
C = 68%, H = 5%, O = 12%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.8%, Ash = 12.7%.
Calculate its HHV using Dulong's formula and LHV assuming no surface moisture.

**Step 1 — Verify Mass Closure**

$$68 + 5 + 12 + 1.5 + 0.8 + 12.7 = 100\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dulong's Formula (HHV in kJ/kg)**

Each elemental mass fraction (in % = kg per 100 kg fuel) is substituted directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 33800 \cdot C + 144200 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270 \cdot S \\[6pt]
&= 33800 \times 68 + 144200 \times \left(5 - \frac{12}{8}\right) + 9270 \times 0.8 \\[6pt]
&= 2298400 + 144200 \times (5 - 1.5) + 7416 \\[6pt]
&= 2298400 + 144200 \times 3.5 + 7416 \\[6pt]
&= 2298400 + 504700 + 7416 \\[6pt]
&= 2810516 \;\text{kJ/kg} \\[6pt]
\therefore HHV &\approx 28.11 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert HHV to LHV (M = 0, dry fuel)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV &= HHV - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9H}{100} + M\right) \\[6pt]
&= 2810516 - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9 \times 5}{100} + 0\right) \\[6pt]
&= 2810516 - 2442 \times 0.45 \\[6pt]
&= 2810516 - 1098.9 \\[6pt]
\therefore LHV &\approx 2809417 \;\text{kJ/kg} \;\approx\; 28.09 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

The coal releases ≈ 28.1 MJ per kg when burned, with only ≈ 0.02 MJ/kg "lost" to vaporising combustion water — because its hydrogen content is modest (5%).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Biomass — Channiwala & Parikh Formula

**Problem:** A rice-husk sample has (dry basis): C = 38.5%, H = 5.2%, N = 0.45%, S = 0.10%, O = 39.1%, Ash = 16.65%. Find HHV (MJ/kg) and LHV (kJ/kg).

**Step 1 — Mass Check**

$$38.5 + 5.2 + 0.45 + 0.10 + 39.1 + 16.65 = 100.00\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Channiwala–Parikh HHV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,Ash \\[6pt]
&= 0.3491(38.5) + 1.1783(5.2) + 0.1005(0.10) - 0.1034(39.1) - 0.0151(0.45) - 0.0211(16.65) \\[6pt]
&= 13.4404 + 6.1272 + 0.0101 - 4.0429 - 0.0068 - 0.3513 \\[6pt]
&= 15.1767 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kJ/kg**

$$HHV = 15.1767 \times 1000 = 15176.7 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute LHV (no surface moisture)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV &= HHV - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15176.7 - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9 \times 5.2}{100}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15176.7 - 2442 \times 0.468 \\[6pt]
&= 15176.7 - 1142.86 \\[6pt]
\therefore LHV &\approx 14033.8 \;\text{kJ/kg} \;\approx\; 14.03 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Rice husk's HHV is nearly **half** of coal's — but it is *renewable* and *carbon-neutral*, justifying its use in cogeneration plants (e.g., Thailand, India, Pakistan sugar mills).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Gaseous Fuel (Biogas) — Summation Method

**Problem:** A biogas sample has the composition: CH₄ = 62%, CO₂ = 35%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 2% (by volume). Calculate HHV, LHV, and volumetric energy density at NTP (T = 293 K, P = 101.325 kPa).

**Reference values (per m³ at STP):**
- CH₄: HHV = 37.71 MJ/m³, LHV = 33.95 MJ/m³
- CO₂, H₂S, N₂ are **non-combustibles** → contribute 0.

**Step 1 — HHV of Biogas Mixture**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{mix} &= 0.62 \times 37.71 + 0.01 \times 0 + 0.35 \times 0 + 0.02 \times 0 \\[6pt]
&= 23.3802 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\[6pt]
&= 23.38 \;\text{MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — LHV of Biogas Mixture**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{mix} &= 0.62 \times 33.95 + 0.01 \times 0 + 0.35 \times 0 + 0.02 \times 0 \\[6pt]
&= 21.049 \;\text{MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Density on Mass Basis (Optional)**

Average molecular weight of biogas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MW_{mix} &= 0.62(16) + 0.35(44) + 0.01(34) + 0.02(28) \\[6pt]
&= 9.92 + 15.40 + 0.34 + 0.56 = 26.22 \;\text{kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Density at NTP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{P \cdot MW}{R_u T} = \frac{101.325 \times 26.22}{8.314 \times 293} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2657.16}{2435.00} = 1.091 \;\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass-based HHV:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{mass} &= \frac{23.38}{1.091} = 21.43 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This biogas is a *medium-grade* fuel (typical Indian/Pakistani digester output). For a 1 m³ domestic stove burning 0.4 m³/h, thermal output ≈ 9.35 MJ/h — sufficient for 2–3 h of cooking.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Renewable Fuels

| Fuel | Formula / Type | HHV (MJ/kg) | LHV (MJ/kg) | Energy Density | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wood (air-dry) | Biomass | 18–21 | 16–19 | Low (bulk) | Domestic heating |
| Rice husk | Biomass | 14–16 | 13–14 | Low (bulk) | Cogeneration |
| Bagasse | Biomass | 17–19 | 16–17 | Low (bulk) | Sugar mill boilers |
| Biogas (60% CH₄) | Gas | — | 21 MJ/m³ | Low (gas) | Engines, stoves |
| Producer gas | Gas | — | 5–6 MJ/m³ | Low (gas) | Gasifiers, IC engines |
| Hydrogen | Gas | 141.8 | 120.0 | 10.8 MJ/m³ (gas) | Fuel cells |
| Bioethanol | Liquid | 29.7 | 26.8 | 21,200 MJ/m³ | Spark-ignition engines |
| Biodiesel | Liquid | 39.8 | 37.2 | 33,000 MJ/m³ | Diesel engines |
| Petrol (reference) | Liquid | 47.3 | 43.4 | 32,000 MJ/m³ | Baseline |
| Diesel (reference) | Liquid | 45.5 | 42.5 | 36,000 MJ/m³ | Baseline |

---

### 3.5 Quick-Derivation: Why the 9H/100 factor?

The reaction for hydrogen combustion is:
$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O_{(l)}$$

- Molar mass of H₂ = 2 kg/kmol → 4 kg H₂ produces 36 kg H₂O.
- So, **1 kg of hydrogen** → 9 kg of liquid water.

Therefore, for **1 kg of fuel** with mass fraction H (kg H / kg fuel), the water produced = (9H/100) kg (since H is usually in %). The latent heat "lost" in LHV calculation is therefore:

$$Q_{latent} = 2442 \times \frac{9H}{100} \;\text{kJ/kg fuel}$$

This justifies the master relation $LHV = HHV - 2442 \times 9H/100$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Content Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fuel Sample] --> B{Fuel Phase?}
    B -- Solid or Liquid --> C[Step 1: Ultimate Analysis]
    B -- Gaseous --> D[Step 1: Volume Composition by Gas Chromatography]
    C --> E{Choose Empirical Correlation}
    E -- Fossil Fuel Coal --> F[Dulong Formula]
    E -- Biomass --> G[Channiwala Parikh Formula]
    E -- Liquid Biofuel --> H[Standard Reference Tables]
    F --> I[Compute HHV]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> J[Step 2: Volume Fraction Weighted Summation]
    J --> K[Compute HHVmix and LHVmix]
    I --> L[Step 3: Apply LHV Correction]
    L --> M{Is Moisture Present?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Subtract Moisture Latent Heat]
    M -- No --> O[Standard HHV to LHV using 9H/100]
    N --> P[Final LHV Report]
    O --> P
    K --> Q[Final Volumetric Energy Density]
    P --> R[Engineering Decision Boiler Engine Turbine]
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Bomb Calorimeter — Functional Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BC[Bomb Calorimeter Setup]
        O[Oxygen Inlet at 25 atm] --> B1[Stainless Steel Bomb Vessel]
        F1[Fuel Pellet in Crucible] --> B1
        W1[Ignition Wires Connected to Fuse] --> B1
        B1 --> W2[Water Jacket with Known Mass m_w]
        W2 --> T1[Precision Thermometer DT]
        T1 --> S1[Stirrer for Uniform Mixing]
        S1 --> W2
        W2 --> J1[Outer Insulating Jacket]
    end
    J1 --> R1[Read DT after Combustion]
    R1 --> EQ[Apply Q = m_w c_w DT + Water Equivalent]
    EQ --> OUT[HHV of Fuel]
```

### 4.3 Logical Decision Tree: HHV vs LHV Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Energy Content Required] --> Q1{Is the application a Boiler or Furnace?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> HHV1[Use HHV]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it an IC Engine, Gas Turbine, or Fuel Cell?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> LHV1[Use LHV]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Cooking Stove or Domestic Burner?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> HHV2[Use HHV if flue condenses]
    Q3 -- No --> LHV2[Use LHV by default]
    HHV1 --> DONE[Final Answer]
    LHV1 --> DONE
    HHV2 --> DONE
    LHV2 --> DONE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Model Answers)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a fuel. Why is LHV preferred for internal combustion engines?**

**Model Answer:**
The Higher Heating Value (HHV) of a fuel is the total heat released during complete combustion when all the water formed (from fuel hydrogen and moisture) is condensed back to liquid, thus recovering its latent heat of vaporisation. The Lower Heating Value (LHV) is the heat released assuming the water leaves the combustion system as vapour, so this latent heat is not recovered.

LHV is preferred for internal combustion engines because the exhaust gases leave the cylinder at very high temperatures (typically 700–900 K) — well above the dew point of water. Therefore, water in the products remains as vapour and its latent heat cannot be practically recovered. Hence, the "useful" energy delivered by the fuel per unit mass equals its LHV. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Dulong's formula for estimating the HHV of a solid fuel from its ultimate analysis. Mention the units clearly.**

**Model Answer:**
Dulong's empirical formula for the Higher Heating Value of a solid fuel is:

$$HHV = 33800\,C + 144200\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9270\,S \;\;\; \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

where C, H, O, and S are the mass fractions (in percent, i.e., kg per 100 kg of fuel) of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulphur, respectively, on a dry basis. The factor $O/8$ accounts for the oxygen already combined with hydrogen as moisture (internal water), and the corresponding hydrogen is therefore not available for combustion. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]

**A bagasse sample (a biomass fuel) has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis, % by mass):**
**C = 44.1%, H = 5.8%, O = 43.6%, N = 0.3%, S = 0.1%, Ash = 6.1%**

**(a) Calculate the HHV using the Channiwala–Parikh correlation.**
**(b) Determine the LHV of the fuel. Discuss the significance of the LHV–HHV gap in a 100 TPH boiler application.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — HHV via Channiwala–Parikh** [7 marks]

The Channiwala–Parikh empirical formula for biomass HHV is:

$$HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,Ash \;\;\; \text{(MJ/kg)}$$

Substituting the mass fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 0.3491(44.1) + 1.1783(5.8) + 0.1005(0.1) - 0.1034(43.6) \\
&\quad - 0.0151(0.3) - 0.0211(6.1) \\[4pt]
&= 15.3953 + 6.8341 + 0.0101 - 4.5082 - 0.0045 - 0.1287 \\[4pt]
&= 17.598 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{HHV \approx 17.60 \;\text{MJ/kg}}$$

**Verification of mass closure:** 44.1 + 5.8 + 43.6 + 0.3 + 0.1 + 6.1 = 100.0% ✓

**Part (b) — LHV and Engineering Discussion** [7 marks]

Using the master relation with M = 0 (no surface moisture reported):

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV &= HHV - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 17600 - 2442 \times \left(\frac{9 \times 5.8}{100}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 17600 - 2442 \times 0.522 \\[4pt]
&= 17600 - 1274.7 \\[4pt]
\therefore LHV &\approx 16325 \;\text{kJ/kg} \;\approx\; 16.32 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gap analysis:** $HHV - LHV = 1275 \;\text{kJ/kg}$ (~7.2% of HHV).

**Significance in a 100 TPH boiler:**
- For a 100 TPH bagasse-fired boiler operating at 70% thermal efficiency, fuel consumption ≈ 100×1000 / (0.70 × 16.32) ≈ 8750 kg/h (using LHV) versus ≈ 8110 kg/h (using HHV).
- Using HHV (incorrect for boilers with hot exhaust > 150 °C) would *underestimate* the fuel requirement by ≈ 7%.
- Practical recommendation: use **LHV for design calculations** of IC engines and high-temperature boilers; use **HHV for low-temperature heating systems and domestic stoves** where flue gas is cooled below 60 °C and water condenses.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A producer gas from a downdraft biomass gasifier has the following composition (by volume):**
**CO = 22%, H₂ = 12%, CH₄ = 3%, CO₂ = 12%, N₂ = 51%**

**(a) Calculate the HHV and LHV of this producer gas in MJ/m³ at STP.**
**(b) A 100 kW<sub>e</sub> biomass gasifier-engine system uses 0.5 m³ of this gas per kWh generated. Estimate the thermal input to the engine and the overall electrical efficiency. Suggest one practical way to improve the energy content.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — HHV and LHV of Producer Gas** [7 marks]

Reference calorific values (at STP, MJ/m³):

| Component | HHV (MJ/m³) | LHV (MJ/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 12.64 | 12.64 |
| H₂ | 12.75 | 10.79 |
| CH₄ | 37.71 | 33.95 |
| CO₂, N₂ | 0 | 0 |

(For CO, HHV = LHV because combustion $CO + 0.5O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ does not produce water.)

**HHV calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{mix} &= 0.22(12.64) + 0.12(12.75) + 0.03(37.71) + 0.12(0) + 0.51(0) \\[4pt]
&= 2.7808 + 1.5300 + 1.1313 + 0 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 5.4421 \;\text{MJ/m}^3 \\[4pt]
\therefore HHV &\approx 5.44 \;\text{MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**LHV calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LHV_{mix} &= 0.22(12.64) + 0.12(10.79) + 0.03(33.95) + 0 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 2.7808 + 1.2948 + 1.0185 + 0 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 5.0941 \;\text{MJ/m}^3 \\[4pt]
\therefore LHV &\approx 5.09 \;\text{MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — System Performance** [7 marks]

For a 100 kW<sub>e</sub> generator set consuming 0.5 m³ of producer gas per kWh:

$$\text{Hourly gas consumption} = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Thermal input to engine (based on LHV — correct for IC engines):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= 50 \times 5.09 = 254.5 \;\text{MJ/h} \\[4pt]
Q_{in} \text{ in kW} &= \frac{254.5 \times 1000}{3600} = 70.69 \;\text{kW}_{th}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Electrical efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{elec} = \frac{P_{elec}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{100}{70.69} \times 100\% \approx 141\% \;\;\text{(ERROR!)}
$$

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Mixing LHV per **m³** with **kW** units causes dimensional chaos. KTU students routinely lose 2–3 marks by NOT converting MJ/h → kW. The correct approach: total thermal energy in 50 m³ = 254.5 MJ/h, which equals 254,500 kJ/h. Converting to kW: 254500 / 3600 = **70.69 kW thermal input**.

**Corrected efficiency calculation:**

$$
\eta_{elec} = \frac{\text{Electrical output per hour}}{\text{Thermal input per hour}} = \frac{100 \;\text{kWh}}{70.69 \;\text{kWh}_{th}} \times 100\% \approx 141\%
$$

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** This efficiency is **physically impossible** (>100%) — it implies the engine produces more energy than the fuel provides. The mistake is using **per kWh generated** for both quantities, double-counting. The correct method is:

> $$\eta_{elec} = \frac{3600 \times P_{elec} \;\text{(kW)}}{\dot{V} \times LHV \times 1000 \;\text{(kJ/h)}}$$

> Substituting correctly:
> $$\eta_{elec} = \frac{3600 \times 100}{50 \times 5.09 \times 1000} = \frac{360000}{254500} = 1.414 \;\;\text{→ STILL > 100%!}$$

> **[!CRITICAL]**
> The conclusion: The **0.5 m³/kWh figure is too low** for a 100 kW set with this LHV. Theoretical minimum gas needed: $100 / (5.09/3.6) = 70.7$ m³/h, or 0.707 m³/kWh. So a realistic gasifier-engine would need 0.7–0.9 m³/kWh. **The student should be ready to comment on the inconsistency** and recommend a re-check of the gasifier operation.

**Suggested improvement (for full marks):**
- **Increase the air-to-fuel ratio reduction** in the gasifier (use sub-stoichiometric air / O₂-enrichment) to raise the CO and CH₄ fractions.
- Use **drier biomass** (moisture < 15%) to prevent dilution of combustible gases by water vapour.
- Install a **gas-cleaning and cooling train** to remove tar and particulates, allowing higher engine efficiency.
- **Reforming the tar** catalytically into additional CO + H₂ can boost LHV by 1–2 MJ/m³.

---

> **[!WARNING]**
> **General KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings for this Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** HHV and LHV can be in **kJ/kg** OR **MJ/m³** — always declare the basis explicitly.
> 2. **Mass vs Volume Basis for Gases:** LHV of CH₄ is 50.02 MJ/kg but 33.95 MJ/m³ (at STP) — the factor differs by ~50x. Verify which one the question wants.
> 3. **Moisture Deduction:** For wet fuels, always include the surface moisture M in the LHV correction: $LHV = HHV - 2442(9H/100 + M)$.
> 4. **Dulong's Formula Domain:** It is most accurate for **C ≥ 70%** fossil coals — for low-rank fuels and biomass, Channiwala–Parikh is preferred.
> 5. **Summation Method Limitation:** Assumes ideal gas mixing and complete combustion — at high pressures, compressibility factors must be applied.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HHV** = total heat including latent heat of water condensation. **LHV** = total heat excluding this latent heat.
- ✅ For **boilers/heating** → use HHV. For **engines, turbines, fuel cells** → use LHV.
- ✅ Dulong's formula: $HHV = 33800C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9270S$ in **kJ/kg** for coal.
- ✅ Channiwala–Parikh: $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211Ash$ in **MJ/kg** for biomass.
- ✅ HHV–LHV bridge: $LHV = HHV - 2442(9H/100 + M)$, where $h_{fg} = 2442 \;\text{kJ/kg}$ at 25 °C.
- ✅ The **9H/100** factor comes from stoichiometry: 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O.
- ✅ Gaseous fuel energy content = volume-fraction weighted sum of component calorific values.
- ✅ Reference values: CH₄ HHV = 55.50 MJ/kg, LHV = 50.02 MJ/kg; H₂ HHV = 141.80 MJ/kg.
- ✅ Volumetric energy density (gas): $E_v = \rho_{gas} \times HHV_{mass}$ or use $MW, P, T$ via ideal gas law.
- ✅ Always **verify mass closure** to 100% (or 99.5% ± tolerance) before applying empirical formulae.
- ✅ For a **biogas digester**, energy content scales linearly with CH₄ fraction — every 1% rise in CH₄ = ≈ +0.36 MJ/m³.
- ✅ **Common unit conversions:** 1 kcal = 4.1868 kJ; 1 toe = 41.868 GJ; 1 boe ≈ 5.8 MMBTU.
- ✅ KTU-preferred approach: report **both HHV and LHV** in every numerical, with units explicitly stated, and conclude with a one-line engineering recommendation.
- ✅ Always include the **empirical formula name** (Dulong / Channiwala–Parikh) in your answer — examiners award marks for naming the correct correlation.
- ✅ **Ash content** must be accounted for in biomass HHV (Channiwala–Parikh includes a $-0.0211 \times Ash$ term).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Power Coefficient?

In the context of **Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)** — the primary focus of the OESFT726 syllabus under Module 2 — the **Power Coefficient ($C_P$)** is formally defined as the dimensionless ratio of the **actual electrical (or mechanical) power extracted by a wind turbine** to the **total available kinetic power present in the wind stream** sweeping through the rotor's swept area.

$$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$$

where the **total available wind power** for a fluid stream of density $\rho$, cross-sectional area $A$, and free-stream velocity $v_1$ is:

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** The Power Coefficient is a measure of the efficiency of a wind turbine rotor in converting the kinetic energy of the wind into useful mechanical (rotational) energy. It is fundamentally constrained by the **Betz Limit**, a thermodynamic ceiling of approximately **59.3 %**.

## 1.2 The Family of Aerodynamic Coefficients

Wind engineering does not rely on $C_P$ alone. Three closely linked dimensionless parameters govern the aerodynamic performance of any horizontal-axis wind turbine (HAWT):

| Coefficient | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Optimal Range |
|-------------|--------|------------------|---------------|
| **Power Coefficient** | $C_P$ | Fraction of wind power converted to rotational power | $\leq 0.593$ (Betz) |
| **Tip Speed Ratio** | $\lambda$ | Ratio of blade-tip linear speed to wind speed | $6 - 8$ (modern 3-blade) |
| **Torque Coefficient** | $C_t$ | Fraction of wind torque converted to shaft torque | $0.05 - 0.15$ |
| **Thrust Coefficient** | $C_T$ | Fraction of wind thrust transferred to the tower | $0.4 - 0.8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$)**: This is **not** a coefficient in the strict sense, but a critical operating parameter defined as:
> $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v_1}$$
> where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s) and $R$ is the rotor radius (m). It is the **independent variable** that links $C_P$ to a turbine's rotational behavior.

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Fishing Net on a River":** Imagine standing in a fast-flowing river holding a circular fishing net. The river carries water (mass × velocity) carrying kinetic energy. The net intercepts some of this water, slowing it down. The kinetic energy lost by the water as it passes through and behind the net is what gets transferred to the net (and your arms). Now, three things govern how much energy the net captures:
> 1. **How solid the net is** — A perfectly solid disc captures the *most*, but the water has to fully stop, creating turbulence and backflow. A *sparse* net lets too much water slip through untouched.
> 2. **The angle of the net** — Tilting it lets water deflect sideways without losing speed.
> 3. **The downstream wake** — If the water behind the net is moving too fast, you wasted potential energy. If it's stopped dead, you created back-pressure that chokes the flow.
>
> **This is exactly the trade-off that defines $C_P$.** The "perfect net" (which captures the theoretical maximum 59.3 % of the river's kinetic energy) corresponds to a wake that retains **1/3** of the upstream speed — this is the **Betz Limit**. No turbine, no matter how brilliant the engineer, can exceed this. Nature forbids it because doing so would require the air *behind* the rotor to reverse direction (negative velocity), choking the inflow.

## 1.4 The Historical & Engineering Significance

The concept of $C_P$ was mathematically established by **Albert Betz** in 1919 in his treatise *"Das Maximum der theoretisch möglichen Ausnützung des Windes durch Windmotoren"*. The significance for KTU-level engineering is twofold:

- **Design Tool:** Every wind turbine manufacturer publishes a $C_P$–$\lambda$ curve. The "sweet spot" on this curve determines the **optimal operating regime** for a given turbine.
- **Performance Benchmark:** It is the single most important figure-of-merit used to compare competing turbine designs in the renewable energy industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** In the Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic directly maps to **CO2** (Analyze the aerodynamic performance of wind energy systems).
