Mastery of $C_P$ derivations and the Betz Limit is mandatory for both Continuous Assessment (CAT) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Actuator Disc Model

To understand *why* $C_P$ exists and what limits it, we use the **Actuator Disc Theory** (also called the **Momentum Theory** or **Rankine-Froude Momentum Theory**). The model makes the following physically justified assumptions:

1. The rotor is replaced by a frictionless, porous **actuator disc** that extracts energy uniformly across its swept area $A$.
2. The flow is **incompressible**, **steady-state**, **axisymmetric**, and **inviscid** (no friction).
3. There is **no rotation** in the wake (a separate correction via the *Blade Element Momentum (BEM)* theory is needed for rotating wakes).
4. The far-wake pressure equals the far-upstream pressure (atmospheric).

### 2.1.1 The Velocity Triplet

The air velocity at three key stream-tube stations is:
- **$v_1$** — Free-stream (undisturbed upstream) wind velocity.
- **$v_d$** — Velocity **at the rotor disc plane**.
- **$v_2$** — Velocity in the **far downstream wake**.

By mass conservation through the stream-tube (whose cross-section expands from $A_1 \to A \to A_2$):

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v_d = \rho A_2 v_2$$

We define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that:

$$v_d = v_1 (1 - a)$$

$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "induction"?** The rotor *induces* a velocity deficit in the incoming flow. The factor $a$ quantifies how much the rotor "slows down" the air. Physically, $0 < a < 0.5$. If $a \geq 0.5$, the wake becomes stagnant or reversed — flow collapses (the **"turbulent wake state"**).

### 2.1.2 Why Does $C_P$ Depend on $a$?

The thrust force on the disc (by momentum theory) is:

$$T = \rho A v_d (v_1 - v_2) = 2 \rho A v_1^2 a (1 - a)$$

The power extracted equals thrust times disc velocity:

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = T \cdot v_d = 2 \rho A v_1^3 a (1 - a)^2$$

Dividing by the available wind power:

$$\boxed{C_P = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = 4a(1-a)^2}$$

This is the **most important single equation** in wind energy engineering at the KTU undergraduate level.

## 2.2 The Betz Limit — Deriving the Theoretical Maximum

To find the maximum of $C_P(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$, we differentiate with respect to $a$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

The non-trivial solution (excluding $a = 1$, which is physically meaningless) gives:

$$a_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Substituting back:

$$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\;\; (\approx 59.26\%)$$

At this optimum, the velocities become:
- $v_d = \frac{2}{3} v_1$
- $v_2 = \frac{1}{3} v_1$ (the wake retains 1/3 of the upstream speed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Betz Limit — Statement:** No wind turbine, regardless of design, can extract more than **16/27 ≈ 59.3 %** of the kinetic energy in the wind. Real-world utility-scale turbines operate in the range **$C_P \approx 0.35 - 0.45$** (35 % – 45 %) due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses.

## 2.3 The $C_P$–$\lambda$ Performance Curve

In real turbines, the rotor blades rotate, so we must introduce the **rotor-relative induction**. The **tangential induction factor** $a'$ (sometimes denoted $a_2$ or $a'$) modifies the power equation:

$$C_P = 4a(1-a)^2 \left(1 + \frac{a'(1-a)}{\lambda}\right) \cdot (\text{rotation correction})$$

A more practical engineering form, valid for a single actuator disc with rotational wake, is:

$$C_P = 4a(1-a)^2 - 4a'^2 \lambda^2 (1-a)$$

For **maximum $C_P$**, the optimal relationship between $a$ and $a'$ is:

$$a'_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1 - 3a}{4\lambda}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher blade count and **lower tip-speed ratios** favor high $C_P$ (closer to Betz). Modern **3-bladed turbines** at $\lambda \approx 7$ typically reach $C_P \approx 0.45 - 0.48$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your KTU Board Examination "Cheat Sheet" — Memorize every row.**

| # | Parameter | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|-----------|----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Wind Power (available) | $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$ | $\rho$ [kg/m³], $A$ [m²], $v_1$ [m/s], $P$ [W] | Cubic dependence on velocity — *doubling wind speed gives 8× power*. |
| 2 | Power Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le C_P \le 16/27$ |
| 3 | Actuator Disc $C_P$ | $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ | $a$ = axial induction factor | From 1-D momentum theory |
| 4 | Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v_1}$ | $\omega$ [rad/s], $R$ [m] | Independent operating variable |
| 5 | Optimal $a$ (Betz) | $a_{\text{opt}} = 1/3$ | — | Gives max $C_P$ |
| 6 | Betz Limit | $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | — | Absolute theoretical ceiling |
| 7 | Torque Coefficient | $C_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{rotor}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R v_1^2}$ | $T$ [N·m] | Fraction of wind torque converted |
| 8 | Thrust Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{F_T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^2}$ | $F_T$ [N] | Axial load on the tower |
| 9 | $C_P$–$C_t$ link | $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_t$ | — | Critical! Power = Torque × $\omega$ |
| 10 | Optimal $a'$ | $a'_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{1-3a}{4\lambda}$ | — | For variable-speed turbines |
| 11 | Air density (ISA) | $\rho = 1.225$ **kg/m³** | At 15 °C, sea level | **Bold = standard reference value** |
| 12 | Swept Area | $A = \pi R^2$ | $R$ [m] | For HAWT |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of $C_P$ |
|-------|----------------------|
| **Wind Farm Micro-Siting** | Wind atlas data is post-processed with turbine $C_P(\lambda)$ curves to predict Annual Energy Production (AEP). |
| **Pitch & Yaw Control** | Modern turbines dynamically adjust blade pitch to *stay on the maximum $C_P$ point* across all wind speeds. |
| **Small Wind Turbines** | Below **Cut-in** speed, $\lambda$ collapses → $C_P \to 0$ → no usable power. |
| **Offshore Floating Turbines** | Wave-induced motion shifts the effective $C_P$ — control systems compensate in real time. |
| **Tidal & Hydro Turbines** | Same coefficient framework applies (with **Betz limit ≈ 59.3 %** for axial-flow hydrokinetic devices). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cross-Reference:** This concept is mathematically identical to the analysis of **axial-flow hydro turbines** covered later in your course, just with **water density ($\rho_w \approx 1000$ kg/m³)** substituted for air density. The Betz limit remains unchanged at 16/27.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — Power Coefficient from First Principles

### Step 1: Set up the control volume

Consider a horizontal-axis wind turbine (HAWT) of rotor radius $R$. Apply the **steady-flow energy equation** to a stream-tube that contracts upstream (area $A_1$), passes through the rotor (area $A$), and expands downstream (area $A_2$).

By **conservation of mass** through the incompressible stream-tube:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v_d = \rho A_2 v_2$$

Here $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s).

### Step 2: Apply conservation of momentum (thrust)

The thrust force $T$ on the rotor equals the rate of change of axial momentum of the air:

$$T = \dot{m} (v_1 - v_2)$$

Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v_d$:

$$T = \rho A v_d (v_1 - v_2)$$

### Step 3: Introduce the axial induction factor $a$

By definition:

$$v_d = v_1 (1 - a)$$

By continuity and momentum balance on the expanding stream-tube (the stream-tube area $A_2$ is related to $A_1$ through the Bernoulli equation applied between far upstream and far wake, where pressures are equal):

$$v_2 = v_1 (1 - 2a)$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the thrust equation

$$T = \rho A [v_1(1-a)] \cdot [v_1 - v_1(1-2a)]$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$v_1 - v_1(1-2a) = v_1 \cdot 2a$$

Therefore:

$$T = \rho A v_1^2 (1-a) \cdot 2a = 2\rho A v_1^2 a (1-a)$$

### Step 5: Compute power extracted

The mechanical power extracted equals thrust times the velocity at the disc:

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = T \cdot v_d = 2\rho A v_1^2 a (1-a) \cdot v_1(1-a)$$

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = 2\rho A v_1^3 a (1-a)^2$$

### Step 6: Compute available wind power

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$$

### Step 7: Form the ratio (definition of $C_P$)

$$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{2\rho A v_1^3 a (1-a)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$

The $\rho A v_1^3$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{C_P = 4a(1-a)^2}$$

> **Logic Note:** Each $\rho$, $A$, $v_1^3$ cancelled exactly because $P_{\text{wind}}$ also scales as $\rho A v_1^3$. This is why $C_P$ is **purely a function of $a$** in the idealised model — the wind speed and air density do not appear in the final coefficient.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — Maximum Power Coefficient (Betz Limit)

### Step 1: State the $C_P$ function to be maximized

$$C_P(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$$

Valid domain: $0 \leq a \leq 0.5$ (for physically meaningful wake states).

### Step 2: Expand the function for easier differentiation

$$C_P(a) = 4a (1 - 2a + a^2) = 4a - 8a^2 + 4a^3$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with respect to $a$

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 - 16a + 12a^2$$

### Step 4: Set derivative to zero (stationary point)

$$12a^2 - 16a + 4 = 0$$

Divide both sides by 4:

$$3a^2 - 4a + 1 = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve the quadratic

Using the quadratic formula with $A = 3$, $B = -4$, $C = 1$:

$$a = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 12}}{6} = \frac{4 \pm 2}{6}$$

Two solutions:

$$a_1 = \frac{6}{6} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad a_2 = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 6: Apply the second-derivative test

$$\frac{d^2C_P}{da^2} = -16 + 24a$$

- At $a = 1$: $\frac{d^2C_P}{da^2} = 8 > 0$ → local **minimum** (physically meaningless: zero flow).
- At $a = 1/3$: $\frac{d^2C_P}{da^2} = -8 < 0$ → local **maximum** ✓.

### Step 7: Compute $C_P$ at $a = 1/3$

$$C_P = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 59.26\%}$$

### Step 8: Verify physical consistency

At $a = 1/3$:
- Velocity at disc: $v_d = v_1 \cdot (2/3)$
- Far wake velocity: $v_2 = v_1 \cdot (1/3)$
- Far wake pressure equals far upstream pressure: ✓ (Bernoulli check confirms this is the unique point where pressure recovers to atmospheric).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this the maximum?** If $a < 1/3$, the rotor "doesn't grab enough air" — energy passes through unused. If $a > 1/3$, the rotor slows the air too much, causing a **pressure buildup** upstream that *chokes* the inflow, reducing total mass flow through the disc. The product has a single interior maximum.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Relationship Between $C_P$, $C_t$, and $\lambda$

### Step 1: Define torque coefficient

The torque (moment) on the rotor shaft:

$$Q = T_{\text{rotor}} \cdot R = C_t \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A R v_1^2$$

(where $A = \pi R^2$ has been substituted appropriately, depending on convention).

### Step 2: Express shaft power as torque times angular velocity

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = Q \cdot \omega$$

### Step 3: Substitute and divide by wind power

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = C_t \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R v_1^2 \cdot \omega = C_t \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 v_1^2 \cdot \omega R$$

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = C_t \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^3 v_1^2 \omega$$

Now divide by $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 v_1^3$:

$$C_P = \frac{C_t \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^3 v_1^2 \omega}{\frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 v_1^3}$$

Simplify:

$$C_P = C_t \cdot \frac{\omega R}{v_1} = C_t \cdot \lambda$$

$$\boxed{C_P = \lambda \cdot C_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This identity is heavily tested in KTU ESE.** It allows you to compute any one of $C_P$, $C_t$, or $\lambda$ if the other two are known.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operational Regimes of $C_P$

| Operating Regime | Wind Speed | TSR ($\lambda$) | Blade Pitch | $C_P$ Behavior | Engineering Action |
|------------------|-----------|----------------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Below cut-in** | $v < 3$ m/s | Irrelevant | Feathered | $C_P \approx 0$ | Turbine parked, no power |
| **Cut-in → Rated** | $3 - 12$ m/s | Optimized via variable-speed control | Fine pitch | $C_P$ held at $C_{P,\max}$ | MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) |
| **Rated speed** | $12 - 25$ m/s | Variable, $\lambda$ decreases as $v$ rises | Increasing pitch | $C_P$ falls **deliberately** | Pitch control limits mechanical load |
| **Cut-out** | $v > 25$ m/s | N/A | Full feather | $C_P \to 0$ | Turbine shut down (safety) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Coefficient System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of how a wind turbine controller uses the $C_P$–$\lambda$ relationship to maximize energy capture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Speed Sensor v1] --> B[Anemometer Signal Conditioning]
    C[Rotor Speed Sensor omega] --> D[Tachometer Signal]
    B --> E[Microprocessor Controller]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Compute Tip Speed Ratio lambda}
    F --> G[Lookup Table: Cp vs lambda Curve]
    G --> H{Cp less than Cp max?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Adjust Blade Pitch Angle theta down]
    H -- No --> J[Increase Pitch Angle theta up]
    I --> K[Variable Pitch Actuator]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Rotor Blades Re-pitch]
    L --> M[New Aerodynamic State]
    M --> N[Generator Torque Adjusted]
    N --> O[Power Output P out]
    O --> P{Cp at Maximum?}
    P -- No --> F
    P -- Yes --> Q[Operating at Betz-Optimal Point]
    Q --> R[Maximum Power Extraction]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#333
    style Q fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The controller continuously loops through wind speed, rotor speed, computed $\lambda$, and the embedded $C_P$ curve. Whenever the operating point drifts from the peak, the blade pitch is adjusted to walk the system back toward maximum $C_P$ — this is the heart of **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** in modern turbines.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Coefficient Relationship Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Wind Power Pwind = half rho A v1 cubed] --> CP[Power Coefficient Cp]
    Q[Shaft Torque Q] --> CT[Torque Coefficient Ct]
    F[Thrust Force FT] --> CTR[Thrust Coefficient CT]
    CP --> E1[Energy Output]
    CT --> E1
    LAMBDA[Tip Speed Ratio lambda] --> CP
    LAMBDA --> CT
    CP -- Cp equals lambda times Ct --> CT
    CTR --> FBD[Structural Load Analysis]
    E1 --> GEN[Generator Output]
    style CP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style CT fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#333
    style CTR fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
    style LAMBDA fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Axial Induction Factor $a$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoExtraction : a equals 0
    NoExtraction --> PartialExtraction : Wind engages rotor
    PartialExtraction --> OptimalExtraction : a equals 0.333
    OptimalExtraction --> PartialExtraction : a drifts from 0.333
    PartialExtraction --> ChokedFlow : a exceeds 0.5
    ChokedFlow --> [*] : Wake reversal flow breakdown
    note right of OptimalExtraction : Cp max equals 16 divided by 27 equals 0.5926
    note left of ChokedFlow : Physically forbidden region
    note right of NoExtraction : Theoretical idle state
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Velocity Stream-Tube Through Actuator Disc

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Far Upstream v1 Area A1] --> B[Atmospheric Pressure p1]
    B --> C[Induction Zone]
    C --> D[Actuator Disc vd Area A]
    D --> E[Wake Recovery Zone]
    E --> F[Far Wake v2 equals v1 minus 2av1 Area A2]
    A1note[v1 undimmed flow] -.-> A
    Fnote[v2 equals one third v1 at Betz point] -.-> F
    style C fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The stream-tube contracts upstream (from $A_1$ to $A$), passes through the rotor disc with a velocity deficit $v_d = v_1(1-a)$, then expands again downstream to $A_2$. The cross-sectional expansion is a direct consequence of mass conservation as the air slows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Betz Limit for a wind turbine.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Betz Limit, established by Albert Betz in 1919, states that **no wind turbine can convert more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3 %) of the kinetic energy in the wind** into useful mechanical energy. It arises from applying conservation of mass and momentum to an idealised actuator disc: $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$, which has a maximum value of 16/27 at the axial induction factor $a = 1/3$. Real turbines achieve 35 % – 45 % due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Power Coefficient and Tip Speed Ratio for a wind turbine.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Power Coefficient ($C_P$):** The dimensionless ratio of mechanical power extracted by the turbine rotor to the total available kinetic power in the wind stream.
> $$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$
> **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$):** The dimensionless ratio of the linear speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity.
> $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v_1}$$
> **(1.5 marks)**
> Together, they form the fundamental $C_P$–$\lambda$ performance curve of any wind turbine.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**A.** With the help of the actuator disc theory, derive the expression for the Power Coefficient of a wind turbine. Show that its maximum value is **16/27**.

**(OR)**

**B.** A three-bladed horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. At a wind speed of **12 m/s**, it produces **2 MW** of electrical power. Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.92$, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.96$. Calculate:
- (a) The available wind power and the actual mechanical power extracted.
- (b) The Power Coefficient $C_P$ of this turbine and comment on its proximity to the Betz Limit.

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Step 1: Set up the actuator disc model (2 marks)

Consider a horizontal-axis wind turbine modelled as a frictionless porous disc of area $A$ in a uniform wind stream $v_1$. By mass conservation through the stream-tube:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v_d = \rho A_2 v_2$$

where $A_1, A, A_2$ are stream-tube cross-sections at far upstream, at disc, and far downstream respectively.

#### Step 2: Introduce the axial induction factor (2 marks)

Define $a$ such that the velocity at the disc is:

$$v_d = v_1 (1 - a)$$

By Bernoulli + momentum theory (which yields the kinematic relation $v_2 = v_1 - 2v_d$ when far upstream and far wake pressures are equal):

$$v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

#### Step 3: Thrust from momentum balance (2 marks)

Thrust force on the disc:

$$T = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v_d \cdot 2 a v_1 = 2\rho A v_1^2 a (1-a)$$

#### Step 4: Power extracted (3 marks)

$$P = T \cdot v_d = 2\rho A v_1^3 a (1-a)^2$$

Available wind power:

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$$

Therefore:

$$C_P = \frac{P}{P_{\text{wind}}} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

#### Step 5: Maximize $C_P$ (3 marks)

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 - 8a(1-a) = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Non-trivial root: $a = 1/3$.

Substituting:

$$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

#### Step 6: Physical interpretation (2 marks)

At this optimum, the wake retains 1/3 of upstream velocity. This is the **Betz Limit**, an absolute thermodynamic ceiling for any wind turbine operating in steady, incompressible flow.

### Model Solution for Question B

**Given:** $D = 80$ m, $v_1 = 12$ m/s, $P_{\text{elec}} = 2$ MW, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{gb} = 0.96$.

#### Part (a) — Available wind power and mechanical power extracted (7 marks)

Swept area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = \pi \times 1600 \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

Available wind power:

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$

$$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.317 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 5.317 \text{ MW}$$

**(3 marks)**

Mechanical power at the rotor shaft (before gearbox & generator):

$$P_{\text{mech}} = \frac{P_{\text{elec}}}{\eta_g \cdot \eta_{gb}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.92 \times 0.96}$$

$$P_{\text{mech}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.8832} \approx 2.265 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 2.265 \text{ MW}$$

**(4 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Power Coefficient and comparison with Betz (7 marks)

$$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{2.265 \times 10^6}{5.317 \times 10^6} \approx 0.426 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 42.6\%$$

**(3 marks)**

Comparison: The Betz Limit is **59.3 %**. This turbine achieves **$C_P \approx 42.6 \%$**, which corresponds to **$\approx 71.8\%$** of the Betz Limit — a **good performance** for a utility-scale turbine, well within the typical operating range of **35 – 45 %** for modern commercial designs. The shortfall from Betz (~16.7 percentage points) is due to:
- Aerodynamic losses (blade drag, tip losses)
- Mechanical losses (gearbox friction)
- Electrical losses (generator, converter)
- Yaw and tower shadow effects
- Wake non-uniformities

**(4 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the densities / efficiencies** when computing $P_{\text{mech}}$ from $P_{\text{elec}}$ — students frequently write $P_{\text{mech}} = P_{\text{elec}}$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up diameters and radii** in the area formula — always use $A = \pi D^2/4$, not $\pi R^2$ confusion.
> 3. **Using 0.593 as the answer for $C_P$** — that's the *theoretical maximum*, not what this turbine *achieves*. Compute the actual ratio.
> 4. **In derivation questions**, students often skip showing $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$ — this kinematic relation comes from equating far-field pressures via Bernoulli's equation between sections (1) and (2). Examiner expects this step shown.
> 5. **Units slip-ups**: $v_1^3$ gives m³/s³, times $\rho$ kg/m³ and $A$ m² gives kg·m²/s³ = W. Confirm dimensional consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Coefficients**

### Definitions (Memorize verbatim)
- ☐ **Power Coefficient** $C_P$ = (Power extracted by rotor) ÷ (Available wind power).
- ☐ **Tip Speed Ratio** $\lambda = \omega R / v_1$.
- ☐ **Torque Coefficient** $C_t$ = (Rotor torque) ÷ (Reference torque).
- ☐ **Thrust Coefficient** $C_T$ = (Rotor thrust) ÷ (Reference thrust).
- ☐ **Axial Induction Factor** $a = (v_1 - v_d)/v_1$.
- ☐ **Tangential Induction Factor** $a' = \omega R / (2 v_1)$ related.

### Key Equations (Board Exam Essentials)
- ☐ $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ — fundamental actuator disc result.
- ☐ $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_t$ — power–torque identity.
- ☐ $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ at $a = 1/3$ — **Betz Limit**.
- ☐ $P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$ — cubic in velocity.
- ☐ Standard air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level, 15 °C).

### Critical Physical Insights
- ☐ **Wake must move forward** — Betz optimum occurs at $v_2 = v_1/3$, never zero (no reverse flow).
- ☐ **$C_P$–$\lambda$ curve is unique** to each turbine design — used as the design fingerprint.
- ☐ **No turbine beats Betz** — even in the absence of all losses, the second law prevents it.
- ☐ **Real turbines hit 35 – 48 %** of $C_{P,\max}$ in field conditions.
- ☐ **Pitch control lowers $C_P$** at high wind — this is *deliberate*, not a flaw.

### Pitfalls to Avoid (from past KTU scripts)
- ☐ Never write "$C_P = 59.3\%$" without specifying "of theoretical maximum".
- ☐ In BEM-based problems, $C_P$ depends on **both** $a$ and $a'$.
- ☐ Below cut-in, $\lambda$ is undefined meaningfully — turbine is parked.
- ☐ Don't confuse $C_T$ (thrust) with $C_t$ (torque) — case-sensitive!

### One-Sentence Takeaway
> **The Power Coefficient quantifies how efficiently a wind turbine converts wind's kinetic energy into shaft power, fundamentally capped at 16/27 (the Betz Limit) — a constraint no engineering ingenuity can overcome, but one that every well-designed turbine strives to approach through optimal Tip Speed Ratio matching and blade pitch control.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Betz Limit Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Betz Limit Theory ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Betz Limit (Betz's Law):** The Betz Limit is the theoretical maximum efficiency at which an open-flow wind energy conversion device (such as a wind turbine) can extract kinetic energy from a moving fluid stream. It is a fundamental theorem in wind energy engineering that establishes an upper bound on the fraction of wind power that can be captured by an ideal, disc-shaped, frictionless turbine rotor. Mathematically, the Betz Limit states that the maximum power coefficient ($C_P$) of a wind turbine is: $$C_{P, \max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.3\%$$ The corresponding optimal axial induction factor is $a = \frac{1}{3}$, and the optimal downstream-to-upstream wind velocity ratio is $\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{1}{3}$. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context:** The law was formulated by German physicist **Albert Betz** in **1919** in his paper *"Das Maximum der theoretisch möglichen Ausnützung des Windes durch Windmotoren"* (The Maximum of Theoretically Possible Utilization of Wind by Wind Motors). It is also known as **Lanchester-Betz limit** because British aerodynamicist **Frederick W. Lanchester** independently discovered the same principle around 1915. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are fishing in a stream using a net. If you stretch the net too wide and try to catch *all* the water, the water will simply pile up behind the net and push the net downstream — or flow around it. You don't catch any fish! If you make the net *very small* and let most water pass through, you barely catch any fish either. The "sweet spot" is somewhere in the middle — the net should be just big enough to slow the water down significantly (so its kinetic energy is transferred to the net) but not so much that the water stops completely (which would block the flow). A wind turbine is exactly that "net" for the air stream. The **Betz Limit** tells us that the sweetest sweet spot allows the turbine to extract exactly **59.3%** of the wind's kinetic energy. You cannot do better, because: - If you try to extract more, the air must slow down too much, causing a "traffic jam" of air molecules that prevents new air from entering the turbine. - If you extract less, you are simply wasting energy that the wind is offering you for free. > [!TIP] > **Key Intuition:** The Betz Limit is to wind energy what the **Carnot Efficiency** is to heat engines — an absolute theoretical ceiling set by the laws of physics (specifically, conservation of mass, momentum, and energy). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Air density (sea level, 15°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Maximum Power Coefficient | $C_{P,\max}$ | **0.5926 (16/27)** | | Optimal Axial Induction Factor | $a_{opt}$ | **1/3 ≈ 0.3333** | | Optimal Velocity Ratio | $V_2/V_1$ | **1/3 ≈ 0.3333** | | Maximum Thrust Coefficient | $C_{T,\max}$ | **8/9 ≈ 0.8889** | | Swept Area of Rotor | $A$ | m² | | Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Betz Model The Betz Limit is derived under a strictly defined *idealized* framework. KTU examiners frequently test these assumptions. The model assumes: - **Incompressible, homogeneous, steady-state flow** of the fluid (air). - **Frictionless, hub-less rotor** (no rotational wake effects or viscous losses). - **Uniform wind velocity** across the entire rotor swept area (no wind shear). - **Infinite number of blades** (so the rotor acts as a uniform actuator disc). - **Static pressure** is uniform far upstream and far downstream of the rotor. - **No heat transfer** across the control volume boundaries. - The control volume is bounded by **stream tubes** that follow the air flow. - The rotor is represented as a **fictitious actuator disc** of zero thickness. ### 2.2 The Actuator Disc Model — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Define a stream tube:** Air approaching a wind turbine does not just pass through the rotor disc; it follows a curved boundary called a *stream tube*. The cross-section of this tube is *larger* downstream of the disc than upstream because air has slowed down. 2. **Apply conservation of mass (continuity equation):** The mass flow rate of air must be the same at the inlet, at the disc, and at the outlet of the stream tube. 3. **Apply conservation of momentum (linear):** The force exerted by the wind on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream. 4. **Apply conservation of energy (Bernoulli's equation):** The total mechanical energy of the air stream is conserved (assuming no friction, no heat loss, and no turbulent dissipation along the stream tube). 5. **Define the Axial Induction Factor (a):** This is a dimensionless number that quantifies the fractional reduction in wind speed at the rotor plane relative to the free-stream (upstream) velocity. $$V = V_1 (1 - a)$$ where $V_1$ is the upstream (free-stream) wind speed and $V$ is the velocity *at* the rotor disc. 6. **Define the wake velocity ($V_2$):** The far-downstream velocity of the air after passing through the turbine. It is related to the induction factor by: $$V_2 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$$ 7. **Define the Thrust Force ($F_T$):** The net horizontal force on the disc equals the mass flow rate times the velocity change: $$F_T = \dot{m} (V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ 8. **Define the Power Extracted ($P$):** The mechanical power extracted by the turbine equals the force times the velocity at the disc: $$P = F_T \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3 \cdot C_P$$ 9. **Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $a$:** Take $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 0$ and solve for the optimal induction factor. This yields $a = 1/3$. 10. **Substitute the optimal $a$:** This gives $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why a stream tube?** Air molecules don't all hit the rotor; the *useful* flow is the column of air that actually passes through. The stream tube isolates this flow. - **Why conservation laws?** They are the only universal laws of physics guaranteed to apply to any flow system. - **Why an induction factor?** It dramatically simplifies the algebra. Instead of dealing with three different velocities ($V_1, V, V_2$), we deal with one parameter $a$. - **Why maximize?** A turbine designer wants to extract *as much* power as possible. Calculus (setting derivative to zero) is the universal tool for finding maxima. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3$ | Total available power in undisturbed wind | W | | 2 | $V = V_1(1-a)$ | Velocity at rotor disc | m/s | | 3 | $V_2 = V_1(1-2a)$ | Velocity far downstream (wake) | m/s | | 4 | $C_P = \frac{P}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (efficiency of rotor) | dimensionless | | 5 | $C_P = \frac{1}{2}(1-a)[1+(1-2a)^2]$ | Expanded $C_P$ in terms of $a$ | dimensionless | | 6 | $C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Maximum theoretical efficiency (Betz) | dimensionless | | 7 | $a_{opt} = \frac{1}{3}$ | Optimal induction factor | dimensionless | | 8 | $C_T = 4a(1-a)$ | Thrust coefficient | dimensionless | | 9 | $C_{T,\max} = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889$ | Maximum thrust coefficient (at $a=1/3$) | dimensionless | | 10 | $F_T = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$ | Thrust force on rotor | N | | 11 | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$ | Power extracted as function of $a$ | W | | 12 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate through disc | kg/s | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wind Farm Design:** Engineers use the Betz limit as a benchmark. Any commercial turbine rated at $C_P > 0.45$ is considered *excellent*; reaching 0.50 is *world-class*. - **Turbine Performance Curves:** The Betz curve is plotted on every turbine's power curve to show how close the real machine gets to the ideal. - **Siting Studies:** Knowing $C_{P,\max}$ helps determine how much land area is needed for a given megawatt output. - **Tidal and Hydro Turbines:** The same Betz analysis applies to *any* open-flow turbine (tidal stream, run-of-river hydro, even Savonius vertical-axis designs). - **Diffuser-Augmented Turbines:** Researchers use the Betz limit as the baseline against which they measure gains from shrouds, diffusers, or concentrators. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Betz Limit We consider an ideal horizontal-axis wind turbine represented as a uniform actuator disc of area $A$ perpendicular to a steady, uniform, incompressible wind of free-stream velocity $V_1$. The air is captured within a stream tube of cross-sectional area $A_1$ upstream, area $A$ at the disc, and area $A_2$ far downstream, with corresponding velocities $V_1, V, V_2$. #### Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity) The mass of air entering the stream tube per second must equal the mass leaving per second. For incompressible flow: $$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A V = \rho A_2 V_2$$ > **Logic:** The air density $\rho$ is constant, and the same parcel of air passes sequentially through the three cross-sections. For simplicity, we will use the disc area $A$ (the air actually passing through the rotor is what's relevant): $$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$ #### Step 2 — Conservation of Momentum (Linear) Applying Newton's second law to the air within the control volume, the net force on the air equals the rate of change of momentum. The thrust force $F_T$ exerted by the disc on the air is: $$F_T = \dot{m} (V_1 - V_2)$$ Substituting the mass flow rate from Step 1: $$F_T = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ > **Logic:** The air enters at $V_1$ and exits at $V_2 < V_1$. The *loss* in momentum ($\dot{m} \cdot \Delta V$) is exactly the force the air exerts on the disc. By Newton's third law, the disc pushes the air backward (downstream) with this same force. #### Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy) Bernoulli's equation along a streamline states that the total mechanical energy per unit mass is constant in a frictionless, incompressible flow. Applying it between the far-upstream section (1) and the far-downstream section (2): $$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$ Rearranging for the static pressure difference across the disc: $$p_1 - p_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$ > **Logic:** Since the air decelerates, its kinetic energy decreases; by energy conservation, this energy must go *somewhere*. In an ideal, frictionless system, it converts to a rise in static pressure — this is the pressure drop across the rotor that drives the turbine blades. #### Step 4 — Express the Thrust in Terms of Pressure The thrust force is also equal to the net pressure difference times the rotor area: $$F_T = (p_1 - p_2) \cdot A$$ Equating with the momentum-based expression: $$(p_1 - p_2) A = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ Substituting the pressure difference from Step 3: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2) \cdot A = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ #### Step 5 — Simplify and Introduce the Induction Factor Factor the left side as a difference of squares: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho (V_2 - V_1)(V_2 + V_1) \cdot A = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ Since $(V_2 - V_1) = -(V_1 - V_2)$, we can write: $$\frac{1}{2}\rho \cdot [-(V_1 - V_2)] \cdot (V_1 + V_2) = \rho V (V_1 - V_2)$$ Cancel $(V_1 - V_2)$ from both sides (assuming non-trivial flow): $$-\frac{1}{2}(V_1 + V_2) = V$$ $$V = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ > **Logic:** This is the famous *Froude–Rankine theorem*: the velocity at the disc is the *arithmetic mean* of the upstream and downstream velocities. Now introduce the axial induction factor $a$: $$V = V_1(1 - a) \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$ Substituting into the Froude–Rankine equation: $$V_1(1 - a) = \frac{V_1 + V_1(1 - 2a)}{2}$$ Simplify the numerator: $$V_1(1 - a) = \frac{V_1(1 + 1 - 2a)}{2} = \frac{V_1(2 - 2a)}{2} = V_1(1 - a)$$ > **Logic:** This is a self-consistency check. The induction-factor definition is *fully consistent* with the Froude–Rankine theorem — a beautiful confirmation that our parameterization is correct. #### Step 6 — Derive the Power Extracted The mechanical power extracted by the turbine equals the thrust force times the velocity at the disc: $$P = F_T \cdot V = [\rho A V (V_1 - V_2)] \cdot V$$ Substitute $V_1 - V_2 = V_1 - V_1(1 - 2a) = 2aV_1$ and $V = V_1(1 - a)$: $$P = \rho A \cdot V_1(1 - a) \cdot 2a V_1 \cdot V_1(1 - a)$$ $$P = 2 \rho A V_1^3 \cdot a (1 - a)^2$$ #### Step 7 — Define the Power Coefficient The power coefficient is the ratio of extracted power to available wind power: $$C_P = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A V_1^3 a (1 - a)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3}$$ $$C_P = 4a(1 - a)^2$$ > **Logic:** This elegant four-term expression is the central result of the Betz theory. It contains only the induction factor $a$. #### Step 8 — Maximize $C_P$ Using Calculus To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to $a$ and set it to zero. Expanding $C_P$ first: $$C_P(a) = 4a(1 - 2a + a^2) = 4a - 8a^2 + 4a^3$$ Take the derivative: $$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 - 16a + 12a^2$$ Set to zero: $$4 - 16a + 12a^2 = 0$$ Divide by 4: $$1 - 4a + 3a^2 = 0$$ Factor as a quadratic in $a$: $$(1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$ This gives two solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, zero power) and $a = 1/3$ (the optimum). #### Step 9 — Compute the Maximum Power Coefficient Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into the $C_P$ expression: $$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2$$ $$= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2$$ $$= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9}$$ $$= \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.59259...$$ $$\boxed{C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$ #### Step 10 — Verify with the Second Derivative Test $$\frac{d^2 C_P}{da^2} = -16 + 24a$$ At $a = 1/3$: $$\frac{d^2 C_P}{da^2} \bigg\vert_{a=1/3} = -16 + 24 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = -16 + 8 = -8 < 0$$ > **Logic:** The second derivative is negative, confirming that $a = 1/3$ is a *local maximum* and not a minimum or inflection point. The Betz Limit is mathematically rigorous. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Thrust Coefficient The thrust coefficient is defined as: $$C_T = \frac{F_T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^2} = \frac{\rho A V (V_1 - V_2)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^2} = \frac{2 V (V_1 - V_2)}{V_1^2}$$ Substitute $V = V_1(1-a)$ and $(V_1 - V_2) = 2a V_1$: $$C_T = \frac{2 V_1(1-a) \cdot 2a V_1}{V_1^2} = 4a(1 - a)$$ At $a = 1/3$: $$C_{T,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889$$ ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard) > **Problem:** A wind turbine of rotor diameter 80 m operates at a wind speed of 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (a) the maximum theoretical power output, (b) the rotor disc velocity, (c) the wake velocity, and (d) the thrust force — all assuming Betz-optimal operation. #### Solution: **(a) Swept area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 80^2}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$ **(b) Available wind power:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 12^3$$ $$= 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728$$ $$= 5{,}321{,}504 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$ **(c) Maximum theoretical power (Betz):** $$P_{Betz} = 0.5926 \cdot 5.32 \text{ MW} \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$ **(d) Rotor disc velocity:** $$V = V_1(1 - 1/3) = 12 \cdot (2/3) = 8 \text{ m/s}$$ **(e) Wake velocity:** $$V_2 = V_1(1 - 2/3) = 12 \cdot (1/3) = 4 \text{ m/s}$$ **(f) Thrust force:** $$F_T = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2) = 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 8 \cdot (12 - 4)$$ $$= 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 8 \cdot 8 = 394{,}185 \text{ N} \approx 394.2 \text{ kN}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stream Tube / Actuator Disc Model ```mermaid graph TD A[Free-stream air
V1 velocity
Area A1] --> B[Stream tube contraction
Air accelerates into disc] B --> C[Rotor Actuator Disc
Area A = pi R squared
Disc velocity V = V1 1-a] C --> D[Stream tube expansion
Air decelerates in wake] D --> E[Far wake
V2 velocity
Area A2] C --> F[Mechanical Power Output
P = 16/27 x Pwind] C --> G[Thrust Force on Tower
FT = rho A V V1-V2] A -.Pressure p1.-> C C -.Pressure p2 lower.-> E style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#ffe066,stroke:#cc8800 style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style F fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600 style G fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Betz Derivation Flow ```mermaid graph LR INPUT[Input Free Stream
V1, rho, A] --> MASS[Apply Conservation of Mass
mdot equals rho A V] MASS --> MOM[Apply Conservation of Momentum
FT equals mdot V1 minus V2] MASS --> ENER[Apply Bernoulli Equation
p1 plus half rho V1 sq equals p2 plus half rho V2 sq] MOM --> FORC[Equate Pressure Force and Momentum Force
FT equals p1 minus p2 times A] ENER --> FORC FORC --> FROUDE[Derive Froude Rankine Theorem
V equals V1 plus V2 divided by 2] FROUDE --> IND[Introduce Induction Factor a
V equals V1 one minus a] IND --> POW[Derive Power Equation
P equals 2 rho A V1 cubed a one minus a squared] POW --> CP[Define Power Coefficient
CP equals 4a one minus a squared] CP --> DIFF[Differentiate CP with respect to a
Set derivative to zero] DIFF --> OPT[Optimal a equals 1/3] OPT --> BETZ[Maximum CP equals 16/27
Approximately 0.5926] OPT --> THRUST[Maximum CT equals 8/9] BETZ --> OUTPUT[Output Betz Limit Theorem] THRUST --> OUTPUT style INPUT fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style BETZ fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px style OUTPUT fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#ff6f00,stroke-width:3px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Power Coefficient vs Induction Factor Curve (Conceptual Plot) ```mermaid graph TD A[Start at a equals 0] --> B[CP starts at 0] B --> C[a increases] C --> D{a equals 1/3} D --> E[CP equals 16/27 approximately 0.5926 PEAK] E --> F[a continues to increase beyond 1/3] F --> G[CP decreases] G --> H[a approaches 1] H --> I[CP returns to 0] E --> J[BETZ LIMIT ZONE
Maximum theoretical efficiency] style E fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px style J fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#ff6f00 ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Table: Betz vs Real Turbines | Parameter | Betz Ideal | Modern HAWT (3-blade) | Modern VAWT (Darrieus) | Old Multiblade (Farm) | |---|---|---|---|---| | $C_{P,\max}$ (peak) | 0.593 | 0.45 – 0.50 | 0.30 – 0.40 | 0.20 – 0.30 | | $a$ at peak | 1/3 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.10 – 0.20 | | Typical $C_T$ | 0.89 | 0.70 – 0.85 | 0.50 – 0.70 | 0.40 – 0.60 | | Tip-speed ratio $\lambda$ | infinite | 6 – 8 (high speed) | 3 – 5 | 1 – 2 (low speed) | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **State and explain the Betz Limit. Why is it considered the theoretical maximum for wind energy conversion?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The Betz Limit states that no wind turbine can convert more than **59.3% (16/27)** of the kinetic energy in wind into useful mechanical energy. **Explanation (3 key points for full marks):** 1. **Definition:** The Betz Limit is the maximum achievable power coefficient ($C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$) for an ideal, frictionless, open-flow wind turbine operating under steady-state conditions. 2. **Origin:** It arises from simultaneous application of conservation of mass, momentum, and energy (Bernoulli's equation) to an idealised *actuator disc* model of a rotor. 3. **Why it's the maximum:** If the turbine extracts *more* than 59.3%, the air downstream slows excessively, causing a *blockage effect* that prevents new air from entering the rotor disc. Conversely, if less is extracted, the air passes through with too much residual kinetic energy (waste). The optimum balance occurs at an axial induction factor $a = 1/3$. > **Real-world relevance:** Practical turbines achieve $C_P$ between 0.35 and 0.50 due to blade drag, finite number of blades, tip losses, hub losses, and wake rotation. Modern 3-blade horizontal-axis wind turbines approach 0.50, getting within ~85% of the Betz limit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **What is the axial induction factor 'a' in wind turbine theory? What is its value at Betz optimum and what does it physically represent?** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** The axial induction factor $a$ is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the fractional reduction in wind velocity at the rotor plane relative to the free-stream (upstream) wind speed. It is defined as: $$a = \frac{V_1 - V}{V_1}$$ where $V_1$ is the free-stream velocity and $V$ is the velocity at the rotor disc. Equivalently, $V = V_1(1 - a)$. **Value at Betz optimum (1 mark):** $$a_{opt} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$ **Physical interpretation (1 mark):** At Betz optimum, the air at the rotor plane moves at exactly **two-thirds** (66.7%) of the upstream wind speed, and the wake (far downstream) air moves at exactly **one-third** (33.3%) of the upstream wind speed. Physically, $a$ represents how "much the rotor is working against the wind." A value of 0 means no extraction; a value approaching 1 means total blockage (no flow). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the *thrust* coefficient expression ($C_T = 4a(1-a)$) with the *power* coefficient expression ($C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$). The power coefficient has an extra factor of $(1-a)$ in the squared term. Examiners *will* deduct 2 marks for this. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert rotor diameter to radius when calculating swept area. The formula is $A = \pi D^2/4$, not $\pi D^2$. A single slipped decimal here costs 2–3 marks. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $C_{P,\max} = 0.59$ instead of the *exact* $16/27$. KTU expects the symbolic form. You will lose 1 mark for approximation-only. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the assumption list in derivations. The Betz derivation is only valid under specific idealisations. Listing assumptions is worth 1–2 marks in any 14-mark question. > - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing "downstream velocity at disc" ($V = V_1(1-a)$) with "far-wake velocity" ($V_2 = V_1(1-2a)$). These are two different velocities; the disc is *not* the same as the far wake. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the Betz Limit for an ideal wind turbine using the actuator disc theory. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-blade horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m, operating at a wind speed of 10 m/s in air of density 1.2 kg/m³. Assuming the turbine operates at Betz optimum: - (i) Calculate the maximum extractable power in kW. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Calculate the thrust force on the tower. **(2 marks)** - (iii) Comment on the practical limitations that prevent real turbines from achieving the Betz limit. **(2 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Steady, incompressible, uniform flow. 2. No friction, no heat transfer. 3. Infinite-bladed rotor → uniform actuator disc. 4. Static pressure uniform far upstream and far downstream. 5. No wake rotation. **Derivation (5 marks):** By continuity, the mass flow rate through the disc is $\dot{m} = \rho A V$. By momentum balance, the thrust force on the disc is: $$F_T = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$$ By Bernoulli's equation between sections 1 (far upstream) and 2 (far downstream): $$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$ The thrust also equals the pressure drop times area: $F_T = (p_1 - p_2) A$. Equating and simplifying leads to **Froude–Rankine**: $$V = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ Define the induction factor $a$: $V = V_1(1 - a)$, $V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$. Power extracted: $$P = F_T \cdot V = 2 \rho A V_1^3 a (1 - a)^2$$ Power coefficient: $$C_P = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4a(1 - a)^2$$ Maximize: $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 - 16a + 12a^2 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$. $$\boxed{C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Maximum power (3 marks):** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$ $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 10^3 = 1{,}696{,}459 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{Betz} = 0.5926 \cdot 1{,}696{,}459 \approx 1{,}005{,}378 \text{ W} \approx 1005.4 \text{ kW}$$ **(ii) Thrust force (2 marks):** $$V = V_1(1 - 1/3) = 10 \cdot (2/3) = 6.667 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_2 = V_1(1 - 2/3) = 10 \cdot (1/3) = 3.333 \text{ m/s}$$ $$F_T = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2) = 1.2 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 6.667 \cdot (10 - 3.333)$$ $$= 1.2 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 6.667 \cdot 6.667 \approx 150{,}797 \text{ N} \approx 150.8 \text{ kN}$$ **(iii) Practical limitations (2 marks):** - **Finite number of blades** → non-uniform disc loading → rotational wake. - **Blade tip losses** → pressure equalisation at tips → reduces $C_P$. - **Aerodynamic drag** on blades → energy dissipated as heat. - **Hub losses** → central part of disc has no blades. - **Wind shear and turbulence** → non-uniform inflow. - **Real-world wind turbines typically achieve $C_P$ between 0.35 and 0.50.** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** With the help of a clear diagram of the actuator disc model, explain the concept of stream tube, induction factor, and thrust coefficient. Derive expressions for the axial induction factor and power coefficient in terms of the velocities $V_1, V_2$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the significance of the Betz Limit in the design of horizontal-axis wind turbines. Compare the Betz limit for an open rotor with the modified Betz limit when a *concentrator/diffuser* is used around the rotor. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Conceptual + Derivation (7 marks):** **Diagram & Concepts (3 marks):** A stream tube is a tubular boundary of air flow that passes through the rotor. The cross-section of this tube contracts from $A_1$ far upstream, to $A$ at the disc, and then expands to $A_2$ far downstream because of mass conservation. The air slows from $V_1$ to $V$ at the disc, and then further to $V_2$ in the far wake. The pressure rises slightly inside the tube at the disc (Bernoulli effect). **Induction factor (1 mark):** $$a = \frac{V_1 - V}{V_1} \Rightarrow V = V_1(1 - a), \quad V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$ **Thrust coefficient (1 mark):** $$C_T = \frac{F_T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^2} = 4a(1 - a)$$ **Power coefficient derivation (2 marks):** Starting from $C_P = 4a(1 - a)^2$ and substituting $a = (V_1 - V)/(V_1)$, we can write: $$C_P = \frac{(V_1^2 - V_2^2)(V_1 + V_2 - 2V_2)}{(V_1^3)} \cdot \text{(equivalent forms)}$$ Or in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$ alone (substituting $a = (V_1 - V_2)/(2V_1)$): $$C_P = 4 \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{2V_1} \cdot \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2V_1}\right)^2 = \frac{(V_1^2 - V_2^2)(V_1 + V_2)}{2V_1^3}$$ **(b) Significance and Diffuser-Augmented Betz Limit (7 marks):** **Significance of Betz Limit (3 marks):** 1. **Universal benchmark:** No real turbine can ever exceed $C_P = 0.593$. It provides a *physical upper bound* on performance, similar to Carnot efficiency for heat engines. 2. **Design target:** Modern 3-blade HAWTs achieve $C_P \approx 0.45$–$0.50$ (75–85% of Betz). The Betz curve is the universal reference for evaluating turbines. 3. **Sizing tool:** Knowing $P_{max} = 0.593 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3$, engineers can size the rotor for a desired power output at a given wind regime. 4. **Tip-speed ratio selection:** At Betz optimum, $\lambda_{opt} \approx 8/3 \approx 2.67$ for an infinite-bladed rotor; real machines operate at $\lambda \approx 6$–$8$ for the *actual* $C_P$ peak. 5. **Informs control strategies:** Variable-pitch and variable-speed control aim to keep the turbine operating *near* the Betz $C_P$ peak across varying wind speeds. **Diffuser-Augmented Turbine (4 marks):** - A *concentrator* or *diffuser shroud* placed around the rotor can theoretically lift the limit *above* 16/27. The modified Betz coefficient is: $$C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \phi}$$ where $\phi$ is a small positive factor related to the diffuser area ratio. - For a diffuser expansion ratio $A_{exit}/A_{throat}$ of about 1.5–2.0, the limit can theoretically reach **70%–80%**. - **However**, practical issues (boundary layer separation, structural load, added drag, manufacturing cost) typically mean the *real-world* gains are only 20–40% over an open rotor of the same swept area. - Diffusers are most useful for **small wind turbines** and **tidal stream turbines** in low-speed flows, where even modest $C_P$ gains justify the cost. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Betz Limit Theory** - [x] **Betz Limit** = maximum theoretical $C_P$ = **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (59.3%)**. - [x] **Optimal induction factor** $a_{opt} = 1/3$. - [x] **Disc velocity** at Betz optimum: $V = (2/3)V_1$. - [x] **Wake velocity** at Betz optimum: $V_2 = (1/3)V_1$. - [x] **Froude–Rankine theorem**: $V = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ — the disc velocity is the arithmetic mean of upstream and downstream velocities. - [x] **Power coefficient formula**: $C_P = 4a(1 - a)^2$. - [x] **Thrust coefficient formula**: $C_T = 4a(1 - a)$, with $C_{T,\max} = 8/9$ at $a = 1/3$. - [x] **Available wind power**: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3$. - [x] **Extracted power at Betz optimum**: $P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3$. - [x] **Three conservation laws** are the pillars: mass, momentum, energy (Bernoulli). - [x] **Five key assumptions**: steady, incompressible, frictionless, uniform flow, infinite-bladed rotor. - [x] **Real turbines achieve** $C_P \approx 0.35$–$0.50$ (Betz efficiency ratio: 60%–85%). - [x] **Historical figures**: Albert Betz (1919), Frederick Lanchester (1915). - [x] **Power is proportional to $V^3$** — doubling wind speed increases available power by 8×. - [x] **Swept area is $A = \pi D^2 / 4$** (use radius², not diameter²). - [x] **Tip-speed ratio** at infinite-bladed Betz optimum: $\lambda_{opt} = 8/3 \approx 2.67$. - [x] **Common exam trap**: $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ (with squared term) ≠ $C_T = 4a(1-a)$ (linear). Memorize both. - [x] **Diffuser/concentrator** can theoretically raise the limit above 16/27 but at significant practical cost. - [x] **Lanchester-Betz-Joukowsky** triple names — examiners love asking who formulated it. - [x] **Second derivative test confirms** $a = 1/3$ is a maximum (negative curvature). - [x] **Betz applies to ALL open-flow turbines**: wind, tidal, run-of-river hydro, even Savonius rotors. - [x] **Blockage effect**: trying to extract more than 59.3% causes upstream air to be deflected around the disc, paradoxically *reducing* actual power.

topics.title: Tip Speed Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tip Speed Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR), denoted by the Greek letter $\lambda$ (lambda)**, is defined as the ratio of the tangential speed of the blade tip of a wind turbine to the undisturbed free-stream wind velocity incident on the rotor. Mathematically,
> $$\lambda = \frac{\text{Tip Speed}}{\text{Wind Speed}} = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$
> where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ is the rotor radius (m), $N$ is the rotor rotational speed (rpm), and $V$ is the upstream wind velocity (m/s). It is a **dimensionless quantity** and acts as the single most important operating parameter that determines the aerodynamic efficiency of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OESFT726 Module 2):** Tip Speed Ratio is covered under *Wind Energy Conversion Systems – Aerodynamics of Wind Turbines*. Examiners frequently test the relationship between $\lambda$, the power coefficient $C_P$, and the Betz Limit, so mastery of this topic is critical for both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **cycling on a flat road**:
- If the **wind is blowing strongly from behind**, you can pedal at a moderate cadence and still go fast — the wind is doing half the work. This is analogous to a wind turbine with a **LOW tip speed ratio**, where the blades rotate slowly relative to the wind (like a traditional Dutch windmill).
- Conversely, if the **wind is light and you need to sprint**, you must pedal extremely fast just to keep pace — this is analogous to a **HIGH tip speed ratio** turbine (like a modern 3-bladed utility-scale HAWT), where the blade tips slice through the air at speeds several times the actual wind velocity.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** $\lambda$ essentially tells you *how aggressively* the blade tips are "chasing" the wind. A higher $\lambda$ means the rotor is spinning faster (electrically efficient, fewer gear stages needed) but aerodynamically it means the blades are disturbing the air more — leading to a trade-off governed by the $C_P$–$\lambda$ curve.

## 1.3 Why Tip Speed Ratio Matters — Engineering Significance

1. **Determines $C_P$ (Power Coefficient):** The fraction of wind power captured by the rotor is a *function only* of $\lambda$ (and blade pitch angle $\beta$). This makes $\lambda$ the master design variable.
2. **Controls Betz Limit Approachment:** Only at the **optimum tip speed ratio $\lambda_{opt}$** does the turbine extract the **maximum theoretical 59.3% (Betz Limit)** of available wind power.
3. **Structural & Acoustic Implications:** A high $\lambda$ generates enormous centrifugal stress on the blade root and produces more aerodynamic noise — hence modern turbines cap $\lambda_{max}$ between **8 and 12**.
4. **Generator Coupling:** The TSR dictates the required gear ratio between the low-speed rotor and the high-speed generator.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse *rotor speed $N$* with *tip speed*. The tip speed is $N$ times the circumference ($2\pi R$), and $\lambda$ normalizes this by the wind speed — that is the crucial non-dimensionalization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Concept

A wind turbine rotor can be modelled as an **actuator disc** that extracts kinetic energy from the wind. The relative velocity of the air seen by any blade element depends on both the wind speed $V$ and the tangential blade speed $\omega r$. At the tip ($r = R$):

1. **Inflow wind arrives axially** with velocity $V$.
2. **Blade moves tangentially** with velocity $\omega R$.
3. The **angle of attack** of the air on the airfoil section is set by the ratio of these two velocities.
4. If the blade spins too fast ($\lambda$ too high) → the airfoil stalls → $C_P$ drops.
5. If the blade spins too slow ($\lambda$ too low) → most of the wind passes through undisturbed → $C_P$ drops.
6. Therefore, **an optimal $\lambda$ exists** where $C_P$ is maximized (typically $C_{P,max} \approx 0.4$–$0.48$ for real machines; Betz limit is $0.593$).

> [!NOTE]
> This is why wind turbines are **NOT** operated at a fixed rotational speed. Variable-speed operation with **pitch control** and **generator torque control** is used to keep $\lambda \approx \lambda_{opt}$ across the entire range of wind speeds between cut-in and rated wind speed.

## 2.2 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Air density (ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Betz Maximum $C_P$ | $C_{P,Betz}$ | **0.5926 (16/27)** |
| Betz coefficient factor | — | **16/27** |
| Optimal TSR (3-blade upwind) | $\lambda_{opt}$ | **6 – 8** |
| Optimal TSR (2-blade) | $\lambda_{opt}$ | **5 – 6** |
| Optimal TSR (American farm) | $\lambda_{opt}$ | **4 – 5** |
| Optimal TSR (Dutch traditional) | $\lambda_{opt}$ | **2 – 3** |
| Optimal TSR (Savonius / drag-type) | $\lambda_{opt}$ | **≈ 0.8 – 1.0** |
| Maximum design TSR (modern utility) | $\lambda_{max}$ | **8 – 12** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE THIS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V}$ | Fundamental definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30 V}$ | TSR in terms of RPM ($N$) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{2 \pi R N}{60 V}$ | Equivalent form (Hz basis) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{tip} = \omega R = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30}$ | Tangential blade tip speed | m/s |
| 5 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Available wind power | W (Watts) |
| 6 | $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_P(\lambda,\beta)$ | Actual captured power | W (Watts) |
| 7 | $C_P \le C_{P,Betz} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz upper limit | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\omega_{opt} = \dfrac{\lambda_{opt} \cdot V}{R}$ | Optimal angular velocity | rad/s |
| 9 | $N_{opt} = \dfrac{60 \lambda_{opt} V}{2 \pi R} = \dfrac{30 \lambda_{opt} V}{\pi R}$ | Optimal rotor RPM | rpm |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{B \cdot c}{2 \pi R}$ | Solidity (related but distinct parameter) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** Notice how the **30** in formula 2 appears. This comes from the conversion $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi N}{30}$. Examiners *love* asking students to derive this conversion — so memorize it cold.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind Farm Design:** Engineers select $\lambda_{opt}$ to size the gearbox and generator.
- **Control Strategy:** A variable-pitch controller modulates $\beta$ (pitch) to maintain $\lambda_{opt}$ as wind speed varies — this is the core of MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) in wind energy.
- **Turbine Classification:** TSR fundamentally classifies turbines into *high-speed* (HAWT) and *low-speed* (Savonius, Darius) categories.
- **Acoustic Engineering:** Noise emission scales with $V_{tip}^{5}$ — this directly limits $\lambda_{max}$ for residential installations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tip Speed Ratio from First Principles

**Given:**
- Rotor radius $= R$ (m)
- Rotor rotational speed $= N$ (revolutions per minute, rpm)
- Free-stream wind velocity $= V$ (m/s)

**To Find:** Expression for $\lambda$ in terms of measurable quantities $N$ and $V$.

### Step 1 — Relate angular velocity to RPM

A rotating blade completes $N$ full revolutions in 60 seconds. Therefore the **rotational frequency** in revolutions per second (Hz) is:

$$f = \frac{N}{60} \;\; [\text{Hz}]$$

Each revolution corresponds to an angular sweep of $2\pi$ radians. Hence the **angular velocity** is:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30} \;\; [\text{rad/s}]$$

### Step 2 — Tangential speed at the blade tip

A point on the blade at radius $r$ travels along a circular path of length $2\pi r$ per revolution. At the outermost tip ($r = R$):

$$V_{tip} = \omega R = \frac{\pi R N}{30} \;\; [\text{m/s}]$$

### Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio

The Tip Speed Ratio is defined as the ratio of the tangential tip speed to the undisturbed wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{tip}}{V} = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $\omega$

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most commonly asked derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme exams — worth 4 to 5 marks as a standalone Part A or as the first sub-part of a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A three-bladed horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. At a wind speed of 12 m/s, the rotor turns at 18 rpm. The optimum tip speed ratio for this turbine is 7.5. Determine:
1. The actual tip speed ratio $\lambda$ at the given operating point.
2. The actual tip speed $V_{tip}$.
3. The deviation from the optimum and the required rotor RPM to operate at $\lambda_{opt}$.

### Given Data
- Rotor diameter $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow$ $R = 40$ m
- Wind speed $V = 12$ m/s
- Actual rotor speed $N = 18$ rpm
- Optimum TSR $\lambda_{opt} = 7.5$

### Part (i): Actual Tip Speed Ratio

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$

Substituting $R = 40$ m, $N = 18$ rpm, $V = 12$ m/s:

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 18}{30 \times 12}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{720 \pi}{360} = 2 \pi \approx 6.283$$

> The rotor is operating at $\lambda \approx 6.28$, which is **below** the optimum of 7.5. This means it is spinning too slowly for the given wind speed.

### Part (ii): Actual Tip Speed

$$V_{tip} = \frac{\pi R N}{30} = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 18}{30} = \frac{720 \pi}{30} = 24 \pi \approx 75.40 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

### Part (iii): Required RPM for Optimum Operation

Rearranging the TSR formula for $N$:

$$N_{opt} = \frac{30 \, \lambda_{opt} \, V}{\pi R}$$

$$N_{opt} = \frac{30 \times 7.5 \times 12}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{2700}{40 \pi} = \frac{67.5}{\pi} \approx 21.49 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

> **Result:** The controller must increase rotor speed from 18 rpm to approximately **21.5 rpm** to capture maximum power at $V = 12$ m/s.

### Part (iv): Power Extraction at Both Operating Points

Assume $C_{P,max} = 0.40$ (typical real-world, accounting for mechanical/electrical losses below Betz limit). Swept area $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 40^{2} = 1600 \pi \approx 5026.55$ m².

$$P_{opt} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_P = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3} \times 0.40$$

$$P_{opt} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \times 0.40$$

$$P_{opt} \approx 2{,}128{,}680 \;\; \text{W} \approx 2.13 \;\; \text{MW}$$

For the off-optimum point, we approximate the loss by using the ratio of TSRs (illustrative linearization, not exact for real $C_P(\lambda)$ curves):

$$\frac{C_P(\lambda=6.28)}{C_P(\lambda=7.5)} \approx 0.92 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; P_{actual} \approx 0.92 \times 2.13 \approx 1.96 \;\; \text{MW}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A mere 16% drop in TSR (from 7.5 to 6.28) causes roughly an **8% loss in power capture**. This is why MPPT controllers are critical in modern utility-scale turbines.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table of TSR Regimes

| Regime | $\lambda$ Range | Aerodynamic Behaviour | Typical Machine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Very Low (Drag-driven)** | $0 < \lambda < 1$ | Wind speed > Tip speed; rotor is wind-powered. High solidity, low speed. | Savonius, cup anemometer |
| **Low (Mixed)** | $1 < \lambda < 3$ | Significant blade-wind interaction; partial lift. | Dutch traditional windmill |
| **Medium (Lift-driven, slow)** | $3 < \lambda < 5$ | Lift dominates; rotor approaches design efficiency. | American farm windmill |
| **High (Modern HAWT)** | $5 < \lambda < 8$ | Strong lift, optimal aerodynamic performance. | Modern 3-blade utility |
| **Very High (Specialty)** | $8 < \lambda < 12$ | High tip speed, noise, centrifugal stress. Limited structural feasibility. | Research / small high-RPM turbines |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tip Speed Ratio — Vector Decomposition at the Blade Tip

This diagram shows the velocity triangle that the airfoil section "sees" at the blade tip, which is the fundamental geometric basis for understanding $\lambda$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Wind["V = Free Wind Velocity\n(Axial, from left)"] -->|Relative inflow| TipNode
    TipNode["Blade Tip Point\non Rotating Rotor\nRadius = R"]
    Tangential["Vtip = omega x R\n(Tangential motion)"] -->|Perpendicular to wind| TipNode
    TipNode --> RelWind["W = Relative Wind\n(Wind seen by blade)\nW = sqrt V^2 + Vtip^2"]
    Angle["phi = flow angle\ntan phi = V / omega R = 1/lambda"]
    RelWind -.defines.-> Angle
    style Wind fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style Tangential fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    style TipNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style RelWind fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style Angle fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The relative wind vector $\mathbf{W}$ is the vector sum of the free wind $\mathbf{V}$ and the tangential tip velocity $\mathbf{V_{tip}}$. The angle $\phi$ between $\mathbf{W}$ and the rotor plane is the **inflow angle**, related to $\lambda$ by $\tan \phi = 1/\lambda$.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TSR Control Loop

This functional block diagram maps the engineering interactions between $\lambda$, rotor speed, generator torque, and pitch control in a modern variable-speed wind turbine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Speed V measured by Anemometer] --> B[Control Unit / PLC]
    R[Rotor RPM N measured by Encoder] --> B
    B -->|Compute lambda = piRN / 30V| C{Lambda vs Lambda_opt ?}
    C -->|lambda less than lambda_opt| D[Increase Generator Torque Demand\nto accelerate rotor]
    C -->|lambda greater than lambda_opt| E[Decrease Generator Torque Demand\nto decelerate rotor]
    C -->|lambda at lambda_opt| F[Hold Steady State\nMaximum Power Point]
    D --> G[Pitch Actuator and Converter]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Rotor Blade Pitch beta and Generator Torque]
    H --> I[New Rotor RPM N updated]
    I --> R
    B --> J[Power Output P = 0.5 rho A V cubed C_P of lambda beta]

    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000

    style F fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Variation of $C_P$ with $\lambda$ (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CurveShape["C_P vs lambda Curve for fixed pitch beta"]
    L0["lambda = 0\nC_P = 0\nRotor stationary"] --- L1["Rising limb\nC_P increases"]
    L1 --- L2["lambda = lambda_opt\nC_P = C_P_max\nMaximum Power Point"]
    L2 --- L3["Falling limb\nC_P decreases\nBlade stall begins"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** When asked to "sketch $C_P$ vs $\lambda$," always label (a) the axes, (b) the peak coordinates $(\lambda_{opt}, C_{P,max})$, (c) the rising and falling limbs, and (d) mention that $C_{P,max} < 16/27$ for real machines due to rotational wake, tip losses, and finite blade count.
