<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) of a wind turbine. State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) of a wind turbine is the ratio of the tangential velocity of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity approaching the rotor. It is given by
> $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$
> where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ is the rotor radius (m), $N$ is the rotor speed in rpm, and $V$ is the wind speed (m/s).
>
> **Significance:** (1) It is the **key operating parameter** that determines the power coefficient $C_P$ of the turbine. (2) For a given blade design and pitch angle, there exists an **optimum tip speed ratio $\lambda_{opt}$** at which $C_P$ is maximized and the turbine extracts the greatest fraction of incident wind power. (3) It governs whether the turbine operates in a drag-dominated, mixed, or lift-dominated regime, and hence dictates the **aerodynamic design classification** of the machine. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why is the tip speed ratio of a Savonius rotor close to 1, while a modern 3-bladed HAWT operates near $\lambda \approx 7$?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Savonius rotor is a **pure drag-type** vertical axis machine. Its blades are designed to be pushed by the wind rather than generate lift, so the rotor cannot rotate faster than the wind without the cupping faces "scooping" the wind backwards. The maximum achievable tip speed is therefore limited to approximately the wind speed itself, giving $\lambda \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$.
>
> A modern three-bladed HAWT, in contrast, uses **airfoil-shaped blades that generate aerodynamic lift**. Lift allows the blade to travel at a tangential velocity several times greater than the wind speed without the relative wind becoming misaligned. This permits high rotational speeds — with $\lambda \approx 7$ being the aerodynamic sweet spot that maximizes the lift-to-drag ratio and hence the power coefficient. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Choice Pattern:** Examiners provide an *either/or* choice between two 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below are **TWO complete, independent alternatives** matching this exact pattern.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**A. (a)** Define Tip Speed Ratio. Derive the expression $\lambda = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30 V}$ from the basic definition. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. At a wind speed of 10 m/s, the rotor rotates at 22 rpm. The optimal tip speed ratio is 6.5. Calculate the actual tip speed ratio, the actual tip speed, and the rotor RPM required to operate at the optimum tip speed ratio. Comment on the power coefficient at both operating points. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Tip Speed Ratio is defined as the ratio of the tangential speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity.

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{tip}}{V} = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Step 1: The rotor makes $N$ revolutions per minute, i.e., $\frac{N}{60}$ revolutions per second.
Step 2: Each revolution equals an angular sweep of $2\pi$ radians. Hence the angular velocity is:

$$\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30} \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

Step 3: The tangential velocity at the blade tip (radius $R$) is:

$$V_{tip} = \omega R = \frac{\pi R N}{30} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

Step 4: Substituting into the definition:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{tip}}{V} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$$

> $\lambda$ is dimensionless, since $\pi$ is dimensionless, $R$ (m) and $V$ (m/s) cancel the minute (converted to second via 30 in denominator). **(7 marks — full derivation shown)**

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 60$ m $\Rightarrow$ $R = 30$ m
- $V = 10$ m/s
- $N = 22$ rpm (actual)
- $\lambda_{opt} = 6.5$

**(i) Actual TSR (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{actual} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 30 \times 22}{30 \times 10} = \frac{22 \pi}{10} = 2.2 \pi \approx 6.91$$

**(ii) Actual tip speed (2 marks):**

$$V_{tip} = \frac{\pi R N}{30} = \frac{\pi \times 30 \times 22}{30} = 22 \pi \approx 69.12 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Required RPM for $\lambda_{opt}$ (2 marks):**

$$N_{opt} = \frac{30 \lambda_{opt} V}{\pi R} = \frac{30 \times 6.5 \times 10}{\pi \times 30} = \frac{650}{30 \pi} \approx 6.90 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

> **Note:** The required RPM (6.90) is *much lower* than the actual 22 rpm — meaning the rotor is **spinning too fast** for this wind speed. The control system must reduce generator torque to slow the rotor down.

**(iv) Comment on $C_P$ (1 mark):**

Since $\lambda_{actual} = 6.91$ is very close to $\lambda_{opt} = 6.5$, the turbine is operating near the maximum power coefficient. The actual $C_P$ is approximately $C_{P,max}$ (within a few percent), which is highly efficient. The rotor must simply be slowed by ~30% in torque-controlled deceleration to land precisely on the MPPT point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**B. (a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the variation of the power coefficient $C_P$ with tip speed ratio $\lambda$ for a fixed pitch angle. What is the physical significance of the peak of this curve? **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A 3-bladed wind turbine with rotor radius 25 m operates at 30 rpm in a 14 m/s wind. The optimum TSR is 7.0 and the maximum power coefficient is 0.42. Calculate (i) the actual tip speed ratio, (ii) the actual power output, and (iii) the percentage of the Betz limit being achieved. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Conceptual Sketch Question (7 Marks)

**Sketch (3 marks):**

```
C_P
 ↑
 │           •  C_P,max
 │         /   \n │       /       \n │     /           \n │   /               \n │ /                   \n •───────────────────────→  λ
 0      λ_opt
```

A proper answer must include:
- Bell-shaped curve.
- Y-axis labeled $C_P$ (power coefficient, dimensionless).
- X-axis labeled $\lambda$ (tip speed ratio, dimensionless).
- Peak marked at $(\lambda_{opt}, C_{P,max})$.
- $C_P = 0$ at both $\lambda = 0$ (no rotation) and $\lambda \to \infty$ (extreme stall).

**Explanation (3 marks):**
For very low $\lambda$, the rotor barely turns and the blades act like flat plates obstructing the wind — minimal energy extraction. As $\lambda$ increases, the relative wind vector tilts and the airfoils develop lift at favorable angles of attack, sharply increasing $C_P$. Beyond $\lambda_{opt}$, the relative wind angle becomes too shallow and the angle of attack exceeds the stall angle, causing flow separation and a rapid drop in lift. Hence $C_P$ falls.

**Significance of the Peak (1 mark):**
The peak $(\lambda_{opt}, C_{P,max})$ represents the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** — the single operating condition at which the turbine extracts the greatest fraction of the available wind power. Modern wind turbines use variable-speed generators and pitch control to track this peak across varying wind speeds. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 25$ m
- $N = 30$ rpm
- $V = 14$ m/s
- $\lambda_{opt} = 7.0$, $C_{P,max} = 0.42$
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**(i) Actual TSR (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{actual} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 25 \times 30}{30 \times 14} = \frac{25 \pi}{14} \approx 5.585$$

**(ii) Actual Power Output (3 marks):**

Swept area $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 25^{2} = 625 \pi \approx 1963.50$ m².

$$P_{actual} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_{P,max}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1963.50 \times (14)^{3} \times 0.42$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1963.50 \times 2744 \times 0.42$$
$$\approx 1{,}385{,}830 \;\; \text{W} \approx 1.39 \;\; \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Percentage of Betz Limit Achieved (2 marks):**

$$\text{Betz Power} = 0.593 \times P_{wind} = 0.593 \times \frac{P_{actual}}{C_{P,max}}$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1.386 \text{ MW}}{0.42} \approx 3.30 \;\; \text{MW}$$

$$\text{Betz Power} = 0.593 \times 3.30 \approx 1.957 \;\; \text{MW}$$

$$\text{\% Betz Achieved} = \frac{1.386}{1.957} \times 100 \approx 70.8\%$$

> The turbine is operating at approximately **70.8% of the Betz limit**, which is realistic for a well-designed modern machine. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **MOST COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES — AVOID THESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s):** Many students write $\lambda = \omega R / V$ and then plug in $N$ directly as if it were $\omega$. This yields a value **30 times too small** and results in **full mark deduction** on that sub-part.
> 2. **Mixing radius and diameter:** Using $D$ in the TSR formula instead of $R$ — always halve the diameter first.
> 3. **Confusing $C_P$ with efficiency $\eta$:** $C_P$ is *aerodynamic* efficiency relative to wind; total turbine efficiency is $C_P \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{elec}$.
> 4. **Units in the final answer:** TSR is dimensionless. If your answer has units, something went wrong.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in conceptual answers:** KTU 2024 Scheme awards marks specifically for a *neat, labeled* diagram in questions on $C_P$–$\lambda$ variation. A paragraph without a sketch typically loses **2 to 3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$ — ratio of tip speed to wind speed, **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Two equivalent forms:** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi R N / (30 V)$ — the second is the "exam form."
- ✅ **Tip speed in m/s:** $V_{tip} = \pi R N / 30$ — memorize the **30** in the denominator.
- ✅ **Optimal TSR values to remember:**
  - 3-blade HAWT → $\lambda_{opt} \approx 6$–$8$
  - 2-blade HAWT → $\lambda_{opt} \approx 5$–$6$
  - Savonius (drag) → $\lambda_{opt} \approx 0.8$–$1.0$
- ✅ **$C_P$–$\lambda$ curve is bell-shaped**, peaking at $\lambda_{opt}$, with $C_{P,max} < 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (Betz limit).
- ✅ **Betz Limit = 16/27 = 0.5926** — never achievable in practice; real turbines achieve $C_{P,max} \approx 0.40$–$0.48$.
- ✅ **High $\lambda$ ⇒ lift-dominated, fast rotation, gearbox-friendly, noisy.**
- ✅ **Low $\lambda$ ⇒ drag-dominated, slow rotation, high starting torque, silent.**
- ✅ **MPPT in wind** is implemented by keeping $\lambda \approx \lambda_{opt}$ via variable-speed generator + pitch control.
- ✅ **Structural limit:** $\lambda_{max} \approx 8$–$12$ due to centrifugal stress, blade tip noise ($V_{tip}^{5}$ scaling), and tower shadow.
- ✅ **For numerical problems:** always extract $R$ (not $D$), convert $N$ properly, and present $\lambda$ as a pure number.
- ✅ **Diagrams must be neat, labeled, and include the peak coordinates** — examiners grade visually.

> **End of Module 2 — Tip Speed Ratio Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, OESFT726 Renewable Energy Engineering.*

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Solidity?

**Solidity ($\sigma$)** is one of the most fundamental non-dimensional design parameters in wind turbine engineering. It is formally defined as the ratio of the total projected blade chord (the sum of all blade chord lengths measured in the plane of rotation) to the rotor's characteristic diameter or circumference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> Solidity ($\sigma$) is the ratio of the total blade area projected onto the rotor's plane of rotation to the swept area of the rotor. It quantifies how much of the rotor disc is "filled" with blades. Mathematically:
> $$\sigma = \frac{\text{Total Blade Chord}}{\text{Rotor Diameter}} = \frac{N \cdot c}{D}$$
> where $N$ = number of blades, $c$ = blade chord length (m), and $D$ = rotor diameter (m).

The same quantity can also be expressed for a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** using the swept area:
$$\sigma_{HAWT} = \frac{N \cdot c}{\pi R}$$

And for a **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)** like the Darrieus rotor:
$$\sigma_{VAWT} = \frac{N \cdot c}{D}$$

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Paddle Wheel Concept"

Imagine two rowing boats crossing a river. **Boat A** has a single, large, broad wooden paddle mounted on its oarlock. **Boat B** has four thin, narrow, racing-style oars rotating on a similar hub.

- **Boat A's paddle** sweeps a lot of water with each revolution — it is **high solidity**. It generates huge force but moves slowly.
- **Boat B's oars** sweep very little water per revolution — it is **low solidity**. They generate less force per revolution but can spin much, much faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Aha!" Moment**
> Solidity is essentially a **density-of-blades** parameter. A high-solidity rotor behaves like a *drag device* (paddle wheel) — it captures lots of torque but must turn slowly. A low-solidity rotor behaves like an *airfoil* (wing) — it must turn fast to generate useful lift, but it is aerodynamically efficient.

## 1.3 Classification of Turbines by Solidity

Wind turbines are universally classified by their solidity into three families, and this classification directly dictates which **power control strategy** is feasible for the machine.

| Solidity Range | Class | Aerodynamic Basis | Typical TSR ($\lambda$) | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $\sigma < 0.1$ | **Low Solidity** | Pure Lift | $6 \text{ to } 10$ (High) | Modern 3-blade HAWT, Darrieus |
| $0.1 \le \sigma \le 0.3$ | **Medium Solidity** | Mixed Lift–Drag | $2 \text{ to } 5$ | Multi-blade farm windmills, some Darrieus |
| $\sigma > 0.3$ | **High Solidity** | Pure Drag | $0.5 \text{ to } 1.2$ (Low) | Savonius, traditional paddle wheels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for Power Control**
> The choice of power control strategy — whether a turbine uses **stall regulation, pitch control, yaw control, or aerodynamic braking** — is **not arbitrary**. It is a direct consequence of the rotor's solidity. A low-solidity rotor cannot self-start and cannot shed power by stalling a tiny chord; it *requires* active pitch control. A high-solidity rotor cannot pitch fast enough; it is governed by passive stall or mechanical furling.

## 1.4 Power Control — The Big Picture

**Power control** in a wind turbine refers to the engineered set of actions taken to ensure that the electrical power extracted from the wind does **not exceed the rated capacity** of the generator, gearbox, and downstream electronics once the wind speed surpasses the **rated wind speed** ($V_{rated}$). Below $V_{rated}$, the turbine is operated to *maximize* power capture. Above $V_{rated}$, the turbine is operated to *limit* power capture.

The available power in the wind is:
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^3$$
where $\rho$ = air density (**≈ 1.225 kg/m³** at sea level), $A$ = swept area, and $V$ = wind speed.

Without power control, a turbine in a 25 m/s storm would attempt to extract about 23 times more power than at its 10 m/s rated point — destroying the machine. Solidity dictates *how* this protection is engineered.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Solidity-Based Power Control

The full power control logic of a wind turbine can be broken down into a five-step causal chain, each step physically driven by the rotor's solidity:

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic Loading of the Blade**
- **Low $\sigma$** blades are thin airfoils operating at high angle of attack ($\alpha$). They are extremely sensitive to $\alpha$ changes.
- **High $\sigma$** blades are broad flat plates or curved scoops. They are insensitive to $\alpha$ but produce massive drag.

**Step 2 — Torque Generation and Starting Capability**
- **Low $\sigma$** rotors have poor starting torque (lift = 0 at $\alpha$ = 0). They typically need a motor or a furled start.
- **High $\sigma$** rotors have excellent self-starting torque (drag is always non-zero).

**Step 3 — Available Control Levers**
- **Low $\sigma$**: Blade pitch ($\beta$), generator torque, and rotor speed ($\omega$) are all highly effective control knobs.
- **High $\sigma$**: Only mechanical interventions (brakes, furling, shutters) are practical. Pitching a wide chord blade is too slow and mechanically stressful.

**Step 4 — Response Time to Wind Gusts**
- **Low $\sigma$** + pitch control: Millisecond response — ideal for utility-scale, variable wind.
- **High $\sigma$** + stall/passive: Slower, second-scale response — acceptable for small, isolated machines.

**Step 5 — Power Quality and Grid Compliance**
- Low $\sigma$ + variable speed + pitch = smooth grid-compatible power.
- High $\sigma$ + fixed speed = simpler but with more flicker and torque transients.

## 2.2 The Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) — The Companion to Solidity

Solidity and Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) work as a *coupled pair*. TSR is defined as:
$$\lambda = \frac{\omega \cdot R}{V} = \frac{\pi \cdot R \cdot n}{V}$$

where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ = rotor radius (m), $n$ = rotational speed (rev/s), and $V$ = free-stream wind speed (m/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Coupled Optimum**
> For any given solidity, there exists an *optimal* TSR ($\lambda_{opt}$) at which the power coefficient $C_P$ is maximized. Empirically:
> $$\lambda_{opt} \approx \frac{1}{\sigma} \quad \text{(for high-solidity drag machines)}$$
> $$\lambda_{opt} \approx \frac{2\pi}{\sigma} \quad \text{(for low-solidity lift machines)}$$
> This coupling is the *deep reason* why high-solidity machines turn slowly and low-solidity machines turn quickly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete formula inventory** required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize these — they are the exam-day essentials.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{N \cdot c}{D}$ | Solidity (VAWT, Savonius) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{N \cdot c}{\pi R}$ | Solidity (HAWT) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R n}{V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Available wind power | W |
| 5 | $P_{turbine} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_P V^3$ | Extracted turbine power | W |
| 6 | $C_P = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (Betz limit $C_P \le 0.593$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_T = \dfrac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2}$ | Thrust coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda_{opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ | Optimum TSR (drag machines) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lambda_{opt} \approx \dfrac{2\pi}{\sigma}$ | Optimum TSR (lift machines) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_P \approx \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz maximum (independent of $\sigma$) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $P_{rated} = P_{wind}(V_{rated}) \cdot C_{P,max}$ | Rated electrical power output | W |
| 12 | $C_P = f(\lambda, \beta, \sigma)$ | General functional form of power coefficient | dimensionless |

> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Air density at standard conditions: **$\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³**
> - Betz limit: **$C_{P,max} = 0.593$** (16/27)
> - Below cut-in speed $V_{cut-in}$: $P = 0$ (brake engaged, control inactive)
> - Between $V_{cut-in}$ and $V_{rated}$: $C_P$ is held at its maximum
> - Above $V_{rated}$: $C_P$ is *deliberately reduced* by the chosen control strategy
> - Above cut-out speed $V_{cut-out}$: Turbine is shut down

## 2.4 The Five Canonical Power Control Strategies

The following five control strategies are the *canonical* options taught in KTU Module 2. Each is matched with the solidity class for which it is best suited.

### Strategy 1 — Passive Stall Regulation
- **Mechanism**: Blade is fixed-pitch. As wind speed rises, angle of attack rises until the airflow separates from the blade → blade "stalls" → lift drops sharply → power is naturally limited.
- **Solidity fit**: **Low to medium** (modern 3-blade HAWTs, $\sigma \approx 0.05\text{–}0.15$).
- **Pros**: No moving parts, fail-safe, low maintenance.
- **Cons**: Power is *not* perfectly constant; sensitive to airfoil contamination; no overspeed protection from the blades alone.

### Strategy 2 — Active Pitch Control
- **Mechanism**: Each blade is rotated about its long axis by a servo/pitch motor. The pitch angle $\beta$ is actively reduced in high winds to shed aerodynamic power.
- **Solidity fit**: **Low** (utility-scale HAWTs, $\sigma \approx 0.05\text{–}0.1$).
- **Pros**: Excellent power regulation, smooth output, primary overspeed protection.
- **Cons**: Expensive, complex, requires pitch bearings, hydraulic/electric actuators.

### Strategy 3 — Active Stall Control
- **Mechanism**: Blades are pitched *into* the wind (toward stall) at high wind speeds — the opposite of pitch control.
- **Solidity fit**: **Medium** (older stall-regulated upgrades, $\sigma \approx 0.1\text{–}0.2$).
- **Pros**: Uses simpler stall aerodynamics, can absorb gusts with stored rotational energy.
- **Cons**: High pitch actuator loads at high wind; rarely used in modern designs.

### Strategy 4 — Yaw Control
- **Mechanism**: The entire nacelle is rotated about the vertical tower axis to misalign the rotor with the wind, reducing effective swept area.
- **Solidity fit**: **High** (small HAWTs, $\sigma > 0.2$).
- **Pros**: Simple, effective for small machines.
- **Cons**: Cyclic yaw loads fatigue the tower; not used for utility-scale.

### Strategy 5 — Aerodynamic / Mechanical Braking & Furling
- **Mechanism**: Tail vane "furls" the rotor out of the wind, or tip brakes / air brakes deploy.
- **Solidity fit**: **High** (small VAWTs, Savonius, $\sigma > 0.3$).
- **Pros**: Extremely simple, cheap, no electronics.
- **Cons**: Abrupt, no fine control, not grid-friendly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check**
> For any KTU problem, you must first **determine the solidity class**, then **justify the choice of control strategy**. Examiners look for this two-step logic.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This System

The solidity-based choice of control strategy determines:
- Whether a wind farm can be connected to a **weak rural grid** (low-solidity, pitch-controlled turbines are preferred).
- Whether a turbine can survive a **typhoon-class storm** (high-solidity, furled machines survive better).
- The **levelized cost of energy (LCOE)** — pitch-controlled HAWTs achieve the lowest LCOE at utility scale.
- The **starting reliability** of off-grid machines (high-solidity machines start in any wind).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Coefficient $C_P$ from First Principles

The derivation of the power coefficient begins with the actuator disc model developed by **Albert Betz** (1919). We define the disc as a one-dimensional porous screen that extracts kinetic energy from the wind.

**Step 1 — Define the induced velocities**
Let $V$ = free-stream wind speed (m/s), $V_d$ = wind speed at the disc (m/s), and $V_w$ = wind speed far downstream in the wake (m/s). By conservation of mass through the stream tube:
$$V \cdot A = V_d \cdot A_d = V_w \cdot A_w$$
where $A$, $A_d$, $A_w$ are the respective stream-tube cross-sectional areas.

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation across the disc**
Across the disc, the pressure jumps by $\Delta p$ to provide thrust. Applying Bernoulli upstream and downstream (neglecting rotation):
$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2$$
$$\Delta p = p_1 - p_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_w^2 - V^2)$$

**Step 3 — Relate induced and free-stream velocities**
Empirically, the disc velocity is the arithmetic mean of upstream and wake velocities (this is the **Froude–Rankine assumption**):
$$V_d = \frac{V + V_w}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express the thrust force on the disc**
The thrust $T$ equals the pressure drop times the disc area $A_d$:
$$T = \Delta p \cdot A_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_d (V^2 - V_w^2)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the power extracted by the disc**
The power extracted equals thrust times disc velocity:
$$P = T \cdot V_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_d (V^2 - V_w^2) \cdot \frac{V + V_w}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by defining the induction factor $a$**
Define the axial induction factor $a$ such that:
$$V_w = V(1 - 2a) \quad \text{and} \quad V_d = V(1 - a)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and simplify**
Substituting into the power expression:
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_d V^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 8 — Define the power coefficient**
$$C_P = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 9 — Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $a$**
$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 - 8a(1-a) = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

Setting the non-trivial root to zero:
$$1 - 3a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into $C_P$**
$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Betz Limit**
> The maximum theoretical power coefficient any wind turbine — *regardless of solidity* — can achieve is **$C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$**. Real turbines achieve $C_P = 0.35\text{–}0.45$ due to rotational wake, blade tip losses, and finite solidity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Solidity–TSR Coupling for a Drag-Based Rotor

For a high-solidity drag turbine (e.g., Savonius, multi-blade farm windmill), we derive the optimum TSR.

**Step 1 — Torque on a single blade element**
For a drag blade moving at velocity $V_{blade} = \omega R$ through air of density $\rho$, the elemental drag force is:
$$dF_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c (\omega R)^2 dR$$

**Step 2 — Elemental torque**
$$dT = R \cdot dF_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c \omega^2 R^3 dR$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from hub to tip**
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c \omega^2 \int_0^R R^3 dR = \frac{1}{8}\rho C_D c \omega^2 R^4$$

**Step 4 — Subtract aerodynamic resistance of the wind (relative wind)**
The actual relative velocity experienced by the blade is $V_{rel} = \omega R - V$ (when the blade moves downwind). The *useful* torque is therefore:
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c (\omega R - V)^2 R$$

**Step 5 — Introduce solidity $\sigma$ and TSR $\lambda$**
With $\sigma = Nc / D$ (per blade equivalent) and $\lambda = \omega R / V$:
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c R^3 \frac{V^2}{R^2}(\lambda - 1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c R V^2 (\lambda - 1)^2$$

**Step 6 — Express power and $C_P$**
$$P = T \omega = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c R V^2 (\lambda - 1)^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda V}{R}$$
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D c V^3 \lambda(\lambda - 1)^2$$

Dividing by the wind power through a *blade-equivalent* area:
$$C_P = C_D \cdot \sigma \cdot \lambda(\lambda - 1)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $\lambda$**
Let $f(\lambda) = \lambda(\lambda - 1)^2 = \lambda^3 - 2\lambda^2 + \lambda$
$$\frac{df}{d\lambda} = 3\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 1 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 12}}{6} = \frac{4 \pm 2}{6}$$

Taking the physically meaningful root ($\lambda > 1$):
$$\lambda_{opt} = 1$$

**Step 8 — Solve for optimum solidity relation**
Since $C_P$ is proportional to $\sigma$, the highest $C_P$ is achieved when $\sigma$ is maximized (subject to the rotor still being able to rotate). For a Savonius with $C_D \approx 1.2$:
$$C_{P,max} \approx 1.2 \cdot \sigma_{max} \cdot 1 \cdot 0 \quad \text{... correction:}$$
$$C_{P,max} = C_D \cdot \sigma \cdot (1)(0)^2 = 0 \text{ at } \lambda = 1$$

This is a degenerate case. The true optimum is found by computing $C_P$ over a sweep of $\lambda$ for a given $\sigma$, and the empirical result is:
$$\boxed{\lambda_{opt} \cdot \sigma \approx 1}$$

This is the famous **solidity–TSR coupling law** for drag-based rotors: doubling the solidity halves the optimum TSR.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Matching Solidity to Control Strategy

The following decision matrix is the **analytical backbone** of every KTU 14-mark question on this topic. Memorize the structure, even if individual numbers vary.

| Parameter | Low $\sigma$ ($< 0.1$) | Medium $\sigma$ ($0.1\text{–}0.3$) | High $\sigma$ ($> 0.3$) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Rotor type** | Modern HAWT, Darrieus | Multi-blade farm, some Darrieus | Savonius, paddle wheel |
| **Driving force** | Lift (Bernoulli) | Mixed | Pure drag (Newton) |
| **$C_{P,max}$ achievable** | $0.40\text{–}0.48$ | $0.20\text{–}0.35$ | $0.10\text{–}0.20$ |
| **Optimum TSR $\lambda_{opt}$** | $6\text{–}10$ | $2\text{–}5$ | $0.5\text{–}1.2$ |
| **Self-starting?** | Poor (needs motoring) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Primary control** | Pitch + Variable speed | Stall or active stall | Furling / Mechanical brake |
| **Secondary control** | Yaw (small) | Dump load | Shutter / Coning |
| **Overspeed protection** | Pitch to feather | Tip brake | Side furling |
| **Cut-out speed (m/s)** | $25$ | $20\text{–}25$ | $15\text{–}20$ |
| **Typical application** | Utility-scale grid | Village / hybrid | Off-grid, water pumping |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Decision Rule**
> For any KTU numerical, follow this exact sequence:
> 1. Compute $\sigma = Nc/D$.
> 2. Compute $\lambda_{opt}$ from the empirical relation.
> 3. Identify the **control class** (low / medium / high).
> 4. Match the **canonical control strategy** from the matrix.
> 5. Verify with $C_P$ and $P_{rated}$ calculations.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Solidity to Control Strategy Decision Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the engineering decision process from blade design (solidity) all the way to the deployed control strategy. This is the architecture used in wind turbine OEM design offices.


# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "solidity" of a wind turbine. State the typical solidity range for a modern three-blade horizontal axis wind turbine (HAWT) and a Savonius rotor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Solidity ($\sigma$) is the ratio of the total projected blade chord to the rotor diameter. For a HAWT: $\sigma = \dfrac{Nc}{\pi R}$ where $N$ is the number of blades, $c$ is the chord length, and $R$ is the rotor radius. It is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the blade-area-to-swept-area ratio and determines whether a rotor is aerodynamically lift-driven or drag-driven.
>
> **Typical Ranges (1 mark):**
> - Modern 3-blade HAWT: **$\sigma \approx 0.03 \text{ to } 0.08$** (low solidity, lift-based).
> - Savonius rotor: **$\sigma \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.2$** (high solidity, drag-based).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Explain why the choice of power control strategy for a wind turbine is fundamentally linked to its solidity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The choice of power control strategy is fundamentally linked to solidity because **solidity determines the aerodynamic regime** of the rotor and hence which control levers are physically effective:

1. **Low-solidity** rotors (e.g., 3-blade HAWT, $\sigma < 0.1$) operate in the **lift regime** with thin airfoil blades. Lift force is highly sensitive to angle of attack and pitch angle, so **active pitch control** is the natural and efficient strategy. (1 mark)

2. **High-solidity** rotors (e.g., Savonius, multi-blade drag machines, $\sigma > 0.3$) produce **drag-based** thrust with broad blades. Pitching such a wide chord is mechanically impractical and too slow, so **passive mechanical interventions** — furling, shutters, or mechanical brakes — are used instead. (1 mark)

3. The **coupling law** $\lambda_{opt} \cdot \sigma \approx \text{const}$ means that as solidity changes, the operating speed and hence the response time available to the control system changes, forcing a different strategy. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Betz limit for the maximum power coefficient of a wind turbine from first principles using the actuator disc model. Show all steps clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3-blade horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m and a blade chord of 1.5 m. Determine the solidity and identify the most suitable power control strategy for this turbine. Justify your answer with reference to its aerodynamic regime, expected $C_P$ range, and the role of pitch control. Take air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and rated wind speed $V_{rated} = 12$ m/s.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Betz Limit (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Define the actuator disc model. The rotor is modelled as a porous disc of area $A_d$ that extracts kinetic energy from the wind stream tube. Let $V$ be the free-stream wind speed, $V_d$ the speed at the disc, and $V_w$ the far-wake speed. By conservation of mass through the stream tube: $V A = V_d A_d = V_w A_w$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Apply Bernoulli's equation upstream and downstream of the disc, neglecting rotational effects:
$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_w^2$$
Therefore the pressure jump across the disc is:
$$\Delta p = p_1 - p_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V^2 - V_w^2)$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* The thrust force on the disc is $T = \Delta p \cdot A_d$. The Froude–Rankine assumption gives the disc velocity as the arithmetic mean: $V_d = \dfrac{V + V_w}{2}$. The power extracted is $P = T \cdot V_d$.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Introduce the axial induction factor $a$ such that $V_w = V(1 - 2a)$ and $V_d = V(1 - a)$. Substituting:
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_d V^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* The power coefficient is therefore:
$$C_P = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A_d V^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $a$:
$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{1}{3}$$

*Step 7 (1 mark):* Substituting back:
$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

This is the **Betz limit** — the theoretical maximum power coefficient for any wind turbine.

---

**Part (b) — Solidity Calculation and Strategy Selection (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute the Solidity:*
Given: $N = 3$ blades, $c = 1.5$ m, $D = 80$ m, so $R = 40$ m.
$$\sigma = \frac{N c}{\pi R} = \frac{3 \times 1.5}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{4.5}{125.66} \approx 0.0358$$

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Classification:*
Since $\sigma = 0.0358 < 0.1$, the rotor is in the **low solidity** class.

*Step 3 (2 marks) — Aerodynamic Regime and $C_P$:*
This places the turbine in the **pure lift regime** with thin airfoil blades operating at high TSR. Expected optimum TSR: $\lambda_{opt} \approx 6\text{–}10$. Expected maximum power coefficient: $C_{P,max} \approx 0.40\text{–}0.48$.

*Step 4 (2 marks) — Power Control Strategy Justification:*
The most suitable strategy is **active pitch control with variable speed generator**. Justification:
- Lift-based blades have strong, well-defined lift-vs-pitch curves — pitching is highly effective.
- Low chord-to-radius ratio means pitch actuators can respond in milliseconds to wind gusts.
- Variable speed allows $C_P$ to track $C_{P,max}$ across all wind speeds below $V_{rated}$.
- The rated power: $P_{rated} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_{rated}^3 \cdot C_{P,max} = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(\pi \times 40^2)(12^3)(0.45) \approx 2.99$ MW — a typical utility-scale value, confirming the use of pitch control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and equations, explain the concept of solidity in wind turbines. Classify wind turbines based on solidity into three categories and tabulate the differences in terms of aerodynamic basis, typical TSR, self-starting capability, and dominant power control strategy.

**(b) [7 marks]** A small water-pumping windmill has 18 blades, each of chord 0.25 m, mounted on a rotor of diameter 4 m. Compute its solidity, identify its control class, and recommend the most appropriate power control strategy. Estimate the optimum rotor RPM at a wind speed of 8 m/s. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Concept and Classification of Solidity (7 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):*
Solidity ($\sigma$) is the ratio of the total projected blade chord length to the rotor diameter (for VAWTs) or circumference (for HAWTs):
$$\sigma_{VAWT} = \frac{N c}{D}, \quad \sigma_{HAWT} = \frac{N c}{\pi R}$$

*Tabular Classification (5 marks):*

| Parameter | Low Solidity | Medium Solidity | High Solidity |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Range | $\sigma < 0.1$ | $0.1 \le \sigma \le 0.3$ | $\sigma > 0.3$ |
| Aerodynamic basis | Pure lift | Mixed lift-drag | Pure drag |
| Typical TSR | $6 \text{ to } 10$ | $2 \text{ to } 5$ | $0.5 \text{ to } 1.2$ |
| Self-starting | Poor (needs motoring) | Moderate | Excellent |
| Dominant control | Active pitch + variable speed | Stall or active stall | Furling + mechanical brake |
| Example | 3-blade utility HAWT | Multi-blade farm mill | Savonius rotor |
| $C_{P,max}$ | $0.40 \text{ to } 0.48$ | $0.20 \text{ to } 0.35$ | $0.10 \text{ to } 0.20$ |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical on High-Solidity Water-Pumping Windmill (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute Solidity:*
Given: $N = 18$ blades, $c = 0.25$ m, $D = 4$ m. Treating as a multi-blade farm mill (HAWT-like):
$$\sigma = \frac{N c}{\pi R} = \frac{18 \times 0.25}{\pi \times 2} = \frac{4.5}{6.283} \approx 0.716$$

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Classification:*
Since $\sigma = 0.716 > 0.3$, this is a **high-solidity** rotor in the **pure drag regime**.

*Step 3 (2 marks) — Control Strategy:*
The recommended control strategy is **passive mechanical furling with a side vane** (or shutter control). Justification:
- The 18 broad blades produce huge drag torque but cannot be pitched quickly.
- The machine is a small, isolated water-pumping unit — grid compliance and smooth regulation are not required.
- A spring-loaded furling tail vane turns the rotor out of the wind above a preset wind speed, providing cheap and reliable overspeed protection.
- A simple mechanical (disc or drum) brake is added as a shutdown mechanism.

*Step 4 (2 marks) — Optimum Rotor RPM:*
For a high-solidity drag rotor, $\lambda_{opt} \approx 1$ (from the coupling law). At $V = 8$ m/s, $D = 4$ m:
$$\lambda_{opt} = \frac{\pi D n}{V} \Rightarrow n = \frac{\lambda_{opt} \cdot V}{\pi D} = \frac{1 \times 8}{\pi \times 4} = \frac{8}{12.566} \approx 0.637 \text{ rev/s}$$
$$N_{rpm} = 0.637 \times 60 \approx 38.2 \text{ rpm}$$

This low RPM is consistent with a high-solidity drag machine driving a piston pump or similar water-pumping load.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi$ in the denominator:** Many students write $\sigma = Nc/D$ for a HAWT, which is the VAWT formula. Always check whether the rotor axis is horizontal or vertical before writing the formula. (Penalty: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up TSR and angular velocity:** $\lambda$ is dimensionless — do not write it in rad/s or rpm. Convert: $\lambda = \pi D n / V$ with $n$ in rev/s.
> 3. **Confusing Betz limit with $C_P$ of real machines:** Betz gives the *theoretical maximum*. Real machines achieve $C_P$ in the range $0.20\text{–}0.45$ depending on solidity.
> 4. **Recommending pitch control for high-solidity rotors:** This is physically impossible due to actuator speed and blade inertia. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this mismatch.
> 5. **Skipping units in numericals:** Always state units for $P$ (W or kW), $V$ (m/s), $R$ or $D$ (m), and $\sigma$ (dimensionless).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **Solidity** = ratio of total blade chord to rotor characteristic length. For HAWT: $\sigma = Nc/(\pi R)$. For VAWT/Savonius: $\sigma = Nc/D$.
- **Three solidity classes:** Low ($\sigma < 0.1$, lift), Medium ($0.1\text{–}0.3$, mixed), High ($\sigma > 0.3$, drag).
- **Betz limit:** $C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — universal upper bound, *independent of solidity*.
- **TSR:** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi R n / V$. Higher $\lambda$ = faster spin.
- **Coupling law:** $\lambda_{opt} \cdot \sigma \approx \text{const}$ → high $\sigma$ means low optimum TSR and vice versa.
- **Power in wind:** $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$. **Power extracted:** $P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_P V^3$.
- **Five control strategies:** Passive stall, Active pitch, Active stall, Yaw, Aerodynamic/mechanical braking & furling.
- **Strategy–solidity match:**
  - *Low $\sigma$* → Pitch + Variable Speed (utility HAWTs).
  - *Medium $\sigma$* → Stall / Active Stall / Dump Load.
  - *High $\sigma$* → Furling + Mechanical Brake (small, off-grid).
- **Self-starting:** Inversely related to aerodynamic cleverness. High-solidity drag machines start in any wind; low-solidity lift machines do not.
- **Real $C_P$ ranges:** Low $\sigma$ achieves 0.40–0.48; Medium 0.20–0.35; High 0.10–0.20.
- **Air density (standard):** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- **Always classify first, then choose control.** This is the examiner's golden scoring sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated electromechanical apparatus designed to harvest the kinetic energy present in moving air masses (wind) and convert it into usable electrical energy through the coordinated operation of aerodynamic rotors, mechanical drive trains, and electrical generators. The fundamental conversion chain follows the energy transformation sequence:

> **Kinetic Energy of Wind → Mechanical Rotational Energy → Electrical Energy**

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, WECS encompasses all subsystems required to capture, condition, convert, and deliver wind-derived power to the grid or to isolated loads, including the **rotor (prime mover)**, **transmission/gearbox**, **generator**, **controller**, and the **supporting tower (nacelle structure)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The Basic Principles of WECS explicitly cover the origin of wind (solar-driven atmospheric circulation), the **Power in the Wind** equation, **Betz's Law and the Betz Limit**, the **Aerodynamic Power Coefficient (Cp)**, the **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)**, and the fundamental classification of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a river holding a paddle wheel. The faster the river flows and the larger the wheel you can intercept, the more water energy you can capture. However, if your wheel blocks the river completely, the water stops behind it and no more water passes through—you actually get *zero* energy because nothing is moving. This is precisely the dilemma faced by a wind turbine.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "River of Air" Analogy:**
> Think of wind as an invisible, free-flowing river of air. A wind turbine is a "paddle wheel" placed in this river. If the turbine extracts *all* the kinetic energy, the air would have to stop on the downstream side—which is physically impossible since the air must keep flowing. The **German physicist Albert Betz** proved in 1919 that the maximum fraction of wind energy that can be extracted by an *ideal* (lossless) turbine is **16/27 ≈ 59.3%**, now known as the **Betz Limit** or **Betz Coefficient (Cp,max)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Air Density (at sea level, 15°C) | **ρ** | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Swept Area of Rotor | **A** | m² |
| Wind Velocity | **v** | m/s |
| Maximum Power Coefficient (Betz Limit) | **Cp,max** | **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (dimensionless)** |
| Tip Speed Ratio | **λ** | dimensionless |
| Gravitational Acceleration | g | 9.81 m/s² |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | Patm | 101.325 kPa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Wind

Wind originates from the **non-uniform heating of the Earth's surface by solar radiation**. The sequence of energy flow is:

1. **Solar radiation** → differentially heats land and water masses.
2. **Temperature gradient** → creates pressure differences in the atmosphere.
3. **Pressure gradient force** → drives air from high-pressure zones to low-pressure zones.
4. **Kinetic energy of moving air (wind)** → harvested by a WECS.

Approximately **2% of the total solar energy incident on Earth is converted into wind energy**, but only a small fraction is practically extractable.

## 2.2 Available Power in the Wind

The kinetic energy of a mass *m* of air moving with velocity *v* is given by classical Newtonian mechanics:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

The mass flow rate of air passing through a cross-sectional area *A* perpendicular to the wind direction is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

Multiplying the mass flow rate by the specific kinetic energy yields the **Power in the Wind**:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the cube of velocity?**
> The cubic dependence on wind speed means that if the wind speed **doubles**, the available power increases by a factor of **8**. This makes wind site selection (high-wind zones) and accurate anemometer measurements critically important for WECS performance forecasting.

## 2.3 The Actuator Disk Concept (1-D Momentum Theory)

To mathematically derive the maximum extractable power, we invoke **Froude-Rankine Actuator Disk Theory**, which models the wind turbine as an idealized porous disk that extracts energy through a pressure drop.

Let:
- **v₁** = upstream (free-stream) wind velocity
- **v** = wind velocity at the disk plane
- **v₂** = downstream (wake) wind velocity

By the continuity equation (mass conservation for incompressible flow):
$$A_1 v_1 = A v = A_2 v_2$$

By the **Froude-Rankine theorem**, the velocity at the disk equals the arithmetic mean of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

The thrust force on the disk is:

$$F = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

The power extracted is:

$$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v = \rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2)$$

## 2.4 The Power Coefficient (Cp) and Betz's Law

The **Power Coefficient** is the ratio of the actual power extracted by the turbine to the total power available in the undisturbed wind:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$

Substituting the actuator disk relations and using an induction factor *a* (where $v = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$), we obtain:

$$C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to *a* and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 - 8a(1-a) = 0$$

$$(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 0 \Rightarrow (1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

The non-trivial solution is $a = \frac{1}{3}$.

Substituting $a = 1/3$ back into the Cp equation:

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Betz's Law (1919):** No ideal wind turbine can convert more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)** of the kinetic energy in wind into mechanical energy. This is a *fundamental thermodynamic-type limit* arising from mass and momentum conservation—it cannot be exceeded by any design, regardless of blade count, shape, or material.

## 2.5 The Tip Speed Ratio (λ)

The **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** is the ratio of the tangential speed of the rotor blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v_1} = \frac{\pi D n}{v_1}$$

Where:
- **ω** = angular velocity of rotor (rad/s)
- **R** = rotor radius (m)
- **D** = rotor diameter (m)
- **n** = rotational speed (rev/s)
- **v₁** = upstream wind speed (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implications of TSR:**
> - **High TSR (λ ≈ 6–8):** Two- and three-bladed **HAWTs** (fast, slender blades). High efficiency.
> - **Low TSR (λ ≈ 1–2):** **VAWTs** and multi-bladed farm windmills (drag-based). Lower aerodynamic efficiency but self-starting and omnidirectional.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Law | Equation | Engineering Use / Significance |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Power in the Wind** | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Determines site-specific energy potential. |
| 2 | **Power Coefficient** | $C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ | Measures aerodynamic efficiency. |
| 3 | **Betz Limit** | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Absolute theoretical upper bound. |
| 4 | **Induction Factor Relation** | $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ | Used to plot Cp vs. a curve. |
| 5 | **Optimal Induction Factor** | $a_{opt} = \frac{1}{3}$ | Yields Betz limit. |
| 6 | **Tip Speed Ratio** | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}$ | Design parameter for blade aerodynamics. |
| 7 | **Torque Coefficient** | $C_t = \frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^2 R}$ | Used in structural load analysis. |
| 8 | **Energy in Wind per Day** | $E = P_{wind} \times 24 \text{ h}$ | Daily energy potential estimate. |
| 9 | **Swept Area of HAWT** | $A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Used in power equation. |
| 10 | **Wind Power Density** | $P/A = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ | Site characterization (W/m²). |
| 11 | **Wake Velocity Ratio** | $v/v_1 = (1-2a)$ | Downstream wind recovery. |
| 12 | **Force on Actuator Disk** | $F = 2\rho A v_1^2 a(1-a)$ | Used to compute thrust loads. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction:** When writing Cp (such as $\vert C_p \vert$ for absolute value) in your exam scripts, use the **\vert** or **\mid** LaTeX commands, not the bare vertical bar `|`, to avoid markdown table rendering errors.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles discussed form the **design foundation** for:
- **Wind farm pre-feasibility studies** (using the power-in-wind equation with Weibull statistics).
- **Rotor blade aerodynamic profiling** (using BEM theory that builds upon actuator disk theory).
- **Pitch and yaw control algorithm development** (regulating Cp and λ to the optimum setpoint).
- **IEC 61400-1 design class selection** (turbine class depends on reference wind speed and turbulence intensity).
- **Offshore floating turbine dynamics** (where aerodynamic loading interacts with hydrodynamic restoring forces).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Power in the Wind

**Step 1: Define the system.**
Consider a cylindrical column of air of cross-sectional area *A* moving with uniform velocity *v* perpendicular to the area.

**Step 2: Compute the mass flow rate.**
The volume of air passing per unit time is $A v$. Multiplying by the air density $\rho$ gives the mass per unit time:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

**Step 3: Recall classical kinetic energy.**
Newton's kinetic energy for a moving mass *m* moving with velocity *v* is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the specific kinetic energy per unit mass.**

$$e_k = \frac{E_k}{m} = \frac{1}{2}v^2$$

**Step 5: Multiply mass flow rate by specific energy.**

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times e_k = (\rho A v) \times \left(\frac{1}{2}v^2\right) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$$

**Step 6: Units check.**
$$[\rho] = \frac{kg}{m^3}, \quad [A] = m^2, \quad [v] = \frac{m}{s}$$
$$P_{wind} = \frac{kg}{m^3} \cdot m^2 \cdot \frac{m^3}{s^3} = \frac{kg \cdot m^2}{s^3} = \text{Watts} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Betz's Law via Actuator Disk Theory

**Step 1: Set up the control volume.**
Define a stream tube enclosing the turbine rotor. Upstream (far away) the wind is at velocity $v_1$ and pressure $p_1$. Downstream (far wake) the wind is at velocity $v_2$ and pressure recovers to $p_1$ (Bernoulli's principle).

**Step 2: Apply the continuity equation.**
For an incompressible, steady flow through the stream tube of cross-section $A_1$ (upstream), $A$ (disk plane), and $A_2$ (downstream):

$$A_1 v_1 = A v = A_2 v_2$$

**Step 3: Apply the Froude-Rankine momentum theorem.**
This theorem states that the velocity at the actuator disk is the arithmetic mean of the upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v_2$ in terms of $v$ and $v_1$.**

$$2v = v_1 + v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v - v_1$$

**Step 5: Apply Newton's second law to compute the thrust force.**
The thrust force equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 6: Substitute $v_2$ and simplify.**

$$F = \rho A v \left[v_1 - (2v - v_1)\right] = \rho A v (2v_1 - 2v) = 2\rho A v (v_1 - v)$$

**Step 7: Compute the power extracted by the turbine.**

$$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v = 2\rho A v^2 (v_1 - v)$$

**Step 8: Introduce the axial induction factor *a*.**
By definition, the velocity at the disk is reduced from the free-stream value by a fraction *a*:

$$v = v_1(1 - a)$$

**Step 9: Express $v_2$ in terms of $v_1$ and *a*.**

$$v_2 = 2v_1(1-a) - v_1 = v_1(2 - 2a - 1) = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

**Step 10: Substitute into the power expression.**

$$P_{extracted} = 2\rho A \left[v_1(1-a)\right]^2 \left[v_1 - v_1(1-a)\right] = 2\rho A v_1^3 (1-a)^2 a$$

**Step 11: Write the total available power in the wind.**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3$$

**Step 12: Form the power coefficient ratio.**

$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A v_1^3 a(1-a)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 13: Maximize $C_p$ with respect to *a*.**

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a)$$

**Step 14: Set the derivative to zero.**

$$4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

This gives $a = 1$ (trivial: no power) or $a = 1/3$ (non-trivial optimum).

**Step 15: Verify it is a maximum via the second derivative.**
The function is zero at $a=0$ and $a=1$, with a positive peak in between; therefore $a = 1/3$ yields the maximum.

**Step 16: Substitute $a = 1/3$ into the Cp equation.**

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

**Step 17: Final numerical value.**

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} = 0.59259... \approx 0.593$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = 0.593 \text{ (Betz Limit)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cp vs Induction Factor

| Induction Factor (a) | $v/v_1 = (1-a)$ | $v_2/v_1 = (1-2a)$ | $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0.00 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.0000 | Unloaded rotor (no extraction) |
| 0.10 | 0.900 | 0.800 | 0.2916 | Lightly loaded |
| 0.20 | 0.800 | 0.600 | 0.4096 | Moderately loaded |
| **0.333** | **0.667** | **0.334** | **0.5926** | **★ BETZ LIMIT (Maximum)** |
| 0.40 | 0.600 | 0.200 | 0.4320 | Beyond optimum (stall regime) |
| 0.50 | 0.500 | 0.000 | 0.2500 | Wake stops (theoretical edge) |
| 0.60 | 0.400 | -0.200 | 0.1536 | Turbulent wake (unphysical region) |
| 1.00 | 0.000 | -1.000 | 0.0000 | Fully blocked (no flow, no power) |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **asymmetry** of the Cp curve. Modern Variable Pitch Wind Turbines operate just *below* $a = 1/3$ to avoid entering the turbulent wake regime (where induction factors > 0.5 produce reverse flow and significant structural fatigue).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m and operates at a wind speed of 12 m/s at an air density of 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate:
(a) The power available in the wind.
(b) The maximum power that can be extracted (Betz limit).
(c) The actual power extracted if the turbine operates at $C_p = 0.40$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Power in the wind**
Swept area:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (b): Maximum extractable power (Betz limit)**

$$P_{max} = C_{p,max} \times P_{wind} = 0.593 \times 5.32 \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (c): Actual power at Cp = 0.40**

$$P_{actual} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Stream Wind v1] --> B[Wind Rotor Blades]
    B --> C[Hub Assembly]
    C --> D[Main Shaft Low Speed]
    D --> E[Gearbox Step Up]
    E --> F[High Speed Shaft]
    F --> G[Generator Rotor]
    G --> H[Power Electronics Converter]
    H --> I[Transformer Step Up]
    I --> J[Electrical Grid or Load Bank]
    K[Yaw Motor Assembly] --> C
    L[Pitch Control System] --> B
    M[Anemometer and Wind Vane] --> N[SCADA Controller]
    N --> L
    N --> K
    N --> H
    O[Tower Structure Nacelle Support] --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The **left side** represents the *energy conversion chain* (aerodynamic → mechanical → electrical). The **bottom loop** represents the *control feedback system* (sensors → SCADA → actuators) that maintains the rotor at its optimal Cp by adjusting blade pitch, nacelle yaw, and generator torque.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Energy Conversion Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage1 Solar Heating of Earth] --> B[Stage2 Atmospheric Pressure Gradient]
    B --> C[Stage3 Kinetic Energy of Wind]
    C --> D[Stage4 Aerodynamic Lift on Blades]
    D --> E[Stage5 Rotational Mechanical Energy]
    E --> F[Stage6 Electrical Energy at Generator Terminals]
    F --> G[Stage7 Grid Compatible AC Power]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cp vs Tip Speed Ratio Curve (Typical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cp_Lambda_Profile["Cp vs Lambda Curve Typical HAWT"]
        A1[Lambda 1 Cp 0.10 Low] --> A2[Lambda 2 Cp 0.20]
        A2 --> A3[Lambda 4 Cp 0.38]
        A3 --> A4[Lambda 6 Cp 0.48]
        A4 --> A5[Lambda 7 Cp 0.50 Peak]
        A5 --> A6[Lambda 8 Cp 0.47 Dropping]
        A6 --> A7[Lambda 10 Cp 0.30 Stall]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** Modern three-bladed utility-scale HAWTs are designed to operate near the **peak of the Cp-λ curve** (typically λ_opt ≈ 7–8). Variable-pitch control dynamically shifts the operating point to track the peak as wind speed changes.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: WECS Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WECS[Wind Energy Conversion System] --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine]
    WECS --> VAWT[Vertical Axis Wind Turbine]
    HAWT --> UPH[Upwind Design]
    HAWT --> DWH[Downwind Design]
    HAWT --> BL2[2 Blade]
    HAWT --> BL3[3 Blade Most Common]
    HAWT --> BLM[Multi Blade Farm Pump]
    VAWT --> SAV[Savonius Drag Type]
    VAWT --> DRV[Darrieus Egg Beater Lift Type]
    VAWT --> GVR[Giromill Variable Pitch]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Wind Energy Conversion System. State Betz's Law. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated system that converts the kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy through a coordinated arrangement of a rotor, gearbox, generator, controller, and support tower.

**Betz's Law (1919):** The maximum fraction of kinetic energy in wind that can be extracted by an ideal, frictionless wind turbine is **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)**. This is known as the **Betz Limit** and is denoted as $C_{p,max} = 0.593$. The corresponding optimum axial induction factor is $a = 1/3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1.5) + Betz Limit statement with value (1.0) + Optimum induction factor (0.5).

### Question 2: Define Tip Speed Ratio. What is its significance in WECS design? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)** is defined as the ratio of the tangential velocity of the rotor blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D n}{v}$$

**Significance in WECS Design:**
1. It determines the **aerodynamic efficiency** (Cp) of the turbine. Each turbine design has an **optimum λ** at which Cp is maximum.
2. **High λ (6–8)** → low blade solidity → high-speed slender blades (utility-scale HAWTs).
3. **Low λ (1–2)** → high blade solidity → drag-based, self-starting turbines (VAWTs, water-pumping windmills).
4. It is the **primary design parameter** used in the selection of blade airfoil profiles and pitch control algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the power available in the wind. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

Step 1: Consider a cylindrical control volume of air with cross-sectional area *A* perpendicular to the wind direction, moving with uniform velocity *v*.

Step 2: The mass flow rate of air through the area is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

Step 3: The specific kinetic energy of air per unit mass is:

$$e_k = \frac{1}{2}v^2$$

Step 4: The power available in the wind equals the mass flow rate multiplied by the specific kinetic energy:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \cdot e_k = (\rho A v) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}v^2\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 \text{ Watts}}$$

**Explanation of cubic dependence:** Since the available power varies as the cube of wind velocity, a doubling of wind speed yields an **eight-fold** increase in available power. This cubic sensitivity makes accurate wind resource assessment and high-wind site selection critical for WECS deployment.

**(b) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** has its rotor shaft oriented horizontally and parallel to the ground. Its principle of operation is as follows:

1. **Aerodynamic Lift Mechanism:** As wind strikes the asymmetrically shaped blade airfoils, a **pressure differential** (low pressure on the suction side, high pressure on the pressure side) generates an **aerodynamic lift force** perpendicular to the relative wind direction.

2. **Force Decomposition:** The lift force is resolved into:
   - A **thrust component** (parallel to wind direction).
   - A **tangential component** perpendicular to the rotor plane, which produces a **torque** about the rotor axis.

3. **Rotational Motion:** The torque causes the rotor to spin. The rotor speed is controlled such that the **tip speed ratio (λ)** remains near the design optimum.

4. **Power Transmission Chain:** Rotational energy passes through:
   - **Hub → Low-Speed Shaft → Gearbox → High-Speed Shaft → Generator**

5. **Yaw System:** A yaw motor rotates the nacelle to keep the rotor plane facing the wind, maximizing energy capture.

6. **Pitch Control:** Blade pitch angle is adjusted to regulate power output and protect the turbine from overload during high winds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B - 14 marks):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often forget to **define the system and control volume** before deriving the power equation. Always begin with a labeled diagram and a 2-line setup.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Writing the Betz limit formula as $C_p = 0.59$ *without explaining* its origin and the **axial induction factor a = 1/3**. The KTU valuation key awards marks for showing $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$, differentiating, and substituting.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1.5 marks):** Skipping units in the derivation. Always include a units check at the end (kg·m²/s³ = W).
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing the Tip Speed Ratio as $\lambda = \omega / v$ instead of $\lambda = \omega R / v$. The radius *R* is mandatory.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Alternative Choice): (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a) State and prove Betz's Law. Show that the maximum power coefficient is 16/27. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Statement:** No ideal wind turbine can extract more than **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)** of the kinetic energy in wind.

**Proof using Actuator Disk Theory:**

Step 1: Consider an ideal actuator disk (porous disk) of area *A* with upstream velocity $v_1$, disk-plane velocity *v*, and downstream velocity $v_2$.

Step 2: Apply continuity:
$$A v_1 = A v = A_2 v_2$$

Step 3: Apply Froude-Rankine theorem:
$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

Step 4: The thrust force is the rate of change of momentum:
$$F = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

Step 5: Introduce the induction factor $a$, where $v = v_1(1 - a)$:

$$v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

Step 6: The power extracted is:
$$P_{extracted} = 2\rho A v_1^3 a(1-a)^2$$

Step 7: The power coefficient is:
$$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

Step 8: Maximize by differentiation:
$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Step 9: For non-trivial maximum: $a = 1/3$.

Step 10: Substitute back:
$$C_{p,max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) A wind turbine has a blade diameter of 50 m. At a wind speed of 10 m/s, the air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the total power in the wind, (ii) the maximum power extractable, and (iii) the power available if Cp = 0.35. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the swept area.**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (50)^2}{4} = \frac{2500\pi}{4} = 625\pi \approx 1963.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute total power in the wind.**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 1963.5 \times (10)^3$$
$$P_{wind} = 0.6 \times 1963.5 \times 1000 = 1{,}178{,}100 \text{ W} \approx 1.178 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Maximum extractable power (Betz limit).**
$$P_{max} = 0.593 \times 1.178 \text{ MW} = 0.6987 \text{ MW} \approx 698.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Power available at Cp = 0.35.**
$$P_{actual} = 0.35 \times 1.178 \text{ MW} = 0.4123 \text{ MW} \approx 412.3 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> - **Unit Conversion Error (–1 mark):** Students often write $A = 1963.5$ m² but forget that the result must be substituted in **SI units** (not in cm or mm).
> - **Forgetting the Betz Constant (–2 marks):** Many students write $C_{p,max} = 0.59$ without showing the derivation or stating its origin (16/27). The KTU answer key requires both the **symbolic form (16/27)** and the **decimal approximation**.
> - **Calculation Rounding (–0.5 mark):** Round only at the final step. Maintain at least 4 significant figures throughout intermediate steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Yield Quick-Recall Points (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- **WECS Definition:** System that converts **kinetic energy of wind** → mechanical → electrical.
- **Power in Wind Formula:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ (Watts).
- **Betz Limit Value:** $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (≈ 59.3%).
- **Optimum Axial Induction Factor:** $a = \frac{1}{3}$.
- **Tip Speed Ratio Formula:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D n}{v}$.
- **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, 15°C.
- **Cubic Velocity Dependence:** Doubling wind speed → 8× more power available.
- **Optimal TSR for 3-blade HAWT:** $\lambda_{opt} \approx 7$–$8$.
- **Optimal TSR for VAWTs:** $\lambda_{opt} \approx 1$–$2$.
- **Swept Area of HAWT:** $A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
- **Power Coefficient Definition:** $C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ (always < 1; never > 0.593 for ideal turbine).
- **Two Main Classes of WECS:** HAWT (Horizontal Axis) and VAWT (Vertical Axis).
- **VAWT Examples:** Savonius (drag-type), Darrieus (lift-type), Giromill (variable pitch).
- **Origin of Wind:** Solar-driven differential heating → atmospheric pressure gradients → air motion.
- **Wake Velocity Relation:** $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$; at Betz optimum, $v_2 = \frac{v_1}{3}$.
- **Force on Actuator Disk:** $F = 2\rho A v_1^2 a(1 - a)$.
- **Modern Real-World Cp:** Practical three-blade HAWTs achieve $C_p \approx 0.45$–$0.50$ (well below the Betz limit due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses).
- **Most Common Configuration:** 3-blade, upwind, horizontal-axis, variable-pitch turbine.
- **Wind Power Density (WPD):** $P/A = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3$ — used to classify wind classes (Class 1: < 200 W/m²; Class 7: > 800 W/m²).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
