topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of WECS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌬️ Classification of Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS):** A WECS is an integrated electromechanical system that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into useful mechanical energy, which is subsequently transformed into electrical energy through a generator. The complete system encompasses the **rotor (turbine blades + hub)**, **drive train (gearbox/shaft)**, **generator**, **tower**, **control electronics**, and the **grid interconnection or storage interface**.

Formally, in the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT726)**, WECS classification is the systematic taxonomy of wind turbines and their supporting subsystems based on **physical geometry**, **rotational dynamics**, **power capacity**, **operational mode**, and **aerodynamic principle**. This taxonomy is essential because the design, installation, and grid-integration strategy differ markedly across categories.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Family Tree of Windmills"** 🌳💨
>
> Imagine a large family of wind-catchers. Just like a family tree is divided into branches (children, grandchildren), WECS is divided into branches. One branch answers: *"Is the main shaft standing tall like a flagpole or lying flat like a record player?"* (HAWT vs VAWT). Another branch asks: *"How big is this windmill — toy size for a garden shed, or skyscraper size for a city?"* (Micro vs Mega). Each branch helps engineers pick the **right tool for the right wind**.

The standard air density at **15 °C and 1 atm** is **1.225 kg/m³**, used universally in all power-in-wind computations.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters

| Classification Axis | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Axis Orientation | Determines yaw mechanism, structural load, and tower shadow behaviour |
| Power Rating | Sets regulatory class, generator size, and grid interconnection protocol |
| Rotational Speed | Decides whether gearbox is mandatory and defines power-electronic control |
| Aerodynamic Principle | Governs self-starting capability and peak aerodynamic efficiency |
| Grid Connectivity | Drives whether storage, dump load, and bi-directional inverters are needed |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of WECS

### **Axis 1 — Axis of Rotation (Orientation)**
- **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT):** Shaft is **parallel to the ground**; nacelle sits atop a tower; blades face the wind. Requires **yaw control**.
- **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT):** Shaft is **perpendicular to the ground**; rotor axis is vertical. No yaw required; can accept wind from any direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common VAWT Sub-types:** Savonius (drag-based, low Cp), Darrieus (lift-based, egg-beater), Giromill (variable-pitch Darrieus), and H-rotor (straight-bladed Darrieus).

### **Axis 2 — Rated Power Capacity (IEC 61400 Classification)**

| Class | Rated Power | Rotor Diameter | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Micro WECS | < 1 kW | < 3 m | Battery charging, rural homes |
| Mini WECS | 1 – 10 kW | 3 – 8 m | Remote cabins, telecom towers |
| Small WECS | 10 – 100 kW | 8 – 25 m | Distributed generation, farms |
| Medium WECS | 100 – 500 kW | 25 – 50 m | Hybrid power systems |
| Large (Utility) WECS | 500 kW – 10 MW | 50 – 180 m | Grid supply, wind farms |
| Mega / Offshore WECS | > 10 MW | > 180 m | Deep offshore wind farms |

### **Axis 3 — Aerodynamic Principle**
- **Lift-type turbines:** Use pressure differential around an airfoil-shaped blade (all modern multi-MW machines are lift-type).
- **Drag-type turbines:** Use the force of wind pushing against a concave surface (Savonius rotor).

### **Axis 4 — Number of Blades**
- **Single-blade:** Lowest cost, lowest visual impact, require counterweight; rare.
- **Two-blade:** Simpler gearbox, higher TSR, lower starting torque.
- **Three-blade:** Industry standard; balances aerodynamic smoothness, cost, and visual appeal.
- **Multi-blade:** Used in traditional water-pumping farms (low TSR, high torque).

### **Axis 5 — Rotational Speed Behaviour**
- **Fixed-speed WECS:** Generator (typically SCIG) is locked to grid frequency; rotor speed varies only slightly (single operating point).
- **Variable-speed WECS:** Rotor speed varies with wind; uses power electronics (DFIG or full-converter SCIG/PMG) to decouple generator from grid.

### **Axis 6 — Rotor Position Relative to Tower**
- **Upwind:** Rotor is in front of the tower (no tower shadow).
- **Downwind:** Rotor is behind the tower (relies on tower shadow for self-yawing, but suffers cyclic loading).

### **Axis 7 — Grid Interfacing**
- **Grid-connected (Utility-interactive):** Synchronized with grid; needs synchronizing equipment and reactive power support.
- **Stand-alone (Off-grid / Autonomous):** Requires battery storage, dump load, and self-regulating controllers.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examination-critical**. Memorize the constants **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** and **Betz coefficient = 16/27 ≈ 0.593**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power in Undisturbed Wind | $P_{wind} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | W | $A = \pi R^{2}$ for circular swept area |
| 2 | Swept Area | $A \;=\; \pi R^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² | R = radius, D = diameter |
| 3 | Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{\omega R}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60\,v}$ | dimensionless | $\omega$ in rad/s, N in rpm |
| 4 | Rotor Solididity | $\sigma \;=\; \dfrac{N_{b}\,c}{\pi R}$ | dimensionless | $N_b$ = blade count, c = chord |
| 5 | Power Extracted by Turbine | $P_{turbine} \;=\; C_{p}\,P_{wind}$ | W | $C_p$ depends on $\lambda$ and pitch $\beta$ |
| 6 | Maximum $C_p$ (Betz Limit) | $C_{p,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{16}{27} \;\approx\; 0.593$ | dimensionless | Theoretically unbreakable cap |
| 7 | Thrust Coefficient | $C_{T} \;=\; \dfrac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{2}}$ | dimensionless | T = axial thrust force |
| 8 | Wind Power Density | $P/A \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{3}$ | W/m² | Used for site classification |
| 9 | Specific Yield | $E_{annual} \;=\; \overline{P} \times 8760 \times CF$ | kWh/year | CF = capacity factor (0.20–0.45 typical) |
| 10 | Generator Output | $P_{gen} \;=\; \eta_{mech}\,P_{turbine}$ | W | $\eta_{mech}$ includes gearbox + generator losses |

> **Use `\vert` for absolute values** — e.g., the speed deviation term is written as $\Delta v = \vert v_{\infty} - v_{wake} \vert$ to avoid breaking the table syntax.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro and Mini WECS** are deployed in hybrid systems (solar + wind + battery) for rural electrification in Kerala's hilly terrain.
- **Medium-to-large HAWTs** dominate the **Muppandal Wind Farm** (Tamil Nadu) and emerging offshore sites off the Gujarat coast.
- **VAWT Savonius** designs are favoured for **building-integrated wind (BIW)** in urban environments where turbulence is omni-directional.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. Multi-line equations use the `\begin{aligned}` environment. Read each step logically — the conversion reasoning is provided immediately beneath every equation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Available in Free Wind Stream

We start with the fundamental kinematic definition of **kinetic energy per unit volume** of moving air.

The kinetic energy of an air parcel of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2}$$

**Reasoning step:** Energy is mass × velocity² coefficient. Mass, in turn, equals density × volume. Substituting:

$$m \;=\; \rho\,V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E_{k} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V\,v^{2}$$

**Reasoning step:** Consider the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) passing through the swept area $A$ at velocity $v$:

$$\dot{m} \;=\; \rho\,A\,v$$

**Reasoning step:** Power is the **time rate of energy flow**, so we multiply $E_k$ per unit volume by the volumetric flow rate $\dot{m}/\rho = A\,v$:

$$\boxed{\;P_{wind} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}\;}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Because power is **cubic in wind speed**, a doubling of wind speed increases available power **eight-fold**. This is why wind-site resource assessment critically depends on accurate anemometer data and Weibull distribution fitting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Betz Limit ($C_{p,\max} = 16/27$)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024. The Betz limit proves that *no* turbine — however perfect — can extract more than **59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy.

### **Step 1 — Define three stream-tube velocities**

Let $v_1$ = upstream (free-stream) velocity, $v$ = velocity at the rotor disc, $v_2$ = downstream wake velocity. By continuity and actuator-disc theory:

$$A_1\,v_1 \;=\; A\,v \;=\; A_2\,v_2$$

### **Step 2 — Write the axial thrust on the rotor**

The thrust equals the rate of momentum change:

$$T \;=\; \rho\,A\,v\,(v_1 - v_2)$$

### **Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's equation on both sides of the disc**

$$p_1 - p_2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2})$$

**Reasoning step:** The pressure jump across the rotor equals the thrust per unit area. Combining Step 2 and Step 3:

$$T/A \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) \quad \text{and} \quad T/A \;=\; \rho\,v\,(v_1 - v_2)$$

Equating both thrust expressions:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\,(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}) \;=\; v\,(v_1 - v_2)$$

**Simplification:**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\,(v_1 + v_2)(v_1 - v_2) \;=\; v\,(v_1 - v_2)$$

Since $v_1 \neq v_2$, cancel the common factor:

$$v \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$$

### **Step 4 — Express extracted power**

The power extracted equals thrust times velocity at the disc:

$$P_{extracted} \;=\; T \cdot v \;=\; \rho\,A\,v^{2}\,(v_1 - v_2)$$

Substituting $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$:

$$P_{extracted} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v\,(v_1 + v_2)(v_1 - v_2)$$

$$P_{extracted} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v\,(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2})$$

### **Step 5 — Form the power coefficient $C_p$**

Divide by the upstream wind power $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{3}$:

$$C_{p} \;=\; \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} \;=\; \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v(v_1^{2} - v_2^{2})}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{3}}$$

$$C_{p} \;=\; \frac{v}{v_1}\,\Bigl(1 - \Bigl(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr)$$

### **Step 6 — Introduce the induction factor $a$**

Define $v = v_1(1 - a)$, so $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$ (from $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1+v_2)$).

**Reasoning step:** The induction factor $a$ quantifies the fractional reduction in wind velocity caused by the rotor.

Substituting:

$$C_{p} \;=\; (1 - a)\bigl[1 - (1 - 2a)^{2}\bigr] \;=\; (1 - a)\bigl[1 - (1 - 4a + 4a^{2})\bigr]$$

$$C_{p} \;=\; (1 - a)(4a - 4a^{2}) \;=\; 4a(1 - a)^{2}$$

### **Step 7 — Maximize $C_p$**

Differentiate with respect to $a$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} \;=\; 4\bigl[(1 - a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1 - a)(-1)\bigr] \;=\; 4(1 - a)(1 - 3a) \;=\; 0$$

Non-trivial root: $a = 1/3$.

**Substitute $a = 1/3$:**

$$C_{p,\max} \;=\; 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \bigl(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\bigr)^{2} \;=\; 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} \;=\; \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_{p,\max} \;=\; \frac{16}{27} \;\approx\; 0.5926 \;\approx\; 59.3\%\;}$$

This is **Albert Betz's theorem (1919)**, the cornerstone of modern wind-energy engineering.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | HAWT (Lift-type, 3-blade) | VAWT (Darrieus) | VAWT (Savonius) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical $C_p$ range | 0.35 – 0.48 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.10 – 0.18 |
| Self-starting | Yes (with pitch control) | No (needs push) | Yes |
| Yaw mechanism | Required | Not required | Not required |
| Cyclic stress | Low (gravity aligned) | Moderate | Low |
| Installation height | Tall tower (hub 50–120 m) | Hub near ground | Hub near ground |
| Noise level | Moderate–High | Low | Very low |
| Suitability for offshore | Excellent (modern monopile) | Poor | Poor |
| Tip-speed ratio $\lambda$ | 6 – 8 | 3 – 6 | 0.8 – 1.2 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete WECS

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **energy and signal flow** through a generic utility-scale HAWT system, illustrating how the aerodynamic, mechanical, electrical, and control subsystems interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Wind Resource v_inf] --> nodeB[HAWT Rotor Blades + Hub]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Main Shaft Low Speed]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Gearbox Step Up Ratio 1 to 90]
    nodeD --> nodeE[High Speed Shaft]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Generator DFIG or PMG]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Power Electronics Converter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step Up Transformer]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Grid Substation]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Utility Grid 50 Hz]
    nodeK[Anemometer + Wind Vane] --> nodeL[SCADA Controller]
    nodeL --> nodeB
    nodeL --> nodeM[Pitch Actuator]
    nodeM --> nodeB
    nodeL --> nodeN[Yaw Drive Motor]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Nacelle Yaw Bearing]
    nodeO --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid forward arrows** indicate the **power flow path** (wind → rotor → grid).
- The **dotted feedback loops** show the **SCADA control architecture**: wind speed, rotor RPM, and generator output are continuously measured and fed back to the controller, which in turn commands **blade pitch**, **yaw orientation**, and **converter switching**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree of WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeWECS[Wind Energy Conversion Systems] --> nodeAXIS[Axis Orientation]
    nodeWECS --> nodePOWER[Rated Power Capacity]
    nodeWECS --> nodeAERO[Aerodynamic Principle]
    nodeWECS --> nodeSPEED[Rotational Speed Behaviour]
    nodeWECS --> nodeGRID[Grid Connection Mode]
    nodeWECS --> nodeBLADES[Number of Blades]
    nodeAXIS --> nodeHAWT[HAWT Horizontal Shaft]
    nodeAXIS --> nodeVAWT[VAWT Vertical Shaft]
    nodeHAWT --> nodeUPWIND[Upwind Configuration]
    nodeHAWT --> nodeDOWNWIND[Downwind Configuration]
    nodeVAWT --> nodeDAR[Darrieus Lift Based]
    nodeVAWT --> nodeSAV[Savonius Drag Based]
    nodePOWER --> nodeMICRO[Micro less than 1 kW]
    nodePOWER --> nodeSMALL[Small 10 to 100 kW]
    nodePOWER --> nodeLARGE[Large 500 kW to 10 MW]
    nodePOWER --> nodeMEGA[Mega Offshore above 10 MW]
    nodeAERO --> nodeLIFT[Lift Type High Cp]
    nodeAERO --> nodeDRAG[Drag Type Self Starting]
    nodeSPEED --> nodeFIXED[Fixed Speed SCIG]
    nodeSPEED --> nodeVAR[Variable Speed DFIG or PMSG]
    nodeGRID --> nodeGC[Grid Connected]
    nodeGRID --> nodeSA[Stand Alone with Battery]
    nodeBLADES --> nodeONE[Single Blade]
    nodeBLADES --> nodeTWO[Two Blade]
    nodeBLADES --> nodeTHREE[Three Blade Industry Std]
    nodeBLADES --> nodeMULTI[Multi Blade Pump Use]
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram of a Modern Variable-Speed HAWT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWIND[Wind Stream] --> nodeBLADE[Aerofoil Blades]
    nodeBLADE --> nodeHUB[Hub]
    nodeHUB --> nodeLS[Low Speed Shaft]
    nodeLS --> nodeGB[Gearbox]
    nodeGB --> nodeHS[High Speed Shaft]
    nodeHS --> nodeGEN[PMSG Permanent Magnet Generator]
    nodeGEN --> nodeACDC[AC to DC Rectifier]
    nodeACDC --> nodeDCDC[DC to DC Boost Stage]
    nodeDCDC --> nodeDCAC[DC to AC Inverter]
    nodeDCAC --> nodeFILTER[LC Filter]
    nodeFILTER --> nodeTRANS[Step Up Transformer]
    nodeTRANS --> nodeGRID[Utility Grid]
    nodeCTRL[MPPT Controller] -.-> nodeACDC
    nodeCTRL -.-> nodeDCDC
    nodeCTRL -.-> nodeDCAC
    nodeSENS[Wind Speed and RPM Sensors] -.-> nodeCTRL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for diagram-based questions:** Always show **two categories of arrows** — solid lines for power flow, dashed lines for control/sensor feedback. Examiners reward clear **labelling of subsystems** more than decorative complexity.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between HAWT and VAWT. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) has its main shaft oriented **parallel to the ground**, while a Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT) has its shaft **perpendicular to the ground**.

**HAWT — Advantages:**
1. Higher aerodynamic efficiency ($C_p$ up to 0.48) due to favourable lift-to-drag ratio.
2. Self-starting without auxiliary motor.

**HAWT — Disadvantage:**
1. Requires yaw mechanism and tall supporting tower — high installation cost.

**VAWT — Advantages:**
1. Omni-directional; no yaw drive needed.
2. Generator and gearbox can be placed at ground level — easier maintenance.

**VAWT — Disadvantage:**
1. Lower $C_p$ and structural fatigue due to cyclic blade loading.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Betz's law. What is its numerical significance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Betz's Law (1919):** *No wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3 %) of the kinetic energy of the incident wind.*

**Numerical significance:** It sets the **theoretical upper bound** on the power coefficient $C_p$. Real machines operate well below this — typically $C_p = 0.35 - 0.45$ — because of rotational wake effects, tip losses, hub losses, and aerodynamic drag.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **🔹 Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Classify Wind Energy Conversion Systems based on **axis of rotation, aerodynamic principle, and rated power capacity**. Discuss the salient features of each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **power available in the wind** and explain why wind-site selection gives critical importance to accurate anemometry. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **🔹 Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the **Betz-limit derivation** step-by-step using the actuator-disc model, and state the maximum theoretical value of $C_p$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. At a wind speed of **12 m/s**, air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, $C_p = 0.40$, and the overall mechanical-electrical efficiency is **0.90**. Compute **(i)** swept area, **(ii)** power in the wind, **(iii)** shaft power extracted, and **(iv)** electrical output power. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Classification (7 Marks):**

The classification is presented as a structured table — examiners award full marks when each category has a **definition + example + characteristic**:

**1. Based on Axis of Rotation:**
- HAWT — three-blade upwind turbines (e.g., GE 1.5 MW); nacelle on top of tower; requires yaw.
- VAWT — Darrieus, Savonius, Giromill; shaft vertical; no yaw required.

**2. Based on Aerodynamic Principle:**
- Lift-type — airfoil-shaped blades (HAWT, Darrieus); high $C_p$.
- Drag-type — cup or scoop-shaped blades (Savonius); self-starting but low $C_p$.

**3. Based on Rated Power Capacity (IEC 61400):**
- Micro (<1 kW) — battery charging, rural homes.
- Small (10–100 kW) — distributed generation, farms.
- Medium (100–500 kW) — village electrification, hybrid systems.
- Large / Utility (500 kW – 10 MW) — grid supply, wind farms.
- Offshore Mega (>10 MW) — deep-sea wind farms.

**(b) Derivation of Power-in-Wind (7 Marks):**

Begin with kinetic energy per unit volume:

$$E_{k,vol} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,v^{2}$$

Mass flow rate through swept area:

$$\dot{m} \;=\; \rho A v$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;P_{wind} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho A v^{3}\;} \quad [A = \pi R^{2}]$$

**Why accurate anemometry matters:**
Power is **cubic in wind speed**. A 5 % error in measured $v$ produces a $\approx 16 \%$ error in $P$. At a prospective site, anemometers are mounted at hub height for **at least one year**, and the data is fitted to a **Weibull probability distribution**:

$$f(v) \;=\; \frac{k}{c}\,\Bigl(\frac{v}{c}\Bigr)^{k-1}\exp\!\Bigl[-\Bigl(\frac{v}{c}\Bigr)^{k}\Bigr]$$

where $k$ = shape factor and $c$ = scale factor. The **mean power density** is:

$$\bar{P}/A \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho c^{3}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(1 + 3/k\bigr)$$

Thus the cube dependence makes anemometer calibration and mast siting the most economical investment in a wind project.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Betz-Limit Derivation (7 Marks):**

Write the full derivation as shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes. State clearly:

1. Stream-tube velocities: $v_1, v, v_2$.
2. Momentum equation: $T = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$.
3. Bernoulli equation: $p_1 - p_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(v_1^2 - v_2^2)$.
4. Combine → $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_1 + v_2)$.
5. Induction factor $a$ → $v = v_1(1-a)$, $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$.
6. $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$.
7. $dC_p/da = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$.
8. $\boxed{C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593}$.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ m, $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.40$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.90$.

**(i) Swept Area:**

$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (80)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 1600\pi \;\approx\; 5026.55 \;\text{m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Power in the Wind:**

$$P_{wind} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3}$$

$$P_{wind} \;=\; 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} \;\approx\; 5.32 \times 10^{6} \;\text{W} \;=\; 5.32 \;\text{MW}$$

**(iii) Mechanical (Shaft) Power Extracted:**

$$P_{shaft} \;=\; C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} \;=\; 0.40 \times 5.32 \;\text{MW} \;\approx\; 2.128 \;\text{MW}$$

**(iv) Electrical Output Power:**

$$P_{elec} \;=\; \eta_{mech} \times P_{shaft} \;=\; 0.90 \times 2.128 \;\text{MW} \;\approx\; 1.915 \;\text{MW}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{elec} \;\approx\; 1.92 \;\text{MW}\;}$$

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls that cost marks in WECS numericals:**
>
> 1. **Mixing units** — Most students forget to convert diameter to radius for the area formula, or plug $N$ in rpm without converting to rad/s in the TSR formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\eta_{mech}$** — When the question says "electrical output", you MUST multiply by the efficiency chain. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for direct $C_p \cdot P_{wind}$ answers.
> 3. **Confusing $C_p$ with Betz limit** — $C_p$ is the *actual* power coefficient of the turbine, which is **always less than 16/27**. Never write $C_p = 16/27$ for a real machine.
> 4. **Betz-derivation step-skipping** — Examiners require each of the seven steps explicitly. Skipping the introduction of the induction factor $a$ leads to a 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Always state "steady, incompressible, frictionless flow" at the start of a Betz derivation.

---

## 5.5 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of WECS:**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** WECS = system that converts wind kinetic energy → mechanical → electrical energy.
>
> ✅ **Primary Classification Axes (7 axes):**
> 1. Axis of rotation → HAWT / VAWT
> 2. Rated power → Micro / Mini / Small / Medium / Large / Mega
> 3. Aerodynamic principle → Lift-type / Drag-type
> 4. Number of blades → 1 / 2 / 3 (industry standard) / Multi
> 5. Rotational speed → Fixed-speed (SCIG) / Variable-speed (DFIG, PMSG)
> 6. Rotor position → Upwind / Downwind
> 7. Grid interfacing → Grid-connected / Stand-alone (off-grid)
>
> ✅ **Must-memorize formulas:**
> - $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$
> - $\lambda = \omega R / v = \pi D N / (60 v)$
> - $C_p = P_{turbine}/P_{wind}$
> - **Betz limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$
> - $P_{elec} = C_p \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$
>
> ✅ **Constants to remember:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A = \pi R^2 = \pi D^2/4$.
>
> ✅ **Key comparisons:** HAWT > VAWT in $C_p$; VAWT > HAWT in omni-directionality; Lift-type > Drag-type in efficiency; Variable-speed > Fixed-speed in energy capture.
>
> ✅ **Real-world linkage:** Muppandal (TN) — utility-scale HAWT; Kerala hilly terrain — micro/mini WECS for hybrid rural electrification; offshore Gujarat — mega-WECS (future).
>
> ✅ **Common essay framing:** "Compare and contrast" → always use a **two-column table**; "Derive Betz limit" → actuator-disc + induction-factor + Bernoulli + differentiation; "Numerical" → always show $A$, $P_{wind}$, $P_{shaft}$, $P_{elec}$ in four labelled steps.

---
