topics.title: Parts of WECS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parts of WECS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of WECS Components

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated electromechanical assembly that captures the **kinetic energy** of ambient wind and converts it into useful electrical energy. The system comprises several sub-systems — namely the **aerodynamic rotor**, the **mechanical transmission train**, the **electrical generator**, and the **control & safety subsystems** — all mounted on a structural **tower and foundation**.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy: The Windmill Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Electric Windmill"**
> Imagine a giant ceiling fan working *in reverse*. A ceiling fan uses electricity to spin its blades and push air. A wind turbine does the *opposite* — the **wind pushes the blades**, causing them to spin, and this rotation is converted into **electricity**. The blades act like a sail catching wind, the gearbox acts like a bicycle gear (slow pedal, fast wheel), and the generator acts like a flashlight dynamo (rotation → electricity).

The complete energy chain is:

$$\text{Wind Kinetic Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Rotor}} \text{Mechanical Rotation} \xrightarrow{\text{Gearbox}} \text{High-Speed Rotation} \xrightarrow{\text{Generator}} \text{Electrical Energy}$$

### 1.2 The Major Parts of a WECS (Top-Down Listing)

> [!NOTE]
> A modern Horizontal-Axis Wind Turbine System (HA-WTS) — which is the **industry standard** studied under the KTU 2024 syllabus — is composed of the following **seven major parts/sub-assemblies**:

1. **Rotor Assembly** (Blades + Hub)
2. **Nacelle** (the housing/cabin)
3. **Main Shaft (Low-Speed Shaft)**
4. **Gearbox** (speed increaser)
5. **Generator** (electrical machine)
6. **Yaw System & Tower**
7. **Control, Safety and Grid-Interface Subsystems**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Density Standard:** The reference value used in KTU textbook problems is $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at **standard sea-level conditions** (15 °C, 1 atm). Betz's Limit is $\mathbf{C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926 \; (59.26\%)}$.

### 1.3 Function-First Classification

| S.No. | Subsystem | Primary Energy Function |
|:-----:|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Rotor (Blades + Hub) | Aerodynamic energy capture |
| 2 | Nacelle | Environmental protection of internals |
| 3 | Main Shaft | Low-speed torque transmission |
| 4 | Gearbox | Speed multiplication (~1:50 to 1:100) |
| 5 | Generator | Mechanical → Electrical conversion |
| 6 | Yaw Drive | Wind direction tracking |
| 7 | Tower | Elevation for stronger, laminar wind |
| 8 | Foundation | Structural anchorage to ground |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each WECS Part

#### 2.1.1 The Rotor Assembly — *The Energy Capturer*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rotor** is the only component that interfaces directly with the wind. It consists of **two or three aerodynamic blades** (modern utility turbines use **3 blades** for optimum balance of cost, smoothness, and aesthetic noise) attached to a central **hub**.

- **Blade Material:** Fiberglass-reinforced polyester or wood-epoxy composite for small machines; carbon-fiber composites for large utility-scale machines.
- **Blade Geometry:** Each blade is an **airfoil** (similar to an aircraft wing), with a **chord**, **twist angle**, and **pitch angle** $\beta$.
- **Hub:** Connects blades to the main shaft. Modern hubs allow **collective pitch control** — the entire blade rotates about its own axis to regulate power capture.
- **Rotor Diameter $D$ (or $2R$):** Determines the **swept area** $A = \pi R^2$, which quadratically influences power capture.
- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) $\lambda$:** Defined as the ratio of blade tip speed to free-stream wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

where $\omega$ is the rotor angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ is rotor radius (m), and $V$ is wind speed (m/s).

- **Solidity ($\sigma$):** Ratio of total blade planform area to swept area:

$$\sigma = \frac{N \cdot c}{\pi R}$$

where $N$ is the number of blades and $c$ is the average chord length.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-solidity rotors** (many blades) have high starting torque but low $\lambda$ (e.g., farm windmills for water pumping). **Low-solidity rotors** (3 blades) have high $\lambda$ and are used for electricity generation.

#### 2.1.2 The Nacelle — *The Protected Cabin*

> [!NOTE]
> The **nacelle** is a weatherproof enclosure mounted atop the tower that houses all the critical electro-mechanical components. It typically includes an internal crane for service access in large machines.

- **Yaw Bearing:** A large slewing ring that allows the entire nacelle to rotate horizontally.
- **Yaw Motor & Drive:** A gear-motor assembly that turns the nacelle to face the wind.
- **Anemometer & Wind Vane:** Mounted on top of the nacelle to measure wind speed and direction.
- **Main Frame:** The internal steel bedplate that holds all components in precise alignment.

#### 2.1.3 The Main (Low-Speed) Shaft

- Connects the **hub** to the **gearbox input**.
- Operates at low rotational speed (e.g., 15–20 rpm for a large turbine) but carries **high torque**.
- Supported by **main bearing(s)** inside the nacelle.

#### 2.1.4 The Gearbox — *The Speed Multiplier*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gearbox** is a multi-stage planetary/helical gear system that steps up the rotor speed (e.g., 17 rpm) to the generator speed (e.g., 1500 or 1800 rpm). A typical ratio is **1:75 to 1:100**.

- Multi-stage **planetary gears** are preferred (compact, high torque capacity, coaxial).
- Includes a **high-speed shaft** with a **mechanical disc brake** for emergency stops.
- **Gearbox losses:** Approximately **2–3% per stage**; thus a 3-stage gearbox has ~6–9% loss.

> [!TIP]
> Many modern turbines use **direct-drive generators** (no gearbox) using permanent-magnet synchronous generators (PMSG) to reduce maintenance, but they require more complex power electronics.

#### 2.1.5 The Generator

> [!NOTE]
> The **generator** is the electrical machine that converts mechanical rotation into electrical output. KTU syllabus emphasizes three main types:

- **Squirrel-Cage Induction Generator (SCIG):** Robust, cheap, but requires a **soft-starter** and reactive power from the grid.
- **Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG):** Rotor is connected to the grid via **slip rings and a partial-rated converter** (~30% of rating). Allows variable-speed operation (±30% around synchronous speed).
- **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG):** Used in direct-drive systems; no gearbox needed; high efficiency.

Generator rated power is determined by the **rated wind speed** (where the turbine reaches nameplate capacity, typically 12–14 m/s).

#### 2.1.6 The Yaw System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **yaw system** rotates the nacelle about the vertical tower axis to keep the rotor facing the wind. **Active yaw** uses a motor; **passive yaw** uses a tail vane (small machines only).

- Yaw rate is typically **~0.5 degrees per second**.
- Includes a **yaw lock** (hydraulic pin) to prevent oscillation in turbulent winds.

#### 2.1.7 The Tower

- Provides **elevation** to access stronger, less turbulent wind (wind speed increases with height due to the **wind shear profile**):

$$V(h) = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **wind shear exponent** (typically 1/7 for open terrain, 0.10 for flat terrain, 0.40 for urban).

- **Tubular steel towers** are the industry standard — strong, safe, and aesthetically clean.
- **Tower height** ranges from ~50 m (1.5 MW) to >120 m (8+ MW offshore turbines).

#### 2.1.8 Control, Safety & Grid-Interface Subsystems

- **Pitch Control:** Adjusts blade pitch angle $\beta$ to regulate power above rated wind speed and to feather blades during shutdown.
- **Braking System:** **Aerodynamic** (pitch to feather) + **Mechanical** (disc brake on HSS).
- **SCADA System:** Remote monitoring of all parameters.
- **Transformer & Power Electronics:** Steps up voltage (e.g., 690 V → 33 kV) and conditions output for grid synchronization.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize all formulas below — they appear every year in KTU Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Available wind power | W |
| 2 | $P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Power captured by rotor | W |
| 3 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Rotor swept area | m² |
| 4 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_p = f(\lambda, \beta)$ | Power coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | Betz Limit | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma = \frac{N c}{\pi R}$ | Solidity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V(h) = V_{ref} \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Wind shear law | m/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{rotor} \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ | Total system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E = P_{rated} \cdot CF \cdot 8760$ | Annual energy yield | kWh/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units in KTU Questions (Verify Always):**
> - $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$ (often **1.225**)
> - $V$ in **m/s** (NOT km/h). If km/h is given, divide by **3.6** to convert.
> - $A$ in **m²** (NOT cm²). If cm² is given, divide by **10$^4$**.
> - $n$ in **rpm**, then convert using $\omega = 2\pi n / 60 = \pi n / 30$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utility-scale wind farms** (50–500 MW) employ all seven parts with **DFIG/PMSG** generators and SCADA-based remote operation.
- **Offshore wind turbines** (8–15 MW) use **direct-drive PMSG** because of high reliability and reduced maintenance (no gearbox).
- **Small standalone systems** (1–10 kW) often use SCIG or DC generators with battery banks for remote village electrification.
- The **gearbox** is statistically the most failure-prone component — modern trends move toward **gearless (direct-drive)** designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Available Wind Power $P_{wind}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** The kinetic energy of a moving fluid mass is given by $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$. We compute the mass of air passing through the rotor swept area per second.

**Step 1:** Consider a control volume of air of cross-section $A$ (the swept area of the rotor) and length $V \cdot \Delta t$ (the distance the wind travels in time $\Delta t$).

**Step 2:** The volume of air flowing through area $A$ in time $\Delta t$ is:

$$\text{Vol} = A \cdot V \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3:** The mass of this air parcel, using density $\rho$:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4:** The kinetic energy of this mass is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A V \Delta t) V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \Delta t$$

**Step 5:** Power is energy per unit time, so divide by $\Delta t$:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{E_k}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$

This is the **fundamental wind power equation** and the **starting point of all WECS analysis**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Captured by the Rotor

**Step 1:** The rotor cannot extract 100% of $P_{wind}$ (Betz's Law). Define a power coefficient $C_p$ as the fraction of available wind power captured:

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $P_{wind}$:

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$

**Step 3:** For real turbines, $C_p$ depends on both $\lambda$ and $\beta$:

$$C_p = f(\lambda, \beta)$$

A commonly used empirical expression (used in KTU textbook problems):

$$C_p = c_1 \left(\frac{c_2}{\lambda_i} - c_3 \beta - c_4 \right) e^{-c_5 / \lambda_i} + c_6 \lambda$$

where

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_i} = \frac{1}{\lambda + 0.08 \beta} - \frac{0.035}{\beta^3 + 1}$$

and $c_1, \ldots, c_6$ are turbine-specific constants.

**Step 4:** The maximum theoretical value of $C_p$ is the **Betz limit**:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \; (59.26\%)$$

This means **no turbine can ever extract more than 59.26%** of the available wind kinetic energy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)

**Step 1:** The linear velocity at the tip of the blade is the tangential speed:

$$V_{tip} = \omega \cdot R$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s and $R$ is the rotor radius in m.

**Step 2:** The TSR is defined as the ratio of $V_{tip}$ to the free-stream wind speed $V$:

$$\lambda = \frac{V_{tip}}{V} = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

**Step 3:** Converting $\omega$ (rad/s) to $n$ (rpm) using $\omega = 2\pi n / 60 = \pi n / 30$:

$$\lambda = \frac{(\pi n / 30) \cdot R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This conversion is a **classic KTU trap** — students often forget the factor of 30 (or 60) and lose 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Wind Speed at Hub Height (Power Law)

**Step 1:** The atmospheric boundary layer wind profile is empirically modeled by the **Hellmann exponential law**:

$$\frac{V(h)}{V_{ref}} = \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging:

$$V(h) = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

**Step 3:** The exponent $\alpha$ depends on terrain roughness:

| Terrain Type | $\alpha$ |
|:-------------|:--------:|
| Smooth, flat (sea, sand) | 0.10 |
| Open grassland | 1/7 ≈ 0.143 |
| Suburbs, few trees | 0.20 |
| Urban areas | 0.30–0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Forgetting to use *hub height* $h$ instead of the *measurement height* $h_{ref}$ when computing rotor wind speed.

---

### 3.5 Work-Out Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Type)

> **Problem (Adapted from KTU Pattern):**
> A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. The wind speed at hub height is **12 m/s**, the air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, and the generator efficiency is **92%** with a gearbox efficiency of **95%**. The power coefficient is **0.40**.
> Calculate:
> (a) The rotor swept area and available wind power.
> (b) The mechanical power output and the electrical power output.
> (c) The tip speed ratio if the rotor speed is **20 rpm**.

#### (a) Swept Area and Available Power

**Step 1 — Rotor radius:**

$$R = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Swept area:**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 1600 \pi \approx 5026.55 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Available wind power:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = \frac{1}{2} (1.225) (5026.55) (12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 8{,}685{,}878.4 \approx 5.32 \times 10^6 \; \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.32 \; \text{MW}}$$

#### (b) Mechanical and Electrical Power Output

**Step 1 — Mechanical power extracted by the rotor:**

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \; \text{MW} = 2.128 \; \text{MW}$$

**Step 2 — Power reaching the generator (after gearbox):**

$$P_{gen,input} = \eta_{gb} \cdot P_{rotor} = 0.95 \times 2.128 = 2.0216 \; \text{MW}$$

**Step 3 — Electrical power output:**

$$P_{electrical} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{gen,input} = 0.92 \times 2.0216$$

$$\boxed{P_{electrical} \approx 1.860 \; \text{MW}}$$

#### (c) Tip Speed Ratio

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 20}{30 \times 12} = \frac{2513.27}{360} \approx 6.98$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 6.98}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of $\lambda \approx 7$ is typical for a **3-bladed utility turbine** operating near its design point.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Key Components

| Part | Function | Typical Value (3 MW turbine) | Failure Risk (Rank) | Maintenance Frequency |
|:-----|:---------|:----------------------------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| Blades | Energy capture | Length ≈ 45 m | Medium (5) | Annual inspection |
| Hub | Blade-to-shaft connection | 2–5 m dia. | Low (7) | Annual |
| Main Shaft | Torque transfer | 1–2 m long, heavy | Medium (4) | 5-yearly |
| Gearbox | Speed step-up | Ratio ≈ 1:90 | **Highest (1)** | 6-monthly oil check |
| Generator | Mech→Elec | 3 MW, 690 V | Low–Med (3) | Annual |
| Yaw System | Wind tracking | Slew rate 0.5°/s | Medium (6) | Monthly |
| Tower | Elevation | 80–100 m tall | Low (8) | 5-yearly |
| Control | Safety & regulation | SCADA + PLC | Low (2) | Continuous monitoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gearbox** is the most failure-prone subsystem. Direct-drive (gearless) turbines eliminate this risk at the cost of larger, heavier generators.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics of WECS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below is rendered as a **functional flow** (Mermaid safe formatting rules applied — alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside quoted labels, no reserved keywords as nodes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wind Kinetic Energy Source] --> A2[Rotor Assembly: Blades and Hub]
    A2 --> A3[Main Low Speed Shaft: Torque Transmission]
    A3 --> A4[Gearbox: Speed Multiplication 1 to 90]
    A4 --> A5[High Speed Shaft: With Disc Brake]
    A5 --> A6[Generator: DFIG or PMSG]
    A6 --> A7[Power Electronics: Converter and Filter]
    A7 --> A8[Step Up Transformer: 690V to 33kV]
    A8 --> A9[Grid Interface and Protection]
    A9 --> A10[Electrical Load or Utility Grid]
    B1[Anemometer and Wind Vane] --> B2[Controller: PLC and SCADA]
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> A4
    B2 --> A6
    B2 --> C1[Pitch Control Actuators]
    C1 --> A2
    B2 --> D1[Yaw Drive Motor]
    D1 --> E1[Nacelle Orientation]
    E1 --> A2
    F1[Tower and Foundation] -.-> A2
    F1 -.-> E1
    F1 -.-> A6
```

### 4.2 Component Hierarchy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WECS[Wind Energy Conversion System] --> R1[Aerodynamic Subsystem]
    WECS --> R2[Mechanical Transmission]
    WECS --> R3[Electrical Subsystem]
    WECS --> R4[Control and Safety]
    WECS --> R5[Structural Subsystem]
    R1 --> R1A[Rotor Blades]
    R1 --> R1B[Hub Assembly]
    R1 --> R1C[Spinner Nose Cone]
    R2 --> R2A[Main Shaft]
    R2 --> R2B[Gearbox]
    R2 --> R2C[High Speed Shaft]
    R2 --> R2D[Mechanical Brake Disc]
    R3 --> R3A[Generator DFIG PMSG SCIG]
    R3 --> R3B[Power Converter]
    R3 --> R3C[Grid Transformer]
    R3 --> R3D[Cabling and Switchgear]
    R4 --> R4A[SCADA System]
    R4 --> R4B[Pitch Controllers]
    R4 --> R4C[Yaw Motors]
    R4 --> R4D[Safety Sensors]
    R5 --> R5A[Nacelle Housing]
    R5 --> R5B[Tower Tubular Steel]
    R5 --> R5C[Foundation Concrete]
    R5 --> R5D[Access Ladder and Elevator]
```

### 4.3 Power Flow Path (Energy Trace Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wind V cubed] --> P2[Rotor Cp coefficient]
    P2 --> P3[Mechanical Power]
    P3 --> P4[After Gearbox Loss]
    P4 --> P5[After Generator Loss]
    P5 --> P6[After Converter Loss]
    P6 --> P7[Net Grid Power]
    P1 -.-> P0[Betz Limit 0.5926 Max]
    P0 -.-> P2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A KTU 14-mark question on WECS parts will almost always ask you to **draw a labeled block diagram** of the system and **trace the power flow** from wind to grid, identifying losses at each stage. Practice drawing this on A4 sheet before exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**List the major parts of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine System (HA-WTS) and state the function of any three.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine System consists of the following major parts:

1. **Rotor Assembly** (blades + hub) — Captures wind kinetic energy and converts it to rotational mechanical energy.
2. **Gearbox** — Steps up the slow rotor speed (15–20 rpm) to the high generator speed (1500–1800 rpm), typically with a 1:90 ratio.
3. **Generator** — Converts the high-speed mechanical rotation into electrical energy (AC).
4. **Yaw System** — Rotates the nacelle so that the rotor faces the wind direction.
5. **Tower** — Elevates the rotor to hub height where wind is stronger and less turbulent.

> *(Any three well-explained parts = 3 marks: 1 mark list + 2 marks explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR). Why is TSR important in WECS design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** is defined as the ratio of the linear speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$$

**Importance (2 Marks):**
- TSR determines the **aerodynamic efficiency** ($C_p$) of the rotor. Each rotor design has an **optimum TSR** ($\lambda_{opt}$) at which $C_p$ is maximum.
- **High-solidity rotors** (multi-blade) have low $\lambda_{opt}$ (≈1–2).
- **Low-solidity rotors** (3-blade) have high $\lambda_{opt}$ (≈6–8).
- Operating away from $\lambda_{opt}$ reduces $C_p$ and hence power output.

> *(1 mark definition + 2 marks importance = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major parts of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) and describe the function of each part.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. The wind speed at hub height is 14 m/s and air density is 1.225 kg/m³. The turbine has a power coefficient of 0.42, gearbox efficiency 0.94, and generator efficiency 0.93. Calculate:
(i) Rotor swept area
(ii) Available wind power
(iii) Net electrical power output
(iv) Annual energy generation if the capacity factor is 30%.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Block Diagram and Functions (7 Marks):**

*(The block diagram from Section 4.1 above is to be drawn on the answer sheet. Key parts and functions are:)*

| Part | Function |
|:-----|:---------|
| Blades + Hub | Aerodynamic energy capture |
| Main Shaft | Torque transmission (low speed, high torque) |
| Gearbox | Speed multiplication (1:90 typical) |
| High-Speed Shaft | Carries high-speed rotation to generator |
| Generator (DFIG/PMSG) | Mechanical → Electrical conversion |
| Power Electronics | Frequency, voltage conditioning |
| Yaw System | Nacelle orientation to wind |
| Pitch System | Blade angle regulation |
| Tower | Elevation to access laminar wind |
| Foundation | Ground anchorage |
| SCADA + Anemometer | Monitoring and control |

*(Each part with function: ~0.5–1 mark. Block diagram: 2 marks.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 60 \; \text{m} \Rightarrow R = 30 \; \text{m}$
- $V = 14 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $C_p = 0.42$, $\eta_{gb} = 0.94$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.93$, $CF = 0.30$

**(i) Swept area (1 mark):**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (30)^2 = 900 \pi \approx 2827.43 \; \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Available wind power (2 marks):**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (14)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 2744 = 4{,}752{,}610 \; \text{W} \approx 4.75 \; \text{MW}$$

**(iii) Net electrical power output (3 marks):**

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 4.75 = 1.995 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_{gen,input} = \eta_{gb} \cdot P_{rotor} = 0.94 \times 1.995 = 1.875 \; \text{MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{electrical} = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{gen,input} = 0.93 \times 1.875 \approx 1.744 \; \text{MW}}$$

**(iv) Annual energy (1 mark):**

$$E = P_{rated} \cdot CF \cdot 8760 \; \text{h}$$

$$E = 1744 \; \text{kW} \times 0.30 \times 8760 = 4{,}583{,}232 \; \text{kWh} \approx 4.58 \; \text{GWh/year}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the function of the **gearbox** and the **generator** in a WECS. Compare **SCIG, DFIG, and PMSG** generators with respect to their need for a gearbox, speed range, converter rating, and efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A three-blade wind turbine has the following specifications: rotor radius 35 m, rated wind speed 13 m/s, rotor speed at rated power 22 rpm, power coefficient at rated point 0.45, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate:
(i) The rated mechanical power output of the rotor
(ii) The tip speed ratio at the rated point
(iii) The torque on the main shaft at rated power

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Gearbox and Generator Functions + Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Gearbox (2 Marks):**
- Function: Steps up rotor speed (15–20 rpm) to generator speed (1500–1800 rpm).
- Typical ratio: **1:75 to 1:100** for utility-scale turbines.
- Type: Multi-stage planetary gears (compact, high torque, coaxial).
- Losses: ~2–3% per stage.
- Most failure-prone subsystem in WECS.

**Generator Function (1 Mark):**
Converts the high-speed mechanical rotation into electrical AC power. The output is at variable frequency/voltage and is then conditioned by power electronics for grid connection.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | SCIG (Squirrel Cage IG) | DFIG (Doubly-Fed IG) | PMSG (Permanent Magnet SG) |
|:--------|:----------------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Gearbox required | Yes | Yes (1:90) | Often No (direct drive) |
| Speed range | Fixed (slip ~1–2%) | ±30% around sync. | Full variable |
| Converter rating | 100% (full-rated) | ~30% (partial) | 100% (full-rated) |
| Efficiency | 95–96% | 96–97% | 97–98% |
| Maintenance | High (gearbox) | High (gearbox + slip rings) | Low (no gearbox) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Reactive power need | From grid (capacitors) | From converter (self) | Self (converter) |
| Typical use | Small/medium turbines | Utility-scale (most common) | Offshore / direct-drive |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R = 35$ m, $V = 13$ m/s, $n = 22$ rpm, $C_p = 0.45$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**(i) Rated mechanical power (3 marks):**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (35)^2 = 1225 \pi \approx 3848.45 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3848.45 \times (13)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 3848.45 \times 2197 = 5{,}176{,}335 \; \text{W} \approx 5.176 \; \text{MW}$$

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.45 \times 5.176 = 2.329 \; \text{MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{rotor} \approx 2.33 \; \text{MW}}$$

**(ii) Tip Speed Ratio (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 35 \times 22}{30 \times 13} = \frac{2419.03}{390} \approx 6.20$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 6.20}$$

> This is consistent with the design point of a 3-blade utility turbine ($\lambda_{opt} \approx 6$–$8$).

**(iii) Torque on main shaft (2 marks):**

From $P = T \cdot \omega$, where $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 22}{60} = \frac{138.23}{60} \approx 2.304 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$T = \frac{P_{rotor}}{\omega} = \frac{2.329 \times 10^6}{2.304} \approx 1{,}010{,}850 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1.011 \; \text{MN·m}}$$

> This **megawatt-meter-scale torque** is why main shafts and gearboxes must be massive and rugged.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in WECS Part Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often plug wind speed in km/h directly into $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$. ALWAYS convert to m/s: $V_{\text{m/s}} = V_{\text{km/h}} / 3.6$. Similarly, swept area in cm² must be converted to m² by dividing by $10^4$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply $C_p$ (–2 marks):** $P_{wind}$ is the *available* power. The rotor *captures* only $C_p \cdot P_{wind}$. If the question asks for "power output of turbine", $C_p$ MUST be applied. If the question asks for "electrical power", all three efficiencies ($C_p$, $\eta_{gb}$, $\eta_{gen}$) must be applied.
>
> 3. **TSR Formula Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Some students use $\lambda = \omega R / V$ but forget to convert $\omega$ to the correct units. Use $\lambda = \pi R n / (30 V)$ when $n$ is in rpm.
>
> 4. **Missing Block Diagram in Theory Questions (–2 marks):** In any "explain the parts of WECS" 7-mark sub-question, a **neatly labeled block diagram is MANDATORY**. Text-only answers lose 2 marks immediately.
>
> 5. **Confusing Betz Limit with $C_p$:** Betz limit (0.5926) is the *theoretical maximum*. Real turbines operate at $C_p \approx 0.35$–$0.45$. Writing $C_p = 0.5926$ for a real turbine is **wrong** unless the question specifically asks for the Betz limit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this on your last revision day):**

- **WECS** = Wind Energy Conversion System — converts wind kinetic energy to electrical energy.
- **Major Parts (7 core):** Rotor, Nacelle, Main Shaft, Gearbox, Generator, Yaw System, Tower (+ Foundation, Control).
- **3-blade rotor** is the industry standard for utility-scale electricity generation.
- **Swept Area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — quadratically governs power.
- **Available Power:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ — cubically depends on wind speed.
- **Captured Power:** $P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$.
- **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ (59.26%) — never exceeded by any turbine.
- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi R n / (30 V)$.
- **Design $\lambda$ for 3-blade HAWT:** $\approx 6$–$8$.
- **High-solidity rotors** (multi-blade) → low $\lambda$, high torque, used for **pumping**.
- **Low-solidity rotors** (3-blade) → high $\lambda$, low torque, used for **electricity generation**.
- **Wind Shear Law:** $V(h) = V_{ref} (h/h_{ref})^{\alpha}$; $\alpha \approx 1/7$ for open terrain.
- **Gearbox** is the most failure-prone component — direct-drive avoids it.
- **SCIG** → cheapest, fixed speed, needs full-rating converter or soft-starter.
- **DFIG** → most common in utility-scale, ±30% speed, partial converter (~30%).
- **PMSG** → used in direct-drive, highest efficiency, no gearbox.
- **Annual Energy:** $E = P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760$ kWh/year; $CF$ typically 25–40%.
- **Torque formula:** $T = P / \omega = P \cdot 60 / (2\pi n)$ — gives main shaft torque in N·m.
- **Block diagram + table of functions** is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark theory questions.
- **Unit conversion discipline** (km/h → m/s, cm² → m²) saves 2–3 marks per question.
- **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at 15 °C, 1 atm.
- **Tower height rule of thumb:** $h \geq 1.5 \times$ rotor diameter for steady wind.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of OTEC System, Methods of OTEC Power Generation - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle (Block Diagram Description of OTEC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principle of OTEC System, Methods of OTEC Power Generation - Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle (Block Diagram Description of OTEC) --- # 🌊 OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion) — Principles & Power Cycles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a marine renewable energy technology that exploits the **natural temperature gradient (ΔT)** existing between the **warm upper layer of the ocean (sun-heated surface water, ~25–28 °C / 298–301 K)** and the **cold deep ocean water (~4–6 °C / 277–279 K, found at depths of ~800–1000 m)** to drive a thermodynamic heat engine and generate **continuous, base-load electricity (and in some configurations, desalinated water)**. For a plant to be thermodynamically viable, the surface-to-deep **ΔT must be ≥ 20 °C (≈ 20 K)**. Theoretically, OTEC operates on the **Carnot cycle** between these two thermal reservoirs. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Terminology:** > - **Warm Surface Water (WSW)** – Upper thermal reservoir / heat source. > - **Cold Deep Sea Water (CDSW)** – Lower thermal reservoir / heat sink. > - **ΔT = T_warm − T_cold** – The thermodynamic driving potential. > - **Tropical Belt** – Latitudes between **25° N and 25° S**, where viable ΔT exists year-round. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you have two buckets of water: - **Bucket A** sitting in the sun (warm, ~28 °C). - **Bucket B** lifted from a deep well (cold, ~5 °C). Now imagine placing a small balloon filled with a special low-boiling liquid over Bucket A. The liquid boils → vapor inflates the balloon → the balloon pushes a tiny turbine → the vapor is cooled back to liquid using Bucket B's water. That is essentially **OTEC** — a permanent, natural temperature difference provided by the Sun heating the ocean's surface while the deep ocean remains cold — used to spin a turbine. Because the Sun re-heats the surface every day, OTEC delivers **24×7 baseload power**, unlike solar PV or wind. > [!TIP] > **Why is OTEC classified as a Renewable Energy source?** > Because the warm water is continuously re-supplied by solar insolation, and the cold water is a stable thermal reservoir — the energy flux is essentially **inexhaustible on a human time scale**. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Minimum viable ΔT | **20 °C (≈ 20 K)** | | Warm surface water temperature, $T_w$ | **298 K (25 °C)** typical | | Cold deep water temperature, $T_c$ | **278 K (5 °C)** typical | | Site latitudes | **25° N – 25° S** (tropical belt) | | Cold water pipe depth | **800 – 1000 m** | | Ideal (Carnot) efficiency | **≈ 6.7 %** at ΔT = 20 K | | Practical plant efficiency | **2.5 – 3 %** | | Net plant capacity factor | **≈ 85 – 95 %** (24×7 operation) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The OTEC Heat Engine OTEC is fundamentally a **low-temperature heat engine** operating between: - A **hot reservoir** (warm surface seawater) at $T_h$ (K) - A **cold reservoir** (deep seawater) at $T_c$ (K) Because $T_h$ is only slightly above ambient (≈ 298 K) and $T_c$ is only slightly below ambient (≈ 278 K), the available ΔT is small. Therefore, OTEC plants must: 1. Process **very large mass flow rates** of seawater to capture meaningful thermal energy. 2. Use **large heat exchangers** (especially for the closed cycle) to compensate for low ΔT. 3. Operate at **near-vacuum conditions** (open cycle) or with **low-boiling refrigerants** (closed cycle) to maximize vapor generation. --- ### 2.2 The Three Principal OTEC Power Generation Methods | Method | Inventor / Year | Working Fluid | By-product | |---|---|---|---| | **Open Cycle (Claude Cycle)** | **Georges Claude, 1930** | Warm seawater itself (vaporized under vacuum) | **Fresh water** + Electricity | | **Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle)** | **James Anderson, 1966** | Low-boiling fluid: Ammonia (NH₃), Propane, or **R-134a** | Electricity only | | **Hybrid Cycle** | — (combines both) | Warm seawater + secondary NH₃ loop | **Fresh water** + Electricity | --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} = \dfrac{T_h - T_c}{T_h}$$ | Maximum (ideal) thermodynamic efficiency | | 2 | $$\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_h}$$ | Same expression in ΔT form | | 3 | $$\eta_{actual} = (0.30 \text{ to } 0.50) \times \eta_{Carnot}$$ | Practical efficiency ratio for OTEC plants | | 4 | $$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{warm} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_w - T_{evap})$$ | Heat transferred in evaporator from warm water | | 5 | $$Q_{out} = \dot{m}_{cold} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{cond} - T_c)$$ | Heat rejected in condenser to cold water | | 6 | $$\dot{W}_{turbine} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$$ | Turbine power output (open & closed cycle) | | 7 | $$\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_{turbine} - \dot{W}_{pump}$$ | Net electrical output | | 8 | $$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$$ | Mass flow of vapor generated per second | | 9 | $$\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference (heat exchangers) | | 10 | $$\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$$ | Heat exchanger sizing equation | | 11 | $$\text{SF} = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{A_{min,cf}}$$ | Sliding/Fouling Factor for heat exchangers | | 12 | $$P_{vapor}(T) \approx \text{Antoine or steam tables}$$ | Saturation pressure of working fluid | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Units Check (KTU Board Pitfall):** > - Temperatures **in Carnot equation MUST be in Kelvin (K)**. > - Mass flow rates in **kg/s**, enthalpy in **kJ/kg**, work in **kW or MW**. > - Specific heat of seawater $c_p \approx 4.0$ **kJ/kg·K** (not 4.18 like pure water — salinity lowers it slightly). --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of OTEC - **Island nations & remote coastal communities:** Provides baseload electricity where diesel transport is expensive (e.g., Hawaii, Maldives, Lakshadweep, Caribbean). - **Co-products:** Fresh water desalination (open & hybrid cycles) is often **more economically valuable than the electricity itself**. - **Mariculture & AC:** Cold deep water can be piped onshore for **aquaculture** (lobster, cold-water fish) and **air-conditioning** (district cooling). - **Hydrogen production:** Pilot projects coupling OTEC with electrolysis for green H₂. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical (Carnot) Efficiency of an OTEC Plant We start from the **second law of thermodynamics** for a reversible heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. **Step 1 — Energy balance on a reversible cycle (Kelvin-Planck statement):** For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the net entropy change of the universe is zero: $$\Delta S_{hot} + \Delta S_{cold} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Express entropy change of each reservoir:** Heat $Q_h$ is extracted from the hot reservoir at $T_h$, and heat $Q_c$ is rejected to the cold reservoir at $T_c$: $$-\dfrac{Q_h}{T_h} + \dfrac{Q_c}{T_c} = 0$$ The negative sign on the first term indicates heat leaving the hot reservoir. **Step 3 — Solve for the ratio of heats:** $$\dfrac{Q_c}{Q_h} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$$ **Step 4 — Apply First Law (steady-state, no heat accumulation):** By energy conservation, the net work output equals the net heat input: $$\dot{W}_{net} = Q_h - Q_c$$ **Step 5 — Define thermal efficiency as work out / heat in:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{net}}{Q_h} = \dfrac{Q_h - Q_c}{Q_h} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_h}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute the temperature ratio from Step 3:** $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} = \dfrac{T_h - T_c}{T_h} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_h}}$$ This is the **single most important equation in OTEC analysis**. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard) **Problem:** An OTEC plant operates between warm surface water at $T_h = 28 \, °C$ and cold deep water at $T_c = 6 \, °C$. The plant processes $\dot{m}_h = 1000$ kg/s of warm seawater with $c_p = 4.0$ kJ/kg·K. The turbine isentropic efficiency is 85 % and generator efficiency is 95 %. Determine: 1. Carnot efficiency. 2. Practical plant efficiency. 3. Heat extracted from warm water (rate). 4. Gross turbine work (assume ideal $W_t = 0.05 \times Q_{in}$). 5. Net electrical output. **Solution — Step by Step:** **Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:** $$T_h = 28 + 273 = 301 \text{ K}, \quad T_c = 6 + 273 = 279 \text{ K}$$ $$\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 22 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Carnot efficiency:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_h} = \dfrac{22}{301} = 0.0731 \approx 7.31 \, \%$$ **Step 3 — Practical plant efficiency (assume 35 % of Carnot):** $$\eta_{actual} = 0.35 \times 0.0731 = 0.0256 \approx 2.56 \, \%$$ **Step 4 — Heat input rate from warm water:** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_h \, c_p \, (T_h - T_{evap})$$ Taking $T_{evap} \approx 26 \, °C$ (2 °C below source — pinch point): $$\dot{Q}_{in} = 1000 \times 4.0 \times (28 - 26) = 8000 \text{ kW} = 8.0 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 5 — Gross turbine work:** $$\dot{W}_t = \eta_t \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.05 \times 8000 = 400 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6 — Net electrical output (after generator losses):** $$\dot{W}_{elec} = \eta_g \times \dot{W}_t = 0.95 \times 400 = 380 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always subtract the **parasitic pumping power** of the cold-water pipe (often 25–30 % of gross output) to obtain the truly **net** deliverable power. Many students quote only gross turbine work and lose 2–3 marks. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Closed vs Hybrid Cycle | Parameter | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle | |---|---|---|---| | **Working fluid** | Seawater (boiled under vacuum) | NH₃ / Propane / R-134a | Seawater + NH₃ | | **Pressure in evaporator** | Very low (≈ 0.03–0.04 bar) | Moderate (≈ 7–10 bar for NH₃) | Two-stage | | **Turbine size** | Large (low pressure, high volume) | Compact | Compact | | **Heat exchanger size** | Not needed (direct contact flash) | Very large | Very large | | **Vacuum required?** | **Yes** (≈ 2–3 % atm) | No (pressurized loop) | Yes (for seawater side) | | **Fresh water produced?** | **Yes** (condensate) | No | **Yes** | | **Turbine corrosion risk** | High (salt water vapor) | None (sealed loop) | Low | | **Capital cost per kW** | Higher (due to large turbine & vacuum system) | Lower (compact hardware) | Highest | | **Practical efficiency** | ~2.0 – 2.5 % | ~3.0 – 3.5 % | ~3.0 % | | **Commercially proven** | Yes (1 MW Japan, 1930s) | Yes (210 kW Hawaii, 1979) | Pilot stage | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic OTEC Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Warm Surface Seawater
28 deg C at 0 to 200 m] --> B[Warm Water Intake
and Screening Pump] B --> C[Evaporator / Heat Exchanger
Vapor Generation] C --> D[Turbine
Expansion and Power Takeoff] D --> E[Electrical Generator
AC Output] E --> F[Step Up Transformer
Grid Interface] F --> G[Switchyard and Load] D --> H[Condenser
Steam Re liquefaction] H --> I[Cold Deep Seawater
5 deg C at 800 to 1000 m] I --> J[Cold Water Pump
High Head Pumping] J --> H C --> K[Warm Water Out
Spent to Sea] H --> L[Cold Water Out
Spent to Sea] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide:** The block diagram traces the two thermal streams (warm and cold) and how they couple through the heat exchangers / turbine. The **warm stream cools as it gives up heat**, the **cold stream warms as it absorbs waste heat**, and both are eventually discharged back to the ocean. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Open Cycle (Claude Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart LR W1[Warm Seawater
28 deg C Surface] --> P1[Warm Water Pump] P1 --> EV[Flash Evaporator
Vacuum 0.03 bar] EV -->|Steam at low P| TRB[Turbine
Low Pressure High Volume] TRB --> GN[Generator] GN --> PWR[Power Output Bus] TRB -->|Exhaust Steam| CND[Surface Condenser] CND -->|Condensate
FRESH WATER| FW[Fresh Water Collection Tank] CND --> V1[Vacuum Pump] V1 --> EV C1[Cold Deep Seawater
5 deg C at 1000 m] --> P2[Cold Water Pump
High Head] P2 --> CND CND --> O1[Spent Cold Water to Sea] EV --> O2[Spent Warm Water to Sea] ``` > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway (Claude Cycle):** > The warm seawater is **itself** the working fluid. By flashing it to steam in a vacuum, you spin the turbine; the steam is condensed using cold deep water — and the **condensate is drinkable fresh water** (the salt stays behind in the evaporator). --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart LR WH[Warm Seawater
28 deg C] --> EV[Evaporator
Shell and Tube HX] EV -->|Vaporized NH3| TRB[Turbine
High Speed Compact] TRB --> GN[Generator] GN --> PWR[Power Output Bus] TRB -->|Exhaust Vapor| CND[Condenser
Shell and Tube HX] CND -->|Liquid NH3| PUMP[Feed Pump
Circulation] PUMP --> EV CD[Cold Deep Seawater
5 deg C] --> P1[Cold Water Pump] P1 --> CND CND --> O1[Spent Cold Water] EV --> O2[Spent Warm Water] ``` > [!TIP] > **Key takeaway (Anderson Cycle):** > The working fluid (**NH₃ or propane**) is sealed inside a loop — seawater never enters the turbine. This avoids corrosion and allows compact, high-speed turbines. However, the heat exchangers must be **huge** to transfer low-grade heat efficiently. --- ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Hybrid Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR W1[Warm Seawater
28 deg C] --> P1[Warm Water Pump] P1 --> EVC[Evaporator Combined
Flash and Indirect HX] EVC -->|Steam| CND2[Surface Condenser] CND2 -->|Fresh Water| FWT[Fresh Water Tank] EVC -->|Heat to NH3 Loop| NH3E[NH3 Evaporator] NH3E --> TRB[Turbine] TRB --> GN[Generator] GN --> PWR[Power Output Bus] TRB --> CND1[NH3 Condenser] CND1 --> PUMP[NH3 Feed Pump] PUMP --> NH3E C1[Cold Deep Seawater
5 deg C] --> P2[Cold Water Pump] P2 --> CND1 P2 --> CND2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Hybrid advantage:** The warm seawater first flashes and gives up its latent heat (producing fresh water), and then its sensible heat is used to evaporate the working fluid (producing power). Both **fresh water and electricity** are co-generated with better overall energy utilization. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *State the principle of Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). What is the minimum temperature difference required for an OTEC plant to operate?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Principle (2 marks):** The principle of OTEC is to harness the **temperature difference (ΔT)** between the warm surface seawater (heat source, ~25–28 °C) and the cold deep seawater (heat sink, ~4–6 °C at ~1000 m depth) by operating a **heat engine** (open, closed, or hybrid cycle) between these two reservoirs. The working fluid is vaporized by warm water, expands through a turbine to produce mechanical work (electricity), and is condensed back to liquid using the cold water. **Minimum ΔT (1 mark):** For an OTEC plant to be thermodynamically and economically viable, the minimum temperature difference required is **ΔT ≥ 20 °C** (i.e., 20 K). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *List any three differences between the Open Cycle (Claude) and Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC systems.* **Model Answer (3 marks — any three valid points):** | # | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Warm seawater itself is the working fluid | Low-boiling fluid (NH₃, propane) is the working fluid | | 2 | Operates under vacuum; large, low-pressure turbine | Operates at moderate pressure; compact, high-speed turbine | | 3 | Produces **fresh water** as a by-product (condensate) | Produces **electricity only** — no fresh water | | 4 | Requires vacuum de-aeration system | Sealed loop — no vacuum system required | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Past Pattern]* **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **principle of operation of a Closed Cycle (Anderson) OTEC system**. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A closed-cycle OTEC plant operates between $T_h = 27 \, °C$ and $T_c = 5 \, °C$. The warm water mass flow rate is 2000 kg/s and the cold water mass flow rate is 1500 kg/s. Specific heat of seawater = 4.0 kJ/kg·K. The plant delivers **12 MW** of gross electrical output. Calculate: 1. The **Carnot efficiency**. 2. The **heat input rate**. 3. The **actual plant efficiency**. 4. The **heat rejected to the cold water**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Working Principle (with block diagram, 4 marks):** Refer to the **Section 4.3 Closed Cycle block diagram** drawn above. The key operational steps are: 1. **Warm seawater** (surface, ~28 °C) is drawn in and pumped through the **shell side** of the **evaporator** (shell-and-tube heat exchanger). 2. Inside the tubes, a low-boiling working fluid — typically **ammonia (NH₃)** with boiling point ≈ −33 °C at 1 atm — absorbs heat and **vaporizes**. 3. The high-pressure NH₃ vapor drives a **turbine** coupled to a generator, producing electricity. 4. The low-pressure exhaust vapor enters the **condenser**, where cold deep seawater (~5 °C, pumped from ~1000 m) absorbs heat and **re-condenses** the NH₃ back to liquid. 5. A **feed pump** recirculates the liquid NH₃ back to the evaporator, completing the closed loop. **Advantages (1.5 marks):** - Compact, high-speed turbine (NH₃ vapor at moderate pressure, ~7–10 bar). - No corrosion problems — seawater never enters the turbine. - Operates continuously 24×7; no fuel cost. - Higher practical efficiency (~3 %) than open cycle. **Disadvantages (1.5 marks):** - Very large heat exchangers required (low ΔT compensation). - NH₃ is toxic — leak detection and safety systems add cost. - No fresh water as by-product. - High parasitic pumping power for cold-water pipe. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** - $T_h = 27 \, °C = 300 \, K$, $T_c = 5 \, °C = 278 \, K$ - $\dot{m}_h = 2000$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_c = 1500$ kg/s - $c_p = 4.0$ kJ/kg·K - $\dot{W}_{gross} = 12$ MW = 12 000 kW **(i) Carnot Efficiency (1.5 marks):** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} = 1 - \dfrac{278}{300} = 1 - 0.9267 = 0.0733$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 7.33 \, \%}$$ **(ii) Heat Input Rate (2 marks):** The actual plant efficiency relates net output to heat input. However, to compute the heat input independently from warm-water enthalpy drop: $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_p \cdot (T_h - T_{evap})$$ Assuming a pinch of 2 °C, $T_{evap} = 25 \, °C$: $$\dot{Q}_{in} = 2000 \times 4.0 \times (27 - 25) = 16000 \text{ kW} = 16 \text{ MW}$$ **Alternatively**, using the gross output divided by an assumed plant efficiency (≈ 35 % of Carnot ≈ 2.57 %): $$\dot{Q}_{in} \approx \dfrac{12}{0.0257} \approx 467 \text{ MW (if using full } \dot{m}_h \text{ enthalpy drop)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always clearly state the **pinch temperature assumption** (typically 2–3 °C) before computing heat duty. Without this, you lose 1 mark for ambiguity. **(iii) Actual Plant Efficiency (1.5 marks):** $$\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{gross}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \dfrac{12}{16} = 0.75$$ This is unrealistically high — indicating our assumption must be refined. Using the more realistic **~35 % of Carnot**: $$\eta_{actual} = 0.35 \times 7.33 \, \% = 2.57 \, \%$$ **(iv) Heat Rejected to Cold Water (2 marks):** By First Law: $$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{gross} = 16000 - 12000 = 4000 \text{ kW} = 4.0 \text{ MW}$$ Verify with cold-water temperature rise: $$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_c \Rightarrow \Delta T_c = \dfrac{4000}{1500 \times 4.0} = 0.67 \, °C$$ (Rise of 0.67 °C in cold water — physically reasonable.) --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Alternative Choice]* **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **principle of operation of an Open Cycle (Claude) OTEC system**. Mention the **role of the vacuum system** and the **co-product obtained**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An OTEC plant operates on the **Hybrid cycle**. Warm surface water is at 28 °C, cold deep water at 6 °C. The plant delivers 5 MW of electrical output with an overall efficiency of 3 %. Determine: 1. The heat input rate. 2. The Carnot efficiency. 3. The actual-to-Carnot efficiency ratio. 4. Comment on the **fresh water output**, assuming 1 kg of fresh water is produced per 1.5 kg of flashed steam. If 600 kg/s of warm seawater is flashed, find the **fresh water rate**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Principle (4 marks) — refer to Section 4.2 block diagram:** In the Claude (Open) Cycle, the **warm seawater itself is the working fluid**. Operation: 1. Warm seawater (~28 °C) is drawn from the surface and admitted into a **flash evaporator** maintained at very low pressure (~0.03 bar, corresponding to saturation temperature ≈ 26 °C). 2. Because the local saturation pressure is below the water's vapor pressure, a portion of the seawater **instantaneously flashes** into low-pressure steam. 3. The steam expands through a **large, low-pressure turbine** driving a generator. 4. The exhaust steam is condensed in a **surface condenser** using cold deep seawater (~5 °C from 1000 m depth). 5. The **condensate is fresh water** — collected as a valuable by-product. 6. A **vacuum pump / de-aerator** continuously removes non-condensable gases to maintain the vacuum. **Role of Vacuum System (1.5 marks):** - Allows seawater to boil at ambient warm temperature (~26 °C instead of 100 °C). - Enables larger ΔT between evaporator and ambient → higher Carnot efficiency. - Without vacuum, the warm seawater would simply not vaporize — no steam, no power. **Co-product (1.5 marks):** The condensate from the condenser is **fresh potable water** — a critical advantage for island nations. Salt and minerals remain in the spent warm seawater and are discharged back to the ocean. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** $T_h = 28 \, °C = 301$ K, $T_c = 6 \, °C = 279$ K, $\dot{W} = 5$ MW, $\eta_{actual} = 3$ %. **(i) Heat Input Rate (2 marks):** $$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{\dot{W}}{\eta_{actual}} = \dfrac{5}{0.03} = 166.67 \text{ MW}$$ **(ii) Carnot Efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{279}{301} = 1 - 0.9269 = 0.0731 = 7.31 \, \%$$ **(iii) Actual-to-Carnot Ratio (1 mark):** $$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\eta_{actual}}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \dfrac{3}{7.31} = 0.410 \approx 41 \, \%$$ This is within the practical range (30–50 % of Carnot). **(iv) Fresh Water Output (2 marks):** Warm seawater flashed: $\dot{m}_{flash} = 600$ kg/s Fresh water rate: $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dfrac{600}{1.5} = 400$ kg/s = **400 L/s = 24 000 L/min = 1.44 ML/h (megalitres per hour)**. > **Comment:** At 400 kg/s (≈ 34.6 million litres/day), this hybrid plant is **highly attractive for tropical island desalination** alongside electricity generation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in OTEC Problems (Verified by Board Examiners):** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Carnot formula → entire efficiency is wrong → 3–4 marks lost. > 2. **Confusing "Open" and "Closed" cycle** definitions in short answers → 2 marks lost. > 3. **Not labelling the block diagram** with temperatures and component names → lose 1–2 marks on "neat diagram" criteria. > 4. **Ignoring parasitic pumping power** when asked for "net output" → 2 marks lost. > 5. **Not stating the ΔT ≥ 20 °C requirement** in definitional questions → 1 mark lost. > 6. **Mixing units** (e.g., enthalpy in kJ/kg but mass flow in g/s) → cascading calculation error → 3–4 marks lost. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ - 🔑 **Principle:** OTEC exploits the **temperature gradient** (~20 K minimum) between warm surface water and cold deep ocean water. - 🔑 **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_C = \Delta T / T_h$ — always use **Kelvin**. - 🔑 **Three Cycles:** - **Open (Claude)** → seawater itself vaporized → **fresh water + electricity**. - **Closed (Anderson)** → NH₃ / propane loop → **electricity only**, compact hardware. - **Hybrid** → combines both → **fresh water + electricity**, best co-generation. - 🔑 **Minimum ΔT = 20 K**; viable latitudes = **25° N – 25° S**. - 🔑 **Cold water pipe depth** = 800–1000 m; high parasitic pumping cost. - 🔑 **Practical efficiency** = 2.5–3.5 %; **Carnot ratio** typically 30–50 %. - 🔑 **Plant capacity factor** = 85–95 % (24×7 baseload). - 🔑 **Co-products:** Fresh water, cold water for **AC & mariculture** — often more valuable than electricity. - 🔑 **Major challenge:** Cold-water pipe deployment & biofouling of heat exchangers.

topics.title: Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geothermal Energy** is defined as the thermal energy generated and stored within the Earth's crust. It is a form of renewable energy derived from the natural heat of the Earth, originating from the original planetary accretion, radioactive decay of minerals, and solar energy absorbed at the surface. The temperature difference between the Earth's deep interior and its surface drives a continuous, sustainable heat flux of approximately **0.087 W/m²** (global mean terrestrial heat flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must understand that geothermal resources exist in four principal forms: (i) Hot Dry Rock (HDR), (ii) Magma Resources, (iii) Hydrothermal (Vapour-Dominated and Liquid-Dominated) Systems, and (iv) Enhanced/Engineered Geothermal Systems (EGS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Earth as a Pressure Cooker

Imagine the Earth as a giant, slow-cooking pressure cooker. Deep beneath our feet, the radioactive decay of elements like Uranium-238, Thorium-232, and Potassium-40 continuously generates heat, just like the heating element in an electric rice cooker. This heat cannot escape quickly because rock is a poor conductor. As pressure builds, the heat is stored in rock formations, underground aquifers, and molten magma chambers.

When we drill a well into the Earth, it is analogous to **poking a small vent into the pressure cooker lid**. The trapped heat then escapes, which we can harness to boil water, drive turbines, or heat buildings. Unlike solar or wind energy, this heat is **always available** — 24 hours a day, 365 days a year — making it a **baseload** renewable source.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Global mean geothermal heat flux:** $\mathbf{0.087 \; W/m^2}$
- **Earth's core temperature:** $\mathbf{\approx 6000 \; °C}$
- **Average geothermal gradient (continental crust):** $\mathbf{25-30 \; °C \; per \; km}$ (can be up to **150 °C/km** in tectonically active zones)
- **Earth's total thermal energy to 10 km depth:** $\mathbf{\approx 1.3 \times 10^{27} \; J}$
- **Resource temperature categories:**
  - Low enthalpy: $< 90 \; °C$
  - Medium enthalpy: $90 - 150 \; °C$
  - High enthalpy: $> 150 \; °C$

> [!NOTE]
> The **geothermal gradient** is the rate of increase in temperature with depth below the Earth's surface. The standard economic depth for geothermal exploitation is typically **3 to 10 km**.

### 1.4 Classification Overview of Geothermal Resources

| Resource Type | State of Heat Carrier | Typical Temperature | Technology Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Dry Rock (HDR) | Solid rock, no natural fluid | 150 – 350 °C | Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS), Hydraulic Fracturing |
| Magma Resources | Molten rock (silicate melt) | 700 – 1300 °C | Experimental, Direct Heating |
| Vapour-Dominated (Dry Steam) | Superheated steam | > 235 °C | Flash/Dry Steam Turbine |
| Liquid-Dominated (Wet Steam/Hot Water) | Hot water with dissolved gases | 150 – 300 °C | Flash Steam, Binary Cycle |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hot Dry Rock (HDR) Resources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU-Standard):** Hot Dry Rock (HDR) refers to deep underground formations of hot, essentially dry crystalline basement rock (typically granite or granodiorite) at depths of **3 to 10 km** with temperatures in the range of **150 – 350 °C**, but lacking the natural permeability and fluid content required for conventional hydrothermal extraction.

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle — Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS)

Since HDR has no natural water, an engineered reservoir must be artificially created. The process involves:

1. **Injection Well Drilling:** A deep well (3–5 km) is drilled into the hot rock formation.
2. **Hydraulic Stimulation / Fracturing:** Cold surface water is pumped down at **high pressure (10 – 40 MPa)** to fracture the rock, creating an artificial heat exchanger (the "reservoir").
3. **Production Well Drilling:** A second well is drilled to intersect the fracture network.
4. **Circulation Loop:** Cold water is injected through the injection well, flows through the hot fractured rock, heats up, and rises as hot water/steam through the production well.
5. **Surface Plant:** The extracted heat is converted to electricity via a binary or flash plant, and the cooled water is reinjected — making it a **closed-loop system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The Fenton Hill Project (New Mexico, USA) and the Soultz-sous-Forêts project (France, Alsace) are pioneering HDR/EGS demonstrators. The Cooper Basin (Australia) and the EGS pilot at FORGE (Utah, USA) are leading next-generation test beds.

#### 2.1.2 Why and How HDR Matters

- **Why:** Approximately **98% of the Earth's heat** is stored in impermeable, dry rock. HDR represents the **largest potential geothermal resource base** by orders of magnitude.
- **How:** By creating artificial permeability, the energy potential locked in HDR formations becomes accessible in regions without natural hydrothermal systems (i.e., almost everywhere on Earth).

---

### 2.2 Magma Resources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Magma resources refer to active or recently solidified **molten silicate rock (magma chambers)** at depths of typically **3 – 10 km**, with temperatures ranging from **700 °C to 1300 °C**. These are the most energy-dense but technically most challenging geothermal resources.

#### 2.2.1 Key Characteristics

- **Energy Density:** Theoretically, a single cubic meter of magma can release the thermal equivalent of **~2 MWh** upon solidification.
- **Energy Extraction Methods:**
  1. **Direct-contact heat exchanger** — circulate a working fluid directly through the magma chamber.
  2. **Indirect-contact system** — drill wells into the surrounding hot rock (the contact aureole) for indirect heat transfer.
  3. **Magma-EGS** — fracture the solidified roof of the magma chamber to create a heat exchange surface.
- **Locations:** Iceland (Krafla caldera), Hawaii (Kilauea), Japan (Unzen), Italy (Vulcano).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** To date, **no commercial power plant** has been successfully operated on direct magma. Pilot projects (e.g., the Magma Energy Pilot in Iceland, US Department of Energy's Sandia Lab experiments in the 1980s) have demonstrated proof-of-concept but face extreme challenges: **corrosion, drilling fluid vaporization, well-bore instability**, and **extremely high temperatures** that destroy conventional drill bits.

---

### 2.3 Hydrothermal Resources (Vapour and Liquid-Dominated Systems)

#### 2.3.1 Vapour-Dominated (Dry Steam) Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A vapour-dominated geothermal reservoir is one in which **steam is the continuous, pressure-controlling phase** in the porous fractured rock formation. Reservoir temperatures are typically **> 235 °C**, and the produced fluid is **superheated dry steam** (no liquid water content).

- **Mechanism:** Deep, naturally fractured aquifers are filled with pressurized steam. When a well is drilled, the steam rushes to the surface due to the high reservoir pressure.
- **Surface Equipment:** A standard **back-pressure steam turbine** is used (no condenser vacuum required, though often installed for efficiency).
- **Iconic Example:** **The Geysers, California, USA** — the world's largest installed geothermal power complex (~1.5 GW capacity). Also **Larderello, Italy** — the world's first geothermal power plant (1904).
- **Why it works:** The fluid is already in the gaseous phase, eliminating the need for flashing and reducing scaling/erosion issues.

#### 2.3.2 Liquid-Dominated (Wet Steam / Hot Water) Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A liquid-dominated reservoir contains **hot liquid water as the continuous phase**, with temperatures typically between **150 °C and 300 °C**. When the fluid reaches the surface, the pressure drop causes "flashing" — a portion of the liquid spontaneously converts to steam.

- **Two Subtypes:**
  1. **High-temperature liquid-dominated (> 200 °C):** Uses **single-flash** or **double-flash steam plants** to separate flashed steam and drive turbines.
  2. **Low-temperature liquid-dominated (< 150 °C):** Generally not suited for electricity generation, used for direct-use heating (greenhouses, space heating, aquaculture).
- **Iconic Examples:** **Wairakei (New Zealand), Cerro Prieto (Mexico), Salton Sea (USA), Nagaoka (Japan).**
- **Why it works:** Hot brine (often with high Total Dissolved Solids, TDS) is naturally present in confined aquifers beneath an impermeable cap rock.

---

### 2.4 Binary Cycle Geothermal Power Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU-Favorite Question):** A Binary Cycle geothermal power plant is a **closed-loop system** in which the **geothermal hot brine transfers its heat to a secondary working fluid (with a low boiling point)** via a heat exchanger. The secondary fluid vaporizes, drives a turbine, and is then condensed and recycled. The geothermal brine **never comes in direct contact** with the turbine.

#### 2.4.1 Why Binary Cycle is the Workhorse of Modern Geothermal Energy

- It enables power generation from **low-to-medium enthalpy resources** (as low as **70 °C – 120 °C**), which are the **most abundant** on Earth.
- The geothermal brine remains in a closed loop, **preventing the release of dissolved gases** (H₂S, CO₂, radon) and **minimizing scale formation** in the reservoir.
- Common working fluids (low-boiling-point organic refrigerants): **Isobutane (R-600a), n-Pentane, R-134a, R-245fa, ammonia (in some designs).**

#### 2.4.2 Component Breakdown

1. **Production Well:** Brings hot brine (typically 120 – 180 °C) to the surface.
2. **Pre-heater & Vaporizer (Heat Exchangers):** Transfer heat to the secondary fluid in a **shell-and-tube** or **plate heat exchanger**.
3. **Turbine-Generator Unit:** Vaporized secondary fluid expands through a turbine.
4. **Condenser:** Working fluid is condensed back to liquid using an **air-cooled** or **water-cooled** condenser.
5. **Pump:** Recycles the working fluid back to the vaporizer (closed loop).
6. **Reinjection Pump:** Sends cooled brine back into the reservoir.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC):** The most widely deployed binary cycle is the **ORC**, which uses an organic fluid. Modern ORC plants using **Kalina cycles** (binary mixture of ammonia and water) can achieve higher thermal efficiencies at lower temperatures by leveraging variable boiling points.

#### 2.4.3 Efficiency Considerations

The thermal efficiency of a binary cycle is governed by the **Carnot efficiency** between the brine inlet and the ambient rejection temperature. Because the temperature difference is small, the **second-law (exergy) efficiency** is more meaningful than the first-law efficiency.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Description / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geothermal Gradient | $G = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta z}$ | Rate of temperature increase with depth; $G \approx 25-30$ °C/km (normal crust) | °C/km |
| Heat Flow (Conductive) | $q = k \cdot G$ | Terrestrial heat flux; $k$ = thermal conductivity of rock (≈ 2.5 W/m·K) | W/m² |
| Thermal Energy in HDR | $Q = \rho_r c_r V (T_r - T_{ref})$ | Stored heat in rock volume $V$; $\rho_r$ = rock density (~2700 kg/m³), $c_r$ = specific heat (~900 J/kg·K) | J |
| Recoverable Heat Fraction | $Q_{rec} = \eta_r \cdot Q$ | $\eta_r$ = heat recovery factor (typically 5% – 20% for EGS) | J |
| Power Output (EGS) | $P = \dot{m} c_w (T_{prod} - T_{inj}) \eta_{plant}$ | Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$, water specific heat $c_w$, plant efficiency $\eta_{plant}$ | W |
| Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_C = 1 - \dfrac{T_{sink}}{T_{source}}$ | Maximum theoretical efficiency; $T$ in Kelvin | dimensionless |
| Binary Cycle Efficiency (ORC) | $\eta_{ORC} \approx 0.10 - 0.20$ | Typical range; depends on source/condenser temperatures | dimensionless |
| Net Power Output | $P_{net} = P_{gross} - P_{aux}$ | $P_{aux}$ = pump, cooling fan, control losses | W |
| Brine Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho_b Q_b}{3600}$ | $\rho_b$ = brine density (~900–1000 kg/m³), $Q_b$ = volumetric flow (m³/h) | kg/s |
| Reservoir Lifetime Estimate | $t = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{P_{net} \cdot 8760 \times 3600}$ | $8760$ = hours per year | years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Utility:** These formulas are essential for **resource assessment, well-field design, and power plant optimization** in geothermal engineering. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Geothermal Technologies Office uses these exact formulations in its GETEM (Geothermal Energy Technology Evaluation Model) tool.

---

### 2.6 Advantages and Disadvantages of Geothermal Energy

#### 2.6.1 Advantages

- **Baseload availability:** Operates **24/7, 365 days a year** with capacity factors of **70% – 95%** (vs. ~25% for solar, ~35% for wind).
- **Small land footprint:** A 50 MW geothermal plant typically occupies **~1–2 km²**, far less than a comparable solar or wind farm.
- **Low operational cost:** No fuel cost; minimal moving parts; long plant life (typically **30+ years**, wells may last 50+ years).
- **Low carbon footprint:** Lifecycle CO₂ emissions of **~45 g CO₂-eq/kWh** (vs. ~820 g for coal).
- **Scalability:** Direct-use applications (heating, cooling, agriculture) are economically viable at small scale.
- **Modular and decentralized:** Suitable for remote, off-grid communities.

#### 2.6.2 Disadvantages

- **High upfront capital cost:** Drilling a deep well costs **$2 – 7 million USD per well** (and exploration drilling has a high dry-hole risk).
- **Site-specific resource:** Only available in geologically favorable regions (volcanic, tectonic plate boundaries).
- **Hazardous gas emissions:** Natural release of H₂S, CO₂, mercury, and radon in some hydrothermal systems.
- **Induced seismicity:** Hydraulic fracturing in EGS can trigger micro-earthquakes (project at Basel, Switzerland, was abandoned in 2009 due to felt M 3.4 events).
- **Brine disposal / scaling:** High-TDS brines cause **scaling and corrosion** in pipes and turbines; require expensive reinjection.
- **Slow response to demand:** Cannot easily ramp up/down like gas turbines.
- **Magma resource challenges:** Extreme temperatures destroy conventional drill bits and materials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When asked to "compare" geothermal with other renewables, students frequently **omit the capacity factor comparison**. Always state that geothermal is a **baseload** source, while solar/wind are **intermittent**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stored Thermal Energy in a Hot Dry Rock (HDR) Reservoir

**Problem Setup:** Compute the total stored thermal energy in an HDR reservoir of volume $V$, mean rock temperature $T_r$, and reference (ambient) temperature $T_{ref}$.

**Step 1: Define the heat content per unit volume of rock.**

The volumetric heat capacity of the rock is $\rho_r \cdot c_r$, where $\rho_r$ is rock density and $c_r$ is specific heat. The heat stored per unit volume relative to $T_{ref}$ is:

$$q_V = \rho_r \, c_r \, (T_r - T_{ref})$$

**Step 2: Total stored thermal energy.**

Multiply the volumetric heat by the total reservoir volume $V$:

$$Q_{stored} = q_V \cdot V = \rho_r \, c_r \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref})$$

**Step 3: Account for recoverable fraction.**

In practice, only a fraction $\eta_r$ of this stored heat can be extracted before the rock cools below the economic threshold (typically 70 °C). So:

$$Q_{rec} = \eta_r \cdot \rho_r \, c_r \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref})$$

**Step 4: Convert to electrical energy over a plant lifetime.**

The recoverable electrical energy is:

$$E_{elec} = \eta_{plant} \cdot Q_{rec} = \eta_{plant} \, \eta_r \, \rho_r \, c_r \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref})$$

**Numerical Example:** A single EGS reservoir of volume $V = 1 \text{ km}^3 = 10^9 \text{ m}^3$, $T_r = 250 \, °C$, $T_{ref} = 25 \, °C$, $\rho_r = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_r = 900 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\eta_r = 0.10$, $\eta_{plant} = 0.12$.

**Step 5: Substitute values into the formula.**

$$Q_{stored} = 2700 \times 900 \times 10^9 \times (250 - 25)$$

$$Q_{stored} = 2.43 \times 10^6 \times 10^9 \times 225$$

$$Q_{stored} = 5.4675 \times 10^{17} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6: Apply recovery and plant efficiency factors.**

$$E_{elec} = 0.12 \times 0.10 \times 5.4675 \times 10^{17}$$

$$E_{elec} = 6.561 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}$$

**Step 7: Convert to MWh.**

$$E_{elec} = \dfrac{6.561 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 1.8225 \times 10^6 \text{ MWh} \approx 1.82 \text{ million MWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This single cubic-kilometer HDR block could, in principle, supply **~208 MW of baseload power continuously for one full year**, illustrating the enormous energy density of even a modest HDR formation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Output from a Binary Cycle Plant

**Setup:** A binary cycle plant receives geothermal brine at $T_{brine,in}$ and rejects it at $T_{brine,out}$. The brine mass flow rate is $\dot{m}_b$.

**Step 1: Heat extracted by the heat exchanger.**

The heat transferred to the secondary working fluid per unit time is:

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = \dot{m}_b \, c_{p,b} \, (T_{brine,in} - T_{brine,out})$$

**Step 2: Heat absorbed by the working fluid (energy balance on the heat exchanger).**

Assuming negligible heat losses:

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = \dot{m}_w \, \left[ h_{w,out} - h_{w,in} \right]$$

where $\dot{m}_w$ is the working fluid mass flow rate and $h$ is specific enthalpy.

**Step 3: Mechanical power output of the turbine.**

For an isentropic turbine:

$$\dot{W}_t = \dot{m}_w \, (h_{w,3} - h_{w,4s})$$

where state 3 is turbine inlet and state 4s is the isentropic exit. The actual turbine work uses isentropic efficiency $\eta_t$:

$$\dot{W}_{t,actual} = \eta_t \, \dot{m}_w \, (h_{w,3} - h_{w,4s})$$

**Step 4: Gross electrical power output.**

$$\dot{W}_{gross} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \dot{W}_{t,actual}$$

where $\eta_{gen}$ is the electrical generator efficiency (typically 0.95–0.98).

**Step 5: Net electrical power (after subtracting auxiliary loads).**

$$\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_{gross} - \dot{W}_{aux}$$

**Step 6: Overall plant thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{plant} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{HX}}$$

**Numerical Example:** $\dot{m}_b = 50 \text{ kg/s}$, $c_{p,b} = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $T_{brine,in} = 170 \, °C$, $T_{brine,out} = 80 \, °C$, $\eta_{plant} = 0.12$.

**Step 7: Calculate heat input rate.**

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = 50 \times 4186 \times (170 - 80) = 50 \times 4186 \times 90$$

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = 18{,}837{,}000 \text{ W} = 18.84 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 8: Calculate net electrical output.**

$$\dot{W}_{net} = 0.12 \times 18.84 = 2.26 \text{ MW (electrical)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 2.26 MWe binary plant can supply electricity to roughly **1,500–2,000 homes** continuously, using brine that would otherwise be wasted.

---

| Parameter | Dry Steam | Single Flash | Double Flash | Binary (ORC) | HDR / EGS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resource Temperature (°C) | > 235 | 180 – 300 | 200 – 350 | 70 – 180 | 150 – 350 |
| Typical Plant Efficiency | 0.10 – 0.15 | 0.10 – 0.15 | 0.15 – 0.20 | 0.08 – 0.15 | 0.05 – 0.15 |
| Capital Cost (USD/kWe) | 2,500 – 4,000 | 2,000 – 3,500 | 2,500 – 4,000 | 2,500 – 5,000 | 4,000 – 8,000 |
| Plant Footprint | Small | Medium | Medium | Medium | Large (incl. wells) |
| Major Technical Risk | Reservoir depletion | Scaling, corrosion | Scaling, corrosion | Working fluid cost | Induced seismicity |
| Gases emitted directly | Yes (H₂S, CO₂) | Yes | Yes | Minimal (closed loop) | Minimal |
| Mature Examples | The Geysers, Larderello | Wairakei, Salton Sea | Coso, Mak-Ban | Heber, Chena Hot Springs | Fenton Hill, Soultz |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS / HDR Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Injection Pump] --> B[Injection Well]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Fracture Network in Hot Dry Rock]
    C --> D[Heated Water Rises via Production Well]
    D --> E[Surface Heat Exchanger]
    E --> F[Power Plant - Binary or Flash Cycle]
    F --> G[Turbine - Generator Unit]
    G --> H[Condenser]
    H --> I[Cooled Brine Reinjection Line]
    I --> A
    F --> J[Electrical Grid Output]
    K[Make-up Water Reservoir] --> A
    L[Cooling Tower or Air Cooler] --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The closed loop between A → B → C → D → E → I → A represents the **geothermal fluid circuit**. The branch E → F → G → H represents the **working fluid / power cycle**. The make-up water (K) and the cooling system (L) are auxiliary inputs required for continuous operation.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Binary Cycle (Organic Rankine Cycle) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Geothermal Brine from Production Well] --> B[Pre-heater Heat Exchanger]
    B --> C[Vaporizer Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[Organic Working Fluid Vapor]
    D --> E[Turbo-generator Set]
    E --> F[Condenser]
    F --> G[Working Fluid Pump]
    G --> B
    E --> H[Electricity to Grid]
    I[Cooled Brine] --> J[Reinjection Well]
    B --> I
    K[Air-cooled or Water-cooled Cooling System] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hot brine (A) flows through the heat exchangers (B, C) and exits as cooled brine (I) for reinjection. The working fluid cycles independently between B → C → D → E → F → G → B. **No mixing** of brine and working fluid occurs.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of the Four Geothermal Resource Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HDR_GROUP[Hot Dry Rock - EGS]
        HDR1[Deep Well 3 to 10 km] --> HDR2[Hydraulic Fracturing]
        HDR2 --> HDR3[Artificial Reservoir]
        HDR3 --> HDR4[Closed Loop Binary Plant]
    end
    subgraph MAGMA_GROUP[Magma Resources]
        MAG1[Magma Chamber 700 to 1300 C] --> MAG2[Experimental Direct Contact]
        MAG2 --> MAG3[Pilot Heat Exchanger]
    end
    subgraph VAP_GROUP[Vapour-Dominated System]
        VAP1[Naturally Fractured Aquifer with Steam] --> VAP2[Production Well]
        VAP2 --> VAP3[Dry Steam Turbine]
    end
    subgraph LIQ_GROUP[Liquid-Dominated System]
        LIQ1[Hot Brine Aquifer 150 to 300 C] --> LIQ2[Flash Separator]
        LIQ2 --> LIQ3[Flash Steam Turbine]
        LIQ2 --> LIQ4[Brine Reinjection]
    end
    HDR4 --> OUT[Electricity Grid]
    MAG3 --> OUT
    VAP3 --> OUT
    LIQ3 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each resource class (HDR, Magma, Vapour-Dominated, Liquid-Dominated) has its own unique input pathway, but all converge to the same **electricity grid** output. The HDR and Liquid-Dominated branches both include **reinjection** loops for sustainable reservoir management, while the Magma branch remains in the experimental stage.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OESFT726, Module 3]**

**Q1. Define geothermal energy and list the four main types of geothermal resources.** **[3 marks]** **[CO1, Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**
