Geothermal energy is the heat energy stored within the Earth's crust, originating from the original planetary formation, radioactive decay of isotopes (U-238, Th-232, K-40), and absorbed solar radiation. It is a renewable, baseload energy source. The four main types of geothermal resources are:

1. **Hot Dry Rock (HDR)** — hot but impermeable crystalline rock requiring engineered reservoirs.
2. **Magma Resources** — molten rock at very high temperatures (700–1300 °C).
3. **Vapour-Dominated (Dry Steam) Systems** — natural reservoirs producing superheated steam.
4. **Liquid-Dominated (Wet Steam) Systems** — natural reservoirs producing hot liquid water/brine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split pattern (KTU 2024):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing/naming the four types with one-line identification.

---

**Q2. State the principle of a Binary Cycle geothermal power plant. Why is it preferred for low-temperature resources?** **[3 marks]** **[CO2, Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

A Binary Cycle plant uses a **secondary working fluid with a low boiling point** (e.g., isobutane, n-pentane, R-134a) that is vaporized by heat exchanged from the geothermal brine in a heat exchanger. The vaporized secondary fluid drives a turbine-generator, then is condensed and recycled in a closed loop. The geothermal brine **does not contact** the turbine and is reinjected into the reservoir.

It is preferred for **low-temperature resources (70 – 180 °C)** because:
1. Low-boiling-point organic fluids vaporize at these low temperatures, allowing electricity generation.
2. The closed loop prevents **scaling, corrosion, and gas emissions** (H₂S, CO₂).
3. It enables power production from **low-enthalpy reservoirs** that are unusable for flash plants.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OESFT726, Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Hot Dry Rock (HDR) geothermal energy** with a neat block diagram of an Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS). Discuss the role of hydraulic fracturing in creating the artificial reservoir. **[7 marks]** **[CO1, CO2, Bloom: Understand / Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of HDR (2 marks):** Hot Dry Rock (HDR) refers to deep underground formations of hot, essentially dry crystalline basement rock (typically granite) at depths of 3–10 km, with temperatures ranging from 150–350 °C, but **lacking the natural permeability and fluid content** for conventional hydrothermal extraction. Since HDR has no natural water, an **engineered (enhanced) reservoir** must be artificially created — this is termed an **Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS)**.

**2. Block Diagram of EGS (3 marks):**

```
[Injection Well] → [Hydraulic Fracture Network in Hot Rock] → [Production Well]
                                                                    ↓
[Reinjection Pump] ← [Cooled Brine] ← [Heat Exchanger] ← [Hot Brine]
                                  ↓
                          [Binary / Flash Power Plant]
                                  ↓
                            [Turbine-Generator]
                                  ↓
                          [Condenser & Grid Output]
```

**3. Role of Hydraulic Fracturing (2 marks):** Hydraulic fracturing involves pumping water at high pressure (10–40 MPa) into the injection well to **fracture the low-permeability hot rock**, creating an extensive network of interconnected cracks. This artificial fracture network acts as a **heat exchanger surface area** (typically 1–5 km² of fracture area), enabling the injected water to circulate, absorb heat, and return as hot fluid through the production well. Without this step, water cannot flow through the rock, and the heat cannot be extracted.

---

**(b)** A geothermal site has a rock density of 2700 kg/m³, specific heat 900 J/kg·K, reservoir temperature 220 °C, reference temperature 25 °C, and reservoir volume $2 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3$. Calculate: (i) the total stored thermal energy, (ii) the recoverable heat assuming a recovery factor of 12%, and (iii) the maximum theoretical electrical energy if plant efficiency is 13%. **[7 marks]** **[CO3, Bloom: Apply / Analyze]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the relevant formula.**

The total stored thermal energy in rock is:

$$Q_{stored} = \rho_r \, c_r \, V \, (T_r - T_{ref})$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$\rho_r = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad c_r = 900 \text{ J/kg·K}, \quad V = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3, \quad T_r - T_{ref} = 220 - 25 = 195 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $Q_{stored}$ (Part i).**

$$Q_{stored} = 2700 \times 900 \times 2 \times 10^9 \times 195$$

$$Q_{stored} = 2{,}430{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^9 \times 195$$

$$Q_{stored} = 4.86 \times 10^{15} \times 195$$

$$Q_{stored} = 9.477 \times 10^{17} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4: Calculate recoverable heat (Part ii).**

With recovery factor $\eta_r = 0.12$:

$$Q_{rec} = 0.12 \times 9.477 \times 10^{17} = 1.1372 \times 10^{17} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5: Calculate maximum electrical energy (Part iii).**

With plant efficiency $\eta_{plant} = 0.13$:

$$E_{elec} = 0.13 \times 1.1372 \times 10^{17} = 1.4784 \times 10^{16} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6: Convert to MWh.**

$$E_{elec} = \dfrac{1.4784 \times 10^{16}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 4107 \text{ MWh} \approx 4.11 \times 10^3 \text{ MWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split pattern:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks including unit conversion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Binary Cycle (Organic Rankine Cycle) geothermal power plant**. List two advantages and two limitations of the binary cycle system. **[7 marks]** **[CO2, Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working Principle (4 marks):** A Binary Cycle plant uses a **secondary, low-boiling-point working fluid** (such as isobutane, n-pentane, or R-134a) to convert geothermal heat into electricity. The hot geothermal brine from the production well passes through a **heat exchanger (pre-heater and vaporizer)**, where it transfers heat to the secondary fluid. The secondary fluid vaporizes, expands through a **turbine** connected to a generator, and is then **condensed** back to a liquid in a condenser. The condensed working fluid is pumped back to the vaporizer, completing a **closed loop**. The cooled brine is **reinjected** into the reservoir.

**2. Schematic (2 marks):**

```
[Hot Brine] → [Pre-heater] → [Vaporizer] → [Cooled Brine Out] → [Reinjection]
                                  ↓
                          [Organic Vapor]
                                  ↓
                              [Turbine] → [Generator] → [Grid]
                                  ↓
                              [Condenser]
                                  ↓
                          [Liquid Working Fluid]
                                  ↓
                              [Pump] → back to Pre-heater
```

**3. Advantages (0.5 mark each):**
- Enables power generation from **low-enthalpy resources (70 – 180 °C)**, which are abundant.
- **Zero direct emissions** of H₂S and CO₂; environmentally clean.
- **No scaling/corrosion** of turbine since brine is isolated.
- High availability and long plant life.

**4. Limitations (0.5 mark each):**
- **Low thermal efficiency** (typically 8–15%) due to small temperature differential.
- High **capital cost** for heat exchangers, working fluid, and reinjection wells.
- Working fluids may have **global warming potential (GWP)** if leaked.

---

**(b)** A binary cycle plant receives geothermal brine at 160 °C and rejects it at 75 °C. The brine mass flow rate is 80 kg/s. The plant's overall efficiency is 11%. Calculate: (i) the rate of heat extraction, (ii) the net electrical power output, and (iii) the daily electrical energy generation in kWh. **[7 marks]** **[CO3, Bloom: Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat extraction rate formula.**

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = \dot{m}_b \, c_{p,b} \, (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (Part i).**

$$\dot{m}_b = 80 \text{ kg/s}, \quad c_{p,b} = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}, \quad T_{in} - T_{out} = 160 - 75 = 85 \text{ K}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = 80 \times 4186 \times 85$$

$$\dot{Q}_{HX} = 334{,}880 \times 85 = 28{,}464{,}800 \text{ W} \approx 28.46 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

**Step 3: Net electrical power output (Part ii).**

$$\dot{W}_{net} = \eta_{plant} \times \dot{Q}_{HX} = 0.11 \times 28.46 \text{ MW}$$

$$\dot{W}_{net} = 3.131 \text{ MW (electrical)}$$

**Step 4: Daily electrical energy (Part iii).**

$$E_{daily} = \dot{W}_{net} \times 24 \text{ hours} = 3.131 \times 1000 \times 24$$

$$E_{daily} = 75{,}144 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 75.14 \text{ MWh/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split pattern:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Geothermal Energy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion from J to MWh or kWh** — a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing the recovery factor ($\eta_r$) with plant efficiency ($\eta_{plant}$)** — these are **two different multipliers**; one is geological, the other is thermodynamic.
> 3. **Stating that geothermal is "intermittent"** — it is NOT; it is **baseload**. Examiners deduct marks for this factual error.
> 4. **Omitting the reinjection step** in HDR/EGS diagrams — this is a **mandatory component** for environmental and reservoir sustainability.
> 5. **Using dry steam terminology incorrectly** — Vapour-Dominated does NOT mean "no water"; it means **steam is the dominant phase**, but trace moisture may exist.
> 6. **Forgetting the working fluid name** in binary cycle answers — examiners expect at least one named fluid (e.g., isobutane, n-pentane).
> 7. **Mixing up geothermal gradient units** — use **°C/km**, not °C/m (the value is ~25–30, not 0.025–0.030).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Geothermal Energy: 60-Second Rapid Recap**

- **Geothermal Energy = Heat from Earth's interior** (radioactive decay + residual planetary heat).
- **Global heat flux ≈ 0.087 W/m²**; gradient ≈ **25–30 °C/km** (normal crust).
- **Four Resource Classes:**
  1. **HDR** → hot, dry, impermeable rock → **EGS with hydraulic fracturing + closed-loop binary plant**.
  2. **Magma** → molten rock at **700–1300 °C** → still **experimental**.
  3. **Vapour-Dominated (Dry Steam)** → superheated steam → **direct steam turbine** (e.g., The Geysers, Larderello).
  4. **Liquid-Dominated** → hot brine (150–300 °C) → **flash steam plant** (single/double flash).
- **Binary Cycle (ORC):** Uses **low-boiling-point secondary fluid** (isobutane, n-pentane) in **closed loop**; ideal for **low-enthalpy resources (70–180 °C)**; **no direct emissions**; **no scaling** of turbine.
- **HDR/EGS Process:** Injection well → hydraulic fracturing → artificial reservoir → production well → heat exchanger → power plant → reinjection.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - Heat stored: $Q = \rho_r c_r V \Delta T$
  - Heat flow: $q = kG$
  - Power: $P = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T \cdot \eta_{plant}$
  - Carnot efficiency: $\eta_C = 1 - T_{sink}/T_{source}$ (use Kelvin).
- **Advantages:** Baseload (CF = 70–95%), small footprint, low CO₂ (~45 g/kWh), no fuel cost, 30+ year life.
- **Disadvantages:** High CAPEX (drilling), site-specific, induced seismicity risk in EGS, scaling/corrosion in hydrothermal, gas emissions (H₂S, CO₂).
- **Conversion Factor:** $1 \text{ MWh} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ J}$.
- **KTU-Favorite Sub-topics:** EGS working principle, binary cycle with neat diagram, HDR vs. hydrothermal comparison, numerical on stored heat, working fluid name in binary cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio-Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biomass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio-Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**Biomass Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process in which solid carbonaceous biomass feedstock (such as wood, agricultural residues, or municipal solid waste) is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air conditions and elevated temperatures (typically **600 °C – 1200 °C**) to produce a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** (or **Synthesis Gas / Syngas** when high in H₂ and CO).

The process deliberately supplies less oxygen than what is required for complete combustion (i.e., the **Equivalence Ratio ER < 1**), thereby forcing carbon-rich solid fuels to be converted into a mixture of **CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂**, and trace hydrocarbons, rather than into CO₂ and H₂O.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The emphasis is on (i) chemistry of gasification, (ii) types of gasifiers (downdraft, updraft, fluidized bed), (iii) producer gas composition, and (iv) performance parameters such as **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** and **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Starved Campfire"

Imagine a campfire in cold weather. If you blow **too much air** (excess oxygen), the wood burns violently with large flames — this is **combustion** (gives CO₂ + H₂O + heat). However, if you **restrict the air supply** and let the wood smoulder on hot embers, you observe bluish flames and a lot of pungent gas is released. This gas can actually be piped and burned elsewhere. 

**Gasification is that "smouldering ember" process deliberately engineered into a reactor:**
- **Limited air** → no full combustion
- **High temperature** → solid carbon breaks down into combustible gases
- **Controlled environment** → producer gas can be cleaned and used in engines, turbines, or for chemical synthesis

## 1.3 Core Process Concept

| Parameter | Typical Range (KTU Reference) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Operating Temperature | **800 °C – 1200 °C** |
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.20 – 0.40** (for air gasification) |
| Producer Gas Calorific Value | **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³** (air-blown) |
| Gasification Efficiency | **60 – 80 %** |
| Major Gas Species | **CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the single most important design and operating parameter. ER = (Actual Air–Fuel Ratio) ÷ (Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio). For gasification, ER is always **less than 1**; for combustion, ER ≈ 1; for lean burn, ER > 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Gasification — Five Pillars of Reactions

The gasifier is essentially a **chemical reactor** in which heterogeneous (solid–gas) and homogeneous (gas–gas) reactions occur simultaneously in distinct thermal zones. KTU examiners heavily test these reactions.

### A. Combustion / Partial Oxidation Zone (Exothermic)
These reactions supply the **process heat** required to sustain the endothermic gasification reactions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \ \text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \ \text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \ \text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### B. Reduction Zone (Endothermic)
These reactions are the **heart of gasification** — they convert solid carbon and CO₂/H₂O into combustible CO and H₂.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 &\longrightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard Reaction)} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \ \text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-Gas Reaction)} \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \ \text{kJ/mol} \\[4pt]
\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \text{(Methanation)} \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \ \text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### C. Water-Gas Shift Reaction (Exothermic, Homogeneous)
Fine-tunes the **H₂ : CO ratio** of the producer gas.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} &\longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \ \text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the student need to memorize these?** KTU board questions often ask: *"Which reaction in a gasifier is endothermic and responsible for the dominant production of CO?"* — Answer: **Boudouard Reaction** (C + CO₂ → 2CO). This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question.

## 2.2 Thermal Zones Inside a Gasifier

| Zone | Temperature | Dominant Reaction(s) | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Drying Zone** | 100 – 200 °C | Moisture evaporation | Removes bound water |
| **Pyrolysis Zone** | 200 – 600 °C | Thermal decomposition of biomass | Releases volatile gases, char, tars |
| **Combustion/Oxidation Zone** | 800 – 1200 °C | C + O₂ → CO₂; C + ½O₂ → CO | Supplies process heat |
| **Reduction Zone** | 600 – 900 °C | Boudouard, Water-Gas | Produces CO and H₂ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Definition | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$ | ER — dimensionless | Key operating parameter; governs T and gas composition |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{gasification}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{gas}}}{Q_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ — % | Overall thermal efficiency |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{CGE}} = \dfrac{CV_g \times m_g}{CV_f \times m_f} \times 100\%$ | CGE — % | Cold Gas Efficiency; most quoted performance metric |
| 4 | $CV_{\text{producer}} = \sum x_i \cdot CV_i$ | MJ/Nm³ | Calorific value from mole fractions $x_i$ |
| 5 | Stoichiometric O₂ for C: $1 \ \text{kmol O}_2$ per kmol C | kmol | Used in air demand calculation |
| 6 | Air = $\dfrac{1}{0.21} = 4.76$ kmol per kmol O₂ | kmol air / kmol O₂ | Standard atmospheric ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value in Markdown:** While writing tables, **never use the pipe character** $\vert$ for absolute value — it will break the table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Biomass gasification enables **decentralized power generation** in rural Kerala and other parts of India via:
- **5 – 500 kWe downdraft gasifier systems** coupled to dual-fuel diesel engines (saving 70–80 % diesel).
- **Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC)** plants for grid-scale biomass power.
- **Syngas** production for **Fischer–Tropsch** synthesis of liquid biofuels (diesel, kerosene).
- **Decentralized cooking gas** in villages via small updraft gasifiers.
- **Industrial process heat** in rice mills, tea factories, and coir processing units in Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Required for Complete Gasification of Carbon

**Statement:** Determine the minimum mass of air required to completely gasify **1 kg of pure carbon (C)** into a mixture of CO and CO₂ under stoichiometric conditions.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction (assuming complete conversion to CO₂ as the limiting case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute molar mass relationship:**
- 1 kmol of C (12 kg) requires 1 kmol of O₂ (32 kg).
- Therefore, 1 kg of C requires $32 / 12 = 2.667$ kg of O₂.

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass:**
- Air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass.
- Air required = $2.667 / 0.232 = 11.49$ kg of air per kg of C.

**Step 4 — Generalize with Equivalence Ratio:**
If gasifier operates at ER = 0.25 (typical for air-blown downdraft gasifier):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual air} &= ER \times \text{Stoichiometric air} \\
&= 0.25 \times 11.49 \\
&= 2.87 \ \text{kg air / kg C}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Producer Gas Composition (Mass Balance Method)

**Given:** A downdraft gasifier is fed with **100 kg of dry biomass** (ultimate analysis: C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 38 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 10 % by mass). Air supplied = 25 % of stoichiometric. Assume all carbon converts to **50 % CO and 50 % CO₂**, and all hydrogen converts to **H₂**.

**Step 1 — Moles of each element in 100 kg biomass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_C &= 45 / 12 = 3.75 \ \text{kmol} \\
n_H &= 6 / 1 = 6.00 \ \text{kmol} \\
n_O &= 38 / 16 = 2.375 \ \text{kmol (intrinsic)} \\
n_N &= 1 / 14 = 0.0714 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of stoichiometric O₂ required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ needed for C} &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2: 3.75 \ \text{kmol} \\
\text{O}_2 \text{ needed for H} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}: 6/4 = 1.50 \ \text{kmol} \\
\hline
\text{Total O}_2 \text{ stoich} &= 5.25 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual O₂ supplied (ER = 0.25):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ actual} &= 0.25 \times 5.25 = 1.3125 \ \text{kmol} \\
\text{Air supplied} &= 1.3125 / 0.21 = 6.25 \ \text{kmol air} \\
\text{N}_2 \text{ from air} &= 6.25 \times 0.79 = 4.94 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Carbon distribution (50 % CO, 50 % CO₂):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{CO} &= 0.50 \times 3.75 = 1.875 \ \text{kmol} \\
n_{CO_2} &= 0.50 \times 3.75 = 1.875 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Hydrogen distribution (assume all H → H₂):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{H_2} &= 6.00 / 2 = 3.00 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Mole fraction of each species in producer gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{total}} &= 1.875 + 1.875 + 3.00 + 4.94 \\
&= 11.69 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{CO} &= 1.875 / 11.69 = 0.160 \ (16.0 \ \%) \\
y_{CO_2} &= 1.875 / 11.69 = 0.160 \ (16.0 \ \%) \\
y_{H_2} &= 3.00 / 11.69 = 0.257 \ (25.7 \ \%) \\
y_{N_2} &= 4.94 / 11.69 = 0.423 \ (42.3 \ \%) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Calorific Value of producer gas (using standard component LHV in MJ/Nm³):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{\text{gas}} &= (0.160)(12.64) + (0.257)(10.79) + (0 \text{ for N}_2) \\
&= 2.02 + 2.77 \\
&= 4.79 \ \text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical air-blown producer gas has CV in the range of **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³** because the high **N₂ dilution** (≈40–50 %) pulls down the heating value. This is why KTU questions often ask why producer gas is unsuitable as a standalone fuel for high-efficiency engines.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) — Worked Example

**Given:** A gasifier processes **50 kg/hr** of biomass (CV = 17 MJ/kg) and produces **120 Nm³/hr** of producer gas (CV = 5.2 MJ/Nm³).

**Step 1 — Energy input (feedstock):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{feed}} &= 50 \times 17 = 850 \ \text{MJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy output (producer gas):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{gas}} &= 120 \times 5.2 = 624 \ \text{MJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cold Gas Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{CGE}} &= \frac{624}{850} \times 100 \\
&= 73.4 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CGE of 73 % is **good** for a small-scale downdraft gasifier (typical range 65 – 80 %). Losses are due to sensible heat in hot gas, char carryover, and unconverted tar.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Biomass Gasification System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock] --> B[Size Reduction and Drying]
    B --> C[Gasifier Reactor]
    D[Air Blower] --> C
    C --> E[Gas Cleaning Cyclone]
    E --> F[Wet Scrubber and Tar Removal]
    F --> G[Filter and Cooling Unit]
    G --> H[Producer Gas Storage]
    H --> I[Application Engine / Boiler / Turbine]
    I --> J[Exhaust and Ash Discharge]
    C --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram represents the complete material flow from raw biomass to end-use application, including critical gas-cleaning stages (cyclone, scrubber, filter) that are **mandatory** before producer gas can be used in an IC engine.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Downdraft Gasifier (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Hopper Top] --> B[Drying Zone 100-200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200-600 C]
    C --> D[Oxidation Zone 800-1200 C]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 600-900 C]
    E --> F[Grate and Ash Discharge]
    F --> G[Producer Gas Outlet Bottom]
    H[Air Inlet Side Nozzles] --> D
    G --> I[Gas Cleaning Train]
    I --> J[Engine or Boiler]
```

**Key Operating Characteristic of Downdraft Gasifier:**
- Air and gas flow **co-currently downward** (parallel flow).
- Tar and pyrolysis vapours **must pass through the high-temperature oxidation zone**, where they are cracked.
- This results in **low tar content** (≈ 50 – 500 mg/Nm³) — the **only gasifier type** that produces engine-grade gas with minimal cleaning.
- KTU board exam often asks: *"Why is the downdraft gasifier preferred for IC engine applications?"*

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Three Major Gasifier Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Type1[UPDRAFT Gasifier]
        U1[Top Feed] --> U2[Drying]
        U2 --> U3[Pyrolysis]
        U3 --> U4[Reduction]
        U4 --> U5[Combustion]
        U5 --> U6[Bottom Gas Outlet]
        U5 --> U7[Grate and Ash]
    end
    subgraph Type2[DOWNDRAFT Gasifier]
        D1[Top Feed] --> D2[Drying]
        D2 --> D3[Pyrolysis]
        D3 --> D4[Combustion]
        D4 --> D5[Reduction]
        D5 --> D6[Bottom Gas Outlet]
        D5 --> D7[Grate and Ash]
    end
    subgraph Type3[FLUIDIZED BED Gasifier]
        F1[Bed Material Silica Sand] --> F2[Biomass Injection Side]
        F2 --> F3[Bubbling or Circulating Bed]
        F3 --> F4[Gas Outlet Top]
        F3 --> F5[Cyclone Recirculation]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `Type1`, `U1`, `D5`) — **no reserved keywords** such as `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone node names.
- All labels inside double-quotes are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no bold/italic/HTML tags.
- Diagram **physical drawings** have been adapted to **block-level functional architecture flow** to comply with Mermaid rendering constraints.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define biomass gasification. List any four advantages of producer gas over direct biomass combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Biomass gasification is the thermochemical conversion of solid biomass into a combustible gas mixture (producer gas) by partial oxidation at sub-stoichiometric air supply (ER < 1) and elevated temperatures (800 – 1200 °C).
- **Advantages (any 4, 1.5 marks):**
  1. Higher overall thermal efficiency (60 – 80 %) compared to direct combustion.
  2. Producer gas can be cleaned, stored, and transported via pipelines.
  3. Versatile end-use — can run IC engines, gas turbines, boilers, or be used in chemical synthesis.
  4. Lower emissions of particulate matter and unburnt hydrocarbons per unit of useful energy.
  5. Enables use of diverse and low-density biomass residues (rice husk, coconut shells, sawdust).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the Boudouard reaction in a gasifier. State its enthalpy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (2 marks):** The Boudouard reaction, $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$, is the **dominant endothermic reaction** occurring in the reduction zone of a gasifier. It converts solid carbon and the CO₂ produced in the combustion zone into carbon monoxide (CO), which is the principal combustible component of producer gas. The reaction is endothermic and proceeds favourably at temperatures above **700 °C**.
- **Enthalpy (1 mark):** $\Delta H = +172.5 \ \text{kJ/mol}$ (endothermic).

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **downdraft gasifier**. Discuss its advantages and limitations over an updraft gasifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomass gasifier is fed with **200 kg/hr** of wood chips having CV = **18 MJ/kg** and moisture content = **12 %**. The producer gas yield is **2.5 Nm³/kg of dry biomass**, with CV = **5.4 MJ/Nm³**. Calculate the **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Downdraft Gasifier — Block Diagram and Discussion (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
[Biomass] → Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion → Reduction → [Producer Gas]
              ↓         ↓             ↓             ↓              ↓
           Moist.    Volatiles    Char + Air    CO + H₂         Cyclone
            out       + Char       burning      formation        Cleaning
```

**Working (2 marks):** Biomass enters from the top, moves downward. Air is injected through nozzles into the combustion zone. The hot combustion gases rise, dry and pyrolyze the descending biomass, while the producer gas exits from the bottom and passes through a high-temperature zone where tars are thermally cracked.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Downdraft | Updraft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gas–air flow | Co-current (downward) | Counter-current (upward) |
| Tar content in gas | **Very low** (50–500 mg/Nm³) | **Very high** (10,000+ mg/Nm³) |
| Suitability for IC engine | **Direct use possible** | Requires extensive cleaning |
| Heat recovery from gas | Difficult (gas exits hot) | Easy (gas exits cold) |
| Moisture tolerance | **Low** (needs dry feed) | **High** (can handle wet biomass) |

#### (b) Cold Gas Efficiency Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Feed rate = 200 kg/hr (as received, with 12 % moisture)
- CV of wood (dry basis) = 18 MJ/kg
- Producer gas yield = 2.5 Nm³/kg of **dry** biomass
- CV of producer gas = 5.4 MJ/Nm³

**Step 1 — Dry biomass feed rate (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry}} &= 200 \times (1 - 0.12) = 176 \ \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy input from dry biomass (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{feed}} &= 176 \times 18 = 3168 \ \text{MJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Producer gas volume produced (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{gas}} &= 176 \times 2.5 = 440 \ \text{Nm}^3\text{/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy output in producer gas (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{gas}} &= 440 \times 5.4 = 2376 \ \text{MJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cold Gas Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{CGE}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{gas}}}{Q_{\text{feed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2376}{3168} \times 100 \\
&= 75.0 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer: CGE = 75.0 %** — This falls within the expected range of 65 – 80 % for a well-operated downdraft gasifier.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
**(a)** Explain the following with reference to biomass gasification: **(i)** Equivalence Ratio, **(ii)** Producer gas composition, **(iii)** Cold Gas Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The ultimate analysis of a biomass sample is: C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 42 %, N = 2 % (by mass, on dry basis). Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this biomass. If the actual air supplied is **30 % of stoichiometric**, determine the **air–fuel ratio (AFR) actually used**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Three Core Concepts (7 marks)

**(i) Equivalence Ratio (ER) — 2 marks:**
ER is the ratio of the actual air–fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio:
$$ER = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$$
- ER = 1 → complete combustion
- ER < 1 → gasification (typical 0.20 – 0.40 for air-blown gasifiers)
- ER > 1 → excess air / lean combustion

**(ii) Producer Gas Composition — 2.5 marks:**
Producer gas typically contains:
- **CO** (15 – 30 %) — main combustible, from Boudouard reaction
- **H₂** (15 – 25 %) — from water-gas reaction
- **CH₄** (2 – 5 %) — from methanation
- **CO₂** (5 – 15 %)
- **N₂** (40 – 55 %) — from air dilution; main reason for low CV

**(iii) Cold Gas Efficiency — 2.5 marks:**
Defined as the ratio of the chemical energy content of the **cold, clean producer gas** to the chemical energy content of the **feedstock**:
$$\eta_{\text{CGE}} = \frac{CV_g \cdot V_g}{CV_f \cdot m_f} \times 100\%$$
Typical values: **65 – 80 %** for downdraft; **50 – 70 %** for updraft.

#### (b) Air Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** C = 50 %, H = 6 %, O = 42 %, N = 2 % (mass % on dry basis), biomass mass = 1 kg.

**Step 1 — Mass of each element in 1 kg biomass (1 mark):**
- $m_C = 0.50$ kg
- $m_H = 0.06$ kg
- $m_O = 0.42$ kg
- $m_N = 0.02$ kg

**Step 2 — Moles of each element (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_C &= 0.50 / 12 = 0.04167 \ \text{kmol} \\
n_H &= 0.06 / 1 = 0.06 \ \text{kmol} \\
n_O &= 0.42 / 16 = 0.02625 \ \text{kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Available O₂ from biomass (1 mark):**
The intrinsic O in biomass offsets some of the O₂ demand from air.
Effective O₂ needed from air for C → CO₂: $0.04167$ kmol
Effective O₂ needed for H → H₂O: $0.06 / 4 = 0.015$ kmol
Total stoichiometric O₂ demand: $0.04167 + 0.015 = 0.05667$ kmol
Subtract intrinsic O from biomass: $0.05667 - 0.02625 = 0.03042$ kmol O₂ from air

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric air required (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air,stoich}} &= \frac{0.03042 \times 32}{0.232} \\
&= \frac{0.9734}{0.232} \\
&= 4.196 \ \text{kg air / kg biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual air at ER = 0.30 (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air,actual}} &= 0.30 \times 4.196 = 1.259 \ \text{kg air / kg biomass}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Actual Air–Fuel Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{\text{actual}} &= \frac{1.259}{1.0} = 1.26 \ \text{kg air / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Stoichiometric air = **4.196 kg/kg**; Actual AFR at ER = 0.30 = **1.26 kg air/kg fuel**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing ER with Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR):** ER is dimensionless (ratio of ratios). AFR has units. Writing "ER = 1.26" is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract intrinsic oxygen** of biomass from stoichiometric O₂ demand — this is a classic 2-mark error in air-requirement problems.
> 3. **Not specifying dry vs. wet biomass basis** in CGE problems — KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the unit-conversion step.
> 4. **Writing the gasifier zones in wrong order** (e.g., reduction above combustion) — zone order must be drying → pyrolysis → combustion → reduction, top to bottom for downdraft.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final answer of numerical problems (MJ/Nm³, Nm³/hr, kg/hr) — a 0.5-mark penalty applies for missing units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Gasification** = partial oxidation of biomass at **ER < 1** to produce **producer gas** (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + CO₂ + N₂).
- **ER** is the master operating parameter; typical range **0.20 – 0.40** for air gasification.
- **Five key reactions:** C+O₂, C+½O₂, **Boudouard (C+CO₂→2CO)**, **Water-Gas (C+H₂O→CO+H₂)**, Water-Gas Shift (CO+H₂O→CO₂+H₂).
- **Boudouard reaction is endothermic** (ΔH = +172.5 kJ/mol) and is the **principal CO-producing reaction**.
- **Four thermal zones:** Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion (Oxidation) → Reduction.
- **Downdraft gasifier** → co-current flow, **low tar**, ideal for **IC engines**.
- **Updraft gasifier** → counter-current flow, **high tar**, ideal for **direct heating/boilers**.
- **Fluidized bed gasifier** → uniform temperature, suitable for **large-scale** and **diverse feedstocks**.
- **Producer gas CV** is **low (4–6 MJ/Nm³)** because of N₂ dilution from air.
- **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** = (Energy in gas) / (Energy in feed) × 100; range 65 – 80 % for downdraft.
- **Stoichiometric air for pure C** = 11.49 kg air/kg C.
- **Air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass** and **21 % by volume** (use whichever is required by the problem).
- **Equivalence Ratio = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_stoichiometric** — dimensionless.
- **Reactor pressure** is usually atmospheric; temperature range **800 – 1200 °C**.
- **Major application in Kerala:** small-scale biomass gasifier + dual-fuel engine for rice mills, coir units, and rural electrification.
- **Always convert to dry basis** before energy and efficiency calculations in CGE problems.
- **The single most-tested KTU question:** *"Compare downdraft and updraft gasifiers."* — memorize the table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio-Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Bio-Ethanol

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bio-ethanol** (also written as **bioethanol** or **ethyl alcohol of biological origin**, chemical formula **C₂H₅OH** or **CH₃CH₂OH**) is a **biofuel of the first generation** produced exclusively through the **fermentation of sugars, starches, or cellulosic biomass** derived from agricultural feedstocks, followed by **distillation and dehydration** to obtain fuel-grade ethanol (anhydrous, ≥ **99.5% v/v purity**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Bio-ethanol is an alcohol-based biofuel manufactured by the **fermentation of carbohydrate-rich biomass** (sugarcane, corn, sugar beet, sorghum, lignocellulosic residues, etc.) using microorganisms (yeast – *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) and subsequently dehydrated to meet fuel-grade anhydrous specifications. It is predominantly used as a **gasoline-blending oxygenate** to enhance octane rating and reduce tailpipe emissions.

### Classification of Bio-Ethanol Generations

| Generation | Feedstock Type | Key Examples | Technology Readiness |
|------------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **1G (First Generation)** | Sugar/Starch crops | Sugarcane juice, corn starch, sugar beet, molasses | Commercial / Mature |
| **2G (Second Generation)** | Lignocellulosic biomass | Bagasse, corn stover, rice straw, wood chips, switchgrass | Pilot / Demonstration |
| **3G (Third Generation)** | Algal biomass | Microalgae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*) | Research / Lab-scale |
| **4G (Fourth Generation)** | Engineered pathways | Genetically modified organisms, synthetic biology | Conceptual / R&D |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rotten-Fruit Wine" Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine you leave a bowl of crushed, sweet fruit on the kitchen counter for a few days. Naturally occurring wild yeasts from the air attack the sugar in the fruit, converting it into alcohol and carbon dioxide — this is exactly what happens when grapes ferment into wine. **Bio-ethanol production is essentially this same natural fermentation process, but industrialized, controlled, and supercharged with selected yeast strains inside massive stainless-steel tanks called *fermenters*.** The dilute "wine" (called *beer* in industry, containing only ~10% alcohol) is then boiled in tall distillation columns to separate the alcohol from water, and finally passed through molecular sieves to strip out the last traces of water, yielding pure, fuel-grade alcohol that can power a car.

### Intuitive Step-by-Step Picture of the Process

1. 🌾 **Harvest** a sugar/starch-rich crop (sugarcane, corn, etc.).
2. 🔨 **Crush / Cook** the crop to release the sugary juice or convert starch → sugar.
3. 🧫 **Ferment** the sugar solution with yeast in a sealed tank for 48–72 hours.
4. 🧪 **Distill** the resulting "beer" (≈10% alcohol) in a column to concentrate ethanol to ~95%.
5. 💎 **Dehydrate** the 95% azeotrope using molecular sieves to reach fuel-grade ≥99.5%.
6. ⛽ **Blend** with gasoline (E10, E85, E100) for use in internal combustion engines.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Fuel-Grade Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Memorize for KTU Board Exam):**

- **Chemical formula:** C₂H₅OH (molar mass = **46.07 g/mol**)
- **Density of anhydrous ethanol:** **ρ = 0.789 kg/L** (at 20°C)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of ethanol:** **26.9 MJ/kg** (≈ 21.2 MJ/L)
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of ethanol:** **29.7 MJ/kg**
- **Research Octane Number (RON):** **108–110**
- **Motor Octane Number (MON):** **89–92**
- **Octane Index (OI = (R+M)/2):** **≈ 100**
- **Heat of vaporization:** **0.91 MJ/kg** (high → cold-start issue)
- **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio:** **9.0 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Self-ignition temperature:** **363 °C**
- **Flash point:** **12.8 °C** (closed cup)
- **Azeotropic composition with water:** **95.6% ethanol + 4.4% water** (by mass), boiling point **78.2 °C**
- **Molecular sieve pore size:** **3 Å (Ångström)** — selectively traps water molecules

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic is high-yield in KTU exams:**
> - Module 4 of *Renewable Energy Engineering* covers **biofuels** (bio-ethanol, biodiesel, biogas).
> - Bio-ethanol is a **"favourite" question** of KTU examiners because it directly tests the student's grasp of **(a) fermentation stoichiometry, (b) distillation mass balance, and (c) blending percentages.**
> - Marks are frequently awarded for **numerical problems** on fermentation yields, theoretical ethanol yield from glucose, distillation reflux calculations, and energy content of E10/E85 blends.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Fermentation — The Core Reaction

The biochemical transformation of glucose into ethanol and carbon dioxide is governed by the **Gay-Lussac Equation (1835)**, which is essentially the application of **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolysis pathway** under anaerobic conditions:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (Yeast)}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$

### Step-by-Step Biochemical Logic

1. **Glucose Uptake:** Yeast cells (typically *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) consume hexose sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose) through facilitated diffusion and active transport.
2. **Glycolysis (EMP Pathway):** One glucose molecule (6-carbon) is broken down into **2 molecules of pyruvate (C₃H₄O₃)**, generating a net of **2 ATP and 2 NADH**.
3. **Decarboxylation:** Pyruvate is decarboxylated by **pyruvate decarboxylase** to form **acetaldehyde**, releasing **1 CO₂ per pyruvate** (2 CO₂ total per glucose).
4. **Reduction:** Acetaldehyde is reduced by **alcohol dehydrogenase** using NADH as a cofactor, producing **ethanol** and regenerating NAD⁺.
5. **Energy Release:** The reaction is **exothermic**; the heat must be removed by cooling jackets to maintain the optimum fermentation temperature of **30–35 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Yield Insight:**
> Every 1 kg of glucose theoretically yields **0.511 kg of ethanol** and **0.489 kg of carbon dioxide**. In real industrial practice, the practical yield is **~90–95% of theoretical** because some sugar is diverted to yeast cell growth (biomass production) and minor by-products (glycerol, organic acids).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU Board Exam favourites**. Master the highlighted ones.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ | Stoichiometric fermentation | Theoretical yield basis |
| 2 | $Y_{th} = \dfrac{2 \times M_{ethanol}}{M_{glucose}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111$ | $Y_{th}$ = theoretical mass yield (kg ethanol / kg glucose) | Maximum possible ethanol |
| 3 | $Y_{practical} = \eta_f \times Y_{th}$ | $\eta_f$ = fermentation efficiency (typically 0.90–0.95) | Real-world yield |
| 4 | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{Y_{practical} \times m_{sugar}}{\rho_{ethanol}}$ | $E_{vol}$ = ethanol volume (L), $m_{sugar}$ = sugar mass (kg), $\rho_{ethanol}$ = 0.789 kg/L | Volume yield per ton of feedstock |
| 5 | $H_{blend} = f_{eth} \cdot LHV_{eth} + (1-f_{eth}) \cdot LHV_{gas}$ | $H_{blend}$ = heating value of blend (MJ/kg), $f_{eth}$ = ethanol mass fraction, $LHV_{gas}$ ≈ 44 MJ/kg | Energy content of E10/E85 |
| 6 | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{LHV_{blend}}{LHV_{gas}} \times 100\%$ | $E_{spec}$ = specific energy (%) | Octane/energy comparison |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{E_{ethanol \, out} \times LHV_{ethanol}}{E_{biomass \, in} \times LHV_{biomass}}$ | $E$ = energy content (MJ) | Net energy balance (NEB) |
| 8 | $C_{p,ethanol} \approx 2.43 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ | Specific heat | Distillation column thermal design |
| 9 | $H_{vap,ethanol} = 0.91 \ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Latent heat | Energy cost of distillation |
| 10 | $A/F_{stoich} = 9.0 : 1$ | Air-to-fuel mass ratio | Engine combustion calculations |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Numerical Patterns

### Pattern A — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from a Given Sugar Mass
*Given:* Mass of glucose = $m_g$ (kg).
*Find:* Maximum possible mass of ethanol.

$$m_{ethanol,max} = 0.5111 \times m_g$$

### Pattern B — Practical Yield with Fermentation Efficiency
$$m_{ethanol,actual} = 0.5111 \times \eta_f \times m_g$$

### Pattern C — Ethanol Volume in Litres
$$V_{ethanol} = \dfrac{m_{ethanol,actual}}{\rho_{ethanol}} = \dfrac{0.5111 \times \eta_f \times m_g}{0.789}$$

### Pattern D — Heating Value of an Ethanol–Gasoline Blend
$$H_{blend} = x_{eth} \cdot H_{eth} + (1 - x_{eth}) \cdot H_{gas}$$

where $x_{eth}$ is the **mass fraction** of ethanol in the blend.

### Pattern E — Stoichiometric Sugar Required for Target Ethanol
$$m_{glucose} = \dfrac{m_{ethanol,target}}{0.5111 \times \eta_f}$$

### Pattern F — Distillation Azeotrope Limitation
Because ethanol and water form an azeotrope at **95.6% ethanol by mass** (boiling point 78.2°C), simple distillation cannot exceed this. To reach fuel-grade **≥99.5%**, **molecular sieve adsorption (3 Å zeolite)** is mandatory.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Bio-Ethanol

| Application Sector | Use Case | Blend Ratio |
|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Road Transport** | Spark-ignition (gasoline) engines | E10 (USA, India), E85 (Brazil, USA flex-fuel), E100 (Brazil) |
| **Aviation** | Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) precursor via Alcohol-to-Jet (ATJ) | Up to 50% SAF blend |
| **Industrial Solvent** | Pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, cleaning agents | Anhydrous grade |
| **Power Generation** | Fuel cells (SOFC, DEFC) | Direct ethanol fuel cells |
| **Hydrogen Production** | Steam reforming of ethanol (SRE) for green H₂ | Research stage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Brazil's ProÁlcool Program** (1975–today) is the world's largest bio-ethanol economy, producing **> 30 billion litres/year** from sugarcane, with ~70% of new cars running on **flex-fuel (E100 / E85)** technology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Theoretical Mass Yield of Ethanol from Glucose

### Given Data
- Molecular weight of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆): $M_g = 180 \ \text{g/mol}$
- Molecular weight of ethanol (C₂H₅OH): $M_e = 46 \ \text{g/mol}$
- Reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From stoichiometry:} \quad & 1 \ \text{mol glucose} \rightarrow 2 \ \text{mol ethanol} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & 180 \ \text{g glucose} \rightarrow 2 \times 46 = 92 \ \text{g ethanol} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & 1 \ \text{g glucose} \rightarrow \dfrac{92}{180} \ \text{g ethanol} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & Y_{th} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \ \dfrac{\text{kg ethanol}}{\text{kg glucose}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent CO₂ co-produced:** 1 mol glucose → 2 mol CO₂ = 88 g. So the mass split is **51.11% ethanol + 48.89% CO₂** (by mass).

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Practical Ethanol Volume from 1 Tonne of Sugarcane

### Given Data
- Mass of sugarcane feedstock: $M_{sc} = 1000 \ \text{kg}$
- Sucrose content of sugarcane: $S_c = 14\%$ (mass basis, typical)
- Sugar extraction efficiency in mill: $\eta_{mill} = 95\%$
- Fermentation efficiency: $\eta_f = 90\%$
- Distillation + dehydration efficiency: $\eta_{dist} = 98\%$
- Density of anhydrous ethanol: $\rho_e = 0.789 \ \text{kg/L}$

### Step 1 — Mass of Sucrose Available
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{sucrose} &= M_{sc} \times S_c = 1000 \times 0.14 = 140 \ \text{kg} \\[4pt]
m_{sugar \, extracted} &= m_{sucrose} \times \eta_{mill} = 140 \times 0.95 = 133 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Ethanol Mass
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{eth,th} &= 0.5111 \times m_{sugar \, extracted} = 0.5111 \times 133 = 67.98 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Ethanol Mass (after fermentation & distillation)
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{eth,actual} &= m_{eth,th} \times \eta_f \times \eta_{dist} \\[4pt]
&= 67.98 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 \\[4pt]
&= 59.96 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Ethanol Volume
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ethanol} &= \dfrac{m_{eth,actual}}{\rho_e} = \dfrac{59.96}{0.789} \approx 75.99 \ \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
> **1 tonne of sugarcane → ≈ 76 litres of anhydrous bio-ethanol** (industrial benchmark for Brazilian mills is 80–85 L/tonne; gap is closed by better agricultural yields and vinasse recycling).

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Heating Value of E10 Gasoline Blend (KTU Favourite Problem)

### Given Data
- E10 blend = 10% ethanol + 90% gasoline (by volume, common KTU assumption)
- LHV of anhydrous ethanol: $LHV_{eth} = 26.9 \ \text{MJ/kg}$
- LHV of gasoline: $LHV_{gas} = 44.0 \ \text{MJ/kg}$
- Density of ethanol: $\rho_e = 0.789 \ \text{kg/L}$
- Density of gasoline: $\rho_g = 0.74 \ \text{kg/L}$

### Step 1 — Mass of Each Component per Litre of Blend
For **1 litre of E10**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{eth} &= 0.10 \times 0.789 = 0.0789 \ \text{kg} \\[4pt]
m_{gas} &= 0.90 \times 0.74 = 0.666 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Energy Content of Each Component
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{eth} &= m_{eth} \times LHV_{eth} = 0.0789 \times 26.9 = 2.122 \ \text{MJ} \\[4pt]
E_{gas} &= m_{gas} \times LHV_{gas} = 0.666 \times 44.0 = 29.30 \ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Energy & LHV per kg of Blend
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 2.122 + 29.30 = 31.42 \ \text{MJ per litre} \\[4pt]
m_{total} &= 0.0789 + 0.666 = 0.7449 \ \text{kg} \\[4pt]
LHV_{E10} &= \dfrac{31.42}{0.7449} = 42.18 \ \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Energy Penalty vs. Pure Gasoline
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Penalty} &= \dfrac{LHV_{gas} - LHV_{E10}}{LHV_{gas}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{44.0 - 42.18}{44.0} \times 100\% = 4.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 10% ethanol blend reduces the volumetric energy density by **~6%** and the gravimetric energy density by **~4%**, leading to a proportional drop in fuel economy. However, the high **octane rating of ethanol (RON = 108)** allows engines to operate at higher compression ratios, partially offsetting this loss.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Pure Ethanol Combustion

### Combustion Equation
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 1 — Moles of O₂ Required per Mole of Ethanol
From stoichiometry: **3 mol O₂ per 1 mol ethanol**.

### Step 2 — Mass of O₂ Required per kg of Ethanol
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2} &= 3 \times \dfrac{32 \ \text{g/mol}}{46 \ \text{g/mol}} = \dfrac{96}{46} = 2.087 \ \dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg ethanol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Air Required (air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass)
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{2.087}{0.232} = 8.99 \approx 9.0 \ \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg ethanol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result to remember:** Stoichiometric A/F ratio for ethanol = **9.0 : 1**, which is **lower than gasoline (14.7 : 1)** — this is why ethanol engines can be more thermally efficient under lean-burn conditions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Ethanol Generations

| Parameter | 1G (Sugarcane/Corn) | 2G (Cellulosic) | 3G (Algal) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------| -----------|
| Feedstock cost | Moderate–High | Low (waste-based) | Very Low |
| Net Energy Ratio (NER) | 1.3 – 1.8 (corn) / 8 – 10 (sugarcane) | 2 – 4 (projected) | 3 – 5 (projected) |
| Lifecycle GHG reduction vs. gasoline | 20 – 60% | 60 – 90% | 70 – 90% |
| Technology maturity | Fully commercial | Pilot / Pre-commercial | Lab / Pilot |
| Major barrier | Food-vs-fuel debate | Pretreatment cost, enzyme cost | Reactor design, light supply |
| Pretreatment required? | **No** (only milling/juicing) | **Yes** (acid/alkali/steam explosion) | Cell disruption needed |
| Fermentation inhibitor issues | None significant | **High** (furfural, HMF from pretreatment) | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Bio-Ethanol Production Process (1G)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Harvesting Sugarcane or Corn] --> B[Cleaning and Size Reduction Milling or Crushing]
    B --> C{Feedstock Type}
    C -->|Sugar-based Sugarcane Beet Molasses| D[Sugar Extraction Juice Extraction]
    C -->|Starch-based Corn Sorghum Wheat| E[Starch Liquefaction and Saccharification]
    D --> F[Clarification and Sterilization of Sugar Solution]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Fermentation Tank with Saccharomyces cerevisiae Yeast 30 to 35 C 48 to 72 h]
    G --> H[Beer Solution Approx 8 to 12 percent Ethanol]
    H --> I[Distillation Column 95.6 percent Azeotrope Achieved]
    I --> J[Dehydration Unit Molecular Sieve 3 Angstrom Zeolite]
    J --> K[Anhydrous Bio Ethanol Purity above 99.5 percent]
    K --> L[Storage and Blending with Gasoline E10 E85 E100]
    H --> M[CO2 Recovery Compressed and Sold to Beverage Industry]
    G --> N[Spent Mash Stillage and Vinasse By products]
    N --> O[Animal Feed or Biogas Anaerobic Digestion]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass and Energy Flow in a Biorefinery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Biomass Feedstock]
        A2[Process Water]
        A3[Steam from Boiler]
        A4[Enzymes and Yeast]
    end

    subgraph Conversion
        B1[Pre treatment]
        B2[Hydrolysis Saccharification]
        B3[Fermentation]
        B4[Distillation]
        B5[Dehydration]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        C1[Anhydrous Ethanol]
        C2[Carbon Dioxide CO2]
        C3[Vinasse Liquid By product]
        C4[Bagasse Solid Fuel]
        C5[Wastewater]
    end

    subgraph Energy Recovery
        D1[Combined Heat and Power CHP Boiler]
        D2[Biogas from Vinasse]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B1 --> C4 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    B4 --> C5
    A3 --> B4
    D1 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting Ethanol Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Engineer Selects Feedstock]) --> Q1{Is feedstock sugar rich?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Sugarcane Molasses Beet| G1[Adopt First Generation 1G Process]
    Q1 -->|No Starch based Corn Sorghum| G2[Saccharification Step Required]
    G2 --> G1
    G1 --> Q2{Is feedstock lignocellulosic?}
    Q2 -->|Yes Bagasse Straw Wood| G3[Adopt Second Generation 2G Process]
    Q2 -->|No Algal Based| G4[Adopt Third Generation 3G Process]
    G3 --> Sub1[Acidic or Alkaline Pre treatment]
    Sub1 --> Sub2[Enzymatic Hydrolysis]
    Sub2 --> Sub3[Simultaneous Saccharification and Fermentation SSF]
    G1 --> Distill[Distillation and Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
    G3 --> Distill
    G4 --> Distill
    Distill --> Product([Anhydrous Bio Ethanol Ready for Blending])
```

## 4.4 Process Flow Schematic — Distillation + Molecular Sieve Dehydration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Beer from Fermenter 10 percent Ethanol] --> H1[Pre heater using Distillate Waste Heat]
    H1 --> DC[Distillation Column Multi Tray 78 to 95 percent Ethanol Output]
    DC --> V1[Top Vapour 95.6 percent Ethanol Azeotrope]
    V1 --> MS[Molecular Sieve Adsorber 3 Angstrom Zeolite Beads]
    MS --> P1[Purged to Strip Water below 0.1 percent]
    MS --> OUT[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.8 percent Purity]
    OUT --> TANK[Storage Tank]
    DC --> B1[Bottom Bottoms Spent Wash to Biogas Plant]
    P1 --> CW[Condensate Water Recycle]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model 1)]**
**Define bio-ethanol. List four commonly used feedstocks for its production.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Bio-ethanol is a **fuel-grade ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH)** produced by the **fermentation of carbohydrate-rich biomass** (sugars, starches, or cellulosic materials) using yeast or bacteria, followed by distillation and dehydration to achieve ≥99.5% purity.
- **Feedstocks (2 marks — any four):** (i) Sugarcane juice and molasses, (ii) Corn (maize) starch, (iii) Sugar beet, (iv) Sorghum, (v) Cellulosic biomass — bagasse, rice straw, wood waste, (vi) Algal biomass.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model 2)]**
**Write the stoichiometric equation for the fermentation of glucose. What is the theoretical mass yield of ethanol per kg of glucose?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reaction (1 mark):**
  $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{Heat}$$
- **Theoretical yield (2 marks):**
  - Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol, of ethanol = 46 g/mol.
  - $Y_{th} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \ \text{kg ethanol / kg glucose}$.
  - **Answer: 0.511 kg of ethanol per kg of glucose** (i.e., ~51.1% mass conversion).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔴 Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024 Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the complete **process flow** for the production of **anhydrous bio-ethanol from sugarcane**, including fermentation, distillation, and dehydration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sugarcane mill processes **500 tonnes/day** of sugarcane with a sucrose content of **13% (by mass)**. The mill extraction efficiency is **95%**, the fermentation efficiency is **90%**, and distillation efficiency is **98%**. Calculate the **daily production of anhydrous ethanol in litres**. Take molecular weight of sucrose = 342 g/mol and ethanol = 46 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The bio-ethanol production process from sugarcane consists of the following sequential stages:

**1. Feedstock Preparation (1 mark):**
Sugarcane is harvested, washed, and passed through roller mills to extract the juice. The fibrous residue (**bagasse**) is sent to the boiler for steam and power generation. Typical juice yield is **~65–70%** of cane mass.

**2. Juice Clarification and Sterilization (1 mark):**
The juice is heated to **~105 °C** to kill wild microorganisms that would otherwise out-compete the yeast. Lime is added for clarification, removing suspended solids and waxes.

**3. Fermentation (2 marks):**
- The clarified juice (~14–16% sucrose) is cooled to **30–35 °C** and pumped into large stainless-steel **fermenters** (200,000–500,000 L capacity).
- *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast is inoculated; fermentation proceeds for **48–72 hours** under anaerobic conditions.
- Reaction: $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 4\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 4\,\text{CO}_2$
- The output is called **"beer"** (industrial term) containing **8–12% v/v ethanol**.

**4. Distillation (2 marks):**
- The beer is preheated (using waste heat from the column) and fed into a **multi-tray distillation column**.
- Ethanol-water forms an **azeotrope at 95.6% ethanol + 4.4% water** (by mass, BP 78.2 °C), which is the ceiling of conventional distillation.
- The "spent wash" bottoms are sent to anaerobic digesters for biogas production.

**5. Dehydration / Anhydrous Recovery (1 mark):**
- The 95.6% azeotrope is passed through **molecular sieve adsorption columns** packed with **3 Å zeolite beads**.
- Water molecules (kinetic diameter ≈ 2.6 Å) are selectively trapped, while ethanol (kinetic diameter ≈ 4.4 Å) passes through, yielding **anhydrous ethanol (≥ 99.5% v/v)**.
- The sieves are periodically regenerated by applying vacuum or a purge of dry ethanol vapour.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Daily cane throughput: $M_{cane} = 500 \ \text{tonnes/day}$
- Sucrose content: $S = 13\% = 0.13$
- Mill extraction efficiency: $\eta_{mill} = 0.95$
- Fermentation efficiency: $\eta_f = 0.90$
- Distillation efficiency: $\eta_{dist} = 0.98$
- MW sucrose = 342 g/mol, MW ethanol = 46 g/mol
- $\rho_{ethanol} = 0.789 \ \text{kg/L}$

**Step 1 — Total mass of sucrose in the cane (1 mark):**
$$
m_{sucrose} = 500 \times 0.13 = 65 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Sucrose available for fermentation after mill losses (1 mark):**
$$
m_{sucrose,fermented} = 65 \times 0.95 = 61.75 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical ethanol yield from sucrose (3 marks):**
- Stoichiometry: 1 mol sucrose (342 g) → 4 mol ethanol (4 × 46 = 184 g).
- $Y_{th} = \dfrac{184}{342} = 0.5380 \ \text{kg ethanol / kg sucrose}$
- Theoretical ethanol: $m_{eth,th} = 61.75 \times 0.5380 = 33.22 \ \text{tonnes}$

**Step 4 — Practical ethanol yield with fermentation + distillation efficiencies (1 mark):**
$$
m_{eth,actual} = 33.22 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 = 29.30 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volume in litres (1 mark):**
$$
V_{ethanol} = \dfrac{29{,}300 \ \text{kg}}{0.789 \ \text{kg/L}} \approx 37{,}135 \ \text{L/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: The mill produces approximately 37,135 litres (≈ 37.1 kL) of anhydrous bio-ethanol per day.**

---

### 🔵 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **second-generation (cellulosic) bio-ethanol production process**. Why is a **pretreatment step** mandatory for lignocellulosic feedstocks? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **E20 blend** contains 20% ethanol (by volume) and 80% gasoline. Given: LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg, LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, density of ethanol = 0.789 kg/L, density of gasoline = 0.74 kg/L. Calculate the **(i)** mass-based LHV of the blend in MJ/kg, and **(ii)** the percentage energy penalty compared to pure gasoline. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Why 2G is needed (1 mark):**
First-generation ethanol uses food crops (sugarcane, corn), creating a **food-vs-fuel conflict**. 2G ethanol uses **lignocellulosic residues** (bagasse, rice straw, corn stover) — non-food, low-cost, and abundantly available.

**2. Why pretreatment is mandatory (2 marks):**
Lignocellulosic biomass consists of three polymers — **cellulose (40–50%)**, **hemicellulose (20–30%)**, and **lignin (15–25%)**. Cellulose microfibrils are encased in a **hemicellulose-lignin matrix** that:
- Prevents enzyme access to cellulose.
- Protects against microbial/enzymatic attack.
- Makes the biomass **recalcitrant** to hydrolysis.
Therefore, **pretreatment** is needed to break the lignin seal, reduce crystallinity, and increase porosity.

**3. Process Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Feedstock collection & size reduction** (chipping, grinding).
2. **Pretreatment** — dilute acid (H₂SO₄), steam explosion, ammonia fiber explosion (AFEX), or organosolv. Goal: dissolve hemicellulose, disrupt lignin, expose cellulose.
3. **Enzymatic hydrolysis (saccharification)** — cellulase enzymes (e.g., from *Trichoderma reesei*) convert cellulose → glucose; hemicellulose → xylose, arabinose.
4. **Detoxification** — removal of inhibitors (furfural, HMF, acetic acid) generated during pretreatment, using overliming, ion exchange, or activated carbon.
5. **Fermentation** — co-fermentation of C5 (xylose) and C6 (glucose) sugars using genetically modified yeasts (*S. cerevisiae* recombinants) or *Zymomonas mobilis*.
6. **Distillation and molecular sieve dehydration** — same as 1G.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** E20 = 20% ethanol + 80% gasoline (by volume).

**Step 1 — Mass of each component in 1 litre of blend (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{eth} &= 0.20 \times 0.789 = 0.1578 \ \text{kg} \\[4pt]
m_{gas} &= 0.80 \times 0.74 = 0.5920 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy content of each component (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{eth} &= 0.1578 \times 26.9 = 4.245 \ \text{MJ} \\[4pt]
E_{gas} &= 0.5920 \times 44.0 = 26.048 \ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total mass and total energy (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= 0.1578 + 0.5920 = 0.7498 \ \text{kg} \\[4pt]
E_{total} &= 4.245 + 26.048 = 30.293 \ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — LHV of blend in MJ/kg (1 mark):**
$$
LHV_{E20} = \dfrac{30.293}{0.7498} = 40.40 \ \text{MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy penalty (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Penalty} = \dfrac{44.0 - 40.40}{44.0} \times 100\% = 8.18\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer: LHV of E20 = 40.40 MJ/kg; Energy penalty vs. gasoline = 8.18%.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks on Bio-Ethanol questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.511 yield factor (or 0.538 for sucrose).** Many students write 92/180 correctly but then forget to apply **fermentation efficiency** in practical problems — losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing mass % and volume % of blends.** E10 = 10% by **volume** unless specified. KTU almost always assumes volume basis — never mix units.
> 3. **Neglecting the density of ethanol (0.789 kg/L) when converting kg → litres.** This single conversion error costs a full mark on the final step.
> 4. **Ignoring distillation + dehydration efficiencies.** A theoretical yield of 0.511 is the *upper bound* — the actual yield is always lower. Examiners explicitly check for this.
> 5. **Not stating the 95.6% azeotrope limit.** If asked why molecular sieves are needed, students often write "to remove water" but **forget the azeotrope concept** — losing 1–2 marks.
> 6. **In 2G questions, students forget "lignin" or "recalcitrance"** — these are key buzzwords examiners look for.
> 7. **Spelling of "Saccharomyces"** — getting it wrong (Sacharomyces, Sachromyces) is a soft-penalty in the marks ledger.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Stoichiometry:** 1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol + 2 mol CO₂; 1 mol sucrose → 4 mol ethanol + 4 mol CO₂.
- ✅ **Theoretical yield:** **0.511 kg ethanol / kg glucose**; **0.538 kg ethanol / kg sucrose**.
- ✅ **Practical yield factor** is usually **0.45–0.49 kg/kg** after all losses.
- ✅ **Density of anhydrous ethanol = 0.789 kg/L** (or 789 kg/m³).
- ✅ **LHV of ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg**; **LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ **RON of ethanol = 108–110**; **MON = 89–92**; **Octane Index ≈ 100**.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F ratio for ethanol = 9.0 : 1** (much leaner than gasoline 14.7:1).
- ✅ **Azeotrope of ethanol-water = 95.6% ethanol by mass**, BP = 78.2 °C — must be broken using **3 Å molecular sieves**.
- ✅ **Fermentation temperature** for *S. cerevisiae*: **30–35 °C**; duration **48–72 h**.
- ✅ **E10, E85, E100** are standard blend notations (% by volume) — know the country: **E10 = India/USA, E85 = Brazil/USA flex-fuel, E100 = Brazil pure ethanol**.
- ✅ **Fermentation efficiency** (η_f) typically **90–95%**; **distillation + dehydration efficiency** (η_dist) typically **97–99%**.
- ✅ **2G ethanol feedstocks = lignocellulosic** (bagasse, rice straw, corn stover) — require **pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis + C5/C6 co-fermentation**.
- ✅ **Recalcitrance** of lignocellulose is due to **lignin-hemicellulose shield** around cellulose microfibrils.
- ✅ **Net Energy Ratio (NER) of sugarcane ethanol = 8–10** (most favourable of all biofuels); corn ethanol = 1.3–1.8.
- ✅ **Largest producers:** **Brazil (sugarcane, > 30 billion L/yr)** and **USA (corn, ~ 50 billion L/yr)** together account for **> 85%** of world production.
- ✅ **CO₂ co-product of fermentation is captured and sold** in beverage industry — reduces GHG and improves process economics.
- ✅ **Bagasse** (fibrous cane residue) is burned in CHP boiler to meet the **entire** energy demand of the biorefinery (energy self-sufficient process).
- ✅ **Bio-ethanol does NOT mix with diesel** — it is a **gasoline substitute**; for diesel, **biodiesel (FAME)** is the parallel biofuel.
- ✅ **Cold-start issue:** Ethanol's high **latent heat (0.91 MJ/kg)** causes cold-start difficulty in engines — solved with **flex-fuel cold-start injectors** or **5–10% gasoline blending**.

> 🎯 **Final One-Liner:** *"Bio-ethanol is the alcohol of biology — brewed like wine, distilled like whisky, dried like a desert, and burned like a fossil fuel, but breathes back the CO₂ it took to grow the sugarcane."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Diesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio-Diesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biodiesel (Bio-Diesel)** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock, such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled cooking greases. It is produced via a chemical process called **transesterification**, in which the triglyceride molecules of the parent oil/fat react with a short-chain alcohol (typically **methanol** or **ethanol**) in the presence of a catalyst (usually a strong base like **NaOH** or **KOH**), yielding **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** and **glycerol** as a by-product.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, biodiesel is classified as a **"first-generation biofuel"** and is governed by strict quality standards — the two most internationally recognized being:
- **ASTM D6751** (United States standard)
- **EN 14214** (European Committee for Standardization standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure biodiesel is denoted as B100.** When blended with petroleum diesel, the blend is denoted by a "B" followed by the volumetric percentage of biodiesel. For example, **B20** = 20% biodiesel + 80% petro-diesel. This is the most common commercial blend in the world.

### 1.2 Chemical Composition Intuition

A typical biodiesel molecule is represented as:

$$R-COO-CH_3$$

where **R** is a long hydrocarbon chain (typically **C12 to C22**) derived from the parent fatty acid. The most common fatty acids encountered in biodiesel feedstocks are:

- **Lauric acid** (C12:0) — found in coconut oil
- **Palmitic acid** (C16:0) — dominant in palm oil
- **Stearic acid** (C18:0) — found in animal tallow
- **Oleic acid** (C18:1) — dominant in olive / canola oil
- **Linoleic acid** (C18:2) — found in soybean oil

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Tailoring" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a thick, syrupy cooking oil (a triglyceride) as a heavy woollen sweater — it is too viscous to wear directly in summer. **Transesterification acts like a tailor** who cuts the sweater into three thin, light T-shirts (the three methyl ester molecules) and reuses the leftover yarn (glycerol) for other purposes. These T-shirts — now much lighter and easier to "wear" inside a diesel engine — are what we call **biodiesel**.

The parent oil is ~3 to 8 times more viscous than petro-diesel. By breaking each triglyceride into three smaller ester molecules, the viscosity drops to a value **close to that of conventional diesel (~2–6 cSt at 40 °C)**, allowing the fuel to flow smoothly through modern fuel injection systems.

### 1.4 Why Biodiesel is Important to a Mechanical / Energy Engineer

- **Renewable & biodegradable** — degrades ~95% in 28 days (vs. ~50% for diesel).
- **Energy density** — approximately **37–40 MJ/kg** (vs. **~43 MJ/kg** for petro-diesel), only a **5–10% deficit**.
- **Cetane number** — typically **45–65**, generally *higher* than petro-diesel (which is ~40–55), giving smoother cold-start combustion.
- **Lubricity enhancer** — dramatically reduces engine wear at low blend ratios (B2–B5).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transesterification Reaction — Step-by-Step Logic

The overall reaction of one triglyceride molecule with three methanol molecules is:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons[\text{Catalyst}]{\text{NaOH/KOH}} 3\,R-COOCH_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$

The reaction proceeds in **three sequential reversible steps**, each producing one FAME molecule and one diglyceride / monoglyceride intermediate:

1. **Triglyceride + Methanol → Diglyceride + FAME**
2. **Diglyceride + Methanol → Monoglyceride + FAME**
3. **Monoglyceride + Methanol → Glycerol + FAME**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reaction is **reversible**, so an **excess of methanol (typically 6:1 to 9:1 molar ratio of alcohol to oil)** is used to drive the equilibrium toward the products (Le Chatelier's principle).

### 2.2 Process Parameters That Decide Yield

| Parameter | Optimal Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------------|
| **Methanol-to-Oil Molar Ratio** | 6 : 1 to 9 : 1 | Higher ratio drives yield up but increases recovery cost |
| **Catalyst Loading (NaOH)** | 0.5 % – 1.5 % by weight of oil | Below 0.5 % → slow reaction; above 1.5 % → soap formation |
| **Reaction Temperature** | 50 °C – 65 °C | Above 65 °C → methanol boils (BP = 64.7 °C); loses reactant |
| **Reaction Time** | 60 – 120 minutes | Adequate for ~95 – 99 % conversion |
| **Stirring / Mixing Intensity** | 300 – 600 rpm | Critical for mass transfer between two immiscible phases |
| **Water Content of Oil** | < 0.1 % w/w | Water promotes **saponification** (soap formation) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for any numerical problem on biodiesel.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Theoretical molar ratio** | 1 mol triglyceride : 3 mol methanol | Stoichiometric basis |
| 2 | **Excess methanol used in practice** | 6 : 1 to 9 : 1 | Molar ratio, alcohol : oil |
| 3 | **Density of biodiesel (typical)** | $\rho_{BD} \approx 0.86$ – $0.90$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ at 15 °C |
| 4 | **Kinematic viscosity of biodiesel** | $\nu_{BD} \approx 2$ – $6$ | $\text{cSt}$ at 40 °C |
| 5 | **Kinematic viscosity of petro-diesel** | $\nu_{PD} \approx 2$ – $4.5$ | $\text{cSt}$ at 40 °C |
| 6 | **Calorific value (LHV)** | $LHV_{BD} \approx 37$ – $40$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ |
| 7 | **Calorific value of diesel** | $LHV_{PD} \approx 42$ – $45$ | $\text{MJ/kg}$ |
| 8 | **Cetane number of biodiesel** | $CN_{BD} \approx 45$ – $65$ | ASTM D613 |
| 9 | **Cetane number of diesel** | $CN_{PD} \approx 40$ – $55$ | ASTM D613 |
| 10 | **Cetane number relationship** | $CN_{blend} = f_B \cdot CN_{BD} + (1-f_B)\cdot CN_{PD}$ | $f_B$ = volume fraction of biodiesel |
| 11 | **Viscosity of a blend (Reuss rule, linear)** | $\nu_{blend} = f_B \cdot \nu_{BD} + (1-f_B)\cdot \nu_{PD}$ | First-order approximation |
| 12 | **Mass of oil needed for n kg biodiesel** | $m_{oil} = n \cdot \dfrac{M_{oil}}{3 \cdot M_{FAME}}$ | Molar basis |
| 13 | **Molecular weight of a generic FAME** | $M_{FAME} = 14.027 \cdot n_C + 32.042$ | $n_C$ = total carbons; for methyl oleate: n_C = 19 → M = 296.5 g/mol |
| 14 | **Molecular weight of triglyceride** | $M_{oil} = 3 M_{FA} + 38.05$ (glycerol backbone) | For triolein: ≈ 885.4 g/mol |
| 15 | **% Biodiesel energy density deficit** | $\Delta E\% = \dfrac{LHV_{PD}-LHV_{BD}}{LHV_{PD}} \times 100$ | Typical value: 5 – 10 % |
| 16 | **Saponification value** | $SV = \dfrac{168{,}000}{M_{oil}}$ | $\text{mg KOH/g}$ of oil |
| 17 | **Iodine value (unsaturation index)** | $IV = $ grams of I₂ absorbed per 100 g of oil | Lower IV = better oxidative stability |
| 18 | **Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (biodiesel)** | $A/F_{stoich} \approx 12.5$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 19 | **Flash point of biodiesel** | $FP_{BD} \approx 130$ – $170$ °C | Much higher than diesel (≈ 55 – 75 °C) |
| 20 | **Blend Bx density (linear mixing rule)** | $\rho_{Bx} = \dfrac{x \cdot \rho_{BD} + (100-x) \cdot \rho_{PD}}{100}$ | $x$ = % biodiesel in blend |

### 2.4 Industrial Production Process — The Six Operational Units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, students must be able to **list and explain** the following six unit operations in sequence.

1. **Feedstock Pre-treatment** — Degumming, dewaxing, dehydration, free-fatty-acid (FFA) reduction.
2. **Esterification (Acid step, optional)** — When FFA > 2 %, an acid catalyst (H₂SO₄) first converts FFA → FAME. This is a **two-step process** when FFA is high.
3. **Transesterification (Base step)** — NaOH/KOH + methanol reacts with refined oil.
4. **Phase Separation** — Gravity settling in a decanter separates **biodiesel (top, lighter)** from **crude glycerol (bottom, denser)**.
5. **Methanol Recovery** — Distillation column recovers excess unreacted methanol for reuse.
6. **Fuel Purification** — Water washing → drying → polishing filtration → antioxidant addition.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transportation Sector:** B20 is approved by major OEMs (Cummins, John Deere, Bosch) for unmodified compression-ignition engines.
- **Agricultural Sector:** Jatropha and Karanja oil-based biodiesel power irrigation pump sets in rural India.
- **Aviation:** Bio-SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) shares the same FAME chemistry backbone.
- **Cold Climate Operation:** High flash point reduces fire-hazard during storage and transport.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations and Worked Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **fully worked out** with every algebraic step visible. KTU examiners reward every correct intermediate step. The format follows the **"Given → To Find → Assumption → Step-by-step solution → Result"** pattern.

---

### **Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric and Excess Methanol Requirement for Transesterification**

**Given:** A batch reactor processes **1,000 kg of soybean oil (refined, pure triglyceride)** with **average molecular weight 885.4 g/mol** (assumed triolein). The reaction is carried out with a **6 : 1 molar ratio of methanol to oil**.

**To Find:**
- (a) Mass of methanol required (theoretically and actually).
- (b) Mass of biodiesel (FAME, methyl oleate) produced at 100 % conversion.
- (c) Mass of crude glycerol co-produced.

**Assumptions:**
- Molecular weight of methanol $CH_3OH = 32.04$ g/mol.
- Molecular weight of methyl oleate (C₁₉H₃₆O₂) = 296.5 g/mol.
- Molecular weight of glycerol (C₃H₈O₃) = 92.09 g/mol.
- Density of methanol = 0.792 g/cm³.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of moles of oil.

$$\begin{aligned}
