n_{oil} &= \dfrac{m_{oil}}{M_{oil}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{885.4 \text{ g/mol}} \\
n_{oil} &= 1129.41 \text{ moles (or } 1{,}129.41 \text{ kmol)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Stoichiometric methanol required (3 mol methanol per 1 mol oil).

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{MeOH,\,stoich} &= 3 \times n_{oil} \\
&= 3 \times 1129.41 \\
&= 3388.23 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of stoichiometric methanol.

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{MeOH,\,stoich} &= n_{MeOH,\,stoich} \times M_{MeOH} \\
&= 3388.23 \text{ kmol} \times 32.04 \text{ kg/kmol} \\
&= 108{,}567.6 \text{ kg} \approx 108.57 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Actual methanol needed at 6 : 1 ratio.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{MeOH,\,actual} &= 6 \times n_{oil} = 6 \times 1129.41 = 6776.46 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{MeOH,\,actual} &= 6776.46 \times 32.04 = 217{,}157.4 \text{ kg} \approx 217.16 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Mass of FAME (biodiesel) at 100 % conversion. Each mole of oil yields 3 moles of FAME.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{FAME} &= 3 \times 1129.41 = 3388.23 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{FAME} &= 3388.23 \times 296.5 = 1{,}004{,}610 \text{ kg} \approx 1004.61 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Mass of glycerol co-produced (1 mol glycerol per 1 mol oil).

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{gly} &= 1129.41 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{gly} &= 1129.41 \times 92.09 = 104{,}005 \text{ kg} \approx 104.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Balance Check:** Oil (1,000 kg) + Methanol stoichiometric (108.57 kg) = Biodiesel (1,004.61 kg) + Glycerol (104.0 kg) = **1,108.61 kg** on both sides. ✓

**Result:**
- (a) Stoichiometric methanol = **108.57 kg**; actual at 6 : 1 = **217.16 kg**.
- (b) Biodiesel produced = **1,004.61 kg** (≈ **1 kg oil → 1 kg biodiesel** — a useful rule of thumb).
- (c) Crude glycerol = **104.0 kg**.

---

### **Derivation 2 — Properties of a B20 Blend (Linear Mixing Rule)**

**Given:**
- B100 density = 0.88 g/cm³; B0 (petro-diesel) density = 0.84 g/cm³.
- B100 LHV = 38.5 MJ/kg; B0 LHV = 43.0 MJ/kg.
- B100 cetane number = 55; B0 cetane number = 50.

**To Find:** Density, LHV, and cetane number of a **B20 blend** (20 % biodiesel by volume, treated here as approximately equal to mass-basis for low-viscosity fuels at moderate precision).

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Density of B20 using the linear mixing rule.

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{B20} &= f_B \cdot \rho_{BD} + (1-f_B) \cdot \rho_{PD} \\
&= 0.20 \times 0.88 + 0.80 \times 0.84 \\
&= 0.176 + 0.672 \\
&= 0.848 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Lower heating value of B20.

$$\begin{aligned}
LHV_{B20} &= 0.20 \times 38.5 + 0.80 \times 43.0 \\
&= 7.70 + 34.40 \\
&= 42.10 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Cetane number of B20.

$$\begin{aligned}
CN_{B20} &= 0.20 \times 55 + 0.80 \times 50 \\
&= 11.0 + 40.0 \\
&= 51.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** % Energy deficit vs. pure diesel.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E\% &= \dfrac{LHV_{PD} - LHV_{B20}}{LHV_{PD}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{43.0 - 42.1}{43.0} \times 100 \\
&= 2.09\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** B20 has $\rho = 0.848$ g/cm³, $LHV = 42.10$ MJ/kg, $CN = 51$, and only a **2.09 %** energy deficit relative to petro-diesel.

---

### **Derivation 3 — Mass of Oil Required for a Target Biodiesel Output**

**Given:** A small-scale plant must produce **5,000 kg of biodiesel per day** from coconut oil. Assume methyl laurate ($C_{13}H_{26}O_2$, M = 214.34 g/mol) is the dominant FAME, and the parent triglyceride is trilaurin (M = 638.99 g/mol). Conversion efficiency = 98 %.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Moles of FAME needed.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{FAME} &= \dfrac{5{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{214.34 \text{ g/mol}} = 23{,}328.6 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Stoichiometric moles of triglyceride (3 mol FAME per 1 mol oil).

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{oil,\,stoich} &= \dfrac{n_{FAME}}{3} = \dfrac{23{,}328.6}{3} = 7776.2 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Correct for 98 % conversion efficiency.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{oil,\,actual} &= \dfrac{n_{oil,\,stoich}}{0.98} = \dfrac{7776.2}{0.98} = 7934.9 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Mass of oil.

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{oil} &= 7934.9 \times 638.99 = 5{,}070{,}312 \text{ g} = 5070.3 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Approximately **5,070 kg of coconut oil** is required per day to produce 5,000 kg of biodiesel at 98 % conversion.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Biodiesel vs. Petro-Diesel

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part-A question pattern** ("Compare biodiesel and diesel in terms of …").

| Property | Petro-Diesel (B0) | Biodiesel (B100) | Engineering Implication |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Source** | Non-renewable, fossil | Renewable, bio-based | Sustainability |
| **Cetane number** | 40 – 55 | 45 – 65 | Biodiesel ignites more readily |
| **Viscosity at 40 °C (cSt)** | 2.0 – 4.5 | 2.0 – 6.0 | Closer match at higher blends may need preheating |
| **Calorific value (MJ/kg)** | 42 – 45 | 37 – 40 | 5 – 10 % lower → higher fuel consumption |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.82 – 0.86 | 0.86 – 0.90 | Slightly denser, marginally higher mass per unit volume |
| **Flash point (°C)** | 55 – 75 | 130 – 170 | **Much safer** to store and transport |
| **Sulphur content** | < 10 ppm (ULSD) | < 10 ppm (often 0) | Both ultra-low; biodiesel is naturally cleaner |
| **Aromatic content** | 20 – 35 % | 0 % | Lower soot and PAH emissions with biodiesel |
| **Oxygen content (wt %)** | 0 | 10 – 12 | Promotes more complete combustion |
| **Biodegradability** | Slow (months) | Fast (≈ 28 days) | Major environmental advantage |
| **Water solubility** | Negligible | Slightly higher | Storage tank draining recommended |
| **Cold-flow properties (Cloud Pt)** | –5 to –15 °C | 0 to +15 °C | Biodiesel can gel in cold climates |
| **Engine modifications** | — | Usually none for B20 | Drop-in compatibility up to B20 |
| **Lubricity** | Low (ULSD) | Excellent | Reduces injector pump wear |
| **NOx emissions** | Baseline | +5 to +10 % | Slight increase due to higher cetane/oxygen |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transesterification Reaction — Mermaid Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **three-step reversible reaction mechanism** of transesterification, the central chemistry behind biodiesel synthesis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Triglyceride TG] -->|Step 1: + CH3OH| B[Diglyceride DG]
    B -->|Step 2: + CH3OH| C[Monoglyceride MG]
    C -->|Step 3: + CH3OH| D[Glycerol GL]
    B -->|Step 1 co-product| E[FAME 1]
    C -->|Step 2 co-product| F[FAME 2]
    D -->|Step 3 co-product| G[FAME 3]
    cat[Base Catalyst NaOH or KOH] -.->|Catalyzes| A
    cat -.->|Catalyzes| B
    cat -.->|Catalyzes| C
```

**How to read this schematic:**
- Each "−" branch loses a **methyl ester (FAME)** molecule.
- All three reversible steps together yield **3 FAME + 1 glycerol** from **1 triglyceride + 3 methanol**.

---

### 4.2 Industrial Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FEED[Feedstock Pre-Treatment]
        F1[Crude Vegetable Oil or Animal Fat] --> F2[Degumming Dewaxing]
        F2 --> F3[FFA Reduction less than 2 percent]
    end
    FEED --> RX
    subgraph RX[Reaction Section]
        R1[Esterification Acid Step optional] --> R2[Transesterification Base Step]
    end
    subgraph SEP[Separation and Recovery]
        S1[Gravity Decanter] --> S2[Methanol Recovery Distillation]
        S2 --> S3[Glycerol Purification Column]
    end
    subgraph PUR[Purification Section]
        P1[Water Washing] --> P2[Drying Vacuum]
        P2 --> P3[Antioxidant Addition and Polishing Filter]
    end
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> P1
    P3 --> PROD[Finished Biodiesel B100]
    S3 --> BYP[Refined Glycerol 99 percent by-product]
```

**Functional block interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Feedstock Pre-Treatment** | Removes gums, waxes, and excess free fatty acids. |
| **Esterification (Acid)** | H₂SO₄ converts FFA → FAME; required if FFA > 2 %. |
| **Transesterification (Base)** | NaOH + methanol converts triglycerides → FAME. |
| **Decanter** | Separates lighter FAME (top) from denser glycerol (bottom). |
| **Methanol Recovery** | Recycles unreacted methanol back to reactor. |
| **Water Washing** | Removes residual catalyst, soap, glycerol traces. |
| **Drying & Polishing** | Achieves ASTM/EN spec water content and clarity. |

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Biodiesel as a Drop-In Fuel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feedstock Selection] --> B[Pre-treatment]
    B --> C[Transesterification]
    C --> D[Biodiesel Storage]
    D --> E{Blend Ratio}
    E -->|B5| F1[Light Commercial Vehicles]
    E -->|B20| F2[Fleet Trucks and Buses]
    E -->|B100| F3[Dedicated Biodiesel Engines]
    E -->|Pure B100| F4[Agricultural Pumps and Gensets]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define biodiesel. List any two properties that make it superior to petro-diesel.**

**Model Answer:**

Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester (predominantly **FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Ester**) of long-chain fatty acids derived from renewable lipid feedstocks such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or used cooking oil. It is produced by the **transesterification** of triglycerides with a short-chain alcohol in the presence of a catalyst.

**Two superior properties vs. petro-diesel:**

1. **Higher cetane number (45 – 65 vs. 40 – 55)** — leads to smoother and more complete combustion, reducing cold-start knocking.
2. **Higher flash point (130 – 170 °C vs. 55 – 75 °C)** — significantly safer for storage, handling, and transport; classified as a non-hazardous fuel.
3. *(Any other valid property: biodegradability, zero sulphur, superior lubricity, renewable source — full credit.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**What is transesterification? Why is an excess of methanol used in the industrial process?**

**Model Answer:**

Transesterification is the **reversible chemical reaction** in which a triglyceride reacts with a short-chain alcohol (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH/KOH) to produce **fatty acid alkyl esters (biodiesel)** and **glycerol** as a by-product.

**Reaction:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,R-COOCH_3 + C_3H_5(OH)_3$$

**Why excess methanol is used:**

- The reaction is **reversible** (equilibrium-controlled).
- According to **Le Chatelier's principle**, an excess of one reactant (methanol) shifts the equilibrium **toward the products**, achieving a higher conversion of triglycerides into FAME.
- Industrial practice uses a **6 : 1 to 9 : 1 molar ratio of methanol to oil** (against the stoichiometric 3 : 1) to push the conversion to **95 – 99 %**.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, Module 4 contains **Part B questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (either/or)**. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions** (A and B), each split into two 7-mark sub-parts with full model solutions.

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Explain the transesterification reaction for biodiesel production in detail. Discuss the role of catalyst, temperature, and molar ratio on the yield of biodiesel.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Reaction Definition:**

Transesterification is a three-step reversible reaction in which the alkyl group of an ester (triglyceride) is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst, producing a mixture of fatty acid alkyl esters (FAME) and glycerol.

**Overall stoichiometry:**

$$\text{TG} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightleftharpoons{\text{NaOH/KOH}} 3\,FAME + Glycerol$$

**2. Three-Step Mechanism (with role of each parameter):**

- **Step 1:** TG + MeOH → Diglyceride + FAME
- **Step 2:** DG + MeOH → Monoglyceride + FAME
- **Step 3:** MG + MeOH → Glycerol + FAME

**3. Role of Catalyst (NaOH or KOH):**

- Acts as a homogeneous base catalyst; speeds up reaction without being consumed.
- Typical loading: **0.5 % – 1.5 % by weight of oil**.
- Below 0.5 % → incomplete reaction; above 1.5 % → **saponification** (forms soap, emulsifies products, difficult separation).
- KOH is preferred when oil has high unsaturation (softens soap).

**4. Role of Temperature:**

- Optimal range: **50 – 65 °C** (just below the boiling point of methanol, 64.7 °C).
- Higher temperatures: increase rate but methanol evaporates → reduced yield.
- Lower temperatures: slow kinetics, incomplete conversion.

**5. Role of Molar Ratio (Methanol : Oil):**

- Stoichiometric: 3 : 1; Industrial practice: **6 : 1 to 9 : 1**.
- Excess methanol drives the equilibrium toward FAME (Le Chatelier's principle).
- Beyond 9 : 1 → diminishing returns, increases recovery cost of unreacted methanol.

**6. Process Outcome:**

A well-controlled reaction yields **95 – 99 % conversion** within **60 – 90 minutes** of mixing, producing a clear, light-yellow biodiesel layer floating atop the denser, dark-brown glycerol layer.

---

#### **Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Compare the properties of biodiesel (B100) and petro-diesel in a tabular form. State at least six properties.**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Property | Petro-Diesel (B0) | Biodiesel (B100) | Engineering Note |
|--------|----------|-------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Source** | Fossil, non-renewable | Vegetable oil / animal fat, renewable | Sustainability |
| 2 | **Cetane number** | 40 – 55 | 45 – 65 | Biodiesel ignites more readily |
| 3 | **Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C (cSt)** | 2.0 – 4.5 | 2.0 – 6.0 | Biodiesel slightly thicker |
| 4 | **Calorific value (MJ/kg)** | 42 – 45 | 37 – 40 | ~5 – 10 % energy deficit for biodiesel |
| 5 | **Flash point (°C)** | 55 – 75 | 130 – 170 | Biodiesel is much safer in storage |
| 6 | **Density (g/cm³)** | 0.82 – 0.86 | 0.86 – 0.90 | Biodiesel slightly denser |
| 7 | **Sulphur content** | < 10 ppm (ULSD) | < 10 ppm (often 0) | Biodiesel cleaner |
| 8 | **Oxygen content (% wt)** | 0 | 10 – 12 | Promotes complete combustion in biodiesel |
| 9 | **Biodegradability** | Slow (months) | Fast (≈ 28 days) | Major environmental plus |
| 10 | **Lubricity** | Low (modern ULSD) | Excellent | Reduces wear in fuel injection systems |

*(Any six properties earn full 7 marks.)*

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**List and briefly explain the major feedstocks used for biodiesel production. Why is jatropha oil preferred in India for biodiesel?**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Feedstocks:**

1. **Edible Vegetable Oils:** Soybean oil (USA, Brazil), rapeseed/canola oil (Europe), palm oil (Malaysia, Indonesia), sunflower oil (Russia, Ukraine).
2. **Non-edible Vegetable Oils:** Jatropha curcas (India, Africa), Karanja / Pongamia pinnata (India), Castor oil, Neem oil.
3. **Animal Fats:** Tallow (beef), lard (pork), poultry fat, fish oil.
4. **Waste/Recycled Oils:** Used cooking oil (UCO), yellow grease, brown grease (sewer trap grease).
5. **Algal Oils (3rd-gen, emerging):** Microalgae like *Chlorella* and *Nannochloropsis* — high lipid yield per hectare, do not compete with food crops.

**Why Jatropha is preferred in India (any 4 valid points):**

- It grows well on **marginal, arid, and degraded lands** — does not compete with food crops.
- **Non-edible oil** (contains toxic phorbol esters) — does not disturb the food chain.
- High oil content (**30 – 40 %** of seed weight).
- **Long productive life** of 30 – 50 years, drought-resistant, low input cost.
- Supports the **National Biodiesel Mission (NBM)** of India, with target of 20 % blending by 2030.
- Generates **rural employment** through plantation programmes.

---

#### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**

**A batch reactor processes 1,500 kg of soybean oil (M = 885.4 g/mol) with methanol (M = 32.04 g/mol) at a 6 : 1 molar ratio. The FAME produced is methyl oleate (M = 296.5 g/mol). Calculate: (i) Mass of methanol used, (ii) Mass of FAME produced at 100 % conversion, (iii) Mass of glycerol (M = 92.09 g/mol) co-produced.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Moles of oil**

$$n_{oil} = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000 \text{ g}}{885.4 \text{ g/mol}} = 1694.12 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2: Mass of methanol at 6 : 1 ratio**

$$n_{MeOH} = 6 \times 1694.12 = 10{,}164.7 \text{ kmol}$$

$$m_{MeOH} = 10{,}164.7 \times 32.04 = 325{,}677 \text{ kg} \approx 325.68 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Mass of FAME (3 mol FAME per 1 mol oil)**

$$n_{FAME} = 3 \times 1694.12 = 5082.35 \text{ kmol}$$

$$m_{FAME} = 5082.35 \times 296.5 = 1{,}506{,}917 \text{ g} \approx 1506.92 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Mass of glycerol (1 mol glycerol per 1 mol oil)**

$$n_{gly} = 1694.12 \text{ kmol}$$

$$m_{gly} = 1694.12 \times 92.09 = 156{,}022 \text{ kg} \approx 156.02 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| (i) Mass of methanol used | **325.68 tonnes (325,677 kg)** |
| (ii) Mass of FAME produced | **1,506.92 kg** |
| (iii) Mass of glycerol co-produced | **156.02 kg** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Biodiesel Problems (Valuator's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Molar Ratio vs. Mass Ratio Confusion:** Students often confuse the *molar* ratio of methanol to oil with the *mass* ratio. **Always convert moles → mass using molecular weight.** Marks lost: 1 – 2 per question.
> 2. **Methanol as Stoichiometric 3 : 1 vs. Industrial 6 : 1:** If the question says "industrial process with 6 : 1", using 3 : 1 = **–1 mark**.
> 3. **Molecular Weight of FAME vs. Triglyceride:** Mixing up triolein (885.4) and methyl oleate (296.5) is a recurring error. **Remember: 1 triglyceride → 3 FAME + 1 glycerol.**
> 4. **Units of Cetane Number / Viscosity:** Always include **CN (no unit)** and **cSt at 40 °C** explicitly.
> 5. **Ignoring Conversion Efficiency:** If the question says "at 95 % conversion", the biodiesel mass must be multiplied by **0.95**, not 1.0.
> 6. **Conflating Esterification (acid step) with Transesterification (base step):** Esterification handles **free fatty acids**; transesterification handles **triglycerides**. Mixing them up = full 7-mark part loss in a 14-mark question.
> 7. **Skipping Mass Balance Verification:** A 30-second check (oil + methanol = FAME + glycerol) is a free full-mark retrieval by the examiner.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Biodiesel (Module 4)**
>
> - **Biodiesel = FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester)** derived from **triglycerides + methanol** in the presence of **NaOH/KOH** catalyst.
> - **B100 = pure biodiesel;** B20 = 20 % biodiesel + 80 % petro-diesel (most common commercial blend).
> - **Transesterification is reversible → excess methanol (6:1 to 9:1) shifts equilibrium to products.**
> - **Three sequential steps:** TG → DG → MG → Glycerol, releasing 1 FAME at each step.
> - **Optimal conditions:** 50 – 65 °C, 0.5 – 1.5 % catalyst, 60 – 120 minutes, < 0.1 % water in oil.
> - **High-yield properties to memorize:**
>   - Calorific value: 37 – 40 MJ/kg
>   - Cetane number: 45 – 65
>   - Flash point: 130 – 170 °C
>   - Viscosity at 40 °C: 2 – 6 cSt
>   - Density: 0.86 – 0.90 g/cm³
> - **Reaction stoichiometry:** 1 mol oil + 3 mol MeOH → 3 mol FAME + 1 mol glycerol.
> - **First-generation feedstocks** = food crops (soybean, rapeseed, palm). **Second-generation** = non-edible (Jatropha, Karanja, UCO). **Third-generation** = algal oils.
> - **Six industrial unit operations:** Pre-treatment → Esterification (acid) → Transesterification (base) → Phase separation → Methanol recovery → Purification.
> - **Quality standards:** ASTM D6751 (USA) and EN 14214 (Europe).
> - **Key advantages:** renewable, biodegradable, high cetane, zero sulphur, high flash point, excellent lubricity.
> - **Key disadvantages:** 5 – 10 % energy deficit, higher NOx, poor cold-flow (gelling), hygroscopic, slight increase in fuel consumption.
> - **Jatropha is India's strategic biodiesel crop** — grows on marginal land, non-edible, drought-resistant, 30 – 50 year productive life.
> - **Linear mixing rule** for blend properties: $X_{blend} = f_B \cdot X_{B100} + (1-f_B) \cdot X_{B0}$.
> - **Biodiesel is oxygenated (10 – 12 % wt O₂)** → more complete combustion → lower CO, HC, and particulate emissions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas Production from Waste Biomass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Biogas Production from Waste Biomass # Module 4 — Biogas Production from Waste Biomass ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biogas** is a **renewable, flammable, gaseous biofuel** produced by the **biochemical breakdown (fermentation) of organic waste biomass in the complete absence of oxygen**, through a process formally known as **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** or **Methanogenesis**. The dominant constituent is **methane (CH₄ = 50–70 %)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂ = 30–40 %)** with traces of **H₂S, N₂, H₂ and water vapor**. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **waste biomass** refers to any organic matter of recent biological origin (excluding fossil fuels) that is discarded as a by-product of agricultural, municipal, animal, or industrial activity. Examples include: - **Agro-residues** — rice straw, bagasse, groundnut shells - **Animal dung** — cattle manure, poultry litter, pig slurry - **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — kitchen waste, food processing waste - **Aquatic biomass** — water hyacinth, algal blooms - **Industrial organic effluents** — distillery spent wash, dairy whey ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach in a Steel Tank" > [!NOTE] > Imagine a **cow's rumen**: it is a warm, dark, oxygen-free chamber where microbes patiently chew through grass and release **burp-methane**. A biogas digester is essentially an **engineered, sealed, heated version of that same rumen**. We feed it organic waste instead of grass, capture the methane instead of letting it escape into the atmosphere, and convert a pollution problem into cooking gas, electricity, and organic fertilizer — all in one go. ### 1.3 Standard Composition & Calorific Value of Raw Biogas | Component | Symbol | Typical Range (% by volume) | |---|---|---| | Methane | CH₄ | **50 – 70** | | Carbon dioxide | CO₂ | **30 – 40** | | Hydrogen sulfide | H₂S | 0.1 – 0.5 | | Nitrogen | N₂ | 0.5 – 2.0 | | Hydrogen | H₂ | < 1.0 | | Water vapor | H₂O | traces | | Oxygen | O₂ | < 0.5 | > [!NOTE] > **Calorific value of biogas** is directly proportional to its CH₄ fraction. Pure methane gives **≈ 35.8 MJ/Nm³**, so typical biogas with 60 % CH₄ yields roughly **20–24 MJ/Nm³**, comparable to LPG (≈ 46 MJ/Nm³) when normalized for energy density. ### 1.4 The 4-Stage "Microbial Relay Race" Anaerobic digestion is *not* a single reaction — it is a **symbiotic relay of four distinct microbial communities**, each consuming the waste product of the previous stage: | Stage | Microbe Group | Substrate | Product | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Hydrolysis** | Hydrolytic bacteria | Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) | Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) | | **2. Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic bacteria | Monomers | Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂ | | **3. Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic bacteria | VFAs, alcohols | Acetate (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂ | | **4. Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic archaea | Acetate, H₂/CO₂ | **CH₄ + CO₂** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Biochemical Stages — Detailed Mechanism ### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (The "Cracking" Stage) - Long-chain **biopolymers** (cellulose, starch, proteins, fats) are too large to pass through microbial cell walls. - Hydrolytic bacteria secrete **extracellular enzymes**: cellulase, amylase, protease, lipase. - End products: **simple sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs)**. - **Rate-limiting step** for high-solid feedstocks (e.g., cattle dung, straw). ### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (The "Souring" Stage) - Acidogens ferment the monomers into **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)** — acetate, propionate, butyrate — plus alcohols, H₂ and CO₂. - This stage is **fast** and the reason digesters can become **sour (pH < 6.5)** if overloaded — a common cause of digester failure. ### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis (The "Bridging" Stage) - VFAs and alcohols longer than C₂ are oxidized to **acetate, H₂ and CO₂** by syntrophic acetogens. - This reaction is **thermodynamically unfavourable** unless H₂ partial pressure is kept very low — a job done by the methanogens of Stage 4. - A classic example of **interspecies H₂ transfer** in nature. ### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis (The "Energy-Yielding" Stage) Two parallel pathways operate: - **Acetoclastic methanogenesis** (≈ 70 % of CH₄): CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ - **Hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis** (≈ 30 % of CH₄): 4H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why all four stages matter in KTU problems:** A digester fails when *any* stage is disturbed. Hydrogen accumulation (poor H₂ consumption) inhibits acetogenesis; VFA accumulation (over-feeding) inhibits methanogens. Designing a stable digester is essentially **balancing four microbial populations**. ## 2.2 Factors Affecting Biogas Yield | Parameter | Optimal Range | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | **Mesophilic 30–40 °C** or Thermophilic 50–55 °C | Below 20 °C → negligible gas; above 60 °C → methanogen death | | **pH** | **6.8 – 7.4** (mildly alkaline) | < 6.2 → acidification; > 8.0 → ammonia toxicity | | **C/N ratio** | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** | Low C/N → NH₃ toxicity; High C/N → N deficiency for microbes | | **HRT** (Hydraulic Retention Time) | 15 – 40 days (mesophilic) | Too short → washout of slow methanogens | | **SRT** (Solid Retention Time) | > HRT | Critical for thermophilic systems | | **Loading rate (OLR)** | 1 – 4 kg VS/m³·day | Overloading → VFA accumulation → souring | | **Mixing** | Gentle, intermittent | No mixing → scum formation; vigorous → disrupts syntrophy | | **Toxicants** | Avoid | NH₃, H₂S, heavy metals, antibiotics, detergents | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic to remember the sweet spot** → **"T-P-C-H-S-L-M"** — Temperature, pH, C/N, HRT, SRT, Loading, Mixing. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Buswell Equation (general):** $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2$ | Stoichiometric split of CH₄ / CO₂ from any organic molecule | mol | | 2 | **Glucose AD (specific Buswell):** $C_6 H_{12} O_6 \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$ | Theoretical max from carbohydrates | — | | 3 | **Volumetric CH₄ yield (theoretical, glucose):** $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{3 \times 22.4}{180} = 0.373 \ \text{Nm}^3 \text{CH}_4 / \text{kg glucose}$ | At STP (0 °C, 1 atm) | Nm³/kg | | 4 | **Methane fraction from Buswell coefficients:** $x_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n/2 - a/8 + b/4}{n}$ | Fractional CH₄ in raw biogas | — | | 5 | **Energy in biogas:** $E_{bio} = V \times \rho_{CH_4} \times \text{CV}_{CH_4} \times x_{CH_4}$ | $V$ = gas volume, $\rho_{CH_4} = 0.716$ kg/Nm³, $\text{CV} = 50$ MJ/kg | MJ | | 6 | **HRT:** $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{Q_{feed}}$ | $V$ = digester volume, $Q$ = daily feed flow | days | | 7 | **OLR:** $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{m_{VS,feed}}{V_{digester} \times t}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg VS/m³·day | | 8 | **Theoretical COD of CH₄:** $1 \ \text{g CH}_4 \equiv 4 \ \text{g COD}$ | Used in wastewater AD design | g COD | | 9 | **Calorific value of biogas:** $\text{CV}_{bio} \approx 0.6 \times 35.8 = 21.5 \ \text{MJ/Nm}^3$ (for 60 % CH₄) | Quick approximation | MJ/Nm³ | | 10 | **Power from biogas:** $P = \dot{V} \times \text{CV}_{bio} \times \eta_{gen}$ | $\eta_{gen} = 0.30$–$0.40$ for IC engines | kW | > [!IMPORTANT] > All vertical bars in this table use `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown. Memorize **Equation 1 (Buswell)** and **Equation 2 (glucose)** — these are the two most-tested formulas in KTU 2024 ESE papers for this module. ## 2.4 Types of Biogas Plants (KTU-Syllabus Classification) | Type | Structure | Gas Storage | Pressure | Common Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Fixed-dome (Chinese)** | Underground masonry dome, immovable | Displaces slurry into compensation tank | Variable (up to 1 bar) | Rural household, Asia | | **Floating-drum (Indian / KVIC)** | Moving steel gasholder floats on slurry | Gas volume = displaced dome height × area | Constant | India — Gobar Gas scheme | | **Plug-flow** | Horizontal, heated, long trough | External gasholder | Constant | Cattle manure, dry matter > 12 % | | **CSTR** (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) | Vertical tank, mechanical/recirculation mixing | External | Constant | Industrial effluents | | **UASB** (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket) | Tall column with sludge blanket | External | Constant | Sewage, distillery spent wash | | **Two-stage (Phase-separated)** | Two reactors — acidogenic + methanogenic | External | Constant | High-rate industrial AD | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rural electrification** — Decentralized 3–250 kW plants in Indian villages. - **Municipal waste management** — Bangalore, Delhi, Hyderabad waste-to-energy plants. - **Industrial effluent treatment** — Distilleries (molasses → biogas) before discharge. - **Compressed Biogas (CBG)** — Purified to > 95 % CH₄, used as auto-fuel under **SATAT** scheme (India). - **Carbon credits & SDGs** — Avoids fugitive CH₄ release (CH₄ has 28–34× the GWP of CO₂). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the General Buswell Equation (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The Buswell equation is a **stoichiometric balance** of an organic molecule of the form $C_n H_a O_b$ undergoing complete anaerobic digestion. It simultaneously balances **C, H, and O atoms** and gives the **exact CH₄ / CO₂ split**. This is the single most-asked derivation in KTU ESE Module 4. ### Starting Reaction (unknown coefficients) $$C_n H_a O_b + x \cdot H_2 O \longrightarrow y \cdot CH_4 + z \cdot CO_2$$ We must determine $x$, $y$, $z$ in terms of $n$, $a$, $b$ from atom balances. ### Step 1 — Carbon Balance Every carbon atom on the LHS must appear in CH₄ or CO₂ on the RHS. $$\text{C: } n = y + z$$ ### Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance Hydrogen appears in $H_a$, $H_2 O$ (LHS) and in $CH_4$ (RHS). Each $H_2O$ contributes 2H; each $CH_4$ contributes 4H. $$\text{H: } a + 2x = 4y$$ ### Step 3 — Oxygen Balance Oxygen appears in $H_a O_b$ (b atoms) and in $H_2O$ (x atoms) on the LHS, and in $CO_2$ (2z atoms) on the RHS. $$\text{O: } b + x = 2z$$ ### Step 4 — Solve the System From Step 1: $z = n - y$. Substitute $z = n - y$ into Step 3: $$b + x = 2(n - y) = 2n - 2y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2n - 2y - b$$ Substitute $x$ into Step 2: $$\begin{aligned} a + 2(2n - 2y - b) &= 4y \\ a + 4n - 4y - 2b &= 4y \\ 8y &= 4n + a - 2b \\ y &= \frac{4n + a - 2b}{8} = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{b}{4} + \frac{a}{8} \end{aligned}$$ So $$y = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}$$ (Re-ordering for textbook style: $\frac{n}{2} + \frac{b}{4} - \frac{a}{8}$.) ### Step 5 — Back-Substitute for $z$ and $x$ $$z = n - y = n - \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}$$ $$x = 2n - 2y - b = 2n - 2\left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) - b = n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}$$ ### Step 6 — Final Buswell Equation $$\boxed{C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_2 O \longrightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_2}$$ ### Step 7 — Verification with Glucose ($C_6 H_{12} O_6$) Plug $n=6$, $a=12$, $b=6$: $$x = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$$ $$y = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$$ $$z = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$ Hence $$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2 \quad \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > Methane fraction for glucose = $3 / (3+3) = 0.5$. This is the *theoretical* maximum; real digesters give 45–55 % because of incomplete degradation, dissolved losses, and trace gases. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — CH₄ Yield from 1 kg Glucose **Problem:** Calculate the theoretical volume of CH₄ (in Nm³) produced at STP from 1 kg of pure glucose. **Given:** - Molar mass of glucose = **180 g/mol** - 1 mol of any gas at STP = **22.4 L = 0.0224 Nm³** **Solution:** Moles of glucose in 1 kg: $$n_{glu} = \frac{1000 \ \text{g}}{180 \ \text{g/mol}} = 5.556 \ \text{mol}$$ Moles of CH₄ produced (3:1 stoichiometry from glucose): $$n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667 \ \text{mol}$$ Volume of CH₄ at STP: $$V_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 22.4 \ \text{L} = 373.3 \ \text{L} = 0.373 \ \text{Nm}^3$$ **Answer:** $V_{CH_4} = 0.373 \ \text{Nm}^3$ per kg glucose. > [!IMPORTANT] > The corresponding energy content (using $\text{CV}_{CH_4} = 35.8$ MJ/Nm³): > $E = 0.373 \times 35.8 = 13.36$ MJ per kg glucose — about **40 %** of the original chemical energy of glucose (glucose HHV ≈ 15.6 MJ/kg), with the balance locked in the undigested biomass and digester losses. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Substrates & Their Theoretical Yields | Substrate | Formula | $n$ | $a$ | $b$ | $y$ (CH₄) | $z$ (CO₂) | $x_{CH_4}$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Glucose** | $C_6H_{12}O_6$ | 6 | 12 | 6 | 3.0 | 3.0 | **0.500** | | **Cellulose unit** | $C_6H_{10}O_5$ | 6 | 10 | 5 | 2.75 | 3.25 | **0.458** | | **Lignin (approx.)** | $C_{10}H_{12}O_3$ | 10 | 12 | 3 | 5.25 | 4.75 | 0.525 | | **Acetic acid** | $C_2H_4O_2$ | 2 | 4 | 2 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.500 | | **Stearic acid** | $C_{18}H_{36}O_2$ | 18 | 36 | 2 | 12.5 | 5.5 | 0.694 | | **Urea (excreta N)** | $CH_4N_2O$ | 1 | 4 | 1 | 0.5 + NH₃ path | — | variable | | **Methane (reference)** | $CH_4$ | 1 | 4 | 0 | 1.0 | 0.0 | 1.000 | **Engineering takeaway:** *Lipids* (e.g., stearic acid) give the **highest** CH₄ fraction (~70 %), *proteins* give medium, *carbohydrates* give the lowest (~46–50 %). This explains why fat-rich wastewaters (dairy, slaughterhouse) are the most attractive for CBG plants. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Digester Sizing Using HRT **Problem:** A small village generates **500 kg of cattle dung per day**. Design a mesophilic anaerobic digester with HRT = 30 days. Assume slurry has **density 1000 kg/m³** and total solids (TS) = 8 %. What should be the digester volume? **Solution:** Total slurry mass to be retained = daily input × HRT $$m_{slurry} = 500 \ \text{kg/day} \times 30 \ \text{days} = 15000 \ \text{kg}$$ Density of slurry ≈ 1000 kg/m³, so $$V_{digester} = \frac{15000 \ \text{kg}}{1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3} = 15 \ \text{m}^3$$ **Answer:** $V_{digester} \approx 15 \ \text{m}^3$ (plus ~20 % allowance for gas space and freeboard → ~18 m³ total). **Rule of thumb used in KTU problems:** - 1 m³ digester handles ≈ 30–40 kg dung/day at mesophilic HRT. - 1 m³ biogas ≈ 0.5 kg cattle-dung-equivalent energy. # 4.
