KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define anaerobic digestion and list its four biochemical stages.** **Model Answer:** Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the **biochemical decomposition of organic matter by microorganisms in the complete absence of oxygen**, producing a methane-rich biogas and a nutrient-rich digestate. The four stages are: 1. **Hydrolysis** — breakdown of polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) into monomers by extracellular enzymes. 2. **Acidogenesis** — fermentation of monomers into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂ and CO₂. 3. **Acetogenesis** — conversion of VFAs/alcohols into acetate, H₂ and CO₂ by syntrophic acetogens. 4. **Methanogenesis** — conversion of acetate and H₂/CO₂ into CH₄ and CO₂ by methanogenic archaea. > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfall:** Students frequently **omit the enzymes** in hydrolysis or **confuse acidogenesis with acetogenesis**. The discriminator is the substrate: *monomers* (acidogenesis) vs *VFAs* (acetogenesis). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is the C/N ratio of 20–30 : 1 considered optimal for biogas production?** **Model Answer:** Microbial cells need carbon for **energy** and nitrogen for **protein synthesis**. The ideal dietary ratio of C to N for anaerobic consortia is **20–30 : 1**. - If **C/N is too low** (e.g., < 15:1, as in poultry litter): excess nitrogen is released as **free ammonia (NH₃)**, which is toxic to methanogens and inhibits CH₄ formation. - If **C/N is too high** (e.g., > 35:1, as in sawdust or rice straw): nitrogen becomes **limiting**, slowing microbial growth and causing incomplete digestion, with residual undegraded fibre. Hence the 20–30 : 1 window balances **energy supply, nutrient supply, and toxicity avoidance**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's tip:** Always quote *both* boundary effects in the same answer — partial credit is lost if you only describe one side. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question is internally optional. **Answer ANY ONE** from the choice given. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **fixed-dome biogas plant** (Chinese model). Discuss its merits and demerits compared to a floating-drum plant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **general Buswell equation** for anaerobic digestion of a generic organic compound $C_nH_aO_b$. Hence calculate the **theoretical volume of methane at STP** obtainable from **2 kg of pure glucose**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) A **fixed-dome (Chinese) biogas plant** is a masonry / concrete underground structure comprising an inlet, digester chamber, gas storage dome, and a displacement / compensation chamber. **Working principle:** 1. A slurry of dung and water (≈ 1:1 by weight) is fed daily through the **inlet pipe** into the digester. 2. Anaerobic digestion occurs in the **digester** at ambient / mesophilic temperature, generating biogas. 3. The biogas accumulates in the **rigid, immovable upper dome** (the *gas holder*). 4. As gas pressure builds, the slurry is pushed out through the **outlet pipe** into a **compensation/expansion tank**, whose rising level is a visual indicator of stored gas volume. 5. The gas is led through a pipe fitted with a **gas valve, water trap, pressure gauge, and H₂S scrubber** to the point of use (stove, lamp, engine). 6. The displaced slurry in the expansion tank is used as **liquid bio-fertilizer**. **Merits vs KVIC (floating-drum) plant:** | Feature | Fixed-Dome | Floating-Drum | |---|---|---| | Construction cost | **Low** (no steel) | **High** (steel gasholder) | | Gas pressure | Variable (0–100 mbar) | **Constant** (~25 mbar) | | Visible gas volume | No (must check expansion tank) | **Yes** (drum height) | | Lifespan | 20+ years | 8–15 years (corrosion) | | Gas leakage | Possible at masonry joints | Minimal | | Maintenance | Higher | Lower | | Suitability | Large-scale community plants | Small family / farm units | #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **Step 1 — Define unknown coefficients** $$C_n H_a O_b + x H_2 O \longrightarrow y CH_4 + z CO_2$$ **Step 2 — Atom balances** - Carbon: $n = y + z$ - Hydrogen: $a + 2x = 4y$ - Oxygen: $b + x = 2z$ **Step 3 — Solve** (as derived in Section 3.1): $$\boxed{y = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}, \quad z = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}, \quad x = n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Plug $n=6$, $a=12$, $b=6$ (glucose)** $$y = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3, \quad z = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$ $$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$$ **Step 5 — Methane volume from 2 kg glucose** Moles of glucose: $$n_{glu} = \frac{2000 \ \text{g}}{180 \ \text{g/mol}} = 11.11 \ \text{mol}$$ Moles of CH₄: $$n_{CH_4} = 3 \times 11.11 = 33.33 \ \text{mol}$$ Volume at STP: $$V_{CH_4} = 33.33 \times 22.4 \ \text{L} = 746.7 \ \text{L} = 0.747 \ \text{Nm}^3$$ **Answer:** $V_{CH_4} \approx 0.747 \ \text{Nm}^3$ per 2 kg of glucose. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** List and briefly explain the **factors affecting biogas production** in an anaerobic digester. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** digester is operated with the following data: - Daily feed = 800 kg of cattle dung slurry, density 1050 kg/m³, TS = 7 %, VS/TS = 0.85 - HRT = 25 days - Methane yield = 0.22 Nm³/kg VS added - Methane fraction in biogas = 0.60 - Generator efficiency = 35 % Calculate: **(i)** digester volume, **(ii)** daily biogas volume, **(iii)** daily methane volume, **(iv)** daily electrical energy output. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) The major factors affecting biogas yield are: 1. **Temperature** — Mesophilic (30–40 °C) gives stable output; thermophilic (50–55 °C) gives higher rate but more sensitive. Psychrophilic (< 20 °C) gives poor yield. 2. **pH** — Optimal 6.8–7.4. VFA accumulation drops pH < 6.2, killing methanogens. 3. **C/N ratio** — Optimum 20–30:1 (see Q2 in Part A). 4. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** — Must exceed the doubling time of methanogens (~3–5 days) to avoid washout; typical 15–40 days. 5. **Loading Rate (OLR)** — 1–4 kg VS/m³·day; overloading causes souring. 6. **Mixing** — Gentle, intermittent mixing enhances contact but vigorous mixing disrupts syntrophic H₂ transfer. 7. **Toxicants** — NH₃, H₂S, heavy metals, antibiotics, pesticides. 8. **Feedstock composition** — Lipid-rich substrates give highest yield; lignin-rich give lowest. #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **Given:** Feed = 800 kg/day, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, TS = 7 %, VS/TS = 0.85, HRT = 25 d, $Y_{CH_4} = 0.22$ Nm³/kg VS, $x_{CH_4} = 0.60$, $\eta_{gen} = 0.35$. **Step 1 — Volatile Solids added per day** $$m_{VS} = 800 \times 0.07 \times 0.85 = 47.6 \ \text{kg VS/day}$$ **Step 2 — Digester volume** $$V_{digester} = \frac{Q_{feed} \times \text{HRT}}{\rho} = \frac{800 \times 25}{1050} = 19.05 \ \text{m}^3$$ (Use 19 m³ working volume; add 20 % freeboard → ≈ 23 m³ total.) **Step 3 — Daily methane volume** $$V_{CH_4} = m_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4} = 47.6 \times 0.22 = 10.47 \ \text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 4 — Daily biogas volume** $$V_{biogas} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{x_{CH_4}} = \frac{10.47}{0.60} = 17.45 \ \text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$ **Step 5 — Daily electrical energy output** Energy in methane (using $\text{CV}_{CH_4} = 35.8$ MJ/Nm³): $$E_{in} = 10.47 \times 35.8 = 374.8 \ \text{MJ/day}$$ Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$E_{in} = \frac{374.8}{3.6} = 104.1 \ \text{kWh/day}$$ After generator efficiency: $$E_{out} = 104.1 \times 0.35 = 36.4 \ \text{kWh/day}$$ **Equivalent continuous power output:** $$P = \frac{36.4}{24} = 1.52 \ \text{kW}$$ **Summary of Answers:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | (i) Digester volume | **19.05 m³** (≈ 23 m³ with freeboard) | | (ii) Daily biogas | **17.45 Nm³/day** | | (iii) Daily methane | **10.47 Nm³/day** | | (iv) Daily electrical energy | **36.4 kWh/day (≈ 1.5 kW continuous)** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Numerical Problems** > 1. **Don't forget the VS/TS ratio** — many students use TS directly and oversize the plant. > 2. **Convert MJ → kWh** before multiplying by generator efficiency, not after. > 3. **Add 15–20 % freeboard** to the digester volume for gas space — this is a KTU-specific must-do. > 4. **Methane yield is per kg *VS* added**, not per kg wet slurry. Wrong units = zero credit. > 5. **HRT uses slurry mass, OLR uses VS mass** — mixing them up is the #1 numerical error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Top 20 KTU 2024 "must-know" bullets for Biogas Production from Waste Biomass:** 1. **Biogas** = CH₄ (50–70 %) + CO₂ (30–40 %) + traces; CV ≈ 20–24 MJ/Nm³. 2. **Process** = **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** — no oxygen, 4 microbial stages. 3. **Four stages** → **H-A-A-M** → **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis. 4. **Hydrolysis is the rate-limiting step** for solid wastes; **methanogenesis is rate-limiting** for soluble wastes. 5. **Buswell equation** (general) and **glucose** (specific) are the two most-asked equations in KTU papers. 6. **Methanogens are archaea, not bacteria** — they are extremely sensitive to O₂, pH shock, and NH₃. 7. **Two methanogenesis pathways:** **Acetoclastic (70 %)** and **Hydrogenotrophic (30 %)**. 8. **Optimal temperature:** Mesophilic 30–40 °C; thermophilic 50–55 °C. 9. **Optimal pH:** 6.8 – 7.4; below 6.2 → souring; above 8.0 → ammonia toxicity. 10. **Optimal C/N ratio:** 20:1 to 30:1. 11. **HRT** = $V/Q$; typical 15–40 days (mesophilic); minimum ≈ 3–5× methanogen doubling time. 12. **OLR** = $m_{VS}/(V \cdot t)$ in **kg VS/m³·day**; safe range 1–4. 13. **Two main plant types:** Fixed-dome (Chinese) and Floating-drum (KVIC). Each has 5 unique features. 14. **Lipids > Proteins > Carbohydrates** in CH₄ yield per kg VS degraded. 15. **Lignin is virtually indigestible** under anaerobic conditions — this is why lignocellulosic waste alone is a poor AD feed. 16. **1 g CH₄ ≡ 4 g COD** (wastewater AD design). 17. **1 mole CH₄ = 22.4 L at STP (0 °C, 1 atm)** — use this for yield calculations. 18. **0.373 Nm³ CH₄ / kg glucose** is the theoretical maximum. 19. **Souring = VFA accumulation** due to overloading; remedy = stop feeding, add buffer, restart slowly. 20. **CBG (Compressed Biogas)** is purified biogas with CH₄ > 95 %; linked to India's **SATAT** scheme. > [!NOTE] > **Two-line recall for the day before exam:** *AD is a 4-stage microbial relay, the sweet spot is 35 °C, pH 7, C/N 25, HRT 30 days — and every kg of glucose can yield at most 0.373 Nm³ of CH₄ under perfect Buswell stoichiometry.*

topics.title: Factors Affecting Biogas Generation, Biogas - KVIC and Janata Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Biogas Generation, Biogas - KVIC and Janata Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Factors Affecting Biogas Generation — Formal Definition **Biogas Generation** is the anaerobic (oxygen-free) biochemical decomposition of organic biomass by a synergistic consortium of microorganisms, producing a gaseous mixture primarily composed of **Methane (CH₄, 50–70%)** and **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂, 30–40%)**, along with trace amounts of H₂S, N₂, and water vapor. The yield, composition, and rate of this process are governed by a tightly coupled set of **biochemical, physical, and operational parameters** known collectively as *Factors Affecting Biogas Generation*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Out of the 8–10 parameters that influence anaerobic digestion, examiners frequently test the student on the *numerical optimum* (e.g., "optimum C:N ratio = 25:1 to 30:1" or "optimum temperature = 35 °C for mesophilic"). Memorizing these numbers is a **3-mark question staple**. ### 1.2 The Two Biogas Plant Models — KVIC and Janata | Model | Governing Body / Origin | Reactor Type | Pressure Variation | |---|---|---|---| | **KVIC Model** | Khadi & Village Industries Commission (Gandhian initiative, 1962) | **Floating Drum** (constant volume, variable pressure) | Pressure ≈ **constant** | | **Janata Model** | Indian domestic adaptation (low-cost variant of Chinese fixed-dome) | **Fixed Dome** (constant volume, variable pressure) | Pressure **variable** | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stomach & Lung" Viewpoint > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Biogas Plant as a Human Digestive System** > - **The Digester** = a giant, air-tight "stomach" where microbes chew (decompose) food (biomass) without oxygen. > - **The Gas Holder / Dome** = a "lung" that inflates as biogas is exhaled, deflates as gas is drawn for cooking. > - **The Slurry Outlet** = the "intestine" that expels the digested residue (used as bio-manure). > - **Temperature, pH, C:N ratio, moisture** = the "vitals" — if body temperature is too high/low or food is too acidic, the stomach stops working. So it is with the digester. In the **KVIC model**, the "lung" is a movable steel drum (like an inverted bucket floating on water). In the **Janata model**, the "lung" is a permanent masonry dome — gas pushes slurry out into a side tank, and the rising slurry level *is* the pressure gauge. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Eight Critical Factors — Structured Logic The biochemical reactions inside an anaerobic digester follow three sequential microbial stages: **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Methanogenesis**. Each stage has a *rate-limiting* factor. Below is the KTU-recommended layered breakdown. ### F1. Temperature (T) - Anaerobic microbes are **highly temperature-sensitive**. - Three operational regimes exist: | Regime | Temperature Range | Microbial Dominance | Gas Yield (per kg VS) | |---|---|---|---| | **Psychrophilic** | < 20 °C (often 10–20 °C) | Slow methanogens | Very low | | **Mesophilic** | **30 – 40 °C (optimum 35 °C)** | *Methanosarcina*, *Methanococcus* | **0.30 – 0.50 m³** | | **Thermophilic** | 50 – 60 °C (optimum 55 °C) | Thermotolerant archaea | 0.50 – 0.70 m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > A 10 °C rise within the mesophilic range can **double the gas production rate** (Q₁₀ rule of microbiology), but the digester becomes more sensitive to ammonia toxicity and requires more energy input. ### F2. pH (Acidity / Alkalinity) - Methanogenic archaea are extremely pH-sensitive. - **Optimum pH range = 6.8 – 7.2** (near neutral). - pH < 6.3 → "sour digester" → acidogenesis dominates, methane production **halts**. - pH > 8.0 → ammonia inhibition. - **Buffering capacity** (provided by NH₄⁺ / HCO₃⁻ system) maintains stability. ### F3. Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C:N) - Microbes need both Carbon (energy) and Nitrogen (protein synthesis). - **Optimum C:N = 20:1 to 30:1** (often quoted as **25:1 to 30:1** in KTU materials). - High C:N (> 35) → nitrogen starvation, slow digestion. - Low C:N (< 15) → ammonia accumulation, pH rises, methanogens inhibited. | Feedstock | Typical C:N | |---|---| | Cow dung | 25:1 ✅ | | Poultry droppings | 15:1 (too low) | | Rice straw | 70:1 (too high) | | Food waste | 20:1 ✅ | ### F4. Moisture / Total Solids (TS) Content - **Optimum moisture = 90% (i.e., Total Solids ≈ 10%)** for wet fermentation. - Slurry must flow — too dry → clogs; too wet → washout of microbes. ### F5. Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) & Solid Retention Time (SRT) - **HRT** = Volume of digester / Daily feed volume - **SRT** = Volume of digester / Daily mass of solids leaving - For mesophilic cow-dung plants: **HRT = 30 – 50 days**; for thermophilic: 15 – 20 days. ### F6. Loading Rate - Volumetric organic loading rate (OLR) = Mass of VS fed per m³ of digester per day. - Typical: **1.0 – 3.0 kg VS / m³·day** for mesophilic CSTR. - Overloading → VFA accumulation → pH crash. ### F7. Mixing / Agitation - Ensures uniform distribution of substrate, temperature, and microbes. - Avoids scum formation, stratification, and dead zones. - Achieved by mechanical stirrers, gas recirculation, or slurry recirculation. ### F8. Toxicity / Inhibitors - **Ammonia (NH₃)** > 1700 mg/L is toxic. - **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** > 200 mg/L inhibits methanogens. - Heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Cr, Ni), antibiotics, detergents, pesticides. - **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFA)** > 2000 mg acetic acid/L → acid shock. ### F9. Particle Size of Feedstock - Smaller particles → larger surface area → faster hydrolysis. - Typical practical limit: 5 – 10 mm after grinding. ## 2.2 The KVIC Model — Operational Logic The **KVIC (Khadi and Village Industries Commission) biogas plant** is the **floating-drum type** also called the *constant-pressure, variable-volume* plant. ### Components 1. **Brick/Masonry Digester Well** (underground cylindrical pit) 2. **Inlet Mixing Tank** — receives fresh dung slurry 3. **Outlet / Displacement Chamber** — receives overflow slurry 4. **Floating Steel Gas Holder (Drum)** — inverted, floats on the slurry surface 5. **Central Guide Pipe / Frame** — keeps the gas holder aligned 6. **Gas Outlet Pipe** — at the top of the drum, with a valve ### Working Principle - Slurry is fed daily; microbial digestion produces gas bubbles. - Bubbles rise and collect in the **inverted drum**. - The drum **rises** as gas accumulates; **falls** as gas is consumed. - **Pressure remains almost constant** (≈ weight of drum / projected area), typically **7 – 10 cm water column**. ### Why "Float"? The drum must displace a weight of slurry equal to its own weight (Archimedes' principle) — this gives it a **buoyancy-determined gas pressure**. ### Advantages - **Constant gas pressure** → predictable burner flame. - Gas-holder **position visually indicates** stored gas volume. - Easy to start, stop, and inspect. - Standardized design → mass-produced in India. ### Disadvantages - Steel drum **corrodes** due to H₂S in biogas (1–2 mm/year). - High **initial cost** due to metal. - Drum is exposed to weather → **paint maintenance** required. - Not suited for large capacities (> 60 m³/day). ## 2.3 The Janata Model — Operational Logic The **Janata model** is a **fixed-dome plant** (also called a *constant-volume, variable-pressure* plant). It is a low-cost adaptation with no moving parts. ### Components 1. **Underground Masonry Digester Chamber** (cylindrical + dome top) 2. **Compensation / Expansion Tank** (side chamber, slightly lower) 3. **Inlet Pipe** (slanted from mixing tank into digester) 4. **Outlet Pipe** (from digester top down into compensation tank) 5. **Gas Outlet Pipe** (at the crown of the fixed dome) 6. **No floating parts** — dome is built of brick or RCC. ### Working Principle - As gas is generated, it **accumulates in the upper dome**. - Since the dome is rigid, gas pressure pushes slurry **out through the outlet pipe into the compensation tank**. - The **height difference (H)** between the slurry level in the digester and the compensation tank equals the **gas pressure head**. - As gas is consumed, slurry flows **back** from the compensation tank into the digester (acts as a one-way check). ### Pressure Equation $$ P_{gas} = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot H $$ Where: - $P_{gas}$ = gas pressure (Pa) - $\rho_{slurry}$ ≈ **1100 kg/m³** (slurry density, slightly higher than water) - $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** - $H$ = difference in slurry level between digester and compensation tank (m) ### Advantages - **No corrosion** — all masonry, no steel. - **Long life (20–30 years)**. - **Lower cost** than KVIC (~60% of KVIC cost). - No moving parts → low maintenance. - Gas stored under **higher pressure** (useful for long pipelines). ### Disadvantages - **Gas pressure varies** as digestion proceeds — flame fluctuates. - Cannot visually "see" the gas quantity stored. - Requires **skilled masonry** for leak-proof construction. - **Gas leakage** through brickwork is a common failure mode. - **Scum removal** is difficult. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula bank** for this topic. No pipes (`|`) are used inside cells to preserve markdown integrity. | S.No | Concept | Formula / Numerical Optimum | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Optimum digester temperature (mesophilic) | $T = 35\ ^\circ C$ | °C | KTU 2024 numerical favorite | | 2 | Optimum pH | $pH = 6.8 - 7.2$ | — | Below 6.3 → sour digester | | 3 | Optimum C:N ratio | $C:N = 25:1 \text{ to } 30:1$ | ratio | Cow dung ≈ 25:1 | | 4 | Optimum moisture content | $M = 90\%$ | % | TS ≈ 10% | | 5 | Hydraulic Retention Time (mesophilic) | $HRT = 30 - 50 \text{ days}$ | days | For cow-dung plant | | 6 | Hydraulic Retention Time (thermophilic) | $HRT = 15 - 20 \text{ days}$ | days | Faster but costlier | | 7 | OLR (mesophilic CSTR) | $OLR = 1.0 - 3.0 \ kgVS/m^3 \cdot d$ | kg VS / m³·day | Overload → pH crash | | 8 | KVIC plant pressure | $P \approx 7 - 10\ cm\ H_2O$ | cm H₂O | Constant | | 9 | Janata plant gas pressure | $P = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot H$ | Pa | Variable with H | | 10 | Slurry density | $\rho_{slurry} \approx 1100 \ kg/m^3$ | kg/m³ | Used in Janata pressure eqn. | | 11 | Biogas composition (typical) | $CH_4 = 50-70\%, \ CO_2 = 30-40\%$ | % by volume | Trace H₂S, N₂, H₂O | | 12 | Calorific value of biogas | $CV = 18 - 25\ MJ/m^3$ | MJ/m³ | Vs. LPG ≈ 50 MJ/m³ | | 13 | Yield from cow dung (mesophilic) | $Y = 0.30 - 0.50 \ m^3/kg\ VS$ | m³/kg VS | — | | 14 | Q₁₀ rule of methanogenesis | Rate doubles per +10 °C | — | Within mesophilic range | | 15 | Toxic NH₃ threshold | $[NH_3] > 1700\ mg/L$ | mg/L | Methanogen inhibition | | 16 | Particle size for hydrolysis | $d \leq 5-10\ mm$ | mm | After grinding | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **KVIC plants** are deployed in rural India for **1–6 m³/day household cooking** (≈ 60 lakh plants installed historically). - **Janata plants** are favored for **community / institutional biogas** setups (schools, hostels) where the **higher-pressure gas** can run small engines (1–10 kW dual-fuel gensets). - Factor-optimization is central to the design of modern **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactors (CSTR)**, **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactors, and **Plug Flow Reactors (PFR)** used in industrial wastewater-to-energy projects worldwide. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Janata Model Gas Pressure Head **Problem (KTU-style):** A Janata biogas plant has a digester slurry level at **1.5 m above the floor** and the compensation-tank slurry level at **0.4 m above the floor**. Both levels are measured at the instant when the digester is half-full of gas. Compute the **absolute gas pressure** inside the dome. Take $\rho_{slurry} = 1100\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$, and atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325\ kPa$. ### Step 1: Identify the controlling principle The gas in the fixed dome exerts pressure by displacing slurry into the expansion tank. The pressure at the gas–slurry interface inside the dome equals the **hydrostatic pressure** of the slurry column of height $H$, where $H$ is the vertical difference between the slurry surface inside the digester (at the gas boundary) and the slurry surface in the compensation tank. ### Step 2: Compute the height difference H The slurry inside the digester has been pushed **downward** by accumulated gas. The compensation tank level is **higher** than the digester slurry level. $$ H = h_{comp} - h_{diges} = 0.4 - 1.5 = -1.1\ m $$ > The negative sign indicates that the digester slurry level is **higher** than the compensation-tank slurry level (i.e., the gas has not yet displaced enough slurry to raise the tank level). In this case, **H** is the height the slurry would have to be raised to equalize — but for the gas to push slurry out, the digester level must be **lower** than the compensation level. **Reinterpretation:** The problem states "digester slurry level at 1.5 m" measured from the dome floor. As gas accumulates, the slurry inside the digester is depressed, so the **free surface of slurry inside the digester is at some level $h_d$**, while in the compensation tank the slurry is at $h_c$. The pressure head is: $$ H = h_c - h_d $$ If $h_c = 0.4\ m$ and $h_d = 1.5\ m$ (both measured from the digester floor), then $H = 0.4 - 1.5 = -1.1\ m$. This means the **compensation-tank level is 1.1 m below** the digester slurry level — the gas pressure is **insufficient** to push slurry up into the compensation tank, implying an **under-pressure** condition. ### Step 3: Compute gauge pressure using the absolute value of H $$ P_{gauge} = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \vert H \vert $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 1100 \times 9.81 \times 1.1 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 1100 \times 9.81 = 10791 $$ $$ P_{gauge} = 10791 \times 1.1 = 11870.1\ Pa $$ ### Step 4: Convert to convenient units $$ P_{gauge} = 11.870\ kPa \approx 0.117\ atm $$ ### Step 5: Compute absolute gas pressure $$ P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101.325 + 11.870 = 113.195\ kPa $$ ### Step 6: Final answer box > **Result:** $P_{gas,gauge} = 11.87\ kPa$ (≈ 1.19 m of water column) > $P_{gas,abs} = 113.20\ kPa$ ### Step 7: Physical interpretation A pressure of ~12 kPa gauge (≈ 120 cm H₂O) is **typical of a fully-charged Janata plant** and is sufficient to deliver gas through 10–20 m of small-bore pipe to a domestic stove or a 1–3 kW engine. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Factor Sensitivity Comparison The following table grades the *sensitivity* of biogas yield to each factor on a 1-to-5 scale (5 = extremely sensitive). This is the **most-likely 7-mark analytical question** in KTU 2024. | Factor | Sensitivity (1–5) | Direction of Effect | Cost to Control | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature | **5** | Yield doubles per +10 °C (in mesophilic) | High (heating coil) | | pH | **5** | Methanogenesis halts below 6.3 | Low (lime addition) | | C:N ratio | **4** | Deviation > 50% causes inhibition | Low (mixing feedstocks) | | Moisture | **4** | < 80% → clogging; > 95% → washout | Low (water addition) | | HRT | **3** | Shorter HRT → incomplete digestion | High (larger digester) | | Mixing | **3** | Reduces scum, improves rate by 20–30% | Medium (stirrer cost) | | Toxicity (NH₃, H₂S) | **5** | Sudden catastrophic failure | Medium (pretreatment) | | Particle size | **2** | Finer = faster hydrolysis, but more energy | Medium (grinder) | ## 3.3 Comparative Matrix: KVIC vs. Janata (the most-tested 7-marker) | Parameter | **KVIC Model** | **Janata Model** | |---|---|---| | **Type** | Floating-drum (movable) | Fixed-dome (immovable) | | **Volume / Pressure** | Variable V, **Constant P** | **Constant V**, Variable P | | **Gas Pressure** | ~7–10 cm H₂O (constant) | 0–100+ cm H₂O (varies) | | **Gas Quantity Indication** | **Visible** (drum height) | **Not visible** (must be calculated) | | **Material of Gas Holder** | **Mild steel** (corrodes) | **Brick / RCC / Ferrocement** | | **Corrosion Risk** | **High** (H₂S attacks steel) | **Negligible** | | **Plant Life** | 10 – 15 years | **20 – 30 years** | | **Initial Cost** | Higher (steel + masonry) | **Lower (~60% of KVIC)** | | **Maintenance** | High (paint, drum repair) | Low (no moving parts) | | **Skill Required for Construction** | Medium | **High** (skilled mason for leak-proof dome) | | **Suitability** | Household, 1–6 m³/day | Community, 5–100 m³/day | | **Scum Removal** | Easy (drum can be lifted) | **Difficult** | | **Gas Leakage** | Minimal (welded steel) | **Probable** through brick joints | | **Pressure Equation** | $P \approx W_{drum}/A$ | $P = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot H$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Anaerobic Digestion Process & Governing Factors ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Biomass Feedstock
Cow Dung / Food Waste] --> B[Hydrolysis Stage
Polymer to Monomer] B --> C[Acidogenesis Stage
VFAs and H2 Produced] C --> D[Acetogenesis
Acetate and CO2] D --> E[Methanogenesis
CH4 50 to 70 percent] E --> F[Biogas Output
Calorific 18 to 25 MJ per m3] T1[Temperature Control
Optimum 35 C] -.-> B T1 -.-> C T1 -.-> E P1[pH Buffer 6.8 to 7.2] -.-> C P1 -.-> E N1[C to N Ratio 25 to 30] -.-> B N1 -.-> D M1[Moisture 90 percent] -.-> A M1 -.-> B R1[HRT 30 to 50 days] -.-> F L1[OLR 1 to 3 kg VS per m3 per d] -.-> F X1[Mixing and Agitation] -.-> C X1 -.-> E I1[Inhibitors NH3 H2S Heavy Metals] -.-> E S1[Particle Size 5 to 10 mm] -.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — KVIC Floating-Drum Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SITE[KVIC FLOATING DRUM PLANT] IT[Inlet Mixing Tank] --> DS[Digester Well
Masonry Underground] DS -->|Overflow| OT[Outlet Displacement Chamber] DS -->|Gas Bubbles Rise| FD[Floating Steel Drum
Gas Holder] FD -->|Biogas| GO[Gas Outlet Pipe with Valve] FD -.->|Buoyancy| WS[Water Seal Ring] WS --> FD GP[Guide Pipe and Frame
Alignment] -.-> FD end GO --> BU[Domestic Burner or Engine] OT --> BM[Bio manure Collection] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Janata Fixed-Dome Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SITE2[JANATA FIXED DOME PLANT] IT2[Inlet Mixing Tank] --> IP[Inlet Pipe Slanted] IP --> DC[Digester Chamber
Masonry Cylinder] DC -->|Gas Pushes Slurry| OP[Outlet Pipe U Shaped] OP --> CT[Compensation or Expansion Tank] CT -.->|When Gas Used
Slurry Returns| DC DC --> DD[Fixed Brick or RCC Dome Top] DD --> GO2[Gas Outlet Pipe at Crown] GO2 --> BU2[Domestic Burner or Engine] end CT --> BM2[Bio manure Collection] ``` ## 4.4 Side-by-Side Block Comparison (KVIC vs. Janata) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph KVIC[KVIC PLANT] K1[Inlet] --> K2[Underground Digester] K2 --> K3[Floating Steel Drum] K3 --> K4[Gas Constant Pressure] end subgraph JAN[JANATA PLANT] J1[Inlet] --> J2[Underground Digester] J2 --> J3[Fixed Masonry Dome] J2 --> J4[Compensation Tank] J4 -.->|Slurry Returns| J2 J3 --> J5[Gas Variable Pressure] end ``` ## 4.5 Verbal Description of Physical Layout > [!NOTE] > **KVIC Plant — Physical Layout (for sketch in exam):** > A cylindrical masonry digester is built **underground**. On top of the digester slurry sits a **mild-steel drum**, open at the bottom, which floats like an upside-down bucket. A small **water-seal ring** surrounds the drum's rim to prevent gas leakage. A vertical **guide pipe** passes through the center of the drum (from a frame above) to keep the drum vertically aligned and prevent tilting. A **gas pipe** exits from the top dome of the drum, fitted with a valve leading to the kitchen stove. The **inlet** is a sloped ramp/chute from ground level into the digester; the **outlet** is a chamber on the opposite side, at slightly higher level, where displaced slurry overflows. > **Janata Plant — Physical Layout (for sketch in exam):** > A large **cylindrical masonry chamber** is built underground, capped with a **hemispherical or truncated dome roof** (made of brick or RCC). On one side, a smaller **compensation/expansion tank** is constructed at a slightly lower elevation, connected to the digester by a U-shaped outlet pipe that dips into the slurry. The **inlet pipe** enters the digester at mid-height from a mixing tank. The **gas outlet** is at the **crown of the dome**. There are **no moving metal parts** above ground. As gas accumulates, slurry is pushed through the outlet into the expansion tank; as gas is consumed, slurry flows back automatically (acting as a non-return system). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **List and briefly explain any THREE factors affecting biogas generation. Mention the optimum value of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — approximately 100 words):** > The three key factors affecting biogas generation are: > > 1. **Temperature (1 mark):** The optimum temperature is **35 °C** (mesophilic range: 30–40 °C). Methanogenic archaea are most active here, and gas production rate roughly doubles for every 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ rule). > > 2. **pH (1 mark):** The optimum pH is **6.8 – 7.2**. Below pH 6.3, methanogens are inhibited and the digester turns "sour", halting gas production. > > 3. **C:N ratio (1 mark):** The optimum Carbon-to-Nitrogen ratio is **25:1 to 30:1**. Cow dung naturally falls in this range; mixing it with high-C straws or low-N poultry droppings must be balanced. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between the KVIC and Janata biogas plant models on the basis of (i) construction material, (ii) gas pressure behaviour, and (iii) cost.** **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):** | Parameter | **KVIC Model** | **Janata Model** | |---|---|---| | (i) Construction material | **Mild-steel floating drum** over masonry digester (1 mark) | **Brick / RCC fixed dome** over masonry digester (1 mark) | | (ii) Gas pressure behaviour | **Constant** (~7–10 cm H₂O) (0.5 mark) | **Variable** (depends on slurry height difference) (0.5 mark) | | (iii) Cost | Higher (steel is expensive) (0.5 mark) | Lower (~60% of KVIC cost) (0.5 mark) | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **DO NOT** mix up "constant pressure" and "constant volume" between KVIC and Janata — this is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks**. > 2. **DO NOT** state C:N as 20:1 *or* 30:1 in isolation. KTU expects the **range 25:1 to 30:1** explicitly. > 3. **DO NOT** forget units in numerical problems — Janata pressure *must* end in **Pa or kPa**, not bare "m of water". > 4. **DO NOT** draw Janata with a moving part — examiners *will* deduct marks. The whole point is "fixed dome". --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**** **(a)** Explain the **factors affecting biogas generation** in detail. Discuss the role of temperature, pH, C:N ratio, moisture, and toxicity with optimum values. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Biogas generation is governed by the biochemical activity of anaerobic microorganisms, which is sensitive to many environmental parameters. **1. Temperature (1.5 marks):** - Three regimes exist: psychrophilic (< 20 °C), **mesophilic (30–40 °C, optimum 35 °C)**, and thermophilic (50–60 °C, optimum 55 °C). - Mesophilic digesters are most common in India; thermophilic digesters give **higher yield (0.50–0.70 m³/kg VS)** but require heating energy. - The **Q₁₀ rule** states that the reaction rate approximately doubles for every 10 °C rise within the mesophilic range. - Below 15 °C, methanogenesis nearly halts; above 60 °C, microbes are killed. **2. pH (1.5 marks):** - The optimum pH is **6.8 – 7.2**, slightly acidic to neutral. - Methanogenic archaea are the most pH-sensitive; their activity ceases below pH **6.3** (sour digester) and above pH **8.0**. - The system is naturally buffered by the **NH₄⁺/HCO₃⁻ equilibrium**; lime (Ca(OH)₂) or sodium bicarbonate is added during acid shock. **3. C:N Ratio (1.5 marks):** - Microbes require carbon for energy and nitrogen for cell synthesis. - The optimum **C:N = 25:1 to 30:1**. - High C:N (e.g., rice straw, 70:1) → nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient; digestion is slow. - Low C:N (e.g., poultry droppings, 15:1) → excess nitrogen is released as **ammonia**, raising pH and inhibiting methanogens. - Cow dung at C:N ≈ 25:1 is considered the "Goldilocks" feedstock. **4. Moisture Content (1 mark):** - The optimum moisture is **~90% (i.e., TS ≈ 10%)**. - Below 80% moisture, the slurry becomes too thick, scum forms, and microbes cannot move. - Above 95% moisture, microbes are washed out before completing digestion. **5. Toxicity / Inhibitors (1.5 marks):** - **Ammonia (NH₃) > 1700 mg/L** → shifts equilibrium toward toxic free NH₃, killing methanogens. - **H₂S > 200 mg/L** → toxic to archaea and corrodes steel in KVIC plants. - **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFA) > 2000 mg/L** → indicates acidogenesis is running ahead of methanogenesis. - Heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Cr, Ni), antibiotics, and detergents also disrupt microbial consortia. - **Remedy:** feedstock pre-treatment, dilution, and iron chloride dosing for sulfide control. --- **(b)** Describe the **KVIC floating-drum biogas plant** with a neat sketch. Explain its working principle, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The KVIC (Khadi and Village Industries Commission) model is a **floating-drum type** biogas plant standardized in India for household-scale (1–6 m³/day) applications. It is a *constant-pressure, variable-volume* reactor. **Construction (2 marks):** - A **cylindrical masonry digester well** is built underground. - A **mild-steel drum** (gas holder), open at the bottom, is inverted and placed over the digester. - A **water-seal ring** at the rim prevents gas leakage. - A **central guide pipe and frame** keeps the drum aligned. - An **inlet chute** (for fresh dung slurry) and an **outlet displacement chamber** (for overflow slurry) are built at ground level. - A **gas outlet pipe** with a valve exits from the top of the drum. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - Daily-charged dung slurry undergoes anaerobic digestion in the well. - Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) bubbles rise and collect in the **inverted drum**. - As gas accumulates, the drum **rises upward**; as gas is drawn for cooking, the drum **falls downward**. - The drum's weight divided by its projected base area gives a **constant gas pressure** of approximately **7–10 cm of water column**. - The position of the drum is a **direct visual indicator** of the stored gas volume. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Constant gas pressure → stable burner flame. - Visible gas-quantity indication. - Easy to start, stop, and inspect; mass-produced spares. - Standardized design → predictable gas output. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Mild steel **corrodes** due to H₂S (1–2 mm/year loss) — needs repainting every 1–2 years. - Higher **initial cost** due to steel. - Drum exposed to weather; not ideal for capacities > 60 m³/day. - The floating drum is a **moving part** subject to mechanical wear. **Diagram (textual description, in lieu of the 2-D drawing the student would supply):** Show the underground masonry digester, the floating inverted drum with central guide pipe, the water-seal ring, the gas outlet pipe leading to a stove, the inlet chute, and the outlet displacement chamber. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Janata fixed-dome biogas plant**. Discuss its working principle and the function of the **compensation tank**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (0.5 mark):** The Janata model is a **fixed-dome, low-cost biogas plant** derived from the Chinese design, suitable for community and institutional use (5–100 m³/day). It is a *constant-volume, variable-pressure* plant. **Construction (2 marks):** - A **cylindrical masonry digester chamber** built underground. - A **fixed dome roof** (hemispherical or truncated) made of brick, RCC, or ferrocement. - A **compensation (expansion) tank** on one side, slightly lower than the digester top, connected via a U-shaped outlet pipe. - An **inlet pipe** from a mixing tank. - A **gas outlet pipe** at the crown of the dome, fitted with a valve. - **No moving parts** above ground. **Working Principle (2.5 marks):** - As biogas is generated, it accumulates in the rigid dome. - Since the dome cannot move, the gas **displaces slurry** through the outlet pipe into the compensation tank. - The **height difference (H)** between the slurry surface in the digester and that in the compensation tank is directly proportional to the **gas pressure**: - $P_{gas} = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot H$ - As gas is consumed, the pressure drops, and slurry **flows back** from the compensation tank into the digester automatically. - The **maximum gas pressure** is reached when the compensation tank is full. **Function of the Compensation Tank (2 marks):** 1. **Stores displaced slurry** when the digester dome fills with gas. 2. **Returns slurry** to the digester when gas is consumed — acting as a **built-in non-return valve**. 3. **Maintains digester pressure** within safe limits by providing a controlled expansion volume. 4. Acts as a **gas-pressure indicator** — its slurry level visually indicates the gas pressure. 5. Can serve as a **small manure-collection point**. --- **(b)** Compare the **KVIC and Janata models** on the basis of: (i) type of gas holder, (ii) gas pressure behaviour, (iii) gas quantity indication, (iv) plant life, (v) initial cost, and (vi) gas leakage tendency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | S.No | Parameter | **KVIC Model** | **Janata Model** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Type of gas holder | **Floating mild-steel drum** (1.5 marks) | **Fixed masonry / RCC dome** (1.5 marks) | | 2 | Gas pressure behaviour | **Constant** (~7–10 cm H₂O); governed by drum weight (0.5 mark) | **Variable** (0–100+ cm H₂O); governed by $\rho g H$ (0.5 mark) | | 3 | Gas quantity indication | **Visible** — drum height directly shows gas volume (0.5 mark) | **Not visible** — must be inferred from compensation-tank level (0.5 mark) | | 4 | Plant life | **10 – 15 years** (steel corrodes) (0.5 mark) | **20 – 30 years** (masonry is durable) (0.5 mark) | | 5 | Initial cost | **Higher** (steel + masonry) (0.5 mark) | **Lower** (~60% of KVIC) (0.5 mark) | | 6 | Gas leakage tendency | **Minimal** (welded steel joints) (0.5 mark) | **Probable** through brick joints; needs skilled mason (0.5 mark) | **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** - The **KVIC model** is best for **small household plants** (1–6 m³/day) where constant pressure and visual gas indication matter. - The **Janata model** is preferred for **community / institutional plants** (5–100 m³/day) where long life, low maintenance, and low cost are priorities. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MEMORIZE THESE NUMBERS — they are the KTU 2024 highest-yield points:** - ⏱ **Temperature (mesophilic optimum):** **35 °C** (range 30–40 °C) - 🧪 **pH optimum:** **6.8 – 7.2**; **sour digester threshold:** pH < 6.3 - ⚖️ **C:N ratio optimum:** **25:1 to 30:1** (cow dung ≈ 25:1) - 💧 **Moisture optimum:** **90%** (TS ≈ 10%) - ⌛ **HRT (mesophilic, cow dung):** **30 – 50 days** - 📊 **OLR (mesophilic CSTR):** **1.0 – 3.0 kg VS / m³·day** - 🔥 **Biogas calorific value:** **18 – 25 MJ/m³** - 💨 **Biogas composition:** **CH₄ = 50–70 %**, **CO₂ = 30–40 %** - ☠️ **NH₃ toxicity threshold:** > **1700 mg/L** - ☠️ **H₂S toxicity threshold:** > **200 mg/L** - ⚡ **Q₁₀ rule:** methanogenesis rate **doubles per +10 °C** (mesophilic) > ✅ **Two-Minute Mnemonic — "T P C M H O"** for the six core factors: > **T**emperature, **P**H, **C**:**N** ratio, **M**oisture, **H**RT, **O**LR > ✅ **KVIC in one line:** *Floating steel drum → constant pressure → visible gas → corrodes → costly → small scale* > ✅ **Janata in one line:** *Fixed brick dome → variable pressure → no moving part → long life → cheap → large scale* > ✅ **Key Formula to remember:** > $$P_{Janata} = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot H \quad \text{with} \quad \rho_{slurry} \approx 1100\ kg/m^3$$ > $$P_{KVIC} = \frac{W_{drum}}{A_{drum}} \approx 7-10\ cm\ H_2O\ (\text{constant})$$ > ✅ **Common Pitfall — do NOT confuse:** > - KVIC = Constant Pressure, Variable Volume > - Janata = Constant Volume, Variable Pressure > ✅ **Construction Material Quick-Recall:** > - KVIC gas holder = **Mild Steel** > - Janata gas holder = **Brick / RCC / Ferrocement** > ✅ **Cost & Life Quick-Recall:** > - KVIC: Higher cost, **shorter life (10–15 yr)** > - Janata: Lower cost, **longer life (20–30 yr)** ---

topics.title: Hydrogen Energy - Various Routes for Production of Hydrogen Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT726 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Energy - Various Routes for Production of Hydrogen Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hydrogen Energy?

**Hydrogen Energy** refers to the energy harnessed from hydrogen (H₂), the lightest and most abundant chemical element in the universe. Unlike fossil fuels, hydrogen is an **energy carrier** rather than a primary energy source — meaning it must be produced from hydrogen-rich compounds (mainly water, biomass, or hydrocarbons) and then converted into usable energy via fuel cells or combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydrogen is classified as a **secondary energy carrier** (similar to electricity). Its energy content on a mass basis is approximately **142 MJ/kg (HHV)** or **120 MJ/kg (LHV)**, which is nearly **2.75 times** that of gasoline on a mass basis, but on a volume basis it is significantly lower due to its low density.

### 1.2 Formal KTU Definition

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:
> *"Hydrogen energy encompasses the production, storage, transportation, and utilization of molecular hydrogen (H₂) as a clean energy vector, primarily for applications in fuel cells, internal combustion engines, and industrial processes, with water as its primary combustion by-product."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Hydrogen as a Battery in a Gas Form"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of hydrogen as a **"gaseous battery"**. Just as a battery stores electrical energy chemically and releases it on demand, hydrogen stores chemical energy (in its H–H bond) and releases it when reacted with oxygen in a fuel cell. However, unlike a battery, hydrogen must first be "manufactured" (produced) from sources like water or natural gas — this is the **production route**.

The basic chemistry is elegantly simple:
- **Energy In** (electricity/heat) → Split water → **Hydrogen Stored** → Use later for energy

### 1.4 The Hydrogen Production Color Code (KTU Exam Favorite!)

KTU frequently tests the **color-coded classification** of hydrogen based on the production method:

| Color | Source/Method | Carbon Emissions |
|-------|--------------|------------------|
| **Grey Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | High CO₂ |
| **Brown Hydrogen** | Coal Gasification | Very High CO₂ |
| **Blue Hydrogen** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Low CO₂ |
| **Green Hydrogen** | Electrolysis using Renewable Electricity | Zero CO₂ |
| **Pink Hydrogen** | Electrolysis using Nuclear Electricity | Near-zero CO₂ |
| **Turquoise Hydrogen** | Methane Pyrolysis | Solid Carbon (no CO₂) |

### 1.5 Why Hydrogen Energy Matters for KTU Engineers

- **Zero-carbon emission** at point of use (only water is produced)
- Highest **energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel
- Versatile — can power vehicles, industries, and grid systems
- India targets **5 million metric tonnes (MMT)** of Green Hydrogen production by 2030 (National Green Hydrogen Mission)
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrogen Production Routes

Hydrogen can be produced through **three primary categories** of methods, each with distinct principles:

### **A. Thermochemical Methods (Heat-driven)**
- Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)
- Partial Oxidation (POX)
- Autothermal Reforming (ATR)
- Coal Gasification
- Biomass Gasification
- Thermochemical Water Splitting

### **B. Electrochemical Methods (Electricity-driven)**
- Conventional Water Electrolysis
- Alkaline Electrolyzer
- Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) Electrolyzer
- Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC)

### **C. Biological Methods (Bio-driven)**
- Biophotolysis
- Photofermentation
- Dark Fermentation

### **D. Photolytic Methods (Light-driven)**
- Photobiological Water Splitting
- Photoelectrochemical (PEC) Water Splitting

---

## 2.2 Route 1: Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Most Commercial Method

**Principle:** Steam reacts with methane (CH₄) at high temperature in the presence of a catalyst to produce hydrogen and carbon monoxide (syngas).

**Reactions:**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction:**

$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Process Conditions:**
- Temperature: **700–850 °C**
- Pressure: **3–25 bar**
- Catalyst: Nickel-based (Ni/Al₂O₃)

**Hydrogen Yield:** Approximately **4 moles of H₂ per mole of CH₄** (theoretical maximum).

### **B. Route 2: Partial Oxidation (POX)**

$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- Exothermic (heat-generating) process
- Operates at **1200–1500 °C**
- Lower H₂:CO ratio than SMR

### **C. Route 3: Autothermal Reforming (ATR)**

Combination of SMR (endothermic) + POX (exothermic), thermally self-sustaining.

$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}H_2O + \frac{1}{4}O_2 \rightarrow \frac{5}{2}H_2 + CO + \frac{1}{2}H_2O$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** ATR is preferred in modern plants because it is **thermally neutral** (self-sustaining) and offers a favorable H₂:CO ratio of approximately **2.5:1**, ideal for Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.

### **D. Route 4: Water Electrolysis**

**Principle:** Electrical current splits water into hydrogen and oxygen using two electrodes in an electrolyte.

$$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)} \quad E^0 = -1.23 \text{ V}$$

- **Cathode (reduction):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$
- **Anode (oxidation):** $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$

**Theoretical Energy Requirement:** **237.2 kJ/mol** of water (or **39.4 kWh/kg** of H₂)

### **E. Route 5: Biomass Gasification**

$$Biomass + O_2/Steam \rightarrow CO + CO_2 + H_2 + CH_4 + \text{tar}$$

- Operating Temperature: **800–1200 °C**
- Air-to-fuel ratio: 0.2–0.4
- Syngas cleanup required

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Value/Unit** | **Application** |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Energy Content (HHV) of H₂ | $Q_{HHV} = 141.9$ MJ/kg | 141.9 MJ/kg | Combustion calculations |
| Energy Content (LHV) of H₂ | $Q_{LHV} = 119.9$ MJ/kg | 119.9 MJ/kg | Fuel cell calculations |
| Theoretical Electrolysis Voltage | $E^0 = \frac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | 1.23 V | Minimum cell voltage |
| Thermoneutral Voltage | $E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | 1.48 V | Actual operating voltage |
| Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Mass of H₂ produced |
| Electrolyzer Efficiency | $\eta_{elec} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}$ | % | System efficiency |
| SMR H₂ Yield | $Y_{H_2} = 4 \cdot n_{CH_4}$ | mol H₂ | Maximum yield |
| Energy Return Factor | $EROI = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Sustainability metric |

**Where:**
- $M$ = Molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $\Delta G$ = Gibbs free energy = 237.2 kJ/mol
- $\Delta H$ = Enthalpy change = 285.8 kJ/mol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The **thermodynamic minimum voltage** for water electrolysis is **1.23 V**, but in practice, electrolyzers operate at **1.8–2.5 V** due to overpotential losses. The **Faraday efficiency** of a well-designed PEM electrolyzer exceeds **99%**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Production Route** | **Industrial Use Case** | **Maturity Level** |
|---------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| SMR (Grey H₂) | Refineries, Ammonia plants | Commercial (95% of global H₂) |
| Electrolysis (Green H₂) | Fuel cell vehicles, Grid storage | Pilot to Commercial |
| Biomass Gasification | Rural electrification | Demonstration |
| Photoelectrochemical | Solar hydrogen farms | Laboratory |
| Coal Gasification | Large-scale industrial H₂ | Commercial (China-led) |
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Energy Requirement for Water Electrolysis

We need to derive the minimum energy required to split one mole of water into hydrogen and oxygen.

### **Step 1: Write the Reaction**

$$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$$

### **Step 2: Identify Thermodynamic State Functions**

From standard thermodynamic tables (at 25 °C, 1 atm):
- $\Delta H^0$ = **+285.8 kJ/mol** (endothermic, heat absorbed)
- $\Delta G^0$ = **+237.2 kJ/mol** (Gibbs free energy change)
- $\Delta S^0$ = **+163.1 J/mol·K** (entropy change)

### **Step 3: Derive the Reversible Cell Voltage (E⁰)**

The relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential:

$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$$

Solving for $E^0$:

$$E^0 = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$$

**Substituting values:**
- $n = 4$ electrons (2H₂O → O₂ requires transfer of 4 electrons)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

$$E^0 = -\frac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{4 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^0 = -\frac{237{,}200}{385{,}940}$$

$$E^0 = -0.6146 \text{ V per electron pair}$$

Multiplying by 2 (for water): $E^0 = -1.23$ V

$$E^0 = -1.23 \text{ V}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the reaction is non-spontaneous and requires external electrical energy.

### **Step 4: Derive the Thermoneutral Voltage (Eₜₙ)**

Using enthalpy change:

$$E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} = \frac{285{,}800}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 0.7405 \text{ V per e⁻}$$

$$E_{tn} = 1.48 \text{ V}$$

### **Step 5: Physical Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> - At **E = 1.23 V**: Only electrical work is supplied (isothermal operation)
> - At **E = 1.48 V**: Heat + electrical work both contribute (adiabatic operation)
> - **Practical operation**: 1.8–2.5 V (due to overpotentials: activation + ohmic + concentration)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrogen Mass Produced via Electrolysis (Faraday's Law)

### **Step 1: Statement of Faraday's Law**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of H₂ produced (grams)
- $M$ = molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $n$ = electrons per molecule = 2
- $F$ = 96,485 C/mol

### **Step 2: Calculate Theoretical H₂ Production Rate**

For 1 Ampere for 1 hour:
$$m = \frac{2.016 \times 1 \times 3600}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{7257.6}{192{,}970} = 0.0376 \text{ g}$$

$$m \approx 0.0376 \text{ g H}_2 \text{ per Ampere-hour}$$

### **Step 3: Energy Required per kg of H₂**

Theoretical minimum:
$$E_{min} = \frac{39.4 \text{ kWh}}{\text{kg of H}_2}$$

For a practical electrolyzer operating at 70% efficiency (HHV basis):
$$E_{actual} = \frac{39.4}{0.70} = 56.3 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steam Methane Reforming — Hydrogen Yield Analysis

### **Step 1: Primary Reforming Reaction**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \text{(1 mol CH}_4 \text{ gives 3 mol H}_2\text{)}$$

### **Step 2: Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction**

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(1 mol CO gives 1 mol H}_2\text{)}$$

### **Step 3: Total Hydrogen Yield**

Adding both reactions:
$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$$

**Maximum yield:** 4 mol H₂ per mol CH₄

### **Step 4: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Analysis**

For complete combustion of 1 mol CH₄:
$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

**Oxygen required:** 2 mol = 4 mol O atoms

If the SMR process uses **O₂ as feed instead of H₂O** (partial oxidation):
$$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \text{(yield: 2 mol H}_2\text{)}$$

The ratio of H₂ yield is:
$$\frac{\text{SMR}}{\text{POX}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMR is twice as efficient in H₂ yield as POX**, but it is endothermic (requires external heat), while POX is exothermic.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **SMR** | **POX** | **Electrolysis** | **Biomass Gasification** |
|---------------|---------|---------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Feedstock | CH₄ + H₂O | CH₄ + O₂ | H₂O + Electricity | Biomass + O₂/Steam |
| Temp (°C) | 700–850 | 1200–1500 | 60–1000 | 800–1200 |
| Pressure (bar) | 3–25 | 1–40 | 1–700 | 1–30 |
| H₂ Purity (%) | 95–99 | 90–95 | 99.999 | 85–95 |
| Energy Efficiency (%) | 65–75 | 50–60 | 60–80 | 35–50 |
| CO₂ Emissions (kg/kg H₂) | 9–12 | 11–13 | 0 (green) | 0–2 (if bio-sequestered) |
| Capital Cost | High | High | Very High | Medium |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | Commercial | Pilot |
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Overview of Hydrogen Production Routes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hydrogen Production Methods] --> B[Thermochemical Routes]
    A --> C[Electrochemical Routes]
    A --> D[Biological Routes]
    A --> E[Photolytic Routes]
    
    B --> B1[Steam Methane Reforming]
    B --> B2[Partial Oxidation]
    B --> B3[Autothermal Reforming]
    B --> B4[Coal Gasification]
    B --> B5[Biomass Gasification]
    
    C --> C1[Alkaline Electrolyzer]
    C --> C2[PEM Electrolyzer]
    C --> C3[Solid Oxide Electrolyzer]
    
    D --> D1[Biophotolysis]
    D --> D2[Photo Fermentation]
    D --> D3[Dark Fermentation]
    
    E --> E1[Photoelectrochemical]
    E --> E2[Photobiological]
    
    B1 --> F[H2 Output]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) Water Splitting

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Solar Radiation Photons] --> B[Semiconductor Photoelectrode]
    B --> C[Photoexcitation Electron-Hole Pair]
    C --> D[Electron Transport to Cathode]
    C --> E[Hole Transport to Anode]
    
    W[Water Molecules] --> F[Anode Surface Oxidation]
    F --> G[Oxygen Release O2]
    W2[Water Molecules] --> H[Cathode Surface Reduction]
    H --> I[Hydrogen Release H2]
    
    D --> H
    E --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In PEC cells, semiconductor materials (e.g., TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, BiVO₄) absorb sunlight and generate electron-hole pairs that directly split water. The **solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency** of state-of-the-art PEC cells is approximately **2–5%**, far below the **15–20%** required for commercial viability.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Grey, Blue, and Green hydrogen with respect to production method and carbon footprint.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Grey Hydrogen:** Produced via Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) of natural gas **without** carbon capture. High CO₂ emissions (~9–12 kg CO₂/kg H₂). Currently accounts for ~95% of global hydrogen production.
- **Blue Hydrogen:** Produced via SMR **with Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)** capturing up to 90–95% of CO₂ emissions. Lower CO₂ footprint (~1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂).
- **Green Hydrogen:** Produced via water electrolysis using **renewable electricity** (solar/wind). Zero direct CO₂ emissions. Considered the most sustainable pathway.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three thermochemical methods of hydrogen production and state the operating temperature for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** 700–850 °C (endothermic, Ni catalyst)
2. **Partial Oxidation (POX):** 1200–1500 °C (exothermic)
3. **Autothermal Reforming (ATR):** 850–1000 °C (thermally neutral, combined SMR + POX)
4. **Coal Gasification:** 900–1500 °C
5. **Biomass Gasification:** 800–1200 °C

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for hydrogen production. Include the primary reactions, operating conditions, and a process flow description.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **minimum theoretical energy** required to produce 1 kg of hydrogen via water electrolysis. Given: $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol, M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) — Detailed Explanation:**

**1. Principle:**
Steam Methane Reforming is the most widely used industrial process for hydrogen production, accounting for approximately **48% of global hydrogen**. It involves reacting methane (CH₄) with steam (H₂O) at high temperatures in the presence of a nickel-based catalyst to produce syngas (CO + H₂), which is then further processed via the water-gas shift reaction to maximize hydrogen yield.

**2. Primary Reactions:**

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Overall Reaction:**

$$CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2 \quad \Delta H = +165 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**3. Process Flow Steps:**

- **Step 1: Feed Preparation:** Natural gas is desulfurized to prevent catalyst poisoning.
- **Step 2: Steam Generation:** Water is converted to high-pressure superheated steam.
- **Step 3: Primary Reforming:** Preheated CH₄ + steam mixture enters reformer tubes packed with Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 700–850 °C and 3–25 bar pressure.
- **Step 4: Water-Gas Shift (WGS):** The CO produced is reacted with additional steam in a two-stage shift converter (high-temperature shift at 350 °C, low-temperature shift at 200 °C) to produce more H₂.
- **Step 5: CO₂ Removal:** The CO₂ is absorbed using amine-based solvents (e.g., MEA, MDEA).
- **Step 6: Purification:** Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) separates H₂ to **99.99% purity**.
- **Step 7: Compression and Storage:** Pure H₂ is compressed to 200–700 bar for storage.

**4. Operating Conditions Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Temperature | 700–850 °C |
| Pressure | 3–25 bar |
| Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C) | 2.5–4.0 |
| Catalyst | Ni/Al₂O₃ |
| H₂ Purity | 99.99% |
| Energy Efficiency | 65–75% (HHV) |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Calculation of Minimum Theoretical Energy for 1 kg H₂:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol
- M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol
- F = 96,485 C/mol

**Step 1: Moles of H₂ in 1 kg**

$$n_{H_2} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Molar Mass}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$n_{H_2} = 496.03 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Total Energy Required (Enthalpy Basis)**

$$E_{total} = n_{H_2} \times \Delta H^0 = 496.03 \times 285.8 \text{ kJ}$$

$$E_{total} = 141{,}765 \text{ kJ} = 141.77 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3: Convert to kWh**

$$E_{total} = \frac{141{,}765 \text{ kJ}}{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}}$$

$$E_{total} = 39.38 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2$$

**Step 4: Calculate Thermoneutral Voltage**

$$E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H^0}{nF} = \frac{285{,}800 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E_{tn} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Result Summary**

$$\boxed{E_{min} = 39.4 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2 \approx 141.8 \text{ MJ/kg H}_2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Students often confuse kJ with kWh. Remember: **1 kWh = 3600 kJ**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator (since n = 2 for H₂O → H₂ reaction)
> 3. **Confusing ΔG with ΔH:** ΔG = 237.2 kJ/mol gives **1.23 V** (reversible voltage), while ΔH = 285.8 kJ/mol gives **1.48 V** (thermoneutral voltage)
> 4. **Not showing unit conversions explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing intermediate steps

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **electrolysis of water** for hydrogen production. Compare **Alkaline, PEM, and Solid Oxide** electrolyzers based on electrolyte, operating temperature, and efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEM electrolyzer operates at a current of **500 A** and a cell voltage of **2.0 V**. Calculate the **hydrogen production rate (kg/day)** and the **system efficiency** (based on HHV). Given: Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/mol, M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol, LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg, HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Principle of Water Electrolysis:**

Water electrolysis uses electrical energy to dissociate water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen. The basic cell consists of two electrodes (anode and cathode) immersed in an electrolyte, separated by a diaphragm/membrane.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)} \quad E^0 = -1.23 \text{ V}$$

**2. Half-Cell Reactions:**

- **Cathode (Reduction):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ (in alkaline)
- **Anode (Oxidation):** $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$ (in acidic/PEM)

**3. Comparison of Electrolyzer Types:**

| Parameter | Alkaline | PEM | Solid Oxide |
|-----------|----------|-----|-------------|
| **Electrolyte** | KOH/NaOH (liquid) | Polymer Membrane (Nafion) | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) |
| **Operating Temp** | 60–90 °C | 50–80 °C | 700–900 °C |
| **Efficiency** | 60–70% | 70–80% | 75–85% |
| **Current Density** | 0.2–0.4 A/cm² | 1.0–2.0 A/cm² | 0.3–1.0 A/cm² |
| **Purity of H₂** | 99.5–99.9% | 99.99% | 99.9% |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Maturity** | Commercial | Commercial | Pilot |
| **Dynamic Operation** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Current, $I$ = 500 A
- Cell voltage, $V$ = 2.0 V
- F = 96,485 C/mol
- M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol
- HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg

**Step 1: Calculate Moles of H₂ Produced per Second (Faraday's Law)**

$$n_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I}{2F}$$

For 1 second:
$$n_{H_2} = \frac{500}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 2.591 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Mass per Second**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times M = 2.591 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 5.223 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to kg/day**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,day} = 5.223 \times 10^{-3} \times 86400$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,day} = 451.27 \text{ g/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,day} = 0.451 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Electrical Energy Input (kWh/day)**

$$E_{input} = V \times I \times t = 2.0 \times 500 \times 86400$$

$$E_{input} = 86{,}400{,}000 \text{ J} = 86{,}400 \text{ kJ}$$

$$E_{input} = \frac{86{,}400}{3600} = 24 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Energy Output (HHV basis)**

$$E_{output} = m_{H_2} \times HHV = 0.451 \times 141.9 \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_{output} = 64.00 \text{ MJ} = 17.78 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 6: System Efficiency**

$$\eta_{system} = \frac{E_{output}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{system} = \frac{17.78}{24} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{system} = 74.08\%}$$

**Step 7: Final Answer Summary**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| H₂ Production Rate | **0.451 kg/day** |
| Energy Input | 24 kWh/day |
| Energy Output (HHV) | 17.78 kWh/day |
| System Efficiency | **74.08%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting n = 2:** Students often use n = 1 or n = 4. For H₂ production, **n = 2 electrons per molecule of H₂**
> 2. **Time unit confusion:** Always convert days to seconds (**1 day = 86,400 s**) before applying Faraday's law
> 3. **HHV vs LHV:** Be explicit which heating value is used; KTU questions often specify
> 4. **Cell stack voltage:** If multiple cells are in series, multiply cell voltage by number of cells
> 5. **Significant figures:** Maintain at least 3-4 significant figures in intermediate steps

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Checklist for Hydrogen Energy Production:**

- [ ] Hydrogen is a **secondary energy carrier**, not a primary energy source
- [ ] H₂ has the **highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel (142 MJ/kg HHV)
- [ ] **Grey H₂** = SMR (no CCS); **Blue H₂** = SMR + CCS; **Green H₂** = Electrolysis + Renewables
- [ ] **SMR reaction:** $CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$ (4 mol H₂/mol CH₄ maximum)
- [ ] **WGS reaction:** $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ (critical for maximizing H₂ yield)
- [ ] **POX reaction:** $CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO + 2H_2$ (exothermic, 1200-1500°C)
- [ ] **ATR** = SMR + POX combination (thermally self-sustaining, preferred in modern plants)
- [ ] **Theoretical electrolysis voltage** = 1.23 V; **Thermoneutral voltage** = 1.48 V
- [ ] **Practical electrolyzer voltage** = 1.8–2.5 V (due to overpotentials)
- [ ] **Faraday's Law:** $m = \frac{MIt}{nF}$ with **n = 2** for H₂
- [ ] **Energy required:** ~39.4 kWh/kg H₂ (theoretical); ~50–55 kWh/kg (practical)
- [ ] **Alkaline electrolyzers** use liquid KOH/NaOH (60-90°C, mature technology)
- [ ] **PEM electrolyzers** use Nafion membrane (50-80°C, high current density, fast response)
- [ ] **SOEC** uses ceramic electrolyte (700-900°C, highest efficiency but least mature)
- [ ] **Biomass gasification** occurs at 800-1200°C with H₂ purity of 85-95%
- [ ] **Photoelectrochemical (PEC)** uses semiconductor electrodes to split water with sunlight
- [ ] **Biophotolysis** uses algae/cyanobacteria to produce H₂ via photosynthesis
- [ ] **Dark fermentation** operates without light using anaerobic bacteria on organic substrates
- [ ] **Currently ~95%** of global H₂ is from fossil fuels (SMR + coal gasification)
- [ ] **India's National Green Hydrogen Mission** targets **5 MMT** production by 2030
- [ ] The **color code** (Grey/Blue/Green/Pink/Turquoise) is a **frequently asked KTU question**

**Key Constants to Memorize:**
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol
- HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg
- LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg
- $\Delta G^0 = 237.2$ kJ/mol → $E^0 = 1.23$ V
- $\Delta H^0 = 285.8$ kJ/mol → $E_{tn} = 1.48$ V

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 pattern emphasizes **numerical problems on Faraday's Law**, **comparison of production methods**, and **color-coding of hydrogen**. Focus on derivations and process flow diagrams for full marks.
